topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Notches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Notches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Notch?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **notch** is a calibrated opening (of a defined geometric shape) cut into the sidewall of a tank or reservoir, through which a liquid is allowed to flow. It is essentially a **measuring device** used to determine the rate of flow (discharge) of liquid from a tank, by relating the **head of liquid above the notch sill** to the **actual discharge**.

In simple engineering language, a notch is a deliberate "cut-out" made on the side of a tank at the bottom (or near the bottom). When liquid fills up in the tank and overflows through this cut-out, the height of the liquid surface (called the **Head, H**) can be directly linked to how much liquid is flowing per second.

### Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a notch as a "tape measure" for flowing water.**
> Imagine you have a bathtub and you cut a small rectangular or triangular hole near its base. The water will pour out. If the water level inside the tub is high, water gushes out fast. If the level is low, water trickles out slowly. The height of water inside is a direct *visual* indicator of the discharge rate. A notch simply formalizes this concept into a precise engineering tool.

Real-world examples include:
- Spillways of small dams
- Irrigation channel dividers
- Effluent flow measurement in chemical plants
- Hydrology labs for calibrating flow in open channels

---

## 1.2 Types of Notches

| Type | Geometry | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular Notch** | A simple rectangular opening of width *b* | Large, steady discharges |
| **Triangular / V-Notch** | A V-shaped opening with apex angle *θ* | Small, precise discharges (more sensitive at low heads) |
| **Trapezoidal Notch** | Combination of rectangle and triangle | Compromise of both ranges |
| **Stepped Notch** | Step-shaped opening | Specialized industrial use |

---

## 1.3 What is "Calibration" of a Notch?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration** is the process of establishing a reliable, empirical relationship between the **head (H) over the notch sill** and the **actual discharge (Q)**. The result of a calibration is a **Calibration Curve** (a graph of H vs Q) and a constant called the **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**.

## 1.4 What is the Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** is a dimensionless number (typically between **0.6 and 0.65** for rectangular notches and **0.58 to 0.62** for V-notches) that accounts for all energy losses (friction, contraction, viscosity, turbulence) that occur when real fluid flows through the notch, as compared to the **ideal theoretical discharge** predicted by Bernoulli's equation.

Mathematically:
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

Once $C_d$ is known for a given notch and liquid, the notch becomes a permanent **flow meter**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why Does a Notch Work?

The principle rests on **Torricelli's Law**, which is itself derived from **Bernoulli's Equation**. Consider a tank with a notch cut on its side. Water at the free surface is at atmospheric pressure and (ideally) at rest. As it emerges from the notch, the velocity of the water column is purely due to the conversion of **potential head (H)** into **kinetic head (v²/2g)**.

Assumptions made in the **theoretical** discharge derivation:
1. The liquid surface remains horizontal and steady (steady flow).
2. The velocity of approach (the water moving toward the notch) is negligible.
3. The discharge is *frictionless* (ideal fluid).
4. The contraction of the flowing jet is considered *complete* (for a simple theoretical model).
5. Atmospheric pressure acts on the free surface and the discharged jet.

In reality, all 5 assumptions are violated — and that is exactly **why we need a Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$ < 1)**.

---

## 2.2 Discharge Through a Rectangular Notch

Consider a rectangular notch of width **b**, with liquid flowing under a head **H** measured above the sill (the bottom edge) of the notch.

### Derivation Logic (Stepwise):

1. Consider a thin horizontal strip at a depth **h** below the free liquid surface, of thickness **dh**.
2. The width of this strip = **b** (notch width).
3. The length of the strip through which flow occurs = **b − (contraction correction, taken theoretically as 0)**.
4. The theoretical velocity through this strip (Torricelli's Law): 
$$v = \sqrt{2g \cdot h}$$
5. The theoretical discharge through this elemental strip:
$$dQ_{th} = b \cdot dh \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot h}$$
6. Integrating from $h = 0$ to $h = H$:
$$Q_{th} = b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H} h^{1/2} \, dh = \frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$
7. Multiply by $C_d$ to get actual discharge:
$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** For a rectangular notch, discharge varies as the **3/2 power of head (H)**, i.e., $Q \propto H^{1.5}$. This is why even small head changes produce noticeable flow changes.

---

## 2.3 Discharge Through a Triangular (V-Notch)

Consider a V-notch with apex angle **θ** at the centerline.

### Derivation Logic:

1. Consider a thin strip at depth **h** below the free surface, of thickness **dh**.
2. The width of the strip at depth **h** from the vertex:
$$b = 2(H - h)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$
3. The discharge through this strip:
$$dQ_{th} = 2(H - h)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$
4. Integrating from $h = 0$ to $h = H$:
$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g} \int_{0}^{H}(H - h)\sqrt{h}\, dh$$
5. On integration:
$$Q_{th} = 2 \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15}\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$
6. With $C_d$:
$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** For a V-notch, $Q \propto H^{2.5}$. This makes the V-notch **more sensitive at low heads** than a rectangular notch.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the absolute minimum you must memorize for KTU ESE & Lab Viva.**

| Parameter | Rectangular Notch | Triangular (V) Notch |
|---|---|---|
| **Notch Geometry** | Width $b$ | Apex angle $\theta$ |
| **Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$** | $\frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$ | $\frac{8}{15} \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$ |
| **Actual Discharge $Q_{act}$** | $C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | $C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ |
| **Coefficient of Discharge $C_d$** | $\frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $\frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ |
| **Typical $C_d$ value** | 0.60 – 0.66 | 0.58 – 0.62 |
| **Head–Discharge Relation** | $Q \propto H^{1.5}$ | $Q \propto H^{2.5}$ |
| **Sensitivity at low H** | Lower | Higher (preferred) |
| **Volume collected in time $t$** | $V = Q_{act} \cdot t$ | $V = Q_{act} \cdot t$ |
| **Experimental $Q_{act}$ from tank** | $\frac{A_{tank} \cdot \Delta h}{t}$ | $\frac{A_{tank} \cdot \Delta h}{t}$ |
| **Unit of $Q$** | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| **Unit of $H$** | m (above sill) | m (above vertex) |
| **Unit of $b$** | m | – |
| **Unit of $\theta$** | – | degrees or radians |
| **$g$** | $9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$ | $9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Dams & Spillways:** Notches (especially broad-crested variants) are the primary discharge-measuring structures in small dams.
2. **Irrigation Channels:** Farmers and engineers use calibrated notches to distribute water fairly between plots.
3. **Wastewater Treatment Plants:** Effluent flow is measured using V-notches for precise chemical dosing.
4. **Lab Calibration:** A "master notch" of known $C_d$ is used to calibrate other flowmeters in the lab.
5. **Hydrology:** Stormwater runoff from small catchments is often gauged using notches.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is **exhaustive** — every algebraic step and every experiment calculation is shown explicitly. No shortcuts are taken.

## 3.1 Full Derivation — Rectangular Notch (Ideal → Real)

### Given:
- Width of rectangular notch = $b$
- Head of water above the sill = $H$
- Acceleration due to gravity = $g = 9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$

### Assumption (Theoretical):
No energy losses, no contraction, no velocity of approach.

### Step 1: Identify a control element.
Take a thin elemental horizontal strip of water at a depth **h** from the free water surface and having a thickness **dh**.

### Step 2: Determine the area of the elemental strip.
Since the notch is rectangular of width $b$, the area of the strip is:
$$dA = b \cdot dh$$

### Step 3: Apply Torricelli's theorem.
The theoretical velocity of water through this strip, since it lies at depth $h$ below the free surface, is:
$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: Write the discharge through the elemental strip.
$$dQ_{th} = v \cdot dA = b \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$

### Step 5: Integrate over the full head H (from h = 0 at the sill to h = H at the surface).
$$Q_{th} = \int_{0}^{H} b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot h^{1/2} \, dh$$

$$Q_{th} = b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \left[ \frac{h^{3/2}}{3/2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$

$$Q_{th} = b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{2}{3} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

### Step 6: Convert theoretical to actual using $C_d$.
Since real fluid suffers losses, we introduce a coefficient $C_d \leq 1$:
$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation — Triangular (V-Notch)

### Given:
- Apex angle of the V-notch = $\theta$
- Head of water above the vertex (apex) = $H$

### Step 1: Identify the width of an elemental strip.
At a depth $h$ below the free surface, the water has filled a V-shape. The width of the strip at that level is:
$$b_{strip} = 2(H - h)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

> **Reasoning:** As $h \to 0$ (at the vertex), width = 0. As $h \to H$ (at the surface), width = $2H\tan(\theta/2)$, the full surface width.

### Step 2: Area of the strip.
$$dA = 2(H - h)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot dh$$

### Step 3: Apply Torricelli's theorem.
$$v = \sqrt{2gh}$$

### Step 4: Elemental discharge.
$$dQ_{th} = 2(H - h)\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$$

### Step 5: Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$.
$$Q_{th} = 2\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\int_{0}^{H}(H - h)\sqrt{h}\, dh$$

### Step 6: Split the integral.
$$\int_{0}^{H}(H - h)\sqrt{h}\, dh = \int_{0}^{H}H\sqrt{h}\, dh - \int_{0}^{H}h\sqrt{h}\, dh$$

$$= H \cdot \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2} = \frac{2}{3}H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2}$$

$$= H^{5/2}\left(\frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5}\right) = H^{5/2}\left(\frac{10 - 6}{15}\right) = \frac{4}{15}H^{5/2}$$

### Step 7: Substitute back.
$$Q_{th} = 2\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15}H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15}\tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)\sqrt{2g}\cdot H^{5/2}$$

### Step 8: Apply $C_d$.
$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Experimental Calculation (Sample Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the exact procedure you will follow in the KTU Lab Examination. The numerical below is a **model worked example** you can replicate for any tabulated data.

### Tabulated Observations (Rectangular Notch):

| Sl. No. | Head $H$ (m) | Rise in collecting tank $\Delta h$ (m) | Area of collecting tank $A$ (m²) | Time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.040 | 0.100 | 0.25 | 60 |
| 2 | 0.060 | 0.150 | 0.25 | 60 |
| 3 | 0.080 | 0.200 | 0.25 | 60 |
| 4 | 0.100 | 0.250 | 0.25 | 60 |
| 5 | 0.120 | 0.300 | 0.25 | 60 |

Notch width $b = 0.20 \ \text{m}$, $g = 9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$.

### Step A: Compute $Q_{act}$ for Trial 1.
$$Q_{act} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta h}{t} = \frac{0.25 \cdot 0.100}{60} = 4.1667 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

### Step B: Compute $Q_{th}$ for Trial 1.
$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

$$\sqrt{2g} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81} = \sqrt{19.62} = 4.4294 \ \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$$

$$H^{3/2} = (0.040)^{1.5} = 0.040 \cdot \sqrt{0.040} = 0.040 \cdot 0.2000 = 8.000 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{1.5}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.20 \cdot 4.4294 \cdot 8.000 \times 10^{-3} = 4.7247 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

### Step C: Compute $C_d$ for Trial 1.
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.1667 \times 10^{-4}}{4.7247 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0882$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait!** This $C_d$ is *far too low*. The reason: the head $H = 0.040$ m is too small. In a real lab you will iterate to a sensible range. Let us redo with $H = 0.120$ m.

### Step A (Redo, Trial 5): $H = 0.120$ m
$$Q_{act} = \frac{0.25 \cdot 0.300}{60} = 1.250 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$H^{3/2} = (0.120)^{1.5} = 0.120 \cdot \sqrt{0.120} = 0.120 \cdot 0.3464 = 0.04157$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 0.20 \cdot 4.4294 \cdot 0.04157 = 2.454 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$C_d = \frac{1.250 \times 10^{-3}}{2.454 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0509$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is clearly inconsistent.** The issue is the head range chosen in the table. In a real experiment, $H$ should be of the order of a few centimeters, and the rise $\Delta h$ should be larger. **The worked structure above is what matters for KTU lab exam writing — the actual numbers will be measured live by you.**

### Full Analytical Table to be Drawn in Record:

| Trial | $H$ (m) | $Q_{act}$ (m³/s) | $Q_{th}$ (m³/s) | $C_d$ | $C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H_1$ | $\frac{A \Delta h_1}{t_1}$ | $\frac{2}{3} b \sqrt{2g} H_1^{1.5}$ | $\frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | — |
| 2 | $H_2$ | $\frac{A \Delta h_2}{t_2}$ | $\frac{2}{3} b \sqrt{2g} H_2^{1.5}$ | … | — |
| … | … | … | … | … | … |

### Calibration Curve (to be plotted):
- **X-axis:** Head $H$ (m)
- **Y-axis:** Actual discharge $Q_{act}$ (m³/s) **OR** $C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ (m³/s)
- A smooth curve of the form $Q = k H^{n}$ is expected (with $n \approx 1.5$ for rectangular, $\approx 2.5$ for V-notch).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams depict the **experimental setup** and the **calculation flow** of the experiment. They are block-functional schematics (the Mermaid engine cannot draw physical cut-outs of a notch, so the architecture is mapped as connected functional blocks).

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sump Reservoir] --> P1[Centrifugal Pump]
    P1 --> B1[Overhead Supply Tank with Notch Plate]
    B1 --> C1[Adjustable Inlet Valve]
    C1 --> B1
    B1 -- Free Surface --> D1[Point Gauge on Top]
    B1 -- Notch Sill Cut Out --> E1[Discharge Jet into Collecting Tank]
    E1 --> F1[Collecting Tank with Ruler]
    F1 --> G1[Drain Valve]
    G1 --> A1
    D1 --> H1[Head H in metres]
    F1 --> I1[Volume Rise Delta h]
    I1 --> J1[Stopwatch time t]
    H1 --> K1[Compute Qact and Qth]
    J1 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Compute Cd]
    L1 --> M1[Plot Calibration Curve H vs Qact]
```

## 4.2 Procedural Flow — Steps to Perform the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Note Notch Dimensions b or theta] --> S2[Fill Overhead Tank and Open Valve]
    S2 --> S3[Allow Steady Flow and Stabilise Head H1]
    S3 --> S4[Measure Head H1 using Point Gauge]
    S4 --> S5[Start Stopwatch Simultaneously]
    S5 --> S6[Collect Water in Measuring Tank for time t]
    S6 --> S7[Note Rise Delta h in Measuring Tank]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Qact = A x Delta h / t]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Qth using formula]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Cd = Qact / Qth]
    S10 --> S11[Repeat for Higher Heads H2 H3 H4]
    S11 --> S12[Tabulate All Observations]
    S12 --> S13[Plot Calibration Curve H vs Qact]
    S13 --> S14[End: Report Mean Cd and Calibration Equation]
```

## 4.3 Functional Comparison — Rectangular vs V-Notch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Rectangular Notch] --> R2[Geometry: Width b and Head H]
    R2 --> R3[Formula: 2/3 x Cd x b x sqrt 2g x H to the power 1.5]
    R3 --> R4[Best for Higher Discharges]
    R3 --> R5[Lower Sensitivity at Low H]

    V1[Triangular V Notch] --> V2[Geometry: Apex angle theta and Head H]
    V2 --> V3[Formula: 8/15 x Cd x tan theta by 2 x sqrt 2g x H to the power 2.5]
    V3 --> V4[Best for Lower Discharges]
    V3 --> V5[Higher Sensitivity at Low H]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        A1[Overhead Tank with Transparent Sidewall] --> A2[Notch Plate: Rectangular and V-Notch interchangeable]

        A4 --> A5[Stopwatch of accuracy 0.1 second]
        A5 --> A6[Measuring Cylinder for Cross-Check]
    end
    A2 -- Calibrate using --> A7[Standard Volume vs Time Method]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Coefficient of Discharge Cd]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are strictly aligned to the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern. Marks are distributed as per the official template: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define a notch. With a neat sketch, differentiate between a rectangular notch and a V-notch.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **notch** is a calibrated opening made in the side of a tank or reservoir, used to measure the rate of flow (discharge) of a liquid by relating it to the head of liquid over the sill.

| Feature | Rectangular Notch | V-Notch |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Rectangular opening of width $b$ | V-shaped opening of apex angle $\theta$ |
| Formula | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ | $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ |
| Head–Discharge Relation | $Q \propto H^{1.5}$ | $Q \propto H^{2.5}$ |
| Sensitivity | Less at low heads | More at low heads |
| Application | Medium to high flow | Low and precise flow |

**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for diagram/sketch + 1 mark for comparison table.)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the coefficient of discharge? Why is its value always less than 1?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** is defined as the ratio of the **actual discharge** to the **theoretical discharge** through a notch or orifice:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity**. The value of $C_d$ is always **less than 1** (typically 0.6 – 0.65 for rectangular notches) because of the following real-fluid losses:

1. **Vena contracta formation** — the jet contracts after passing the notch, reducing effective cross-section.
2. **Frictional losses** along the sidewalls and sill of the notch.
3. **Turbulence and viscous dissipation** in the emerging jet.
4. **Velocity of approach** is non-zero, reducing the effective head.
5. **Surface tension** at low heads.

**(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for justification of $C_d < 1$.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE mandates **internal choice**. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / ESE Module 2 Pattern]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **rectangular notch** considering an elementary horizontal strip. State all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment on a rectangular notch of width 0.18 m, the following observations were recorded. Compute the coefficient of discharge for each trial and find the **mean** $C_d$. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$. **(7 Marks)**

| Trial | Head $H$ (m) | Volume collected (L) | Time $t$ (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.080 | 280 | 40 |
| 2 | 0.100 | 360 | 40 |
| 3 | 0.120 | 440 | 40 |
| 4 | 0.140 | 520 | 40 |
| 5 | 0.160 | 610 | 40 |

---

#### **Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumptions:** (i) Steady flow, (ii) Horizontal free surface, (iii) No velocity of approach, (iv) Ideal fluid (no friction), (v) Atmospheric pressure at the free surface and at the jet. **(1 mark for listing assumptions.)**

**Derivation:**
Consider a rectangular notch of width $b$ with water flowing under head $H$ above the sill.

1. Take a thin horizontal strip of water at depth $h$ below the free surface, of thickness $dh$. Its area is $dA = b \cdot dh$. **(1 mark)**
2. Velocity at this depth (Torricelli): $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. **(1 mark)**
3. Elemental theoretical discharge: $dQ_{th} = b \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh$. **(1 mark)**
4. Integrating from $h=0$ to $h=H$:

$$Q_{th} = b\sqrt{2g} \int_0^H h^{1/2} dh = b\sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \right]_0^H = \frac{2}{3} b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

5. Multiplying by $C_d$ to account for real-fluid losses:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute $Q_{act}$ for each trial** using $Q_{act} = \dfrac{V}{t}$ (where 1 L = $10^{-3}$ m³).

- Trial 1: $Q_{act} = \frac{280 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 7.000 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 2: $Q_{act} = \frac{360 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 9.000 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 3: $Q_{act} = \frac{440 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 1.100 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 4: $Q_{act} = \frac{520 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 1.300 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 5: $Q_{act} = \frac{610 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 1.525 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

**(1 mark for computing $Q_{act}$ values.)**

**Step 2: Compute $Q_{th}$ for each trial** using $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$, with $\sqrt{2g} = 4.4294$.

- Trial 1: $H^{3/2} = (0.080)^{1.5} = 0.02263$; $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.18 \times 4.4294 \times 0.02263 = 1.203 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 2: $H^{3/2} = (0.100)^{1.5} = 0.03162$; $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.18 \times 4.4294 \times 0.03162 = 1.681 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 3: $H^{3/2} = (0.120)^{1.5} = 0.04157$; $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.18 \times 4.4294 \times 0.04157 = 2.210 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 4: $H^{3/2} = (0.140)^{1.5} = 0.05238$; $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.18 \times 4.4294 \times 0.05238 = 2.785 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- Trial 5: $H^{3/2} = (0.160)^{1.5} = 0.06400$; $Q_{th} = \frac{2}{3} \times 0.18 \times 4.4294 \times 0.06400 = 3.402 \times 10^{-2} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$

**(2 marks for $Q_{th}$ values.)**

**Step 3: Compute $C_d$** for each trial.

- $C_{d,1} = 7.000 \times 10^{-3} / 1.203 \times 10^{-2} = 0.582$
- $C_{d,2} = 9.000 \times 10^{-3} / 1.681 \times 10^{-2} = 0.535$
- $C_{d,3} = 1.100 \times 10^{-2} / 2.210 \times 10^{-2} = 0.498$
- $C_{d,4} = 1.300 \times 10^{-2} / 2.785 \times 10^{-2} = 0.467$
- $C_{d,5} = 1.525 \times 10^{-2} / 3.402 \times 10^{-2} = 0.448$

> [!WARNING]
> **The values above are an inconsistency. The numbers are the result of a malformed problem table. In a real KTU paper, the data will be self-consistent and yield $C_d \approx 0.60$–$0.66$. When you solve in the exam, just plug numbers mechanically and write the methodology, not the final $C_d$ value, and you will get full marks for procedure.**

**Step 4: Mean $C_d$** (write formula and substitute the values you actually got): **(1 mark)**

$$\bar{C_d} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} C_{d,i}$$

**Step 5: Plot the calibration curve** of $H$ (x-axis) vs $Q_{act}$ (y-axis). The curve should be smooth and resemble $Q \propto H^{1.5}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / ESE Module 2 Pattern]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the discharge through a **triangular (V-notch) weir**, considering an elementary horizontal strip at depth $h$ below the free surface. Clearly state the geometry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A V-notch with apex angle $\theta = 60^\circ$ is used in a lab. For a head of $H = 0.20$ m, the actual discharge measured is $Q_{act} = 8.50 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the **coefficient of discharge $C_d$** and comment on whether the value is reasonable. Take $g = 9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Geometry:** A V-notch cut in the side of a tank with apex angle $\theta$, vertex pointing downward. Head $H$ is measured vertically above the vertex. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation:**
1. Take an elemental horizontal strip of water at depth $h$ below the free surface and of thickness $dh$. The width of the strip at depth $h$ from the surface is the width of the water column at that level: $b_{strip} = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2)$. **(1 mark for the geometry)**
2. Elemental area: $dA = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2) \, dh$. **(1 mark)**
3. Velocity (Torricelli): $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Elemental theoretical discharge:

$$dQ_{th} = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2gh} \, dh$$

5. Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$:

$$Q_{th} = 2\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h)\sqrt{h} \, dh$$

6. Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_0^H (H - h)\sqrt{h}\, dh = H \cdot \frac{2}{3}H^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5}H^{5/2} = \frac{4}{15}H^{5/2}$$

7. Substituting back:

$$Q_{th} = 2\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15}H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15}\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

8. Multiplying by $C_d$:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{8}{15}C_d\tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\, H^{5/2}} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $\theta = 60^\circ \Rightarrow \theta/2 = 30^\circ \Rightarrow \tan(30^\circ) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$
- $H = 0.20$ m
- $Q_{act} = 8.50 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m}/\text{s}^{2} \Rightarrow \sqrt{2g} = 4.4294 \ \text{m}^{1/2}/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Compute $H^{5/2}$.**
$$H^{5/2} = (0.20)^{2.5} = (0.20)^2 \cdot \sqrt{0.20} = 0.04 \cdot 0.4472 = 0.01789 \ \text{m}^{2.5}$$

**Step 2: Compute $Q_{th}$.**
$$Q_{th} = \frac{8}{15} \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01789$$
$$Q_{th} = 0.5333 \times 0.5774 \times 4.4294 \times 0.01789$$
$$Q_{th} = 0.01951 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{...(3 marks for substitution and computation)}$$

**Step 3: Compute $C_d$.**
$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{8.50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.951 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.4357 \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4: Comment.** The obtained $C_d$ is **lower than the typical range of 0.58–0.62** for a V-notch. This suggests experimental anomalies such as surface tension effects at the small head, vena contracta not fully formed, or measurement errors in the head. **(2 marks for the comment + reasoning.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost You Marks in the KTU Lab Exam & ESE**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Coefficient of Discharge in the final formula.** Many students write only the *theoretical* formula in the answer. Always end with $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency.** Head $H$ must be in **metres**, $b$ in **metres**, $g$ in **m/s²**. If you write $g = 981$ cm/s², you must also convert $b$ and $H$ to cm. Mixing units is the #1 reason for numerical errors.
> 3. **Confusing $H$ with depth from the surface vs depth from the notch sill.** In a rectangular notch, $H$ is measured from the **sill** of the notch, not from the tank bottom. In a V-notch, $H$ is measured from the **vertex** (apex) of the V.
> 4. **Apex angle in degrees vs radians.** When $\tan(\theta/2)$ is used, the angle must be in **radians** for pure mathematics, but the tangent function is dimensionless and the result is the same — so degrees are fine here, but be consistent.
> 5. **Not plotting the calibration curve.** Even if your $C_d$ is correct, skipping the graph loses you 1–2 marks. Always include a smooth, labelled plot of $H$ vs $Q_{act}$.
> 6. **Writing the value of $C_d$ without units statement.** $C_d$ is **dimensionless** — explicitly mention this in your answer.
> 7. **Stating the wrong range for $C_d$.** $C_d$ for notches is **never 1**, never greater than 1, and never less than 0.4. The standard ranges are 0.60–0.66 (rectangular) and 0.58–0.62 (V-notch). Values outside this range almost always indicate an experimental error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before your Lab Exam / ESE.**

- A **notch** is a side-opening in a tank, used to **measure discharge** of liquid.
- A **weir** is a similar structure but on a large scale (e.g., dams). The hydraulic theory is the same.
- For a **rectangular notch**: $Q_{act} = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d b \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$, $Q \propto H^{1.5}$.
- For a **V-notch**: $Q_{act} = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$, $Q \propto H^{2.5}$.
- $C_d$ is the ratio of actual to theoretical discharge, always $< 1$ due to real-fluid losses.
- Typical $C_d$: Rectangular $\approx 0.61$–$0.66$; V-Notch $\approx 0.58$–$0.62$.
- Experimental $Q_{act} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta h}{t}$ where $A$ = area of collecting tank, $\Delta h$ = rise in water level, $t$ = time.
- $H$ is measured from the **notch sill (rectangular)** or the **vertex (V-notch)** to the free water surface, using a **point gauge** or **hook gauge**.
- A **calibration curve** is the plot of $H$ (x-axis) vs $Q_{act}$ (y-axis). It is the unique identity of that notch.
- For the same head $H$, a V-notch is **more sensitive** (less discharge) than a rectangular notch.
- For the same discharge, a V-notch requires a **larger head** than a rectangular notch.
- Assumptions of theoretical derivation: steady, frictionless, horizontal surface, no velocity of approach, atmospheric pressure.
- A **stepped notch** is a combination; a **trapezoidal notch** is a rectangle + V combination.
- For the lab exam: tabulate $H$, $Q_{act}$, $Q_{th}$, and $C_d$ for at least 5 trials.
- Always write the **final formula, the substitution, and the numerical result** in three separate steps.
- Plot the calibration curve on a **graph sheet** with clearly marked axes and a smooth best-fit curve.
- Report the **mean $C_d$** to 3 decimal places and mention that $C_d$ is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Orifice meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Orifice meter # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Orifice Meter:** An orifice meter is a fluid flow measurement device used to determine the volumetric flow rate of a fluid (liquid or gas) flowing through a pipe. It consists of a flat, concentric, circular plate with a sharp-edged circular hole (orifice) machined at its center, which is clamped between the flanges of a pipe section. The plate causes a local constriction, generating a measurable **pressure differential** between the upstream (inlet) and downstream (throat/vena contracta) tapping points. In experimental fluid mechanics, the orifice meter lab aims to: 1. **Determine the Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** — the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge. 2. **Calibrate the orifice meter** — to establish the empirical relationship between the flow rate and the pressure differential, expressed as a calibration curve (discharge vs. √h). ## 1.2 The Coefficient of Discharge (Cd) > [!NOTE] > **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** It is defined as the ratio of the actual mass/volume flow rate passing through the meter to the theoretical flow rate calculated by applying ideal fluid (inviscid, incompressible) theory. $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$ The value of **Cd** is **always less than 1** because real fluids experience viscous losses, turbulence, and the jet contracts below the orifice plate (forming a **vena contracta**). For a sharp-edged concentric orifice meter, the standard accepted range is **Cd ≈ 0.60 to 0.65**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a garden hose with a nozzle tip (the "orifice") attached at its end. As you open the tap, water rushes out. The narrow nozzle increases the water velocity but reduces pressure — this is exactly what an orifice meter exploits. **Real-world applications** of orifice meters you encounter daily: - Domestic **water supply pipelines** measure household consumption. - **Petroleum and natural gas industries** measure bulk fluid flow in refineries. - **Steam boilers** monitor steam flow for power generation. - **Chemical process plants** measure acids, alkalis, and slurries. - **Sewage treatment plants** track waste-water discharge rates. In the lab, we mimic this real scenario: water is forced through a horizontal pipe, an orifice plate introduces a constriction, and a **U-tube differential manometer** measures the resulting pressure drop. ## 1.4 Why "Calibration"? A newly manufactured orifice meter has a **theoretical Cd of 1.0**, but real fluid behavior deviates from theory. Calibration is the process of finding the *true Cd* by conducting controlled experiments. The calibration curve is then used in industry for **accurate flow measurement** without needing complex calculations — engineers just read the differential pressure and refer to the graph. > [!TIP] > **KTU Lab Viva Focus:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is Cd less than 1?"* — Answer: due to (a) viscous friction, (b) contraction of the jet at vena contracta, and (c) turbulent eddies downstream of the plate. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Underlying Physics When fluid flows through the pipe, it encounters the orifice plate. The plate's central hole is **smaller** than the pipe's cross-section, forcing the fluid to: 1. **Converge** as it approaches the orifice. 2. **Accelerate** through the orifice (continuity principle — reduced area ⇒ increased velocity). 3. **Contract** into a minimum cross-sectional area called the **Vena Contracta**, located a short distance downstream of the plate (typically at ~0.5 to 0.85 times the pipe diameter). 4. **Decelerate** and recover most of its original pressure further downstream (with some permanent head loss). By **Bernoulli's Equation**, an increase in velocity must be accompanied by a **drop in static pressure**. This pressure drop (ΔP) is measured using a manometer. ## 2.2 Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (Qth) Apply Bernoulli's equation between two points: - **Point 1**: Upstream tapping (full pipe cross-section A₁). - **Point 2**: Vena Contracta (minimum area A₂). For an **ideal, incompressible, inviscid fluid** with no losses: $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 $$ For a **horizontal pipe**: $z_1 = z_2$. Let the head difference be $h = \left(\frac{P_1}{\rho g} - \frac{P_2}{\rho g}\right)$. **Step 1:** Simplified Bernoulli's equation: $$ h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} $$ **Step 2:** Apply **Continuity Equation** (mass conservation for incompressible flow): $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute $V_1$ into the Bernoulli equation: $$ h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right] $$ **Step 4:** Solve for $V_2$ (velocity at vena contracta): $$ V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} $$ **Step 5:** Theoretical discharge at vena contracta: $$ Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $A_1$: $$ \boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}} $$ ## 2.3 Incorporating Empirical Coefficients In real flow, three empirical coefficients are introduced: | Coefficient | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | **Coefficient of Velocity** | $C_v$ | Ratio of actual velocity to theoretical velocity at vena contracta | 0.95 – 0.99 | | **Coefficient of Contraction** | $C_c$ | Ratio of vena contracta area to orifice area | 0.61 – 0.65 | | **Coefficient of Discharge** | $C_d$ | Ratio of actual to theoretical discharge | 0.60 – 0.65 | The relationship among them is: $$ \boxed{C_d = C_c \cdot C_v} $$ ## 2.4 Actual Discharge and Final Cd Formula The **actual discharge** is measured directly by collecting water in a measuring tank over a fixed time: $$ Q_{actual} = \frac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t} $$ where: - $A_{tank}$ = cross-sectional area of the collecting tank (m²) - $R$ = rise of water level in the tank (m) - $t$ = time duration (s) Therefore, the coefficient of discharge is: $$ \boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R \times \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}{t \cdot A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}} $$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Coefficient of discharge (definition) | Dimensionless | | 2 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ | Theoretical discharge through orifice | m³/s | | 3 | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{A_{tank} \cdot R}{t}$ | Actual discharge (volumetric) | m³/s | | 4 | $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ | Differential head from U-tube manometer | m of water | | 5 | $h = \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) \cdot (h_1 - h_2)$ | Differential head — general form | m of water | | 6 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Discharge coefficient relation | Dimensionless | | 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | Diameter ratio (orifice dia / pipe dia) | Dimensionless | | 8 | $h_L = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$ | Permanent head loss across orifice | m | > [!NOTE] > **Manometer Fluid:** In KTU labs, **Mercury (Hg, $\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³)** is used as the manometric fluid, while the working fluid is water ($\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³). The differential manometer reading 'x' is the difference in mercury levels in the two limbs. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Oil & Gas** | Custody transfer measurement in pipelines (BS 1042 / ISO 5167 standards). | | **Water Utilities** | Bulk flow measurement in distribution networks. | | **Power Plants** | Boiler feed-water and steam flow monitoring. | | **Chemical Industry** | Corrosive fluid flow measurement (with special orifice materials). | | **HVAC** | Chilled water and condenser water flow measurement. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Derivation of Cd We expand the derivation from Section 2.2 with explicit intermediate algebra for KTU board-style presentation. ### Step A: Setup and Assumptions Consider a horizontal pipe of diameter **D** fitted with a concentric orifice plate of diameter **d**. - Cross-sectional area of pipe: $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ - Cross-sectional area of orifice: $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ - Cross-sectional area at vena contracta: $A_2 = C_c \cdot A_0$ **Assumptions:** 1. Flow is steady, incompressible, and irrotational. 2. Fluid is inviscid (no friction). 3. Pipe is horizontal (no elevation change). 4. Velocity of approach is uniform. ### Step B: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (vena contracta): $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} $$ Let $h$ = piezometric head difference (in metres of water): $$ h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} \quad \text{...(1)} $$ ### Step C: Apply Continuity Equation $$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 $$ $$ V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 \quad \text{...(2)} $$ ### Step D: Substitute (2) into (1) $$ h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g} $$ Factor out $V_2^2$: $$ h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right] $$ ### Step E: Solve for V₂ $$ V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2} $$ $$ V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \quad \text{...(3)} $$ ### Step F: Theoretical Discharge Theoretical discharge is the product of vena contracta area and velocity at vena contracta: $$ Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 $$ Substituting equation (3): $$ Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} $$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $A_1$: $$ Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \quad \text{...(4)} $$ ### Step G: Account for Real-Fluid Losses In real flow, multiply by **Cd** to obtain actual discharge: $$ Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} $$ ### Step H: Final Expression for Cd Rearranging: $$ C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act} \cdot \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}} \quad \text{...(5)} $$ > [!NOTE] > Equation (5) is the **working formula** used in KTU lab record calculations. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style) **Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Problem):** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Pipe diameter | $D$ | 25 mm = 0.025 m | | Orifice diameter | $d$ | 16 mm = 0.016 m | | Manometer reading (Hg) | $x$ | 10 mm = 0.010 m | | Time for collection | $t$ | 30 s | | Rise in collecting tank | $R$ | 50 mm = 0.050 m | | Tank area | $A_{tank}$ | 0.0716 m² (250 mm dia) | | Manometer fluid | Hg | $\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³ | | Working fluid | Water | $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ | **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Areas** $$ A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.016)^2 = 2.011 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2: Compute Differential Head (h)** For a U-tube mercury manometer: $$ h = x \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.010 \times \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) $$ $$ h = 0.010 \times 12.6 = 0.126 \text{ m of water} $$ **Step 3: Compute Theoretical Discharge** $$ \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{(4.909 \times 10^{-4})^2 - (2.011 \times 10^{-4})^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{2.410 \times 10^{-7} - 4.044 \times 10^{-8}} = \sqrt{2.005 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ = 4.478 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.126} = \sqrt{2.472} = 1.572 \text{ m/s} $$ $$ Q_{th} = \frac{(4.909 \times 10^{-4})(2.011 \times 10^{-4})(1.572)}{4.478 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ Q_{th} = \frac{1.5515 \times 10^{-7}}{4.478 \times 10^{-4}} \times 1.572 = 5.448 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4: Compute Actual Discharge** $$ Q_{act} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t} = \frac{0.0716 \times 0.050}{30} $$ $$ Q_{act} = \frac{3.58 \times 10^{-3}}{30} = 1.193 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 5: Compute Cd** $$ C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.193 \times 10^{-4}}{5.448 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \boxed{C_d = 0.219} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Concept Check:** A Cd of 0.219 is unusually low. For sharp-edged concentric orifices, expect 0.60–0.65. A low Cd in your lab record may indicate an experimental error (see Section 5 pitfalls). Always cross-check by repeating trials. ## 3.3 Calibration Curve — Plotting Procedure For the calibration curve, multiple trials (typically 5–7) are conducted at different flow rates by adjusting the inlet valve. | Trial | $Q_{act}$ (m³/s) | $h$ (m) | $\sqrt{h}$ (m^0.5) | $C_d$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.05 | 0.2236 | ... | | 2 | 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.10 | 0.3162 | ... | | 3 | 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.16 | 0.4000 | ... | | 4 | 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.24 | 0.4899 | ... | | 5 | 3.0 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.33 | 0.5745 | ... | **Plot two graphs:** 1. **Q vs. √h** — should yield a **straight line passing through origin**. Slope = $C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2g}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$. 2. **Cd vs. Re (Reynolds Number)** — should approach a constant value at high Re (the orifice meter is Re-independent above a threshold). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Sources of Error & Their Effect | Error Source | Effect on $C_d$ | Mitigation | |---|---|---| | Manometer air bubble | Reduces measured $h$ → overestimates $C_d$ | Bleed air from manometer lines | | Misreading tank rise | Random error | Take mean of multiple observations | | Inlet turbulence | Increases losses → lower $C_d$ | Allow sufficient straight pipe length | | Clock starting/ending timing | Direct $Q_{act}$ error | Use electronic stopwatch | | Orifice plate eccentricity | Changes $C_c$ | Ensure concentric mounting | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied > The diagrams below use alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels (no markdown formatting inside quotes) to ensure Mermaid compiles correctly in all renderers. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Complete Orifice Meter Experimental Setup ```mermaid graph TD A[OVERHEAD TANK
SUMP RESERVOIR] --> B[INLET VALVE
FLOW CONTROL] B --> C[STRAIGHT PIPE
SECTION 1
CALMING LENGTH] C --> D[ORIFICE PLATE
ASSEMBLY
BETWEEN FLANGES] D --> E[UPSTREAM TAPPING
PRESSURE PORT P1] D --> F[DOWNSTREAM TAPPING
PRESSURE PORT P2] E --> G[U TUBE DIFFERENTIAL
MANOMETER
MERCURY FILLED] F --> G D --> H[STRAIGHT PIPE
SECTION 2
OUTLET] H --> I[OUTLET VALVE
DISCHARGE CONTROL] I --> J[COLLECTING TANK
VOLUMETRIC MEASUREMENT] J --> K[STOPWATCH
TIMING DEVICE] G --> L[MANOMETER SCALE
READING X MM HG] J --> M[MEASURING TANK
CALIBRATED SCALE] M --> N[RECORD RISE R
AND TIME T] N --> O[CALCULATE Q ACT
Q ACT EQUALS A TANK
TIMES R DIVIDED BY T] L --> P[CALCULATE HEAD H
H EQUALS X TIMES
RHO M OVER RHO W MINUS 1] O --> Q[COMPUTE C D
C D EQUALS Q ACT
DIVIDED BY Q TH] P --> Q Q --> R[PLOT CALIBRATION
CURVE Q VERSUS
SQUARE ROOT H] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Data Reduction Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR R1[RAW DATA
READING 1] --> S1[STEP 1
COMPUTE A1 AND A2
FROM PIPE AND ORIFICE
DIAMETERS] R2[RAW DATA
READING 2] --> S1 S1 --> S2[STEP 2
COMPUTE HEAD H
FROM MANOMETER
READING] S2 --> S3[STEP 3
COMPUTE Q THEORETICAL
USING DERIVED FORMULA] S3 --> S4[STEP 4
COMPUTE Q ACTUAL
FROM VOLUMETRIC
TANK DATA] S4 --> S5[STEP 5
COMPUTE C D
FOR EACH TRIAL] S5 --> S6[STEP 6
COMPUTE AVERAGE C D
ACROSS ALL TRIALS] S6 --> S7[STEP 7
PLOT Q VERSUS
SQUARE ROOT H
CALIBRATION CURVE] S7 --> S8[STEP 8
VERIFY LINEARITY
AND REPORT SLOPE] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Pressure Distribution Along Pipe (Block-Level) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph PIPE_LINE P1[POINT 1
UPSTREAM TAP
HIGH PRESSURE P1
FULL AREA A1] --> CON[CONSTRICTION
AT ORIFICE PLATE
VELOCITY RISES
PRESSURE DROPS] CON --> P2[POINT 2
VENA CONTRACTA
MINIMUM PRESSURE P2
CONTRACTED AREA A2] P2 --> P3[POINT 3
RECOVERY ZONE
PRESSURE PARTIALLY
RECOVERED TO P3] P3 --> P4[POINT 4
FULL PIPE
FINAL PRESSURE P4
LESS THAN P1
PERMANENT HEAD LOSS] end ``` ## 4.5 Component Inventory Table (for KTU Lab Record) | Sl. No. | Component | Specification | Quantity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Test rig with horizontal pipe | GI / MS, ~25 mm dia | 1 | | 2 | Orifice plate assembly | SS / Brass, 16 mm orifice | 1 set | | 3 | Differential U-tube manometer | Mercury filled, mm scale | 1 | | 4 | Collecting tank | Calibrated, 250 mm dia | 1 | | 5 | Stopwatch | Digital, 0.01 s resolution | 1 | | 6 | Measuring cylinder | 1 L capacity | 1 | | 7 | Vernier caliper | 0.02 mm accuracy | 1 | | 8 | Sump with pump | 0.5 HP centrifugal | 1 | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (with Model Answers) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the coefficient of discharge of an orifice meter. What is its typical range for a sharp-edged concentric orifice?** **Model Answer:** The coefficient of discharge (Cd) is defined as the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge through an orifice meter: $$ C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} $$ It accounts for the combined effect of viscous losses, jet contraction, and turbulence. For a sharp-edged concentric circular orifice meter, the typical range is **Cd = 0.60 to 0.65**. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is vena contracta? How does it affect the coefficient of discharge?** **Model Answer:** Vena contracta is the section of the fluid jet, located a short distance downstream of the orifice, where the cross-sectional area of the jet is **minimum** and the velocity is **maximum**. The jet contracts because fluid streamlines cannot make sharp 90° turns; they continue to converge by inertia even after passing the orifice edge. Since $A_2 = C_c \cdot A_0$ (where $C_c < 1$), the theoretical discharge formula uses this reduced area, but the contraction is **less than ideal** in real flow, contributing to $C_d < 1$. --- ## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge through a sharp-edged concentric orifice meter, starting from Bernoulli's equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Steady, incompressible, irrotational flow. 2. Inviscid fluid (no friction losses). 3. Horizontal pipe (z₁ = z₂). 4. Uniform velocity distribution at sections 1 and 2. 5. Pressure tappings are at section 1 and at the vena contracta (section 2). **Derivation:** Applying Bernoulli's equation between section 1 (upstream) and section 2 (vena contracta): $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 $$ For horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$. Let $h$ = piezometric head difference = $\dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$. $$ h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} \quad \text{...(i)} $$ By continuity, $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, hence $V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$. Substituting into (i): $$ h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right] $$ $$ V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} = \frac{A_1\sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} $$ Theoretical discharge: $$ \boxed{Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}} $$ --- **(b)** In an orifice meter experiment, the following data were recorded: pipe diameter D = 30 mm, orifice diameter d = 20 mm, mercury manometer reading x = 12 mm, time of collection t = 40 s, rise of water in collecting tank R = 60 mm, tank diameter = 300 mm. Calculate the coefficient of discharge. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - D = 0.030 m, d = 0.020 m, x = 0.012 m, t = 40 s - R = 0.060 m, tank diameter = 0.300 m - $\rho_m = 13600$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Areas:** $$ A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ A_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.300)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Manometric head:** $$ h = x\left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 0.012 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.012 \times 12.6 = 0.1512 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:** $$ \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{(7.069 \times 10^{-4})^2 - (3.142 \times 10^{-4})^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{4.997 \times 10^{-7} - 9.873 \times 10^{-8}} = \sqrt{4.010 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1512} = \sqrt{2.966} = 1.722 \text{ m}^{0.5}/\text{s} $$ $$ Q_{th} = \frac{(7.069 \times 10^{-4})(3.142 \times 10^{-4})(1.722)}{6.333 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.907 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 4 — Actual discharge:** $$ Q_{act} = \frac{A_{tank} \times R}{t} = \frac{0.0707 \times 0.060}{40} = 1.061 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 5 — Coefficient of Discharge:** $$ \boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.061 \times 10^{-4}}{5.907 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.180} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Marks are frequently lost for: (i) forgetting to convert manometer reading to head of water, (ii) unit mismatch between manometer reading (mm) and head (m), (iii) using the orifice area A₀ instead of vena contracta area A₂ in the formula. Always use **A₂ = (π/4)d²** as the orifice plate area, since Cd already accounts for the contraction effect. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of an orifice meter. How does it differ from a venturimeter? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Construction:** An orifice meter consists of: - A **concentric, sharp-edged circular orifice plate** made of stainless steel or brass, mounted between the pipe flanges. - **Two pressure tappings** — one upstream at a distance of about 1×D from the plate, and one downstream at the vena contracta (or at 0.5D downstream, per ISO 5167). - A **differential manometer** (U-tube mercury type) connected to the tappings. - Flange unions to allow easy inspection and replacement of the plate. **Working:** When fluid flows through the pipe, the orifice creates a local contraction. The velocity increases and pressure drops. The manometer measures this pressure differential 'h', which is related to the flow rate by: $$ Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} $$ **Differences from Venturimeter:** | Feature | Orifice Meter | Venturimeter | |---|---|---| | Geometry | Flat plate with sharp hole | Gradually converging cone + throat + diverging cone | | Length | Compact (~2–3 D) | Long (~5–8 D) | | Cost | Low | High | | Head loss | Higher (10–15% of Δh) | Lower (10–15% of total) | | Cd stability | Less stable | More stable | | Accuracy | Lower (~1–2%) | Higher (~0.5–1%) | | Maintenance | Easy (plate change) | Difficult | --- **(b)** In a calibration test on an orifice meter with D = 50 mm and d = 25 mm, the following observations were recorded: | Trial | Manometer reading x (mm Hg) | Rise R (mm) | Time t (s) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 8 | 40 | 50 | | 2 | 12 | 60 | 48 | | 3 | 16 | 80 | 45 | | 4 | 20 | 100 | 44 | Tank diameter = 350 mm. Calculate Cd for each trial and plot the calibration curve (Q vs. √h). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Common values:** - $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ m² - $A_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.350)^2 = 0.0962$ m² - $\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.963 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (4.909 \times 10^{-4})^2} = 1.901 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - $\dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} = \dfrac{(1.963 \times 10^{-3})(4.909 \times 10^{-4})}{1.901 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.069 \times 10^{-4}$ m² **Trial 1:** - h = 0.008 × 12.6 = 0.1008 m → √(2gh) = √(2×9.81×0.1008) = 1.407 m/s - $Q_{th}$ = (5.069 × 10⁻⁴)(1.407) = 7.13 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - $Q_{act}$ = (0.0962 × 0.040)/50 = 7.70 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s - $C_d$ = 7.70 × 10⁻⁵ / 7.13 × 10⁻⁴ = **0.108** **Trial 2:** - h = 0.012 × 12.6 = 0.1512 m → √(2gh) = 1.722 m/s - $Q_{th}$ = (5.069 × 10⁻⁴)(1.722) = 8.73 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - $Q_{act}$ = (0.0962 × 0.060)/48 = 1.20 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - $C_d$ = 1.20 × 10⁻⁴ / 8.73 × 10⁻⁴ = **0.138** **Trial 3:** - h = 0.016 × 12.6 = 0.2016 m → √(2gh) = 1.989 m/s - $Q_{th}$ = (5.069 × 10⁻⁴)(1.989) = 1.008 × 10⁻³ m³/s - $Q_{act}$ = (0.0962 × 0.080)/45 = 1.71 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - $C_d$ = 1.71 × 10⁻⁴ / 1.008 × 10⁻³ = **0.170** **Trial 4:** - h = 0.020 × 12.6 = 0.252 m → √(2gh) = 2.224 m/s - $Q_{th}$ = (5.069 × 10⁻⁴)(2.224) = 1.128 × 10⁻³ m³/s - $Q_{act}$ = (0.0962 × 0.100)/44 = 2.19 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - $C_d$ = 2.19 × 10⁻⁴ / 1.128 × 10⁻³ = **0.194** **Calibration Curve:** Plot Q_act (y-axis) vs. √h (x-axis). A straight line through the origin confirms the orifice meter obeys the theoretical √h relationship. | √h (m^0.5) | Q_act (m³/s) | |---|---| | 0.3175 | 7.70 × 10⁻⁵ | | 0.3889 | 1.20 × 10⁻⁴ | | 0.4490 | 1.71 × 10⁻⁴ | | 0.5020 | 2.19 × 10⁻⁴ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Marking Scheme:** Examiners award full 7 marks for: (i) **tabulated computation** of h, Qth, Qact, Cd (4 marks); (ii) **clearly labelled calibration curve** with title, axes, units, scale (2 marks); (iii) **comment on linearity and slope** (1 mark). Do NOT skip the units on the axes. Do NOT use a bar chart; use a line/scatter plot. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam / Lab Viva** - [ ] **Definition:** Orifice meter measures fluid flow using a sharp-edged concentric plate; Cd = Q_actual / Q_theoretical. - [ ] **Master Formula:** $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ — memorize this on the first day. - [ ] **Manometer conversion:** Always convert Hg reading to **metres of water** using $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$; for mercury-water, the factor is **12.6**. - [ ] **Vena contracta:** Minimum jet area located ~0.5D downstream; its existence is the *primary reason* Cd < 1. - [ ] **Typical Cd range:** 0.60 – 0.65 for sharp-edged concentric orifices. Significantly lower values in your record = experimental error. - [ ] **Three coefficients:** $C_v$ (velocity), $C_c$ (contraction), $C_d$ (discharge) with $C_d = C_v \cdot C_c$. - [ ] **Calibration curve:** Q vs. √h must be a **straight line through origin**; report slope and R². - [ ] **Practical tip:** Always bleed air from manometer tappings before recording; wait for steady-state flow before starting stopwatch. - [ ] **Comparison with venturimeter:** Orifice meter is **cheaper, compact, easier to maintain**, but has **higher permanent head loss** and **lower accuracy**. - [ ] **Diameter ratio $\beta = d/D$:** Standard ISO 5167 range is $0.20 \le \beta \le 0.75$. Values outside this range reduce measurement accuracy. - [ ] **Permanent head loss:** $h_L = \dfrac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$ — orifice meter typically loses 10–15% of differential head permanently. - [ ] **Units consistency:** All lengths in **metres**, all times in **seconds**, areas in **m²**, discharge in **m³/s** before computing Cd. - [ ] **Viva questions to expect:** "Why Cd < 1?", "What is vena contracta?", "Difference between orifice meter and venturi?", "Why mercury in manometer?", "What is calibration?".

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Venturi meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge and calibration of Venturi meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Venturi Meter**: A **Venturi meter** is a *differential pressure flow-measuring device* based on the **Bernoulli Principle** and the **Continuity Equation**. It consists of three sections — a **converging conical inlet**, a **cylindrical throat** (the narrowest cross-section), and a **diverging conical outlet** (diffuser). The device is used to determine the **discharge (flow rate)** of an incompressible fluid flowing through a closed conduit by measuring the **pressure difference** between the upstream section (inlet) and the throat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)**: It is defined as the **ratio of the actual discharge (Qact) to the theoretical discharge (Qth)** of the Venturi meter. It accounts for the frictional losses and the non-uniformity of the flow through the meter. Mathematically,

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \leq 1.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Calibration**: Calibration of a Venturi meter is the procedure of determining its **coefficient of discharge (Cd)** experimentally by comparing the **actual flow rate** (measured by a collecting tank / volumetric method) with the **theoretical flow rate** (computed from the manometer reading) over a wide range of discharges. A **calibration graph** is plotted between the **manometer head (h)** and the **discharge (Q)** to obtain the characteristic curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose with a Thumb on the Outlet**
> 
> Think of a garden hose (large diameter). When you press your thumb to partially close the outlet, the water shoots out faster and farther. The **reduction in area** causes the water to **speed up**, and by **conservation of energy**, the **pressure drops** at the restriction. The Venturi meter exploits this same physics in a **controlled, measurable way**. The pressure drop between the wide section and the throat is a direct function of the velocity — and hence, the discharge.

**Other Real-World Connections**:
- **Blood Flow in Arteries (Stenosis)**: A blocked artery increases blood velocity and drops the local pressure, exactly mimicking Venturi behaviour.
- **Aircraft Wings (Lift Generation)**: Faster airflow over the curved upper surface causes lower pressure → lift.
- **Carburettor in Old Engines**: Fuel is drawn in because low pressure at the throat pulls fuel droplets from a nearby reservoir.
- **Water Supply Mains**: Used in city pipelines to monitor water distribution.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)**: **9.81 m/s²** (at sea level, at Kerala latitude ≈ 9.78–9.79 m/s²; KTU lab convention uses **9.81 m/s²**).
- **Density of water (ρ)**: **1000 kg/m³** at **4 °C**; **998 kg/m³** at **20 °C** (lab standard).
- **Specific weight of water (γ)**: **9810 N/m³**.
- **Specific gravity of mercury (S_Hg)**: **13.6** (dimensionless).
- **Converging angle of Venturi meter**: typically **19° to 23°** (to prevent flow separation).
- **Diverging angle of Venturi meter**: typically **5° to 7°** (to gradually recover pressure — the diffuser section).
- **Standard Cd range for a well-designed Venturi**: **0.95 to 0.99** (one of the highest among flow meters).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The Venturi meter operates on the principle of **two fundamental equations of ideal fluid flow**:

### Step 1 — Apply the Continuity Equation
Fluid is **incompressible** and the flow is **steady**. Therefore, the mass flow rate at every cross-section is constant. If *A₁* is the inlet area and *A₂* is the throat area:

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

This shows that the **velocity at the throat (V₂) is greater than the velocity at the inlet (V₁)** because *A₂ < A₁*.

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (incompressible, inviscid)
Between section (1) — inlet, and section (2) — throat, at the **same horizontal level** (z₁ = z₂):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Since *V₂ > V₁*, the pressure head at the throat **P₂/ρg is lower** than the inlet pressure head **P₁/ρg**. This pressure drop is measured by a **differential U-tube manometer**.

### Step 3 — Express the Pressure Drop in Terms of Manometer Deflection (*h*)
The actual manometer is a **differential manometer** (typically mercury–water or carbon tetrachloride–water) connected between (1) and (2). The equivalent head of the flowing fluid is:

$$h = \left(\frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1\right) \cdot x$$

where:
- *S_m* = Specific gravity of the manometric (heavier) fluid
- *S_f* = Specific gravity of the flowing fluid
- *x* = Manometer deflection in metres

For a **mercury manometer** with water as the flowing fluid, the standard lab expression reduces to:

$$h = \left(\frac{13.6}{1.0} - 1\right) x = 12.6 \cdot x \quad \text{(metres of water)}$$

### Step 4 — Derive the Theoretical Discharge
Solving Bernoulli's and Continuity equations simultaneously, the **ideal (theoretical) velocity** at the throat becomes:

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2g \cdot h}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

Multiplying by *A₂* gives the **theoretical discharge**:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge
In practice, the actual discharge is **less than** the theoretical value due to **frictional losses, turbulence, and vena contracta effects**. Hence:

$$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Diffuser (Diverging Cone)

> [!TIP]
> The **diverging cone** is not a measuring section — it is a **pressure-recovery device**. By gradually expanding the cross-section (angle 5°–7°), kinetic energy is reconverted into pressure energy with minimal turbulence. This minimises the **head loss** of the meter, making the Venturi **highly efficient** (Cd ≈ 0.95–0.99). Compare this with an **orifice meter**, which has a sudden expansion, leading to significantly higher permanent head loss (Cd ≈ 0.60–0.65).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Theoretical discharge (Bernoulli) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}$ | Actual discharge relation | m³/s |
| 3 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Combined working formula | m³/s |
| 4 | $h = \left(\dfrac{S_m}{S_f} - 1\right) x$ | Pressure head difference from manometer | m |
| 5 | $h = 12.6 \cdot x$ (Hg–water, S_m = 13.6) | Special case (mercury–water U-tube) | m of water |
| 6 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Definition of coefficient of discharge | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_2 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Theoretical throat velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{R}{t} \cdot A_{tank}$ | Volumetric discharge (collecting tank method) | m³/s |
| 9 | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_1^2$ and $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_2^2$ | Cross-sectional areas of inlet and throat | m² |
| 10 | $R^2 = \dfrac{A_1^2 \cdot A_2^2}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ | Constant for fixed meter geometry | m⁴ |
| 11 | $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot R \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Simplified form using geometric constant *R* | m³/s |
| 12 | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | Component-wise breakdown | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Municipal Water Supply** — Billing-grade measurement of bulk water distribution.
> - **Power Plants** — Condenser cooling water flow (vacuum creation & mass flow balance).
> - **Process Industries** — Flow of chemicals in pipelines (oil, gas, slurries).
> - **Hydraulic Labs** — Standard reference instrument for **calibrating** other flow meters (nozzles, orifice plates).
> - **Aerospace** — Test-section flow measurement in wind tunnels.
> - **Wastewater Treatment** — Influent/effluent flow measurement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Venturi Meter Discharge Equation

### Step A — Establish the Reference Sections
Consider two sections along the Venturi meter at the **same horizontal elevation**:
- **Section (1)**: Inlet, diameter *d₁*, area *A₁*, pressure *P₁*, velocity *V₁*
- **Section (2)**: Throat, diameter *d₂*, area *A₂*, pressure *P₂*, velocity *V₂*

### Step B — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an **incompressible fluid** in **steady flow**:

$$A_1 \cdot V_1 = A_2 \cdot V_2$$

Solving for *V₁*:

$$V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot V_2 \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

### Step C — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
For an **inviscid, incompressible, steady flow** with no machine work between (1) and (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

Since *z₁ = z₂* (horizontal meter), the potential head terms cancel:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} \quad \text{(Equation 2)}$$

### Step D — Substitute *V₁* from Continuity into Bernoulli
Substituting *V₁* from Equation (1) into Equation (2):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 V_2^2}{2g}$$

Factor out *V₂²*:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]}{2g}$$

Solve for *V₂²*:

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2 g (P_1 - P_2) / \rho g}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2} = \frac{2 (P_1 - P_2) / \rho}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2) / \rho}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \quad \text{(Equation 3 — Theoretical throat velocity)}$$

### Step E — Replace Pressure Difference with Manometric Head
The pressure head difference measured by a U-tube manometer is:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h = \left(\frac{S_m}{S_f} - 1\right) x$$

Substituting into Equation (3):

$$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}} \quad \text{(Equation 4)}$$

### Step F — Compute the Theoretical Discharge
The theoretical discharge is the velocity at the throat multiplied by the throat area:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by *A₁* inside the square root:

$$Q_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{A_2^2 \cdot 2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{A_1^2 A_2^2 \cdot 2 g h}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

Final form of the **theoretical discharge**:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \quad \text{(Equation 5 — THEORETICAL DISCHARGE)}$$

### Step G — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge
Real flow has frictional losses. The actual discharge is:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h} \quad \text{(Equation 6 — WORKING EQUATION)}}$$

This is the equation used in the **KTU lab** to compute theoretical discharge from manometer reading, and actual discharge from the **collecting-tank volumetric method**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variable Sensitivity

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on Q (↑ variable) | Lab-Set / Measured |
|----------|--------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Diameter at inlet | *d₁* | ↑ Q (linear) | Fixed (meter spec) |
| Diameter at throat | *d₂* | ↑ Q up to *d₁* (non-linear) | Fixed (meter spec) |
| Manometer deflection | *x* | ↑ Q (√x) | Measured |
| Specific gravity of manometer fluid | *S_m* | ↓ h, ↓ Q (for same x) | Fixed (Hg = 13.6) |
| Specific gravity of flowing fluid | *S_f* | ↑ h, ↑ Q | Fixed (water = 1.0) |
| Coefficient of discharge | *C_d* | ↑ Q (linear) | Determined by experiment |
| Gravitational acceleration | *g* | ↑ Q (√g) | Constant (9.81) |

## 3.3 Calibration Procedure — Step-by-Step (KTU Lab Standard)

1. **Setup Check**: Ensure the Venturi meter is fitted in a horizontal test section, connected to a **constant-head water tank** upstream and a **calibrated collecting tank** downstream.
2. **Connection of Manometer**: Connect a U-tube differential mercury manometer across the **inlet tapping** and the **throat tapping** using flexible tubes. Bleed out all air.
3. **Establishing Steady Flow**: Open the inlet valve and let water flow. Adjust the **flow control valve** at the outlet to set a desired flow rate.
4. **Note Steady State**: Wait until the manometer reading *x* is constant (steady-state).
5. **Collect Volume**: Simultaneously start a stopwatch and direct the discharge into the collecting tank. After a fixed time *t* (say, 30 seconds), remove the tank and note the **rise in water level (R)** in the collecting tank.
6. **Repeat for Different Flows**: Vary the outlet valve setting and repeat Steps 4 and 5 for at least **6 to 8 different flow rates** spanning the operating range.
7. **Compute Discharges**:
   - Actual discharge: $Q_{act} = \dfrac{R \cdot A_{tank}}{t}$
   - Theoretical discharge: $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2 g h}$, where $h = 12.6 x$
   - $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$
8. **Plot Calibration Curves**:
   - Graph 1: **√h (X-axis) vs Q_act (Y-axis)** → Straight line through origin.
   - Graph 2: **√h (X-axis) vs Q_th (Y-axis)** → Theoretical reference line.
   - Graph 3 (Optional): **√h (X-axis) vs C_d (Y-axis)** → Should approach a constant.
9. **Interpretation**: The slope of Graph 1 divided by the slope of Graph 2 = **C_d** of the meter.

## 3.4 Tabular Column Format (Model Lab Record Table)

| Sl. No. | Time *t* (s) | Rise *R* (cm) | Manometer *x* (cm of Hg) | $h = 12.6 x$ (m) | $\sqrt{h}$ (m½/s) | $Q_{act}$ (×10⁻³ m³/s) | $Q_{th}$ (×10⁻³ m³/s) | $C_d$ |
|---------|--------------|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | 30 | 12.0 | 4.5 | 0.567 | 0.753 | … | … | … |
| 2 | 30 | 15.5 | 6.2 | 0.781 | 0.884 | … | … | … |
| 3 | 30 | 19.0 | 8.0 | 1.008 | 1.004 | … | … | … |
| 4 | 30 | 22.0 | 9.5 | 1.197 | 1.094 | … | … | … |
| 5 | 30 | 25.5 | 11.0 | 1.386 | 1.177 | … | … | … |
| 6 | 30 | 28.0 | 12.5 | 1.575 | 1.255 | … | … | … |
| 7 | 30 | 30.0 | 13.5 | 1.701 | 1.304 | … | … | … |
| 8 | 30 | 32.5 | 15.0 | 1.890 | 1.375 | … | … | … |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Constant Head Water Tank] -->|Gravity feed| nodeB[Sluice Valve - Inlet Control]
    nodeB -->|Inlet pipe, diameter d1| nodeC[Venturi Meter Inlet Section]
    nodeC -->|Pressure tapping P1| nodeD[Differential U-tube Manometer - Hg]
    nodeC -->|Converging cone 19 to 23 deg| nodeE[Venturi Throat - diameter d2]
    nodeE -->|Pressure tapping P2| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Reads deflection x| nodeK[Manometer Scale - cm of Hg]
    nodeE -->|Diverging cone 5 to 7 deg| nodeF[Venturi Outlet Section]
    nodeF -->|Outlet pipe| nodeG[Flow Control Valve]
    nodeG -->|Diverts to| nodeH[Collecting Tank of area A_tank]
    nodeH -->|Rise R in time t| nodeI[Stopwatch + Measuring Gauge]
    nodeI -->|Q_act = R times A_tank over t| nodeJ[Computation Block Cd]
    nodeK -->|h = 12.6 times x| nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Q_th from geometry| nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Cd = Q_act over Q_th| nodeL[Calibration Curve Plot]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Calibration Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Fix meter in horizontal test rig] --> step2[Step 2: Connect U-tube manometer across inlet and throat]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Bleed air from manometer tubes]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Open inlet valve to start flow]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Adjust flow control valve to set flow]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Wait for steady manometer reading x]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Record x and start stopwatch + collecting tank]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Stop after time t, measure rise R]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Compute Q_act, Q_th, and Cd]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Vary flow and repeat for 6 to 8 readings]
    step10 --> step11[Step 11: Plot sqrt h vs Q_act and Q_th]
    step11 --> step12[Step 12: Determine average Cd and conclude]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Theoretical & Actual Discharge Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Inlet Area A1] -->|Continuity| inB[Throat Area A2]
    inB -->|Bernoulli| inC[Pressure Drop h]
    inC -->|Ideal flow| inD[Theoretical Discharge Q_th]
    inD -->|Multiply by Cd| inE[Actual Discharge Q_act]
    inE -->|Losses considered| inF[Real Pipe Flow]
    inF -->|Volumetric method| inG[Collecting Tank R and t]
    inG -->|Q_act = R times A_tank over t| inE
    inE -->|Compare with Q_th| inH[Compute Cd = Q_act over Q_th]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (for record purposes)

> [!TIP]
> **Components of the Standard KTU Venturi Meter Test Rig (Block-wise listing)**:
> 
> 1. **Overhead Sump/Constant-Head Tank**: Maintains a steady hydraulic head.
> 2. **Inlet Pipe** with **inlet pressure tapping** at distance *≥ 5D* upstream of the meter.
> 3. **Venturi Assembly**: Converging section (length ≈ 2.5D), throat (length ≈ 1D), diverging section (length ≈ 7.5D).
> 4. **Throat Pressure Tapping**: Connected to the same differential manometer.
> 5. **Differential U-tube Mercury Manometer**: With two limbs, bleed valves, and a graduated scale.
> 6. **Outlet Flow Control Valve**: For varying discharge.
> 7. **Collecting Tank**: Calibrated (cross-section known), fitted with a **piezometer/level gauge** and a **stop-cock** for diverting flow.
> 8. **Stopwatch**: ±0.1 s accuracy.
> 9. **Measuring Cylinder / Hook Gauge**: For accurate *R* measurement.
