**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
The main components are: (i) **supply (drive) pipe**, (ii) **waste (impulse) valve**, (iii) **delivery (check) valve**, (iv) **air vessel**, and (v) **delivery pipe**. The **air vessel** functions as a **pulsation dampener** — it stores water at high pressure during each water-hammer stroke and releases it smoothly during the refill phase, ensuring that the flow in the delivery pipe is **continuous rather than intermittent**. The trapped air cushion also prevents the delivery pipe from experiencing damaging pressure transients, prolonging the life of the system.

*(Self-Assessment: 3 marks — 1 for naming components, 1 for stating air vessel's dampening role, 1 for continuous-flow benefit.)*

---

## Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 marks each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**Q.A. (a) [7 marks] Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a single-acting hydraulic ram. What are its advantages and limitations?**

**Model Solution Outline:**
- **Construction (2 marks):** Draw a labelled sketch showing supply tank, drive pipe of diameter D, ram body, waste valve at the bottom, delivery check valve at the top, air vessel, and delivery pipe of diameter d rising to the delivery tank. Annotate H, h, D, d, L.
- **Working (3 marks):** Describe the **four phases** of the cycle — acceleration, snap-close, water-hammer/delivery, and refill. Emphasise that no external energy is supplied; the ram is *self-actuating*.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** No electricity/fuel, low maintenance, long life (>20 years), simple to install, ideal for remote hilly regions.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Low overall efficiency (40–70%), waste of water through the waste valve, requires a minimum supply head (typically 1 m), and delivery rate is small.

**Q.A. (b) [7 marks]** In an experiment on a hydraulic ram, the following observations were recorded: **H = 2.5 m**, **h = 10 m**, **D = 50 mm**, **d = 20 mm**, **Q = 4.0 L/s**, **Q_d = 0.5 L/s**. Find (i) the **Rankine efficiency**, (ii) the **D'Aubuisson coefficient C** based on the experimental data, and (iii) the **waste discharge q**.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rankine efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{Q_d \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} = \frac{0.5 \times 10}{4.0 \times 2.5} = \frac{5.0}{10.0} = 0.50 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 50\%
$$

**(ii) D'Aubuisson's coefficient C from experiment:**
Rearranging the formula:
$$
C = \frac{Q}{Q_d} \cdot \frac{h \cdot d^{2}}{H \cdot D^{2}} \quad \text{(note: } C \text{ defined for the experimental ratio)}
$$
Wait — substitute the **measured** ratio $\frac{Q}{Q_d} = \frac{4.0}{0.5} = 8$ and the head/diameter terms:
$$
C = \frac{Q}{Q_d} \cdot \frac{h}{H} \cdot \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1}
$$
Actually, by definition (from the formula):
$$
\frac{Q}{Q_d} = \frac{H}{h \cdot C \cdot (d/D)^{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; C = \frac{H \cdot Q_d}{h \cdot Q \cdot (d/D)^{2}}
$$

$$
C = \frac{2.5 \times 0.5}{10 \times 4.0 \times (20/50)^{2}} = \frac{1.25}{40 \times 0.16} = \frac{1.25}{6.4} = 0.195
$$

This value ($C \approx 0.20$) is *lower* than the textbook 0.55–0.65 because (i) the experimental $Q_d$ includes refill-phase losses, and (ii) drive-pipe friction reduces effective velocity. **Report the value as obtained and remark on the deviation.**

**(iii) Waste discharge:**
$$
q = Q - Q_d = 4.0 - 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ L/s}
$$
Waste fraction $= 87.5\%$ of the supply — a typical figure for high-head lifts.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**Q.B. (a) [7 marks] Derive D'Aubuisson's formula for a hydraulic ram, clearly stating the assumptions involved.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Velocity in the drive pipe follows Torricelli's law with an empirical coefficient **C**: $V = C\sqrt{2gH}$.
2. The same water that surges through the drive pipe also constitutes the slug delivered to the air vessel in one stroke.
3. The waste valve closes instantaneously, and all losses are lumped into the coefficient **C**.
4. Flow is steady in the time-averaged sense; the air vessel perfectly smoothens pulsations.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Step 1 — Drive-pipe discharge: $Q_{\text{drive}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2} \cdot C\sqrt{2gH}$

Step 2 — Delivery-pipe surge discharge: $Q_{\text{inst}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d^{2} \cdot C\sqrt{2gH}$

Step 3 — Ratio:
$$
\frac{Q_{\text{drive}}}{Q_{\text{inst}}} = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^{2}
$$

Step 4 — Time-averaged supply $Q$ is greater than the instantaneous delivery because water is supplied continuously, while delivery happens only during a fraction of the cycle. Energy considerations show this fraction equals $\dfrac{h}{H}$ scaled by the area ratio, giving:

$$
\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_d} = \frac{H}{h \cdot C \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^{2}}}
$$

**Q.B. (b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic ram is required to deliver **5 L/s** against a head of **30 m**. The available supply head is **3 m**, and the velocity coefficient for the drive pipe is **C = 0.65**. If the ratio of delivery-pipe diameter to supply-pipe diameter is **d/D = 0.2**, determine the required **supply discharge Q** and the **diameter D** of the supply pipe.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply D'Aubuisson's formula to find Q:**
$$
Q = Q_d \cdot \frac{H}{h \cdot C \cdot (d/D)^{2}}
$$
Substitute: $Q_d = 0.005$ m³/s, $H = 3$ m, $h = 30$ m, $C = 0.65$, $(d/D)^{2} = 0.04$:

$$
Q = 0.005 \times \frac{3}{30 \times 0.65 \times 0.04} = 0.005 \times \frac{3}{0.78} = 0.005 \times 3.846 = 0.01923 \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

So $Q \approx 19.23$ L/s.

**Step 2 — Rankine efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{Q_d \cdot h}{Q \cdot H} = \frac{0.005 \times 30}{0.01923 \times 3} = \frac{0.15}{0.0577} = 2.60 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 260\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall #2 — Efficiencies > 100% indicate a design mismatch.** The chosen diameter ratio (d/D = 0.2) and coefficient C = 0.65 are *too optimistic* for the requested head ratio $h/H = 10$. In practice, the designer would (i) increase the drive-pipe length **L** to give the water more time to accelerate, (ii) increase the drive-pipe diameter **D** (or decrease **d**), or (iii) accept a lower $Q_d$. The exam expects the student to **report the calculated value, then comment on the impracticality**. Deduct 1 mark if the student blindly accepts $\eta > 100\%$ as a valid design.

**Step 3 — Compute D for a given velocity in the drive pipe.**
The supply-pipe velocity is $V = C\sqrt{2gH} = 0.65 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3} = 0.65 \times 7.668 = 4.984$ m/s.

Since $Q = A_D \cdot V$:

$$
A_D = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{0.01923}{4.984} = 3.859 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_D}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.859 \times 10^{-3}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{4.914 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0701 \text{ m} \approx 70 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Delivery-pipe diameter:** $d = 0.2 \times 70 = 14$ mm.

**Final Answer:** $Q \approx 19.2$ L/s, $D \approx 70$ mm, $d \approx 14$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top Reasons for Marks Deducted**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $g$**: Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²; students who omit this lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up $Q$ and $Q_d$**: $Q$ is the *total supply* (largest), $Q_d$ is the *delivered* (smallest), and $q$ is the *wasted*. Confusing these three in the formula yields absurd answers.
> 3. **Not commenting on $\eta > 100\%$**: Always end a numerical problem with a brief physical interpretation.
> 4. **Sketch missing the air vessel**: A hydraulic-ram sketch without an air vessel is marked incomplete (deduct 1 mark).
> 5. **Using Rankine efficiency incorrectly**: Some texts use $\eta = 1 - q/Q$ (the *loss fraction*); the KTU convention is $\eta = Q_d h/(QH)$. Stick to the latter unless the question specifies.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydraulic Ram** = self-actuated pump using **water-hammer effect**; no external power.
- **Key variables**: $H, h, D, d, L, Q, Q_d, q, C, N, \eta$.
- **Continuity**: $Q = Q_d + q$.
- **D'Aubuisson's formula**: $\dfrac{Q}{Q_d} = \dfrac{H}{h \cdot C \cdot (d/D)^{2}}$ — memorize this in *all three* equivalent forms.
- **Rankine efficiency**: $\eta = \dfrac{Q_d h}{QH}$ — typical experimental range **40 – 70%**.
- **Velocity coefficient $C$**: from experiment, $C = 0.55 - 0.65$ for new smooth pipes; lower for old/rougher pipes.
- **Phases of cycle**: Acceleration → Snap-Close → Water-Hammer/Delivery → Refill.
- **Components**: supply pipe, waste valve, delivery check valve, air vessel, delivery pipe.
- **Air vessel** ensures *continuous* delivery by smoothing the pulsating water-hammer surges.
- **Design ratios**: a *small* delivery pipe (small $d$) gives a *high* $Q_d/Q$ ratio, but the absolute $Q_d$ also drops.
- **Practical applications**: rural water supply, hill-station overhead tanks, off-grid farms, low-cost irrigation.
- **Lab report essentials**: (i) labelled schematic, (ii) observation table with $H, h, D, d, L$ at the top, (iii) sample calculation of $Q$, $Q_d$, $q$, $C$, $\eta$, (iv) comparison of measured $C$ with the standard 0.6 ± 0.05, and (v) a brief **"Sources of Error"** section (pipe friction, valve-bounce timing, air-vessel recharge, measurement of $h$).
- **Exam trick**: if you ever get $\eta > 100\%$, immediately interpret it as an **optimistic design assumption** (coefficient $C$ taken too high) — never accept it as a *real* efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Reynolds experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical fluid mechanics laboratory experiment conducted by **Sir Osborne Reynolds in 1883** to visually demonstrate and distinguish between the two principal regimes of viscous fluid flow through a pipe — namely, **laminar (streamline) flow** and **turbulent flow** — and to determine experimentally the **critical Reynolds Number (Re)** at which the transition between these regimes occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Reynolds experiment is a visualization-based experimental setup used to study the nature of fluid flow (laminar/turbulent) through a circular pipe by injecting a tracer dye, and to compute the dimensionless Reynolds number that governs the flow regime classification.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are standing on a bridge watching a smooth, slow-flowing river. The water glides in **perfectly parallel layers**, almost like glass sheets sliding over each other — this is what we call **laminar flow**. Now imagine the same river during heavy rainfall or when rocks obstruct its path — the water becomes chaotic, forming **eddies, swirls, and random fluctuations**. This is **turbulent flow**.

The Reynolds Experiment does exactly this in a controlled laboratory environment:

- A **transparent glass tube** carries water at a controlled rate.
- A thin stream of **colored dye (methylene blue or ink)** is injected at the entrance of the tube.
- If the water flows **slowly**, the dye forms a **straight, undisturbed line** down the center of the tube → **Laminar Flow**.
- If the water flows **rapidly**, the dye **breaks apart, mixes, and diffuses chaotically** → **Turbulent Flow**.
- The flow rate at which this transition happens gives us the **Critical Reynolds Number**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Critical Reynolds Number for pipe flow: **Re_critical ≈ 2000** (lower critical) to **4000** (upper critical)
> - Dye used: **Methylene Blue** (or red ink) due to high visibility and non-reactivity with water
> - Standard water properties used: $\nu$ (kinematic viscosity) at room temperature ≈ **1.003 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** at 20°C

## 1.3 Key Physical Insights

- The Reynolds number is a **dimensionless quantity** — it has no units.
- It represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** in a flowing fluid.
- When **inertial forces dominate** (high Re) → turbulent flow.
- When **viscous forces dominate** (low Re) → laminar flow.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

## 1.4 Significance in KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is listed under Module Experiment 2 of PCIEL307 and is a **mandatory practical** for the KTU 2024 B.Tech Civil Engineering laboratory curriculum. Students are expected to (a) conduct the experiment, (b) tabulate observations, (c) calculate Re for varying flow rates, and (d) identify the critical Re.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — What Determines Flow Regime?

The behaviour of a real (viscous) fluid flowing through a pipe is governed by the **relative magnitude** of two competing forces:

### 2.1.1 Inertial Forces (Driving Force)
These arise from the fluid's **mass × acceleration** and tend to keep the fluid moving forward. They promote **chaos and instability**.

$$F_{\text{inertial}} \propto \rho V^2 D^2$$

### 2.1.2 Viscous Forces (Resisting Force)
These arise from the fluid's **internal friction (stickiness)** and tend to **dampen disturbances**, keeping flow smooth.

$$F_{\text{viscous}} \propto \mu V D$$

### 2.1.3 The Ratio — Reynolds Number

$$\text{Re} = \frac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho V^2 D^2}{\mu V D} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

Where $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

## 2.2 Flow Classification Based on Reynolds Number

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Dye Behaviour | Physical Characteristics |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| **Laminar Flow** | Re < 2000 | Straight, unbroken line | Parallel layers, no mixing |
| **Transitional Flow** | 2000 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 | Wavy, occasionally breaking | Unstable, intermittent bursts |
| **Turbulent Flow** | Re > 4000 | Completely diffused, chaotic | Random eddies, vigorous mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Value:** The **lower critical Reynolds number Re = 2000** is the threshold for KTU board examinations. Below this value, flow is *guaranteed* laminar. Above it, laminar flow may still exist transiently, but turbulent flow becomes the stable state above Re = 4000.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Experiment

1. **Setup preparation:** Fill the overhead tank with clean water; ensure steady overflow maintains a constant head.
2. **Establish slow flow:** Open the control valve slightly to allow a small trickle of water through the glass tube.
3. **Dye injection:** Inject dye through the needle into the centre of the bell-mouthed entrance.
4. **Observation — Laminar:** At low flow rates, the dye appears as a **single, sharp, straight coloured line** along the tube axis.
5. **Gradual increase:** Slowly increase the flow rate using the control valve.
6. **Transition point:** At some critical flow rate, the dye line starts to **oscillate and break**.
7. **Turbulent regime:** At higher flow rates, the dye **completely diffuses and colours the entire cross-section**.
8. **Data collection:** For each flow rate, measure the **discharge (Q)** using the collecting tank and stopwatch.
9. **Calculation:** Compute velocity $V = Q/A$ and Reynolds number $\text{Re} = VD/\nu$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Standard SI Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|------------------|
| Re | Reynolds Number | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless |
| V | Mean velocity of flow | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s |
| Q | Volume flow rate (discharge) | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{tank}}}{t}$ | m³/s |
| A | Cross-sectional area of pipe | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | m² |
| D | Internal diameter of glass tube | Measured using vernier caliper | m |
| $\rho$ | Density of water | $\rho \approx 998$ kg/m³ (at 20°C) | kg/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s (at 20°C) | Pa·s or N·s/m² |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu \approx 1.003 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s (at 20°C) | m²/s |
| Re_cr | Critical Reynolds Number | $\approx 2000$ (lower) / $\approx 4000$ (upper) | Dimensionless |
| h | Head of water in tank | Measured by piezometer or scale | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet Tip for KTU Exams:** When solving numerical problems, always state the **assumed water temperature** and the corresponding **kinematic viscosity** explicitly. Examiners allocate marks for this.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline design:** Engineers use Re to predict pressure drop and pumping requirements.
- **Chemical engineering:** Reactor design requires knowing whether mixing will be laminar (poor) or turbulent (efficient).
- **Aerospace:** Flow over aircraft wings is classified using Re to predict boundary layer behaviour.
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow through arteries is analysed via Re to detect abnormalities.
- **Hydraulic machines:** Selection of pumps and turbines depends on flow regime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Velocity from Discharge

### Given:
- Volume of water collected in measuring tank = $V_{\text{tank}}$ (m³)
- Time taken to collect this volume = $t$ (seconds)
- Diameter of the glass observation tube = $D$ (m)

### Step 1 — Discharge calculation
The discharge (volume flow rate) through the pipe is the volume collected per unit time:

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}}{t}$$

### Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the pipe
For a circular cross-section:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Mean velocity
By continuity, the mean velocity equals the discharge divided by the area:

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}/t}{\pi D^2/4} = \frac{4 V_{\text{tank}}}{\pi D^2 \, t}$$

This is the velocity used in the Reynolds number formula.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reynolds Number Expression

### Step 1 — Start with the inertial-to-viscous force ratio

$$\text{Re} = \frac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute dimensional forms

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V^2 D^2}{\mu V D}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

### Step 4 — Express in terms of kinematic viscosity $\nu$

Since $\nu = \mu / \rho$, substituting $\mu = \rho \nu$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Re} &= \frac{\rho V D}{\rho \nu} \\
\text{Re} &= \frac{V D}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Verify dimensions

$$[\text{Re}] = \frac{[\text{m/s}] \times [\text{m}]}{[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]} = \frac{\text{m}^2/\text{s}}{\text{m}^2/\text{s}} = \text{Dimensionless} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem Statement:
In a Reynolds experiment, water at **20°C** flows through a glass tube of internal diameter **$D = 25$ mm**. The volume of water collected in the measuring tank is **$V_{\text{tank}} = 2.5$ litres** in **$t = 60$ seconds**. The dye is observed to flow as a **straight, unbroken line**. Determine the Reynolds number and classify the flow regime.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $V_{\text{tank}} = 2.5 \text{ litres} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $t = 60 \text{ s}$
- $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (at 20°C)
- Dye behaviour: straight line → laminar flow expected

### Step 1 — Calculate discharge

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}}{t} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60}$$

$$Q = 4.1667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate cross-sectional area

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.025)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Calculate mean velocity

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.1667 \times 10^{-5}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$V = 0.0849 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Reynolds Number

$$\text{Re} = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{0.0849 \times 0.025}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{2.1225 \times 10^{-3}}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Re} = 2116.15 \approx 2116}$$

### Step 5 — Classification

Since **Re = 2116 > 2000**, this is in the **transitional zone**, though the dye observation of a straight line suggests it is on the verge of transition. If the flow rate is slightly reduced, Re would drop below 2000, confirming laminar flow.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Observation Table

| Sl. No. | Trial | Volume Collected (m³) | Time (s) | Discharge Q (m³/s) | Velocity V (m/s) | Reynolds Number Re | Flow Regime Observed |
|---------|-------|----------------------|----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | I | 1.0 × 10⁻³ | 60 | 1.667 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.0340 | 846 | Laminar |
| 2 | II | 2.0 × 10⁻³ | 60 | 3.333 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.0679 | 1692 | Laminar |
| 3 | III | 2.5 × 10⁻³ | 60 | 4.167 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.0849 | 2116 | Transition |
| 4 | IV | 4.0 × 10⁻³ | 60 | 6.667 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.1358 | 3385 | Transition |
| 5 | V | 6.0 × 10⁻³ | 60 | 1.000 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.2037 | 5077 | Turbulent |

## 3.5 Sources of Experimental Error (KTU Frequently Asked)

1. **Fluctuations in head** due to inadequate overflow arrangement.
2. **Inaccurate volume measurement** in the collecting tank.
3. **Temperature variation** affecting $\nu$.
4. **Dye injection disturbance** at very low flow rates.
5. **Air bubbles** in the glass tube distorting dye visibility.
6. **Improper alignment** of the dye needle with the tube axis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Experiment Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Overhead Water Tank with Overflow Spillway] --> B[Constant Head Maintenance]
    B --> C[Control Valve for Flow Regulation]
    C --> D[Bell Mouth Entrance]
    D --> E[Transparent Glass Observation Tube]
    F[Dye Reservoir] --> G[Dye Injection Needle]
    G --> D
    E --> H[Outlet Discharge]
    H --> I[Measuring Collecting Tank]
    I --> J[Stopwatch Timing]
    J --> K[Volume and Time Data]
    K --> L[Calculation of Q and V]
    L --> M[Computation of Reynolds Number Re]
    M --> N{Flow Regime Classification}
    N -->|Re less than 2000| O[Laminar Streamline Flow]
    N -->|Re between 2000 and 4000| P[Transitional Flow]
    N -->|Re greater than 4000| Q[Turbulent Chaotic Flow]
    O --> R[Visual Confirmation: Straight Dye Line]
    P --> S[Visual Confirmation: Wavy Dye Line]
    Q --> T[Visual Confirmation: Diffused Dye]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Fill Tank and Maintain Constant Head] --> S2[Step 2: Open Valve Slightly]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Inject Dye at Bell Mouth]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Observe Dye Pattern in Tube]
    S4 --> S5{Is Dye Straight}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Record as Laminar]
    S5 -->|No and Wavy| S7[Record as Transitional]
    S5 -->|No and Diffused| S8[Record as Turbulent]
    S6 --> S9[Measure Volume and Time]
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Calculate Q and V]
    S10 --> S11[Compute Reynolds Number]
    S11 --> S12[Repeat for Higher Flow Rates]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Layout of Apparatus

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph ReservoirSection [Water Supply System]
        R1[Main Water Supply] --> R2[Overhead Tank]
        R2 --> R3[Overflow Pipe]
        R2 --> R4[Inlet to Test Section]
    end
    subgraph TestSection [Observation Section]
        R4 --> V1[Flow Control Valve]
        V1 --> BM[Bell Mouth Inlet]
        D1[Dye Container] --> N1[Dye Injection Needle]
        N1 --> BM
        BM --> GT[Glass Tube Diameter D]
        GT --> OUT[Outlet]
    end
    subgraph MeasurementSection [Data Collection System]
        OUT --> CT[Collecting Tank]
        CT --> SW[Stopwatch]
        SW --> CL[Calculation Block]
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree for KTU Lab Examination Viva

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the purpose of Reynolds experiment] --> A1[To study flow regimes and find critical Reynolds number]
    Q2[Which dye is used and why] --> A2[Methylene blue due to high visibility and water solubility]
    Q3[What is critical Reynolds number for pipe flow] --> A3[Lower critical 2000 and Upper critical 4000]
    Q4[Why is the tank fitted with an overflow] --> A4[To maintain constant head and thus constant pressure]
    Q5[What does Re less than 2000 indicate] --> A5[Laminar streamline flow]
    Q6[Is Reynolds number dimensional] --> A6[No it is dimensionless]
    Q7[How is mean velocity calculated] --> A7[V equals Q divided by A equals 4Q over pi D squared]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Reynolds Number. State its significance in fluid flow classification.**

**Model Answer:**

The Reynolds Number is a **dimensionless parameter** that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces acting on a flowing fluid.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Significance:**
- It classifies flow as **laminar (Re < 2000)**, **transitional (2000 < Re < 4000)**, or **turbulent (Re > 4000)**.
- It predicts whether a flow will be smooth or chaotic.
- It serves as a **scaling parameter** for dynamic similarity between model and prototype in fluid mechanics experiments.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State the principle of the Reynolds experiment. What observations confirm laminar and turbulent flow?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** The Reynolds experiment visualizes fluid flow through a transparent tube by injecting a coloured dye tracer, and observes the dye behaviour as flow rate is gradually varied.

**Observations:**
- **Laminar flow confirmed:** Dye forms a **single, straight, unbroken coloured line** along the axis of the tube.
- **Turbulent flow confirmed:** Dye **completely diffuses, mixes, and colours the entire cross-section** of the tube due to chaotic eddies and cross-mixing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the apparatus and procedure of the Reynolds experiment to determine the critical Reynolds number for flow through a pipe.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Reynolds experiment, water at 25°C flows through a glass tube of internal diameter 20 mm. The volume of water collected is 3 litres in 50 seconds, and the dye moves as a straight line. Calculate the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime. Given: $\nu = 0.897 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s at 25°C.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Apparatus and Procedure:**

The apparatus consists of:
1. **Overhead tank** with overflow arrangement to maintain constant head.
2. **Bell-mouthed inlet** to ensure smooth entry of water.
3. **Transparent glass tube** of known diameter D (measured with vernier caliper).
4. **Dye reservoir** with a fine needle injection tube.
5. **Control valve** at the upstream end.
6. **Collecting tank** at the downstream end.
7. **Stopwatch** for timing.

**Procedure:**
1. Fill the overhead tank and maintain constant head using overflow.
2. Open the control valve slightly to allow a small, steady flow.
3. Inject dye through the needle; observe the dye pattern.
4. For low flow rates, record volume collected (V) and time (t).
5. Compute discharge Q = V/t, velocity V = Q/A, and Re = VD/ν.
6. Gradually increase flow rate and repeat observations.
7. Note the flow rate at which the dye line first becomes wavy (lower critical).
8. Continue until the dye fully diffuses (turbulent regime).
9. Plot or tabulate Re vs. flow rate and identify the critical Re.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $V_{\text{tank}} = 3 \text{ litres} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $t = 50 \text{ s}$
- $\nu = 0.897 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Dye behaviour: straight line

**Step 1 — Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}}{t} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{50} = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.020)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-5}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1910 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Reynolds Number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{0.1910 \times 0.020}{0.897 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{3.819 \times 10^{-3}}{0.897 \times 10^{-6}} = 4257.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{Re} \approx 4258}$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

Since Re ≈ 4258 > 4000, the flow is in the **turbulent regime**. However, the dye was observed as a straight line, which contradicts the calculation. This discrepancy indicates that the flow is at the lower boundary of turbulence where laminar behaviour may still briefly persist, or there is experimental error in volume/time measurement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for Reynolds number starting from the ratio of inertial and viscous forces. State the critical Reynolds number for pipe flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a Reynolds experiment conducted at 30°C, water flows through a tube of 15 mm diameter. At a particular flow rate, the discharge measured is 0.5 litres in 30 seconds, and the dye pattern is wavy. Determine: (i) the mean velocity, (ii) the Reynolds number, and (iii) classify the flow. Given: $\nu = 0.801 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s at 30°C.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Inertial force per unit area (dynamic pressure):

$$F_{\text{inertial}} \propto \rho V^2$$

Viscous force per unit area (shear stress):

$$F_{\text{viscous}} \propto \mu \frac{V}{D}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V^2}{\mu V / D} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

**Critical Reynolds Number:**
- Lower critical: **Re_cr = 2000**
- Upper critical: **Re_cr = 4000**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- $V_{\text{tank}} = 0.5 \text{ litres} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $t = 30 \text{ s}$
- $\nu = 0.801 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- Dye behaviour: wavy → transitional flow expected

**(i) Mean Velocity:**

$$Q = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0943 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Reynolds Number:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{0.0943 \times 0.015}{0.801 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.415 \times 10^{-3}}{0.801 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{Re} = 1766$$

**(iii) Classification:**

Since **Re = 1766 < 2000**, the flow is **laminar**, despite the observed wavy dye pattern. The wave observation indicates the flow is approaching the transition zone. Reducing the flow rate further would yield a perfectly straight dye line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — especially mm → m for diameter and litres → m³ for volume. This alone costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Not stating water temperature** and corresponding kinematic viscosity. Examiners allocate **1 mark** explicitly for this.
> 3. **Confusing Re_cr = 2000 (lower) with 4000 (upper).** The KTU answer key typically uses **2000** as the threshold.
> 4. **Not verifying dimensional homogeneity** of the final Re value.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** in Part B — a neat labelled diagram carries **2 marks**.
> 6. **Not relating Re back to flow classification** in the final line of the numerical answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Notes for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Aim:** To visualize laminar and turbulent flow through a pipe and determine the critical Reynolds number.
- **Inventor:** Sir Osborne Reynolds (1883).
- **Apparatus essentials:** Overhead tank with overflow, bell-mouth entrance, glass tube, dye reservoir with needle, control valve, collecting tank, stopwatch.
- **Dye:** Methylene blue or red ink.
- **Reynolds Number formula:**
  $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$
- **Critical Re for pipe flow:** Lower = **2000**, Upper = **4000**.
- **Laminar:** Re < 2000 → straight dye line.
- **Transitional:** 2000 < Re < 4000 → wavy or fluctuating dye line.
- **Turbulent:** Re > 4000 → fully diffused dye.
- **Discharge equation:**
  $$Q = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}}{t}$$
- **Velocity equation:**
  $$V = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water:**
  - At 20°C: $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
  - At 25°C: $\nu = 0.897 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
  - At 30°C: $\nu = 0.801 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- **Significance:** Re is dimensionless, governs flow regime, enables dynamic similarity in model studies.
- **Common KTU viva questions:**
  1. Why is the bell mouth used? → To ensure smooth, disturbance-free entry.
  2. Why is constant head maintained? → To ensure steady, uniform flow rate.
  3. Why does dye break in turbulent flow? → Due to random eddy formation and cross-stream mixing.
  4. Can Re be negative? → No; it's a ratio of magnitudes.
  5. What happens if the pipe is inclined? → The Re value is unchanged as it depends on V and D, not pipe orientation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of metacentric height and radius of gyration of floating bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of metacentric height and radius of gyration of floating bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentric Height (GM)** is the distance between the **Centre of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and its **Metacentre (M)**. It is the most critical geometric parameter that decides the **static stability** of a floating or submerged body in a fluid.

When a floating body is tilted by a small angle **θ**, the **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** shifts from its original position to a new position **B'**. The vertical line passing through the new centre of buoyancy **B'** intersects the original axis of symmetry (vertical line through G) at a point called the **Metacentre (M)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Radius of Gyration (k)** of the waterline area of a floating body is defined as the distance from the axis of rotation (about which the body tilts) at which the entire area of the waterline plane could be concentrated to give the same second moment of area as the actual distribution.
> 
> Mathematically: $k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A}$
> 
> where **I** is the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the longitudinal axis of tilting, and **A** is the area of the waterline plane.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Passenger Standing in a Tilting Bus**
> 
> Imagine you are standing in a moving bus. When the bus suddenly tilts (turns a sharp corner), your body leans. If you try to stand with your feet together and lean, you will fall easily. But if you spread your legs apart (creating a wider base), you can resist the tilt and recover. 
> 
> The "spreading of legs" is exactly what a **wider waterline area** does for a ship. A wider ship (larger area A) will be more stable because it has a higher metacentric height.
> 
> Think of a **narrow canoe** versus a **wide cargo ship**:
> - **Canoe (small A):** Tilts easily, low GM, less stable → you fall into the water
> - **Cargo Ship (large A):** Resists tilting, high GM, more stable → carries tonnes of cargo safely
> 
> The **radius of gyration (k)** is like the "effective spread" of the base — it tells you how well the waterline area is distributed from the central axis.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Centre of Gravity | G | Point where the weight of the body acts vertically downward |
| Centre of Buoyancy | B | Centroid of the displaced fluid volume |
| Metacentre | M | Point where the vertical line through the new B' intersects the original vertical axis |
| Metacentric Height | GM | Distance from G to M (positive ⇒ stable) |
| Metacentric Radius | BM | Distance from B to M |
| Displaced Volume | V | Volume of fluid pushed aside by the submerged portion |
| Waterline Plane Area | A | Area of the horizontal cross-section at the water surface |
| Second Moment of Area | I | Geometric property of the waterline cross-section |
| Radius of Gyration | k | $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ |

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of fresh water (ρ)** = **1000 kg/m³**
> - **Specific weight of water (w)** = **9810 N/m³** (or 9.81 kN/m³)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **101.325 kPa** (relevant when air-pressure correction is needed)
> - Experimental tilt angle θ is kept small (typically **5° to 15°**) so that **tan θ ≈ sin θ ≈ θ (in radians)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory: Stability of a Floating Body

A floating body is said to be in **stable equilibrium** if, after being slightly tilted, it returns to its original position. The criteria for stability revolve around the relative position of G and M.

### 2.1.1 The Three Cases of Stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **GM > 0 (Stable):** Metacentre M lies **above** G → A restoring couple is created → Body returns to upright position.
> - **GM = 0 (Neutral):** M coincides with G → No restoring moment → Body remains in the tilted position.
> - **GM < 0 (Unstable):** M lies **below** G → An overturning couple is created → Body capsizes.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Tilt Analysis

1. **Initial State:** Body floats with weight W acting at G and buoyancy F_B = ρgV acting upward at B. G and B lie on the same vertical axis.

2. **Tilting:** Body is rotated through a small angle θ about a longitudinal axis. The submerged volume changes shape → the centroid of this new volume (new B') shifts laterally.

3. **Locating Metacentre:** A vertical line is drawn upward through the new B'. The intersection of this vertical line with the original axis (G-B line) is the **Metacentre M**.

4. **Couple Formation:** The weight W (downward at G) and the new buoyancy force (upward at B') form a **couple**. If M is above G, this couple is **restoring** (counteracts the tilt).

5. **Stability Criterion:** The body is stable if and only if **GM > 0**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all the high-yield equations you must memorise for KTU exams. These formulas are the **backbone of all numerical problems** in Module Experiment 2.

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho \, g \, V = w \, V$ | Buoyant force (Archimedes' Principle) | N |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho g}$ | Displaced volume from equilibrium | m³ |
| 3 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Distance from B to M (Metacentric radius) | m |
| 4 | $k^2 = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Radius of gyration squared of waterline area | m² |
| 5 | $BM = \dfrac{A \, k^2}{V}$ | BM expressed using k | m |
| 6 | $GM = BM \pm BG$ | Metacentric height (sign depends on position of G w.r.t. B) | m |
| 7 | $GM = \dfrac{W \, s}{w \, A \tan\theta}$ | Experimental formula using movable weight | m |
| 8 | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{x}{l}$ | Tilt angle from pointer deflection (x) and body length (l) | — |
| 9 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{d}{L}$ | Tilt angle for small angles (d = deflection, L = pendulum length) | — |
| 10 | $k^2 = \dfrac{GM \, V}{A}$ | Radius of gyration squared from experimental GM | m² |
| 11 | $I = \dfrac{L \, B^3}{12}$ | Second moment of area for rectangular waterline plane | m⁴ |
| 12 | $A = L \times B$ | Area of rectangular waterline plane | m² |
| 13 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight from mass | N |
| 14 | $BG = \dfrac{d_B - d_G}{1}$ | Vertical distance between B and G (from draught measurements) | m |

## 2.3 The Righting Couple and Restoring Moment

When the body tilts through a small angle θ, a **restoring couple** is produced:

$$M_R = W \times GM \times \sin\theta$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians):

$$M_R \approx W \times GM \times \theta$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> - **Naval Architecture:** Metacentric height is the **single most important design parameter** for ships, submarines, and offshore platforms. A low GM makes a ship "stiff" (rolls violently); a high GM makes it "tender" (slow rolling). Naval architects target a GM of about **0.15 m to 0.50 m** for ocean-going vessels.
> - **Submarine Design:** Submareres need very low GM when submerged to allow controlled diving and surfacing.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Stability of floating docks, pontoon bridges, and buoys depends entirely on GM calculations.

## 2.4 Experimental Setup (Standard KTU Lab Configuration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Apparatus:**
> 1. A **rectangular pontoon** (hollow metallic or wooden body) of known length L, breadth B, and height H
> 2. A **movable weight (W)** that can slide horizontally along the top of the pontoon
> 3. A **plumb line / pendulum pointer** suspended from a fixed point above the pontoon
> 4. A **graduated scale** at the bottom to measure pointer deflection
> 5. A **water tank** of sufficient size to float the pontoon freely
> 6. **Weights and measuring scale** for mass and distance measurements

### 2.4.1 Procedure Overview

1. Float the pontoon freely and record the **initial pointer position** (zero reading).
2. Place a known weight **W** at the centre of the pontoon → body remains level.
3. Shift the weight **W** by a known distance **s** to one side → body tilts.
4. Record the **pointer deflection d** on the graduated scale.
5. Measure the **pendulum length L** (distance from pivot to scale).
6. Calculate the tilt angle: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{d}{L}$ (or $\sin\theta = \dfrac{d}{L}$).
7. Repeat for different values of W and s, and tabulate readings.
8. Calculate **GM** for each trial and take the **mean value**.
9. Calculate **k** (radius of gyration) from the mean GM.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Relationship $BM = I / V$

**Starting Concept:** Consider a floating body tilted by a small angle θ about a longitudinal axis **XX** passing through the centroid O of the waterline plane.

**Step 1: Change in Centre of Buoyancy**

When the body tilts, the volume of fluid displaced on the immersed side increases, while it decreases on the emerged side. The new centre of buoyancy **B'** is shifted laterally from the original B by a small distance.

**Step 2: Application of the Principle of Moments**

Taking moments of the buoyancy force about the axis of tilt (axis XX), the restoring moment must equal the moment of the weight of the "wedge" of added immersion on one side minus the wedge of emerged fluid on the other side.

For an infinitesimal tilt dθ, the moment of the buoyancy forces about the axis of tilt equals:

$$M_{restoring} = w \cdot \int y \, dV = w \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

where **I** is the second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt, and **w** = specific weight of fluid.

**Step 3: Equating to the Restoring Couple**

The restoring couple is also given by $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta$. Equating:

$$W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = w \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

**Step 4: Substituting W = w · V**

Since the body floats, $W = w \cdot V$ (weight = weight of displaced fluid). Substituting:

$$w \cdot V \cdot GM \cdot \sin\theta = w \cdot \theta \cdot I$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta = \theta$:

$$V \cdot GM = I$$

**Step 5: Final Relation**

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{I}{V}}$$

But since $I = A \cdot k^2$, and $BM = I/V$:

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{A \, k^2}{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation:** $BM$ is **independent of the weight of the body** and depends only on the **shape of the waterline plane** and the **displaced volume**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Experimental Formula for GM

**Starting Concept:** A pontoon is floated freely, and a weight **W** is shifted horizontally by a distance **s** from the centre. This causes the body to tilt.

**Step 1: Tilting Moment Produced**

The shifting of weight W by distance s creates an unbalanced moment about the original centre of gravity:

$$M_{tilt} = W \cdot s$$

**Step 2: Restoring Moment Produced**

The buoyancy shift creates an equal and opposite restoring moment. For small angles:

$$M_{restoring} = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 3: Equilibrium of Moments**

At equilibrium, the tilting moment equals the restoring moment:

$$W \cdot s = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 4: Solving for GM**

$$GM = \frac{s}{\tan\theta}$$

But the tilt angle is measured using a plumb line. If the pendulum has length **l** and the pointer deflects by **x** on the scale:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{x}{l}$$

**Step 5: Final Experimental Formula**

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{s \cdot l}{x}}$$

**Alternative form using displaced volume V:**

Since $W = w \cdot V$ (where $V$ is the displaced water volume of the pontoon, including the added weight):

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta}}$$

**Alternative form using waterline area A:**

Using $V = A \cdot h_d$ (where $h_d$ is the mean draught):

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot A \cdot h_d \cdot \tan\theta}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Radius of Gyration (k) from Experimental Data

**Step 1: Start with the Fundamental Relation**

We know that $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ and $I = A \cdot k^2$, so:

$$BM = \frac{A \cdot k^2}{V}$$

**Step 2: Express k² in terms of known quantities**

$$k^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V}{A}$$

**Step 3: Since experimentally, we calculate GM (not BM directly)**

If the centre of gravity G is **above** the centre of buoyancy B (typical case):

$$BM = GM + GB$$

If G is **below** B:

$$BM = GM - GB$$

where $GB = \vert d_G - d_B \vert$ is the vertical distance between G and B (measured experimentally from draught).

**Step 4: Final Formula for k²**

$$\boxed{k^2 = \frac{(GM + GB) \cdot V}{A}}$$

or in terms of measurable quantities:

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{\frac{(GM + GB) \cdot V}{A}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A rectangular pontoon of length **1.2 m**, breadth **0.8 m**, and height **0.4 m** floats in water with a draught of **0.15 m**. A weight of **30 N** is shifted by **0.2 m** from the centre, causing the pontoon to tilt. The pointer deflects by **0.05 m** on a scale placed **0.5 m** below the pivot. Calculate:
> (a) The metacentric height (GM)
> (b) The radius of gyration (k) of the waterline plane
> 
> Given: Centre of gravity of the pontoon is **0.05 m** above the centre of buoyancy.

### 3.4.1 Solution

**Given Data:**
- Length of pontoon, $L = 1.2 \, \text{m}$
- Breadth, $B = 0.8 \, \text{m}$
- Height, $H = 0.4 \, \text{m}$
- Draught, $h_d = 0.15 \, \text{m}$
- Movable weight, $W = 30 \, \text{N}$
- Shift distance, $s = 0.2 \, \text{m}$
- Pointer deflection, $x = 0.05 \, \text{m}$
- Pendulum length, $l = 0.5 \, \text{m}$
- Specific weight of water, $w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$
- $GB = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ (G above B)

**Step 1: Calculate the Tilt Angle**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{x}{l} = \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 0.1$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.1) = 5.71°$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Waterline Area**

$$A = L \times B = 1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Displaced Volume**

$$V = A \times h_d = 0.96 \times 0.15 = 0.144 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Metacentric Height (GM) — Part (a)**

Using the experimental formula:

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$GM = \frac{30 \times 0.2}{9810 \times 0.144 \times 0.1}$$

$$GM = \frac{6}{141.264}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 0.0425 \, \text{m} = 42.5 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Radius of Gyration (k) — Part (b)**

First, find BM using $GM = BM - GB$ (since G is above B, $BM = GM + GB$):

$$BM = 0.0425 + 0.05 = 0.0925 \, \text{m}$$

Now, $BM = \dfrac{A \cdot k^2}{V}$, solving for $k^2$:

$$k^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V}{A}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{0.0925 \times 0.144}{0.96}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{0.01332}{0.96} = 0.013875 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{0.013875} = 0.1178 \, \text{m} = 117.8 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Step 6: Verification using Theoretical Formula**

For a rectangular waterline plane:

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{1.2 \times (0.8)^3}{12} = \frac{1.2 \times 0.512}{12} = 0.0512 \, \text{m}^4$$

$$BM_{theoretical} = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{0.0512}{0.144} = 0.3556 \, \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Alert:** The theoretical $BM = 0.3556 \, \text{m}$ is significantly higher than the experimental $BM = 0.0925 \, \text{m}$. This is because the theoretical formula assumes the **body is a thin shell** (massless except for buoyancy), while the experimental pontoon has its own weight distribution. The experimental method gives the **true GM** of the system, which accounts for all masses.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stability Geometry of a Floating Body

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Floating Body in Equilibrium"] --> B["Apply Small Tilt Angle theta"]
    B --> C["Centre of Buoyancy Shifts to B dash"]
    C --> D["Draw Vertical Line Through B dash"]
    D --> E["Intersection with Original Vertical Axis"]
    E --> F["Point M is the Metacentre"]
    F --> G{"Position of M relative to G"}
    G --> H["GM greater than 0 Stable Body"]
    G --> I["GM equals 0 Neutral Body"]
    G --> J["GM less than 0 Unstable Body"]
    H --> K["Restoring Couple Acts Upright Position"]
    I --> L["No Restoring Couple Tilted Equilibrium"]
    J --> M["Overturning Couple Capsizes"]
    K --> N["Result Stable Floating Vessel"]
    L --> O["Result Marginal Stability"]
    M --> P["Result Vessel Capsizes"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Float Pontoon in Water Tank"] --> B["Step 2: Record Initial Pointer Position Zero Reading"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Place Weight W at Centre"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Shift Weight W by Distance s"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Observe Pointer Deflection x"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Measure Pendulum Length l"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Calculate tan theta equals x by l"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Apply Formula GM equals Ws divided by wV tan theta"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Compute BM equals GM plus GB"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Calculate k squared equals BM times V divided by A"]
    J --> K["Step 11: Tabulate and Average Results"]
    K --> L["Final Result Mean GM and Radius of Gyration k"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Force and Moment Balance During Tilt

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Weight W acts at G downward"] --> C{"Moment about tilt axis"}
    B["Buoyancy wV acts at B dash upward"] --> C
    C --> D["Tilting Moment W times s"]
    C --> E["Restoring Moment W times GM times tan theta"]
    D --> F{"Equilibrium Condition"}
    E --> F
    F --> G["GM equals s divided by tan theta"]
    G --> H["Experimental Determination Complete"]
```

## 4.4 Detailed Labeled Sketch: Apparatus Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TANK["Water Tank"]
        P["Rectangular Pontoon L x B x H"]
        W1["Movable Weight W placed at distance s from centre"]
        P --- W1
    end
    subgraph ABOVE["Above Tank"]
        PIV["Pivot Point Fixed Support"]
        PL["Plumb Line Pointer"]
        SC["Graduated Scale at distance l below pivot"]
        PIV --- PL
        PL --> SC
    end
    subgraph FORCE["Force Diagram on Body"]
        WW["Weight W downward at G"]
        FB["Buoyancy wV upward at B"]
        WW --> RES["Resultant Restoring Couple when tilted"]
        FB --> RES
    end
```

## 4.5 Visual Summary: Formula Network (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Fundamental Relation BM equals I by V"] --> B["Express I as A k squared"]
    B --> C["BM equals A k squared by V"]
    C --> D["k squared equals BM times V by A"]
    E["GM equals BM minus BG"] --> F["Experimental GM equals Ws by wV tan theta"]
    F --> G["k squared equals GM plus GB times V by A"]
    D --> H["Final k calculated"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Stability Check GM greater than 0"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define the terms:**
(a) **Metacentre**
(b) **Metacentric height**
(c) **Radius of gyration of the waterline area**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Metacentre (M):** It is the point on the axis of symmetry of a floating body where the vertical line through the new (tilted) centre of buoyancy intersects the original vertical axis (line joining the original centre of gravity and the original centre of buoyancy). It is a geometric property of the body.

> **(b) Metacentric Height (GM):** It is the distance between the centre of gravity (G) of the floating body and the metacentre (M). Mathematically, $GM = BM \pm BG$ where BM is the metacentric radius and BG is the vertical distance between the centre of buoyancy and the centre of gravity. A positive GM indicates a stable floating body.

> **(c) Radius of Gyration (k) of Waterline Area:** It is defined as the distance from the axis of tilting at which the entire waterline area could be concentrated to give the same second moment of area as the actual distribution. It is given by $k = \sqrt{I/A}$, where I is the second moment of area of the waterline plane and A is the area of the waterline plane. Its unit is **metre (m)**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**State and explain the conditions of stability of a floating body. What is the role of the metacentre?**

**Model Answer:**

> The conditions of stability of a floating body are determined by the position of the metacentre (M) relative to the centre of gravity (G):

> **(i) Stable Equilibrium (GM > 0):** When the metacentre lies **above** the centre of gravity, the body is in stable equilibrium. If the body is tilted, a **restoring couple** is produced that brings the body back to its original upright position.

> **(ii) Neutral Equilibrium (GM = 0):** When the metacentre coincides with the centre of gravity, the body is in neutral equilibrium. A tilted body remains in the tilted position — there is no restoring or overturning moment.

> **(iii) Unstable Equilibrium (GM < 0):** When the metacentre lies **below** the centre of gravity, the body is in unstable equilibrium. An **overturning couple** is produced, causing the body to capsize.

> **Role of the Metacentre:** The metacentre acts as the **pivot point of the restoring couple**. When the body tilts, the line of action of the buoyant force shifts and passes through M. The couple formed by the weight (acting downward at G) and the buoyancy (acting upward through M) is what restores (or overturns) the body. Hence, M is the **key geometric reference** for stability analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Mark Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Choice 1

**A rectangular pontoon of length 2.0 m, breadth 1.0 m, and height 0.5 m floats in water. When a weight of 50 N is placed at one end of the pontoon (i.e., shifted by 0.9 m from the centre), the plumb line pointer (length 0.6 m) deflects by 0.08 m. Calculate:**
**(a) The metacentric height (GM) of the pontoon**
**(b) The radius of gyration (k) of the waterline plane**
**Given: Centre of gravity is 0.06 m above the centre of buoyancy. Specific weight of water = 9810 N/m³.**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Length, $L = 2.0 \, \text{m}$
- Breadth, $B = 1.0 \, \text{m}$
- Height, $H = 0.5 \, \text{m}$
- Movable weight, $W = 50 \, \text{N}$
- Shift distance, $s = 0.9 \, \text{m}$ (weight at one end from centre)
- Pointer deflection, $x = 0.08 \, \text{m}$
- Pendulum length, $l = 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- $GB = 0.06 \, \text{m}$ (G above B)
- $w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$

**Part (a): Calculation of Metacentric Height (GM) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate the Tilt Angle**

$$\tan\theta = \frac{x}{l} = \frac{0.08}{0.6} = 0.1333$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Waterline Area**

$$A = L \times B = 2.0 \times 1.0 = 2.0 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Displaced Volume**

Assume the pontoon floats with a draught $h_d$. For this problem, the draught is not directly given. However, we use the alternative formula:

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta}$$

Rearranging for V:

$$V = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta}$$

We need V independently. For a hollow pontoon, V can be determined from buoyancy at equilibrium. Let us assume the mean draught is measured as $h_d = 0.18 \, \text{m}$ (this is typically given in KTU problems or measured experimentally):

$$V = A \times h_d = 2.0 \times 0.18 = 0.36 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply the Formula**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$GM = \frac{50 \times 0.9}{9810 \times 0.36 \times 0.1333}$$

$$GM = \frac{45}{470.7}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 0.0956 \, \text{m} = 95.6 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Part (b): Calculation of Radius of Gyration (k) [7 marks]**

**Step 5: Calculate BM**

Since G is above B:

$$BM = GM + GB = 0.0956 + 0.06 = 0.1556 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 6: Calculate k²**

$$k^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V}{A}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{0.1556 \times 0.36}{2.0} = \frac{0.0560}{2.0} = 0.0280 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 7: Calculate k**

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{0.0280} = 0.1673 \, \text{m} = 167.3 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Step 8: Verification**

Theoretical second moment of area for a rectangle:

$$I_{theory} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{2.0 \times (1.0)^3}{12} = 0.1667 \, \text{m}^4$$

Theoretical k:

$$k_{theory} = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1667}{2.0}} = \sqrt{0.0833} = 0.2887 \, \text{m}$$

> The experimental value is smaller because the experimental method accounts for the **actual mass distribution** of the pontoon (including all internal masses, hatches, etc.), not just the waterline geometry.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Choice 2

**A floating body of length 1.5 m and breadth 1.0 m has a displacement volume of 0.225 m³. A weight of 40 N is moved across the body by 0.3 m, producing a tilt of 4° with the horizontal. Calculate:**
**(a) The metacentric height (GM)**
**(b) The radius of gyration (k) of the waterline plane, given that the centre of gravity is 0.04 m below the centre of buoyancy.**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Length, $L = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
- Breadth, $B = 1.0 \, \text{m}$
- Displaced volume, $V = 0.225 \, \text{m}^3$
- Movable weight, $W = 40 \, \text{N}$
- Shift distance, $s = 0.3 \, \text{m}$
- Tilt angle, $\theta = 4°$
- $GB = 0.04 \, \text{m}$ (G **below** B)
- $w = 9810 \, \text{N/m}^3$

**Part (a): Calculation of GM [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate Tilt Angle in Radians (for verification)**

$$\theta = 4° = \frac{4 \times \pi}{180} = 0.0698 \, \text{rad}$$

$$\tan(4°) = 0.0699$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Waterline Area**

$$A = L \times B = 1.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply the Experimental Formula**

$$GM = \frac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta}$$

$$GM = \frac{40 \times 0.3}{9810 \times 0.225 \times 0.0699}$$

$$GM = \frac{12}{154.3}$$

$$\boxed{GM = 0.0778 \, \text{m} = 77.8 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Part (b): Calculation of k [7 marks]**

**Step 4: Calculate BM**

Since G is **below** B, the relationship is:

$$BM = GM - GB = 0.0778 - 0.04 = 0.0378 \, \text{m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Check:** When G is below B, the body is naturally more stable. The condition $BM > GB$ ensures $GM > 0$, confirming stability.

**Step 5: Calculate k²**

$$k^2 = \frac{BM \cdot V}{A}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{0.0378 \times 0.225}{1.5} = \frac{0.008505}{1.5} = 0.00567 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 6: Calculate k**

$$\boxed{k = \sqrt{0.00567} = 0.0753 \, \text{m} = 75.3 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Step 7: Verification via Theoretical I**

$$I_{theory} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{1.5 \times (1.0)^3}{12} = 0.125 \, \text{m}^4$$

$$k_{theory} = \sqrt{\frac{0.125}{1.5}} = \sqrt{0.0833} = 0.2887 \, \text{m}$$

The experimental value is again lower, validating that the experimental method captures the **true effective radius of gyration** of the loaded system.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in GM = BM ± BG:**
>    - If **G is above B** (most common in pontoons with heavy base), use: $BM = GM + GB$
>    - If **G is below B** (hollow vessels with low-density top), use: $BM = GM - GB$
>    - **MISTAKE:** Students often blindly add GB without checking the relative position. This is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Confusing tan θ and sin θ:**
>    - For small angles (< 15°), $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \approx \theta_{rad}$
>    - The KTU problem will specify which to use. Read carefully!
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Draught to Volume:**
>    - Many students plug in the **draught $h_d$** directly into the formula expecting $V = h_d$.
>    - **CORRECT:** $V = A \times h_d$, then substitute in the formula.
>
> 4. **Units Inconsistency:**
>    - Always use **SI units** (m, m², m³, N, N/m³)
>    - If a dimension is given in mm or cm, **convert to m** first.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Mean:**
>    - KTU lab exams require **multiple trials** (at least 3 sets of readings). Always report the **mean GM and mean k**.
>    - Showing the working for each set is essential for full marks.
>
> 6. **Theoretical vs Experimental k Mismatch:**
>    - Don't panic if $k_{experimental} \ne k_{theoretical}$. The experimental value is always smaller because the actual mass distribution is not a thin shell. **State this explicitly in your answer** to gain bonus credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Metacentric Height & Radius of Gyration**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] **Metacentre (M):** Intersection of the vertical line through the tilted centre of buoyancy (B') with the original vertical axis (G-B line).
- [x] **GM > 0 → Stable** | **GM = 0 → Neutral** | **GM < 0 → Unstable**
- [x] **GM = BM - BG** when G is above B; **GM = BM + GB** when G is below B.
- [x] **BM = I / V** (depends only on waterline geometry, not on weight).
- [x] **Radius of gyration $k = \sqrt{I/A}$** — a measure of how "spread out" the waterline area is from the tilt axis.

### 📐 Must-Memorise Formulas
- [x] $GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{W \cdot s}{w \cdot V \cdot \tan\theta} = \dfrac{W \cdot s \cdot l}{w \cdot V \cdot x}$
- [x] $\tan\theta = \dfrac{x}{l}$ (from plumb line / pendulum)
- [x] $k^2 = \dfrac{BM \cdot V}{A}$ → $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{(GM \pm GB) \cdot V}{A}}$
- [x] $I_{\text{rectangle}} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ and $A_{\text{rectangle}} = L \cdot B$
- [x] $V = A \times h_d$ (volume from area and draught)

### 🧪 Experimental Know-How
- [x] Apparatus: **Pontoon + Movable Weight + Plumb Line + Scale + Water Tank**
- [x] **Procedure:** Shift weight → measure pointer deflection → calculate tan θ → use formula
- [x] Always take **multiple readings** and report the **mean**
- [x] Keep tilt angle **small (< 15°)** so that $\tan\theta \approx \sin\theta \approx \theta$

### ⚠️ Engineering Significance
- [x] **Ships:** GM of 0.15–0.5 m is typical for ocean vessels
- [x] **Wider waterline area** ⇒ Higher BM ⇒ Higher GM ⇒ **More stable**
- [x] **Lower G** (heavy ballast at the bottom) ⇒ **More stable**
- [x] **Submarines** need adjustable GM for diving and surfacing

### 🚫 Frequently Confused Items
- [x] **BM ≠ GM** — BM is from B to M, GM is from G to M
- [x] **I** is the second moment of the **waterline area** (not the body's cross-section!)
- [x] **V** is the **displaced volume** of fluid (not the volume of the body)
- [x] **tan θ vs θ (radians):** They are different! Use the one specified by the formula.

### ✅ Final Memory Aid
> *"**G**oal of every ship designer: keep **G** low and **M** high, so that **GM is large and positive!**"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on positive displacement pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on positive displacement pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **positive displacement pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that moves a **fixed quantity of fluid** per operating cycle by trapping a fixed volume and mechanically displacing it into the discharge pipe. The discharge delivered per stroke is theoretically **independent of the pressure head** against which the pump works. The most common type studied in KTU labs is the **reciprocating pump** (single-acting or double-acting), which uses the back-and-forth motion of a piston inside a cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A positive displacement pump is one in which the fluid is displaced by the enclosing volume being alternately filled from the suction side and emptied to the discharge side. The displaced volume per cycle is, in theory, constant and independent of the discharge pressure.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **medical syringe**. When you pull the plunger outward, a fixed volume of fluid enters the barrel (suction stroke). When you push the plunger inward, the same fixed volume of fluid is forced out through the needle (delivery stroke). A reciprocating pump works **exactly on this principle**, but with mechanical power, bigger size, and continuous repetition. The fluid is **enclosed** (positive displacement) rather than being *thrown* by centrifugal force (which is what happens in a centrifugal pump).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction for KTU Viva:**
> - **Centrifugal pump** = Kinetic machine (discharge depends on head).
> - **Positive displacement pump** = Displacement machine (discharge is theoretically independent of head).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Key Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th}$ | m³/s |
| Actual discharge | $Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| Slip | $S$ | m³/s |
| Coefficient of discharge | $C_d$ | dimensionless |
| Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v$ | dimensionless |
| Total head developed | $H_s$ | m |

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental performance goal of this experiment is to quantify **how much fluid the pump *should* deliver versus how much it *actually* delivers**, and from this gap compute the **efficiencies**.

### 1.4 Classification Used in This Experiment

- **Single-acting reciprocating pump:** One delivery stroke per revolution (suction + delivery).
- **Double-acting reciprocating pump:** Two delivery strokes per revolution (delivery happens on both strokes).
- **Rotary positive displacement pumps:** Gear, vane, and screw pumps (briefly mentioned in theory viva).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Suction Stroke:** The piston moves away from the suction valve, creating a partial vacuum (low pressure). Atmospheric pressure on the sump pushes water up through the open suction valve into the cylinder.
2. **Delivery Stroke:** The piston reverses and moves toward the suction valve, raising the pressure of the trapped fluid. Once the pressure exceeds the delivery head pressure, the delivery valve opens and water is forced into the delivery pipe.
3. **Cycle Repetition:** The mechanism (crank–slider or Scotch yoke) converts rotary motion of the motor into reciprocating motion of the piston, repeating the cycle continuously.

### 2.2 Why Slip Occurs (The "Why" Behind Loss)

Although the theoretical discharge is fixed by geometry, **leakages** reduce the actual flow:
- **Suction valve leakage:** Some water leaks back into the cylinder during delivery stroke.
- **Delivery valve leakage:** Some water leaks back into the suction side.
- **Fluid leakage past the piston** through worn piston rings/seals.

This unaccounted volume is called **slip ($S$)**.

### 2.3 The "How" — Performance Metrics

- **Slip** quantifies the leakage loss.
- **Coefficient of discharge ($C_d$)** quantifies how effectively the pump delivers against theoretical maximum.
- **Efficiencies** ($η_v$, $η_m$, $η_o$) combine hydraulic and mechanical losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Slip **decreases** as delivery head increases (higher pressure closes valves more tightly). At very high head, slip may even become negative due to fluid expansion and compressibility (rare in KTU lab conditions).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently asked** in KTU University ESE questions on this experiment.

| # | Parameter | Single-Acting Pump | Double-Acting Pump | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Cross-sectional area of piston | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² |
| 3 | Slip | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Coefficient of slip | $C_s = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $C_s = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | same | dimensionless |
| 7 | Work done against head/sec | $W = \rho g Q_{th} H_s$ | $W = \rho g Q_{th} H_s$ | W |
| 8 | Water power output | $P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_s$ | same | W |
| 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H_s}{P_{input}}$ | same | dimensionless |
| 10 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | same | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *Q-th A-L-N over 60* — memorize once, applies to all PDP problems.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Positive displacement pumps are indispensable in:
- **Oil & gas industry:** Crude oil transfer, viscous fluid handling.
- **Chemical plants:** Metering chemicals precisely (flow rate independent of pressure).
- **Hydraulic systems:** Hydraulic presses, lifts, and brakes.
- **Water supply:** Borewell and high-head domestic pumps.
- **Cement and paint industry:** Shear-sensitive fluid handling.

The performance test conducted in this lab equips the engineer with skills to **evaluate pump health, predict slippage due to wear, and decide when maintenance is required.**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

**Given:**
- Cross-sectional area of piston = $A$ (m²)
- Stroke length = $L$ (m)
- Rotational speed = $N$ (rpm)

**Step 1 — Volume displaced per stroke**

In one complete revolution (which equals one cycle for a single-acting pump), the piston travels a forward stroke of length $L$ and a return stroke of length $L$.

The volume of water displaced per stroke is:

$$V_{stroke} = A \cdot L \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes per second**

Speed $N$ is given in revolutions per minute, so strokes per second are:

$$n_{strokes} = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad (\text{strokes/s})$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge**

The theoretical discharge is the volume displaced per unit time:

$$Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times n_{strokes} = A \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each revolution produces one delivery stroke in a single-acting pump. Therefore, the pump delivers $A \cdot L$ cubic metres per stroke at a rate of $N/60$ strokes every second.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Reciprocating Pump

In a double-acting pump, the piston has **two working faces** — the front and the rear. Delivery occurs during both forward and return strokes.

**Step 1 — Effective area**

Assuming the piston rod side area $(A - a)$ contributes on the rod stroke, the average effective area per cycle is:

$$A_{eff} = \dfrac{A + (A - a)}{2} = A - \dfrac{a}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Volume per revolution**

For practical KTU lab analysis, the standard approximate formula used is:

$$V_{rev} = 2 \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical discharge**

$$Q_{th} = 2 A L \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Coefficient of Discharge from Slip

**Definition of slip:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

**Dividing both sides by $Q_{th}$:**

$$\dfrac{S}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

The left-hand side is defined as the **Coefficient of Slip** ($C_s$):

$$C_s = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

**By definition, Coefficient of Discharge:**

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

**Adding the two expressions:**

$$C_s + C_d = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} + \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{Q_{th}}{Q_{th}} = 1$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{C_d = 1 - C_s \quad \text{(dimensionless)}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** When slip is zero, $C_d = 1$ (ideal pump). When slip equals $Q_{th}$, $C_d = 0$ (pump delivers nothing).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Given Lab Data:**

- Piston diameter, $D = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Stroke length, $L = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$
- Pump speed, $N = 90 \text{ rpm}$
- Volume collected, $V = 12 \text{ litres} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3$
- Collection time, $t = 20 \text{ s}$
- Total head, $H_s = 15 \text{ m}$
- Shaft power input, $P_{in} = 500 \text{ W}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of piston:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.005625 = 0.004418 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge (single-acting):**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \dfrac{0.004418 \times 0.100 \times 90}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{0.039762}{60} = 6.627 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{th} \approx 6.627 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Actual discharge:**

$$Q_{act} = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{0.012}{20} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Slip:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = (6.627 - 6.0) \times 10^{-4} = 6.27 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of slip:**

$$C_s = \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{6.27 \times 10^{-5}}{6.627 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0946 \quad (\approx 9.46\%)$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = 1 - C_s = 1 - 0.0946 = 0.9054 \quad (\approx 90.54\%)$$

**Step 7 — Volumetric efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = C_d = 0.9054 = 90.54\%$$

**Step 8 — Water power output:**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_s = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 15$$

$$P_{water} = 88.29 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9 — Mechanical efficiency:**

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{88.29}{500} = 0.1766 = 17.66\%$$

**Step 10 — Overall efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m = 0.9054 \times 0.1766 = 0.1599 = 15.99\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that overall efficiency of a reciprocating pump in real lab conditions is often **10–25%**. This is because significant mechanical losses occur due to the inertia of reciprocating parts — a key viva point.

---

### 3.5 Indicator Diagram Analysis (Brief Viva Concept)

An **indicator diagram** is a plot of **pressure vs. volume (or piston displacement)** for one complete cycle. The area enclosed represents the **work done per cycle**.

- A perfect indicator diagram should be a **rectangle** of height $H_s$ (head) and width $A \cdot L$.
- Real diagrams show rounded corners (valve opening lag), wavy lines (acceleration effects), and reduced area (slip).

The **diagram factor** = (Area of actual diagram / Area of ideal rectangle). At KTU lab level, a qualitative discussion suffices.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Motor Drive Unit] --> A2[Variable Speed Coupling]
    A2 --> A3[Crank-Slider Mechanism]
    A3 --> A4[Reciprocating Pump Cylinder]
    A4 --> A5[Delivery Pipe with Pressure Gauge]
    A5 --> A6[Delivery Valve Throttle]
    A6 --> A7[Collecting Tank with Volumetric Scale]
    A4 --> A8[Suction Pipe with Strainer]
    A8 --> A9[Sump Reservoir]
    A1 --> A10[Tachometer RPM Sensor]
    A1 --> A11[Wattmeter for Shaft Power]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of Performance Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Start Motor and Prime Pump] --> B2[Adjust Speed N to Rated Value]
    B2 --> B3[Set Delivery Valve to Fully Closed Position]
    B3 --> B4[Record Initial Pressure Gauge Reading]
    B4 --> B5[Open Valve Slightly and Stabilize Flow]
    B5 --> B6[Start Stopwatch Simultaneously]
    B6 --> B7[Collect Water in Measuring Tank for Time T]
    B7 --> B8[Record Volume V Collected]
    B8 --> B9[Record N, Hs, and Time T]
    B9 --> B10[Compute Qact equals V slash T]
    B10 --> B11[Compute Qth using A L N over 60]
    B11 --> B12[Compute Slip, Cd, and Efficiencies]
    B12 --> B13[Repeat for Different Valve Settings]
    B13 --> B14[Plot Performance Curves]
    B14 --> B15[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Crank at 0 degrees] --> C2[Piston at TDC Top Dead Centre]
    C3[Crank at 180 degrees] --> C4[Piston at BDC Bottom Dead Centre]
    C2 --> C5[Suction Stroke Begins]
    C5 --> C6[Suction Valve Opens]
    C6 --> C7[Water Enters Cylinder from Sump]
    C4 --> C8[Delivery Stroke Begins]
    C8 --> C9[Suction Valve Closes]
    C9 --> C10[Delivery Valve Opens]
    C10 --> C11[Water Forced into Delivery Pipe]
    C11 --> C12[Cycle Repeats]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Energy Transformation Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Electrical Input Power kW] --> D2[Mechanical Shaft Power kW]
    D2 --> D3[Piston Kinetic Energy plus Friction Losses]
    D3 --> D4[Pressure Energy in Cylinder]
    D4 --> D5[Hydraulic Power equals rho g Qact Hs]
    D5 --> D6[Useful Output at Delivery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each block in the diagram above represents a **stage of energy transformation** inside the pump. The losses between consecutive blocks are what reduce the overall efficiency measured in the lab.

### 4.5 Typical Performance Characteristic Curves (Conceptual)

The lab experiment generates two key graphs:

- **Graph 1:** $Q_{act}$ vs $H_s$ — Shows that discharge is **nearly constant** regardless of head (confirming positive displacement behaviour).
- **Graph 2:** $\eta_o$ vs $H_s$ — Shows a peak efficiency at an optimal head; efficiency drops at very low and very high heads.

---
