<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define slip and coefficient of discharge of a reciprocating pump.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Slip:** Slip is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a positive displacement pump due to leakage past valves and piston seals.

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

**Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$):** It is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge, expressed as a decimal or percentage. It quantifies the volumetric effectiveness of the pump.

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - C_s$$

For an ideal pump with no leakage, $C_d = 1$ (or 100%).

---

#### **Q2. Why is the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump less than the theoretical discharge?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

The actual discharge is less than theoretical due to **slip**, which is caused by:

1. **Leakage through suction valve** during the delivery stroke.
2. **Leakage through delivery valve** during the suction stroke.
3. **Leakage past the piston** through piston rings or gland packing.
4. **Air leakage** into the suction pipe due to imperfect sealing.
5. **Delayed closure** of valves due to inertia, allowing backflow.

Additionally, the actual discharge may be **greater** than theoretical at high heads due to fluid compressibility, but this is a rare case in water-pump lab tests.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Adapted]`

**Q.A.(a)** Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump. Explain the term *coefficient of discharge* and how it relates to slip. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define parameters:**

Let $A$ = cross-sectional area of the piston (m²), $L$ = length of stroke (m), $N$ = pump speed in rpm.

**Step 2 — Volume per stroke:**

During one stroke, the piston sweeps a cylindrical volume:

$$V_{stroke} = A \cdot L \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

**Step 3 — Strokes per second:**

A single-acting pump completes **one delivery stroke per revolution**:

$$n = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{strokes/s}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = V_{stroke} \times n = A \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Relation to slip and $C_d$:**

The **coefficient of discharge** is defined as:

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

Since $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - S$:

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{th} - S}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}} = 1 - C_s$$

Therefore, slip and $C_d$ are **complementary** indicators of pump performance.

---

**Q.A.(b)** A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 100 mm and a stroke of 150 mm. It runs at 60 rpm. The pump delivers water against a head of 20 m. If the actual discharge measured is 4.5 litres/s, determine: (i) coefficient of discharge, (ii) slip, (iii) volumetric efficiency, and (iv) water power output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $L = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$
- $H_s = 20 \text{ m}$
- $Q_{act} = 4.5 \text{ L/s} = 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 \times 60}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 = 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1781 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.819$$

> ⚠️ **This exceeds 1, which is physically impossible for a single-acting pump under normal conditions.** This indicates that either the pump is double-acting OR the data is inconsistent. KTU exam-tip: Always cross-check with the type of pump specified.

**Corrected interpretation (assuming double-acting):**

$$Q_{th, \text{double}} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} = 2 \times 1.1781 \times 10^{-3} = 2.3562 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$C_d = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.3562 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.91$$

> This still exceeds 1. Therefore, the data must be inconsistent, or the question expects the answer in symbolic form.

**Step 4 — Slip:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 2.3562 \times 10^{-3} - 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = -2.1438 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

The negative slip is **physically possible** only when the fluid experiences expansion or the pump has air entrainment effects.

**Step 5 — Volumetric efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = C_d = 1.91 \text{ (191\%)}$$

**Step 6 — Water power:**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q_{act} H_s = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 882.9 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{water} \approx 882.9 \text{ W} = 0.883 \text{ kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Adapted]`

**Q.B.(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. List the main components and state the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**

The single-acting reciprocating pump consists of:

1. **Cylinder:** Hollow cylindrical chamber where the piston reciprocates.
2. **Piston:** A close-fitting plunger that slides inside the cylinder.
3. **Suction Pipe:** Connects the sump to the cylinder, fitted with a strainer at the bottom and a non-return (suction) valve at the top.
4. **Suction Valve:** A one-way valve that opens only during the suction stroke.
5. **Delivery Pipe:** Carries water from the cylinder to the delivery tank, fitted with a delivery (non-return) valve.
6. **Delivery Valve:** A one-way valve that opens only during the delivery stroke.
7. **Crank and Connecting Rod:** Converts rotary motion from the motor into reciprocating motion of the piston.
8. **Gland Packing:** Prevents leakage along the piston rod.
9. **Air Vessel:** Fitted on the delivery pipe to smoothen pulsating flow.

**Working:**

- **Suction Stroke:** The crank rotates, the piston moves from top dead centre (TDC) to bottom dead centre (BDC). This creates a low pressure in the cylinder, causing the suction valve to open and water to rush in from the sump.
- **Delivery Stroke:** The piston moves from BDC back to TDC. The suction valve closes, water pressure rises, the delivery valve opens, and water is forced into the delivery pipe.

**One delivery occurs per revolution**, hence the name *single-acting*.

---

**Q.B.(b)** During a performance test on a single-acting reciprocating pump, the following data were recorded:

| S.No | $N$ (rpm) | $H_s$ (m) | Volume Collected (L) | Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 80 | 10 | 14.0 | 30 |
| 2 | 80 | 15 | 13.8 | 30 |
| 3 | 80 | 20 | 13.5 | 30 |

Pump bore = 80 mm, stroke = 120 mm.

Determine: (i) actual discharge at each head, (ii) theoretical discharge, (iii) coefficient of discharge, (iv) volumetric efficiency, and (v) comment on the trend. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $L = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.12 \text{ m}$, $N = 80 \text{ rpm}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.08)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12 \times 80}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 8.0424 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.804 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 3 — Tabulated Calculations:**

| S.No | $H_s$ | $Q_{act} = V/t$ (L/s) | $Q_{th}$ (L/s) | $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ | $\eta_v = C_d$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $14.0/30 = 0.467$ | 0.804 | 0.580 | 58.0% |
| 2 | 15 | $13.8/30 = 0.460$ | 0.804 | 0.572 | 57.2% |
| 3 | 20 | $13.5/30 = 0.450$ | 0.804 | 0.560 | 56.0% |

**Step 4 — Trend Analysis:**

As delivery head $H_s$ **increases**, both $Q_{act}$ and $C_d$ show a slight **decrease**. This is because at higher heads:
- Valve leakage increases marginally.
- Effective valve seating improves but flow losses increase.
- Power losses increase.

The discharge variation is small (within ~4%), which is a **characteristic feature of positive displacement pumps** — confirming that discharge is nearly independent of head.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently lose marks by mixing mm and m. Always convert bore and stroke to metres **before** computing area.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 60:** $N$ is in rpm, but $Q_{th}$ must be in m³/s. Missing the division by 60 inflates the answer by 60× — instant zero.
> 3. **Confusing $C_d$ with $\eta_v$:** In simple positive displacement pump tests, $C_d$ **equals** $\eta_v$. Students who compute them separately waste time and risk confusion.
> 4. **Single-acting vs Double-acting:** Always read the pump specification. Using the wrong formula loses 4–5 marks.
> 5. **Negative slip:** If $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$, slip is negative. Report this correctly with physical interpretation (fluid expansion, air entrainment) — do not blindly assume the data is wrong.
> 6. **Pressure gauge height:** Total head $H_s$ = gauge head + static delivery height + losses. Read your lab manual carefully for which one is meant.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as the **last-page cheat sheet** for your KTU viva and ESE preparation.

- ✅ A **positive displacement pump** delivers a fixed volume per cycle, independent of head.
- ✅ **Theoretical discharge** for single-acting: $Q_{th} = A L N / 60$.
- ✅ **Theoretical discharge** for double-acting: $Q_{th} = 2 A L N / 60$.
- ✅ **Slip** = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ — caused by valve and piston leakage.
- ✅ **Coefficient of slip** = $(Q_{th} - Q_{act}) / Q_{th}$.
- ✅ **Coefficient of discharge** = $Q_{act} / Q_{th} = 1 - C_s$.
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency** = $C_d$.
- ✅ **Water power** = $\rho g Q_{act} H_s$.
- ✅ **Mechanical efficiency** = Water Power / Shaft Power Input.
- ✅ **Overall efficiency** = $\eta_v \times \eta_m$.
- ✅ Discharge is **nearly constant** with head (PDP signature).
- ✅ Slip **decreases** with increase in head (valves seat better).
- ✅ **Air vessel** smoothens pulsating flow and reduces acceleration head losses.
- ✅ **Indicator diagram** area represents work done per cycle.
- ✅ Always convert mm → m before computing $A$.
- ✅ Always remember the factor of **60** in rpm → strokes/s conversion.
- ✅ Negative slip = physically possible at high heads due to fluid expansion.
- ✅ The experiment outcome (CO) tested is **CO2: Analyse the performance of hydraulic machines.**

---

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — PCIEL307, Module Experiment 2**
> *Performance test on positive displacement pumps*

topics.title: Performance test on centrifugal pumps, determination of operating point and efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on centrifugal pumps, determination of operating point and efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **centrifugal pump** is a **rotodynamic hydraulic machine** that converts the **mechanical energy of a rotating impeller** into **kinetic energy and subsequently pressure energy of the fluid**, thereby raising the fluid from a lower elevation to a higher elevation against gravity and frictional resistances. The **performance test on a centrifugal pump** is a controlled laboratory experiment in which the discharge ($Q$), the **manometric head ($H_m$)**, the **shaft input power ($P_{in}$)**, and the **overall efficiency ($\eta_{o}$)** are simultaneously measured under varied throttling conditions (load variations) to plot the **characteristic curves** ($H$–$Q$, $\eta$–$Q$, and $P$–$Q$) and to **determine the operating point** — i.e., the intersection of the pump characteristic curve and the system resistance curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO):**
> This experiment directly addresses **CO2**: *"Conduct performance tests on hydraulic machines, evaluate the operating point, efficiency, and prepare characteristic curves."* It is universally a **mandatory lab viva and record experiment** under Module 2 of PCIEL307.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pedalling a bicycle wheel inside a water bucket. As you spin the wheel fast, water is flung outward by **centrifugal action** and slung out through a side outlet, while a low-pressure region at the centre sucks in more water from the bottom. That is *exactly* what a centrifugal pump does — except it spins an **impeller** (a wheel with curved vanes) at high speed using an electric motor.

Now imagine the delivery pipe has a **small tap** (valve). When you almost close the tap, the pump has to push against a lot of resistance, so it delivers less water but at higher pressure. When the tap is fully open, the pump delivers a lot of water but at a lower pressure. The **performance test** is simply the act of opening the tap little-by-little and noting down *how much water came out* ($Q$), *how high it was pushed* ($H$), and *how much electricity was consumed* ($P$). The **operating point** is the single sweet spot where the pump's natural output exactly matches the demand of the pipe network — like a person whose walking speed exactly matches the speed of an escalator (no running, no waiting).

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units used in the KTU laboratory record:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - Density of water at 30 °C: $\rho = 996 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
> - Specific weight of water: $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$ (or $\gamma = 9.81 \; \text{kN/m}^3$)
> - Motor pulley speed: $N$ in **rev/s** (not rpm) for the rope-brake power formula.
> - Head ($H$) in **metres of water column (mwc)**.
> - Discharge ($Q$) in **LPS** (litres per second) or **m³/s**.

## 1.4 Industrial / Engineering Relevance

| Application Domain | Why Performance Test is Critical |
|---|---|
| **Municipal water supply** | Selects pump size to match daily demand and pipe network. |
| **Irrigation schemes (Kerala)** | Matches pump to sprinkler/drip line pressure requirements. |
| **Thermal power plants** | Boiler feed pumps demand very high manometric head. |
| **Fire-fighting systems** | Pump must deliver rated Q at rated H during emergency. |
| **HVAC and high-rise buildings** | Pump must overcome high static lifts plus friction. |

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner expects you to **state the engineering application** of the test in the viva. Always quote **"matching the pump to the system"** as the single most important reason for conducting the test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Centrifugal Pump (Foundation Recap)

The pump operates on the **Euler's equation for turbomachines**:

$$H = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

For the radial-entry case (typical in centrifugal pumps) where the absolute velocity at inlet is purely radial, $V_{w1} = 0$, so:

$$H_{impeller} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

When a **rectangular vane** is used (uniform flow), $V_{w2} = u_2$ and the head becomes maximum:

$$H_{max} = \frac{u_2^2}{g}$$

But in practice, vanes are **backward-curved** and **finite in number**, so:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{Q \cot\beta_2}{\pi D_2 B_2}$$

which produces the famous **parabolic theoretical pump characteristic**:

$$H_{th} = H_0 - K Q^2$$

where $H_0$ is the shut-off head (the head developed when $Q = 0$).

## 2.2 Why Does the Pump Characteristic Curve Drop as Q Increases?

- As discharge increases, the **relative velocity at exit** increases.
- This causes the **whirl component $V_{w2}$ to decrease** (since the fluid leaves with a larger radial component).
- Hence $H = V_{w2} u_2 / g$ falls, producing the **drooping curve** seen in the lab.

## 2.3 The Operating Point — Engineering Heart of the Experiment

The system (pipe network) requires a head given by:

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g} + \sum K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$

Since $V \propto Q$, the **system curve** is also approximately quadratic:

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + C Q^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Operating Point is defined as the intersection of the Pump Head-Discharge curve and the System Head curve.** At this point, the **head offered by the pump exactly equals the head demanded by the system**, and the corresponding $Q$ and $H$ are the actual working values.

## 2.4 Efficiencies of a Centrifugal Pump (Four Stages)

The mechanical-to-hydraulic energy conversion suffers losses at every stage:

1. **Mechanical losses** (bearing friction, windage): $P_{impeller} = P_{shaft} - P_{mech,loss}$
2. **Hydraulic (manometric) losses** (friction in impeller passages, eddies): $H_m \le H_{impeller}$
3. **Volumetric (leakage) losses** (recirculation from high-pressure to low-pressure zone through the clearances): $Q_{actual} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{leak}$

The three primary efficiencies are:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_{impeller}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{\text{2}\pi N (T_1 - T_2)}{\text{2}\pi N T_1} = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q}{(\pi D_2 B_2 - \pi D_1 B_1) V_{f2}}$$

$$\eta_{man} = \frac{H_{manometric}}{H_{impeller}} = \frac{gH_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$$

The **overall efficiency** is the product of all three:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$$

## 2.5 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula the examiner can test in PCIEL307. Memorise the boxed ones — they fetch the bulk of the marks.**

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discharge (volumetric method) | $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{A_c \times R}{t}$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Discharge (LPS conversion) | $Q$ | $Q_{LPS} = \dfrac{A_c \times R \times 1000}{t}$ | L/s |
| 3 | Manometric Head | $H_m$ | $H_m = h_d + h_s \pm z$ | mwc |
| 4 | Velocity of impeller tip | $u_2$ | $u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s |
| 5 | Input Power (rope brake) | $P_{in}$ | $P_{in} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N_{rope}}{60}$ | W |
| 6 | Shaft Power (also $P_{in}$) | $P_{shaft}$ | $P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \omega_{brake}$ | W |
| 7 | Input Power (electrical) | $P_{elec}$ | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W |
| 8 | Output / Water Power | $P_{water}$ | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m$ | W |
| 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o$ | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$ | — |
| 10 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man}$ | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{g H_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ | — |
| 11 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech}$ | $\eta_{mech} = 1 - \dfrac{W_2}{W_1}$ | — |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol}$ | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | — |
| 13 | Specific Speed (for pump) | $N_s$ | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | — |
| 14 | NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) | $NPSH$ | $NPSH = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\gamma} - \dfrac{p_{vap}}{\gamma} - h_s$ | m |
| 15 | Pump characteristic (theory) | $H$ | $H = H_0 - K Q^2$ | m |
| 16 | System curve | $H_{sys}$ | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + C Q^2$ | m |
| 17 | **Operating Point condition** | — | $H_{pump} = H_{sys}$ | — |
| 18 | Head due to impeller (Euler) | $H_{imp}$ | $H_{imp} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | m |
| 19 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2}$ | m³/s |
| 20 | Slip coefficient (Stodola) | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{\pi \sin\beta_2}{z}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for $z$ in $H_m$:**
> - If the delivery pressure gauge is **above** the suction pressure gauge (vertical offset) **by** $z$ metres, **add** $z$ to $(h_s + h_d)$: $H_m = h_s + h_d + z$.
> - If the delivery gauge is **below** the suction gauge, **subtract** $z$: $H_m = h_s + h_d - z$.
> - **Verify with the lab setup diagram in Section 4.**

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the Performance Test

- **Pump Selection**: The plotted $H$–$Q$ curve lets the engineer choose a pump whose curve intersects the system curve at the **design point** (Best Efficiency Point, BEP).
- **Affinity Laws**: Once tested at one speed, the performance at another speed is predicted from:
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$
- **Cavitation Prevention**: NPSH margin is read from the test; insufficient margin causes cavitation damage.
- **Cost Minimisation**: Operating far from BEP wastes electricity; the test quantifies the saving.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Manometric Head from Pressure Gauge Readings

**Statement:** Prove that $H_m = h_s + h_d \pm z$ for a centrifugal pump test rig.

**Setup (Refer Section 4 diagram):** Apply Bernoulli's equation between the **suction pressure gauge tapping** (point 1) and the **delivery pressure gauge tapping** (point 2), assuming both points lie on the centreline of the pipe (i.e., same elevation difference $z$, where $z$ is the vertical separation).

$$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + \text{losses}$$

But pipe diameter at suction and delivery is **identical** in the standard test rig ⇒ $V_1 = V_2$ (continuity). Pipe friction between the two tapping points is **negligible** over a short distance. Therefore:

$$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} - \frac{p_2}{\gamma} = z_2 - z_1 = \pm z$$

The **manometric head** is the total head rise imparted by the pump, defined as:

$$H_m = \left(\frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2\right) - \left(\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1\right)$$

With $V_1 = V_2$, the kinetic energy terms cancel:

$$H_m = \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\gamma} \pm z = h_d - (-h_s) \pm z = h_s + h_d \pm z$$

$$\boxed{H_m = h_s + h_d \pm z \;\; \text{metres of water}}$$

> The sign of $z$ is **positive** if the delivery gauge is **higher** than the suction gauge (i.e., the gauge elevations differ in the same sense as the head addition); otherwise it is **negative**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer (Rope Brake Test)

**Statement:** Show that the shaft power input is $P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N_{rope}}{60}$.

**Setup:** A rope wound over the motor pulley (or brake drum) of radius $R$ has one end attached to a **spring balance** reading $W_1$ and the other end to a **dead weight** $W_2$. The torque transmitted by the pulley shaft is the difference in tensions times the radius.

**Net force on pulley rim** = $(W_1 - W_2) \; \text{N}$ (tangential, opposing motion).

**Tangential braking force** = $(W_1 - W_2) \; \text{N}$

**Braking torque** $T_b = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R \;\; \text{N·m}$

**Angular velocity** of the pulley $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N_{rope}}{60} \;\; \text{rad/s}$

**Power absorbed by the brake** = braking torque × angular velocity:

$$P_{shaft} = T_b \cdot \omega = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R \cdot \frac{2 \pi N_{rope}}{60}$$

Since the rope diameter is small, the rope diameter is sometimes subtracted from the pulley diameter to get the **effective** diameter. But in the KTU lab form, the simple form is accepted:

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N_{rope}}{60} \;\; \text{Watts}}$$

> **Note on units:** $W_1, W_2$ in **N** (newtons) — not kgf! Multiply kgf readings by $9.81$ first. $N_{rope}$ in **rpm**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Overall Efficiency from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$.

**Step 1 — Output (Hydraulic) Power:**
The water leaving the pump at discharge $Q$ m³/s with a manometric head $H_m$ m has gained $\rho g H_m$ joules per kg of fluid.

Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho Q \;\; \text{kg/s}$.

Energy imparted per second = $\dot{m} \cdot (\rho g H_m)/(\rho) = \rho Q \cdot g H_m$ J/s.

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Input (Shaft) Power** from rope brake:

$$P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Efficiency Definition:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\text{Useful Power Output}}{\text{Power Input}} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{(W_1 - W_2) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \frac{60 \cdot \rho g Q H_m}{2 \pi N (W_1 - W_2)} = \frac{30 \cdot \rho g Q H_m}{\pi N (W_1 - W_2)}}$$

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked-Out KTU Laboratory Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of calculation KTU expects you to enter into the lab record. The numbers are realistic; the methodology is exactly the format examiners look for.**

### Problem Statement

In a performance test on a **single-stage centrifugal pump** with a **rope-brake dynamometer**, the following readings are taken when the delivery valve is **partially throttled** to a particular setting. The collecting tank has a **cross-section of 0.5 m × 0.5 m**, the rise of water level in **15 seconds** is **120 mm**, the suction gauge reads **-0.20 mwc** (vacuum), the delivery gauge reads **+14.5 mwc**, the delivery gauge is **0.30 m above** the suction gauge, the spring balance reads **42 N**, the dead weight is **10 N**, the tachometer reads **1440 rpm** at the motor pulley, and the pulley diameter is **200 mm**. The impeller diameter is **180 mm** and the vane exit angle is **30°**.

**Calculate:** (a) Discharge $Q$, (b) Manometric head $H_m$, (c) Shaft power $P_{shaft}$, (d) Water power $P_{water}$, (e) Overall efficiency $\eta_o$, (f) Velocity of impeller tip $u_2$, (g) Theoretical head from Euler's equation.

### Step (a): Discharge

$$A_c = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$R = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.120 \;\; \text{m}$$

$$t = 15 \;\; \text{s}$$

$$Q = \frac{A_c \times R}{t} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.120}{15} = \frac{0.030}{15} = 0.0020 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 2.0 \;\; \text{L/s}$$

### Step (b): Manometric Head

Since the delivery gauge is **0.30 m above** the suction gauge, the static head $z = +0.30$ m (added).

$$H_m = h_s + h_d + z$$

The suction gauge reads **-0.20 mwc** (vacuum), so $h_s = -0.20$ m in the **pressure-head sense** (or $h_s = 0.20$ m of vacuum — the convention is to treat vacuum as positive suction head). Most KTU answer scripts use $h_s = 0.20$ mwc when the gauge shows a vacuum.

$$H_m = 0.20 + 14.50 + 0.30 = 15.00 \;\; \text{mwc}$$

### Step (c): Shaft Power

$$P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

$$W_1 - W_2 = 42 - 10 = 32 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 32 \cdot \frac{2 \pi \cdot 1440}{60} = 32 \cdot 150.80 = 4825.6 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$P_{shaft} \approx 4.826 \;\; \text{kW}$$

### Step (d): Water Power

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0020 \times 15.00$$

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.030 = 294.3 \;\; \text{W} \approx 0.2943 \;\; \text{kW}$$

### Step (e): Overall Efficiency

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{294.3}{4825.6} = 0.0610 = 6.10 \;\; \%$$

> The efficiency is low because of a heavily throttled delivery valve. As the valve is **opened** in subsequent readings, $\eta_o$ will rise, reach a maximum (BEP), and then fall — the classical **inverted U-shape**.

### Step (f): Impeller Tip Velocity

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N_{imp}}{60}$$

Assuming the impeller and motor pulley rotate at the same speed (no gear reduction):

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \times 0.180 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{814.30}{60} = 13.57 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

### Step (g): Theoretical Head from Euler

For a backward-curved vane, $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$. Without $V_{f2}$ data, the **maximum theoretical head** (rectangular vane, $\beta = 90°$) is:

$$H_{max} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} = \frac{(13.57)^2}{9.81} = \frac{184.15}{9.81} = 18.77 \;\; \text{m}$$

The **actual** theoretical head using $V_{f2} = Q/(\pi D_2 B_2)$ with assumed width $B_2 = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$:

$$V_{f2} = \frac{0.0020}{\pi \times 0.180 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.0020}{0.00679} = 0.295 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot\beta_2 = 13.57 - 0.295 \times \cot 30° = 13.57 - 0.295 \times 1.732$$

$$V_{w2} = 13.57 - 0.511 = 13.06 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

$$H_{impeller} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{13.06 \times 13.57}{9.81} = \frac{177.22}{9.81} = 18.07 \;\; \text{m}$$

$$\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_{impeller}} = \frac{15.00}{18.07} = 0.830 = 83.0 \;\; \%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How a Reading Affects Each Output

| Observation in the Lab | $Q$ | $H_m$ | $P_{shaft}$ | $P_{water}$ | $\eta_o$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Delivery valve **closed** more | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ slightly | ↓ |
| Delivery valve **opened** more | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ slightly | ↑ then ↓ | ↑ reaches BEP then ↓ |
| Speed $N$ **increased** | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ (if no cavitation) |
| Rope pulled **tighter** ($W_1$ ↑) | — | — | ↑ (artificial) | — | ↓ (false reading) |
| Suction line **air leak** | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↓↓ |

> [!TIP]
> KTU practical exams often ask: *"If the delivery valve is closed, what is the discharge?"* — Answer: **zero**, but the pump still develops a **shut-off head**, and the input power is **minimum** (since no water is being moved).

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Centrifugal Pump Test Rig Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sump Tank] -- Suction Pipe with Gate Valve --> nodeB[Centrifugal Pump Unit]
    nodeB -- Delivery Pipe --> nodeC[Delivery Control Valve Throttle]
    nodeC -- Piezometer Ring 1 --> nodeD[Pressure Gauge SUCTION hs mwc]
    nodeB -- Shaft Coupling --> nodeE[Electric Motor]
    nodeE -- Motor Pulley --> nodeF[Tachometer N rpm]
    nodeF -- Rope with Dead Weight W2 --> nodeG[Brake Drum]
    nodeG -- Rope to Spring Balance W1 --> nodeH[Spring Balance Reading]
    nodeC -- Outlet Pipe --> nodeI[Collecting Tank with Measuring Scale]
    nodeI -- Return Pipe --> nodeA
    nodeB -- Connecting Shaft --> nodeJ[Impeller with Backward Curved Vanes]
    nodeK[Voltmeter and Ammeter] -.Monitors.-> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Test Procedure (Sequential Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Prime the pump and check water level in sump] --> stepB[Close delivery valve fully and start motor]
    stepB --> stepC[Open delivery valve slightly to first setting]
    stepC --> stepD[Record tachometer reading N rpm]
    stepD --> stepE[Record spring balance W1 and dead weight W2]
    stepE --> stepF[Record suction gauge hs and delivery gauge hd]
    stepF --> stepG[Note water level in collecting tank and start stopwatch]
    stepG --> stepH[After 15 seconds record new water level]
    stepH --> stepI[Calculate Q, Hm, Pshaft, Pwater and overall efficiency]
    stepI --> stepJ{All throttle settings completed?}
    stepJ -- No --> stepK[Open delivery valve to next setting and repeat] --> stepD
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepL[Plot H vs Q, eta vs Q, P vs Q characteristic curves]
    stepL --> stepM[Mark operating point and best efficiency point BEP]
    stepM --> stepN[Stop motor and drain system]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Stages & Loss Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inBlock[Electrical Input V x I x cos phi] --> motorBlock[Motor Efficiency 85 to 90 percent]
    motorBlock --> shaftBlock[Shaft Power Mechanical]
    shaftBlock -- Mechanical Loss Bearing Friction Windage --> mechLoss[Reduced Power at Impeller Coupling]
    mechLoss --> impBlock[Impeller Imparts Head via Euler Equation]
    impBlock -- Hydraulic Loss Eddy Friction in Passages --> manLoss[Manometric Head Hm]
    impBlock -- Volumetric Loss Recirculation Leakage --> volLoss[Reduced Actual Discharge Q]
    manLoss --> outBlock[Water Power rho g Q Hm Output]
    volLoss --> outBlock
    outBlock --> finalEff[Overall Efficiency eta o equals Pwater divided by Pin]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Conceptual Plot — Pump Curve, System Curve, and Operating Point

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    plotA["Graph Region: Head H in mwc versus Discharge Q in LPS"]
    pumpCurve["Pump Characteristic Curve: H equals H0 minus K times Q squared - DROOPS DOWN"]
    sysCurve["System Resistance Curve: H equals Hstatic plus C times Q squared - RISES UP"]
    pumpCurve --> intersectPoint
    sysCurve --> intersectPoint
    intersectPoint["OPERATING POINT Q_op H_op: Intersection where Pump Head equals System Head"]
    intersectPoint --> bepPoint["BEP at design Q_Highest eta"]
    bepPoint --> npshMark["NPSH Margin Check: avoid cavitation zone to the right"]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Schematic — Sign Convention for Manometric Head

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tank1[Sump Water Level] -- Suction Pipe --> sgauge["Suction Gauge hs mwc - often vacuum negative reading"]
    sgauge --> pumpUnit["Centrifugal Pump impeller rotates"]
    pumpUnit --> dgauge["Delivery Gauge hd mwc positive high pressure"]
    dgauge --> outlet1[Delivery Pipe to Collecting Tank]
    offsetArrow["Vertical separation z metres: +0.30 m if delivery higher -0.30 m if delivery lower"]
    dgauge -.- offsetArrow
    sgauge -.- offsetArrow
    formulaBox["Result: Hm = hs + hd plus or minus z metres of water column"]
    offsetArrow --> formulaBox
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Safety Note (KTU 2024 Mandatory):** Always earth the motor body, do not touch the rotating rope, and keep the spring balance **vertical**. The rope brake must be cooled with water (wet rope) to avoid overheating and inconsistent $W_1$ readings.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) — Short Answer Type

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "operating point" of a centrifugal pump. How is it determined from the characteristic curves?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **operating point** of a centrifugal pump is the point of **intersection of the pump head-discharge (H–Q) characteristic curve and the system resistance (H_sys–Q) curve**. It represents the **steady-state working condition** at which the head offered by the pump exactly equals the head demanded by the pipe network. The corresponding discharge and head values are the actual $Q$ and $H$ at which the pump will operate. It is determined experimentally by:
1. Conducting the performance test under multiple throttle settings.
2. Plotting the H–Q curve of the pump.
3. Plotting the H_sys–Q curve of the given pipe system using $H_{sys} = H_{static} + C Q^2$.
4. Marking the intersection of the two curves.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three efficiencies of a centrifugal pump and write the formula for overall efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three primary efficiencies of a centrifugal pump are:
1. **Manometric efficiency** $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{gH_m}{V_{w2} u_2}$ — measures the effectiveness of head conversion inside the impeller.
2. **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_{mech} = 1 - \dfrac{W_2}{W_1}$ — measures the transmission loss through bearings.
3. **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ — measures leakage losses.

The **overall efficiency** is the product of the three:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each) — KTU ESE Module Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A centrifugal pump is tested in the laboratory using a rope-brake dynamometer. The collecting tank has a cross-section of 0.40 m × 0.40 m. With the delivery valve at a particular setting, the water rises by 150 mm in 20 seconds. The suction gauge reads **260 mm of Hg vacuum** and the delivery gauge reads **1.45 bar**. The distance between the gauge centres is **0.25 m**, with the delivery gauge **above** the suction gauge. The spring balance reads 50 N, the dead weight is 12 N, the tachometer reads 1500 rpm, the impeller diameter is 200 mm, and the vane exit angle is 35° (assume impeller width $B_2$ = 15 mm). Calculate:
(i) Discharge in LPS
(ii) Manometric head in mwc
(iii) Shaft power in kW
(iv) Overall efficiency
(v) Manometric efficiency

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to conduct a performance test on a centrifugal pump and plot its characteristic curves. What is the significance of the **best efficiency point (BEP)**?

#### Model Solution for Part A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Collecting tank area: $A_c = 0.40 \times 0.40 = 0.16 \text{ m}^2$
- Rise: $R = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$
- Time: $t = 20 \text{ s}$
- Suction gauge: $h_s = 260 \text{ mm Hg} = 0.260 \times 13.6 = 3.536 \text{ mwc}$ (vacuum ⇒ positive suction head)
- Delivery gauge: $h_d = 1.45 \text{ bar} = 1.45 \times 10.2 = 14.79 \text{ mwc}$
- Gauge separation: $z = +0.25 \text{ m}$ (delivery above suction)
- $W_1 = 50 \text{ N}$, $W_2 = 12 \text{ N}$
- $N = 1500 \text{ rpm}$
- $D_2 = 0.200 \text{ m}$, $\beta_2 = 35°$, $B_2 = 0.015 \text{ m}$

**(i) Discharge:**

$$Q = \frac{A_c \times R}{t} = \frac{0.16 \times 0.150}{20} = \frac{0.024}{20} = 0.0012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.2 \text{ LPS}$$

**(ii) Manometric Head:**

$$H_m = h_s + h_d + z = 3.536 + 14.79 + 0.25 = 18.576 \text{ mwc} \approx 18.58 \text{ mwc}$$

**(iii) Shaft Power:**

$$P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = (50 - 12) \cdot \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 38 \times 157.08 = 5969.0 \text{ W} = 5.969 \text{ kW} \approx 5.97 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.0012 \times 18.576 = 218.71 \text{ W} \approx 0.219 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{0.219}{5.969} = 0.0367 = 3.67 \;\;\%$$

**(v) Manometric Efficiency:**

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.200 \times 1500}{60} = 15.708 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 B_2} = \frac{0.0012}{\pi \times 0.200 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.0012}{0.009425} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}$$

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2 = 15.708 - 0.1273 \times \cot 35° = 15.708 - 0.1273 \times 1.4281$$

$$V_{w2} = 15.708 - 0.1818 = 15.526 \text{ m/s}$$

$$H_{impeller} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \frac{15.526 \times 15.708}{9.81} = \frac{243.88}{9.81} = 24.86 \text{ m}$$

$$\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_{impeller}} = \frac{18.58}{24.86} = 0.7473 = 74.73 \;\;\%$$

#### Model Solution for Part A(b) — 7 Marks

**Procedure for Performance Test:**
1. **Priming:** Fill the pump casing and suction pipe completely with water to expel air. (Centrifugal pumps are **non-self-priming**.)
2. **Initial setup:** Close the delivery valve fully; switch on the motor; then slowly open the delivery valve to the first setting.
3. **Steady-state reading:** Wait until the manometer readings become steady.
4. **Take readings:** Record (a) tachometer reading $N$, (b) spring balance $W_1$ and dead weight $W_2$, (c) suction gauge $h_s$ and delivery gauge $h_d$, (d) initial water level in collecting tank.
5. **Measure discharge:** Start the stopwatch simultaneously; after a fixed interval (say 15–20 s), record the new water level. The discharge is $Q = A_c \times R / t$.
6. **Repeat:** Open the delivery valve further to the next setting and repeat steps 3–5 for about **8–10 throttle positions**.
7. **Plot curves:** Calculate $H_m$, $P_{shaft}$, $P_{water}$, and $\eta_o$ for each setting. Plot the three characteristic curves:
   - **H vs Q** (pump characteristic) — drooping
   - **$\eta_o$ vs Q** (efficiency curve) — inverted U
   - **$P_{shaft}$ vs Q** (power curve) — slightly rising
8. **BEP Identification:** The discharge at the **maximum of the $\eta_o$–$Q$ curve** is the **Best Efficiency Point**.

**Significance of BEP:**
- At BEP, the pump operates with **minimum energy loss per unit flow**.
- Vibration, noise, and bearing wear are **minimum** at BEP.
- It is the **recommended continuous operating point** for long service life.
- Deviation from BEP causes **cavitation**, **radial thrust imbalance**, and reduced life.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A centrifugal pump delivers water against a static head of 12 m. The friction loss in the suction and delivery pipes taken together is given by $h_f = 0.05 Q^2$ where $Q$ is in LPS. The pump has the characteristic $H = 25 - 0.02 Q^2$ (Q in LPS, H in mwc). Determine:
(i) The operating discharge and operating head.
(ii) The power required to drive the pump if its overall efficiency is 70%.
(iii) The discharge if the speed is increased by 20% (use affinity laws).

**(b)** What is priming? Why is a centrifugal pump called a non-self-priming pump? List the safety precautions to be observed in the lab while conducting the performance test.

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Static head: $H_{static} = 12 \text{ m}$
- System friction: $h_f = 0.05 Q^2$ (with Q in LPS)
- Pump curve: $H_{pump} = 25 - 0.02 Q^2$
- $\eta_o = 0.70$

**System curve:**

$$H_{sys} = H_{static} + h_f = 12 + 0.05 Q^2$$

**(i) Operating Point — equate pump and system curves:**

$$25 - 0.02 Q^2 = 12 + 0.05 Q^2$$

$$25 - 12 = 0.05 Q^2 + 0.02 Q^2$$

$$13 = 0.07 Q^2$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{13}{0.07} = 185.714$$

$$Q = 13.63 \text{ LPS} \approx 0.01363 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Operating head:**

$$H = 25 - 0.02 \times (13.63)^2 = 25 - 0.02 \times 185.78 = 25 - 3.716 = 21.28 \text{ mwc}$$

**(ii) Shaft Power:**

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01363 \times 21.28 = 2846.4 \text{ W} \approx 2.846 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_o} = \frac{2.846}{0.70} = 4.066 \text{ kW} \approx 4.07 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Affinity Law — speed increased by 20%:**

$$N_2 = 1.20 N_1$$

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right) = 13.63 \times 1.20 = 16.36 \text{ LPS}$$

$$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 21.28 \times 1.44 = 30.64 \text{ mwc}$$

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 4.07 \times 1.728 = 7.03 \text{ kW}$$

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Priming:**
Priming is the process of **filling the pump casing and the suction pipe completely with the liquid** to be pumped, before starting the pump. This expels any entrapped air and creates a continuous liquid column, ensuring that the pump can generate the necessary suction lift without air pockets breaking the flow.

**Why Centrifugal Pump is Non-Self-Priming:**
A centrifugal pump cannot develop suction if **air remains in the casing**, because air is much lighter than water and the small pressure difference created by the impeller is insufficient to lift a column of air against the static suction head. The impeller only "lifts" liquid, not air. Hence the pump must be **manually primed** with water before starting — making it inherently **non-self-priming**. (Self-priming pumps use an auxiliary water reservoir and a side channel to expel air automatically.)

**Safety Precautions in the Lab:**
1. **Electrical safety:** Ensure proper **earthing** of the motor body; check insulation of cables.
2. **Mechanical safety:** Keep **hands, hair, and loose clothing** away from the rotating rope and pulley.
3. **Rope brake safety:** Use a **wet rope** to prevent overheating; do not let the rope burn or break.
4. **Pressure safety:** Do not **fully close** the delivery valve for long durations; excessive pressure may damage seals.
5. **Eye protection:** Wear **safety goggles** in case of a hose burst.
6. **Sump level:** Maintain **sufficient water in the sump** to prevent air ingress from vortex formation.
7. **Emergency stop:** Know the location of the **emergency stop button** and the main isolator.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion of pressure gauge readings** — Most common error. $1 \text{ bar} = 10.2 \text{ mwc}$ and $1 \text{ mm Hg} = 0.0136 \text{ mwc}$. Mixing units will yield wrong $H_m$ and **lose 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of $z$** in $H_m = h_s + h_d \pm z$. If the delivery gauge is lower, $z$ is **negative**.
> 3. **Rope brake formula error** — Using $N$ as the impeller speed when the rope is on a different pulley. The rope speed and the impeller speed may differ if there is a **gear train**; verify with the lab manual.
> 4. **Spring balance units** — Reading in **kgf** but plugging into a formula that expects **N**. Multiply kgf by $9.81$ first. **1 mark deducted** for every instance.
> 5. **Confusing overall efficiency with manometric efficiency** — They are **not equal**. Overall efficiency is the ratio of water power to shaft power, while manometric efficiency relates $H_m$ to $H_{impeller}$.
> 6. **Not plotting the operating point** — The examiner specifically looks for the **intersection** marked clearly on the H–Q graph. An unmarked graph **loses 1 mark**.
> 7. **Plotting with disconnected lines** — Use a **smooth curve** for the H–Q plot. Stray zig-zag lines indicate rushed lab work.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operating point** = intersection of **pump H–Q curve** and **system resistance curve**.
- **Manometric head** is the head actually imparted to the fluid, measured via **two pressure gauges** (suction + delivery) plus the **vertical offset $z$**.
- **Discharge** is measured using a **collecting tank and stopwatch**: $Q = A_c R / t$.
- **Shaft power** is measured by the **rope-brake dynamometer**: $P_{shaft} = (W_1 - W_2) \cdot 2 \pi N / 60$.
- **Water (output) power**: $P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m$ in **watts**.
- **Overall efficiency**: $\eta_o = P_{water} / P_{shaft} = (\rho g Q H_m) / P_{shaft}$.
- **Manometric efficiency**: $\eta_{man} = H_m / H_{impeller}$, where $H_{impeller} = V_{w2} u_2 / g$.
- **Impeller tip speed**: $u_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$.
- **Whirl velocity**: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2} \cot \beta_2$ for backward-curved vanes.
- **Theoretical characteristic** is a downward parabola: $H = H_0 - K Q^2$.
- **System characteristic** is an upward parabola: $H_{sys} = H_{static} + C Q^2$.
- **Affinity laws** — $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the **maximum** of the $\eta_o$–$Q$ curve.
- **Priming** is mandatory for centrifugal pumps (non-self-priming).
- **NPSH** must be checked to prevent **cavitation** on the suction side.
- **Specific speed** $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ helps classify the pump as centrifugal/mixed/axial.
- The test rig must be **earthed**, the rope kept **wet**, and the **spring balance vertical**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on Impulse turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on Impulse turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **impulse turbine** is a hydraulic turbomachine in which the available hydraulic energy of water is first completely converted into a **kinetic energy jet** by a nozzle (or a set of nozzles), and this high-velocity jet is then allowed to strike the **runner buckets (vanes)** at atmospheric pressure. The entire pressure drop occurs in the nozzle, while the runner operates under **atmospheric pressure** throughout its energy-transfer zone — the water does not undergo any appreciable pressure change while passing over the bucket.

The **performance test on an impulse turbine** is a standard laboratory experiment conducted on a **Pelton wheel test rig** to experimentally determine the operational characteristics of the turbine by measuring its **brake power (BP)**, **water power (WP)**, and **overall efficiency (η)** under varying load and speed conditions. The test results are graphically represented through **constant-head, variable-speed characteristic curves**, which are mandatory deliverables of the experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Frequent Exam Phrasing):**
> *"An impulse turbine is one in which the pressure of water remains constant during its passage through the runner. All the available head is converted into a high-velocity jet at the nozzle, and the kinetic energy of the jet is then converted into mechanical work by impulse action on the moving buckets."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing still and you **throw a tennis ball** at a spinning playground merry-go-round (like pushing a swingset at a park). The ball hits the metal edge and pushes it, causing the whole thing to spin faster. The ball **transfers its momentum** (a form of kinetic energy) to the wheel.

Now think of the tennis ball as a **jet of water shooting out of a high-pressure nozzle**, and the merry-go-round as the **Pelton wheel with curved buckets**. The faster the jet, the more "punch" it delivers, the faster the wheel rotates. If you put a brake on the wheel, you create useful work output — and that is exactly what the lab test measures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Impulse"?** The word *impulse* in physics is the rate of change of momentum (Force × Time). Since the bucket **changes the direction of the jet's velocity**, the jet's momentum changes — and this rate of change is what produces the **impulsive force** that turns the runner.

## 1.3 Apparatus Identified (Test Rig Inventory)

A standard KTU-lab **Pelton wheel test rig** consists of the following instruments:

| # | Instrument | Purpose |
|---|------------|---------|
| 1 | **Pelton wheel runner** with single/multi-jet nozzle | Test specimen |
| 2 | **Pressure gauge** (Bourdon type) at the nozzle inlet | Measures net head H |
| 3 | **Nozzle with flow-regulating needle valve** | Controls discharge and jet velocity |
| 4 | **Collecting/measuring tank** with stopwatch | Measures actual discharge Q |
| 5 | **Spring balance (× 2) + Rope brake** on the brake drum | Applies and measures frictional load |
| 6 | **Tachometer** (digital or analog) | Measures runner speed N in rpm |
| 7 | **Dead weights** | Calibration of the spring-balance system |
| 8 | **Spear valve / Inlet valve** | Main supply cutoff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric used in KTU labs:** All heads are expressed in **metres of water (m)**, flow rate in **litres/second (L/s)** or **m³/s**, and power in **watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The Pelton wheel (impulse turbine) operates on the **Euler Equation for Turbomachines**. Let us dissect the energy-conversion sequence:

- **Step 1 — Pressure-to-Kinetic Conversion:** Water enters the nozzle under a head **H**. Inside the converging nozzle, all the potential head is transformed into velocity head. At the nozzle exit, the pressure equals atmospheric pressure.
  - The velocity of the jet: V₁ = √(2gH) (theoretical), or V₁ = Cᵥ√(2gH) (with coefficient of velocity).

- **Step 2 — Jet Strikes the Buckets:** The high-velocity jet hits the **double-symmetrical buckets** (each bucket is split into two halves by a central ridge). The jet is **deflected through an angle of nearly 165°–170°** — almost reversing its direction.

- **Step 3 — Momentum Transfer → Impulsive Force:** The change in momentum of the jet exerts a force on the bucket. This force acts at a radius equal to the **mean radius of the buckets (R)** and produces a **torque T = F × R** that rotates the runner.

- **Step 4 — Mechanical Work Extraction:** The rotating shaft is connected to a **rope brake drum**. Friction between the rope and the drum (tightened via a spring balance) dissipates the mechanical energy as heat — this frictional force is the **brake load** that we measure.

- **Step 5 — Power Output Calculation:** The product of frictional load and the peripheral velocity of the drum gives the **Brake Power**.

## 2.2 Euler Equation Applied to the Pelton Wheel

The work done per unit weight of water striking the runner is given by the **Euler turbine equation**:

$$W = \frac{V_1 u}{g} \left(1 + k \cos\beta\right) - \frac{u^2}{g}$$

where:

- **V₁** = velocity of jet at nozzle exit
- **u** = tangential velocity of the bucket (= π D N / 60)
- **β** = bucket exit angle (typically 165° → cos β is negative)
- **k** = ratio of relative velocity at exit to that at inlet

> [!NOTE]
> **For an ideal Pelton wheel (no friction):** k = 1, β = 180° (the jet is completely reversed), and the expression simplifies to: u = V₁/2 — which is the **condition for maximum efficiency**. This is a **HIGH-YIELD** KTU result.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical jet velocity | V₁ = Cᵥ √(2gH) | m/s |
| 2 | Ideal jet velocity | V = √(2gH) | m/s |
| 3 | Actual discharge | Q = C_d · a · √(2gH) | m³/s |
| 4 | Discharge by tank method | Q = Volume / time | m³/s |
| 5 | Peripheral (bucket) velocity | u = π D N / 60 | m/s |
| 6 | Speed ratio | K_u = u / V₁ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Flow ratio | K_f = Q / (A √(2gH)) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Water Power | WP = ρ g Q H | W |
| 9 | Brake Power (rope brake) | BP = 2π N (W − S) R / 60 | W |
| 10 | Overall efficiency | η_o = BP / WP | % |
| 11 | Hydraulic efficiency | η_h = (Kinetic energy of jet − kinetic energy of water leaving) / KE of jet | % |
| 12 | Mechanical efficiency | η_m = BP / Shaft Power (or BP / Runner Power) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard symbol conventions used by KTU valuators:**
> - **H** = net head available at the nozzle inlet (m)
> - **g** = 9.81 m/s² (acceleration due to gravity)
> - **ρ** = 1000 kg/m³ (density of water)
> - **W** = tight-side spring balance reading (kgf or N)
> - **S** = slack-side spring balance reading (kgf or N)
> - **R** = effective radius of brake drum (m)
> - **N** = runner speed (rpm)
> - **D** = mean bucket-circle diameter (m)
> - **a** = area of nozzle (m²)
> - **C_d** = coefficient of discharge of nozzle (≈ 0.98 for a clean, sharp-edged nozzle)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Performance testing of impulse turbines is not just an academic exercise. In the **hydroelectric power industry** (BHEL, NHPC, KSEB projects in Kerala such as **Idukki, Periyar, Sabarigiri**), every installed Pelton wheel undergoes an **ISO 6007 / IEC 41 model acceptance test** before commissioning. The characteristic curves obtained help:

- Select the **best operating point (BEP)** for maximum efficiency.
- Predict **part-load behaviour** during low-flow seasons.
- Verify the **guarantee curves** offered by manufacturers.
- Design the **governor settings** for grid frequency regulation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from the Rope-Brake Dynamometer

The **rope brake** is a friction-type dynamometer. A rope is wrapped around the rotating brake drum, with its two ends connected to a **dead weight (W)** hanging from one side and a **spring balance (S)** pulled on the other side. The frictional contact between the rope and the drum dissipates the runner's mechanical energy as heat — but it also allows us to *measure* that mechanical energy.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1 — Net frictional force on the drum.**

The two sides of the rope exert forces **W** and **S** in opposite directions. The net tangential force resisting the motion of the drum is:

$$F_{net} = (W - S) \cdot g \quad \text{(in Newtons, when W, S are in kgf)}$$

If W and S are read directly in **Newtons** from calibrated balances, no `g` multiplier is needed.

**Step 2 — Torque transmitted by the friction force.**

Torque is force multiplied by the moment arm (the brake-drum radius R):

$$T = F_{net} \times R = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert rotational speed from rpm to rad/s.**

One revolution = 2π radians, and one minute = 60 seconds. Therefore:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 4 — Power = Torque × Angular velocity.**

$$BP = T \cdot \omega = (W - S) \cdot g \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{BP = \frac{2\pi N \cdot (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When W and S are read in **kgf** and R in **metres**, the formula yields BP in **kgf·m/s**. Multiply by **9.81** to convert to **Watts**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Water Power

The water entering the nozzle at the head **H** possesses **potential energy per unit weight = H**. The mass flow rate of water is **ρ × Q**, and the rate at which this water delivers its energy to the turbine (i.e., water power) is:

$$WP = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

> Substituting ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s²:
> $$WP = 9810 \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{[W]}$$
> If Q is in **L/s**, convert to m³/s by dividing by 1000.

## 3.3 Derivation of Overall Efficiency

By definition, overall efficiency is the ratio of useful mechanical power output to the hydraulic power input from the water:

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R g / 60}{\rho g Q H}$$

The `g` cancels, yielding:

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60 \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot H}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem Statement:**
> In a Pelton wheel performance test, the following readings are observed:
> - Net head at nozzle, **H = 30 m**
> - Diameter of jet, **d = 25 mm**
> - Mean diameter of bucket circle, **D = 0.6 m**
> - Effective brake-drum radius, **R = 0.25 m**
> - Spring balance readings: **W = 8 kgf, S = 1 kgf**
> - Runner speed, **N = 500 rpm**
> - Actual discharge, **Q = 0.025 m³/s**
>
> Calculate: **(i)** Brake Power, **(ii)** Water Power, **(iii)** Overall Efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (i) — Brake Power:**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi \cdot 500 \cdot (8 - 1) \cdot 0.25 \cdot 9.81}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 500 \cdot 7 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 9.81}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{6.2832 \cdot 500 \cdot 7 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 9.81}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{53{,}945.7}{60}$$

$$\boxed{BP \approx 899.1 \text{ W} \approx 0.899 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (ii) — Water Power:**

$$WP = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 30$$

$$WP = 245.25 \times 30 = 7357.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{WP \approx 7.358 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step (iii) — Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{899.1}{7357.5}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} \approx 0.1222 = 12.22\%}$$

**Step (iv) — Verification of the jet velocity & speed ratio (cross-check):**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30} = \sqrt{588.6} = 24.26 \text{ m/s}$$

$$u = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.6 \cdot 500}{60} = \frac{942.48}{60} = 15.708 \text{ m/s}$$

$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{15.708}{24.26} \approx 0.647$$

> The speed ratio is close to the **design optimum of 0.46–0.50** but on the higher side — meaning the runner is rotating too fast relative to the jet, hence the low efficiency. The student should comment on this in the viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Pelton Wheel Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SUMP OR OVERHEAD TANK] -->|Suction by pump| B[SUPPLY VALVE]
    B --> C[PRESSURE GAUGE H MEASURED]
    C --> D[FLOW REGULATING NEEDLE VALVE NOZZLE]
    D -->|High-velocity jet| E[PELTON WHEEL BUCKETS]
    E --> F[BRAKE DRUM]
    F --> G[ROPE WITH SPRING BALANCE W AND S]
    E --> H[TACHOMETER N rpm]
    D --> I[COLLECTING TANK FOR Q]
    I --> J[STOPWATCH t sec]
    E --> K[TAIL RACE WATER OUTLET]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Water flows from the sump → through the supply valve → past the pressure gauge (giving **H**) → through the needle valve into the **nozzle** → exits as a high-velocity **jet** → strikes the **Pelton wheel buckets** → water falls into the tail race. A **rope brake** on the brake drum measures the **load**, and a **tachometer** measures **speed**. Discharge **Q** is measured volumetrically in the collecting tank.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[HYDRAULIC ENERGY INPUT rho g Q H] --> P2[NOZZLE]
    P2 --> P3[CONVERSION TO KINETIC ENERGY 0.5 rho Q V squared]
    P3 --> P4[BUCKET IMPULSE FORCE F rho Q V one minus V two]
    P4 --> P5[SHAFT MECHANICAL POWER RUNNER POWER]
    P5 --> P6[BRAKE DRUM FRICTION]
    P6 --> P7[BRAKE POWER 2 pi N W minus S R g DIVIDED BY 60]
    P7 --> P8[HEAT DISSIPATION TO ATMOSPHERE]
    P5 -.-> P9[MECHANICAL LOSSES BEARING WINDAGE]
    P3 -.-> P10[NOZZLE LOSSES C d LESS THAN 1]
    P4 -.-> P11[HYDRAULIC LOSSES BUCKET FRICTION SPLITTING]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for the lab report:**
> - The **overall efficiency η_o = η_h × η_m × η_v** (volumetric efficiency is 1.0 in impulse turbines since water passes through once and is not recirculated internally).
> - The dashed lines from P3, P4, P5 represent **losses** in the system — these are what the efficiency calculations capture.

## 4.3 Block Diagram of the Test Procedure (Sequential Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[START] --> S2[CLOSE NOZZLE NEEDLE FULLY]
    S2 --> S3[START PUMP AND OPEN SUPPLY VALVE]
    S3 --> S4[NOTE PRESSURE GAUGE H AND ZERO LOAD SPEED]
    S4 --> S5[OPEN NOZZLE SLIGHTLY]
    S5 --> S6[APPLY ROPE BRAKE LOAD VIA SPRING BALANCE]
    S6 --> S7[RECORD N rpm W kgf S kgf Q L per s t sec]
    S7 --> S8[INCREMENT LOAD REPEAT 6 TO 8 TIMES]
    S8 --> S9[PLOT N vs BP, N vs eta, N vs Q]
    S9 --> S10[END OF TEST]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**What is an impulse turbine? How does it differ from a reaction turbine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **impulse turbine** is a hydraulic machine in which the entire pressure head of water is first converted into kinetic energy by a nozzle, and the resulting high-velocity jet is then made to strike the runner buckets. The pressure of water remains **constant (atmospheric)** during its passage through the runner. The energy transfer is purely by **impulse (momentum change)**.

In contrast, a **reaction turbine** operates under a pressure differential across the runner. Water flows through a closed system of guide vanes and runner blades, and energy transfer is by both **pressure difference and kinetic change** (combined impulse and reaction action). Examples: **Pelton wheel** (impulse) vs. **Francis / Kaplan** (reaction).

| Aspect | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Pressure change in runner | None | Significant |
| Casing | Not required (atmospheric) | Fully sealed, pressurized |
| Water contact with runner | Partial (only the jet) | Full (vanes are submerged) |
| Typical heads | High (> 300 m) | Medium/Low |

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**Define the terms: (i) Speed ratio, (ii) Jet ratio, and (iii) Coefficient of velocity as applied to a Pelton wheel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Speed ratio (K_u or K_s):** The ratio of the peripheral velocity of the bucket to the velocity of the jet.

$$K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{\pi D N / 60}{C_v \sqrt{2gH}}$$

Its optimum value for maximum efficiency lies between **0.43 and 0.48**.

**(ii) Jet ratio (m):** The ratio of the **jet diameter (d)** to the **mean bucket-circle diameter (D)**.

$$m = \frac{d}{D}$$

The KTU design optimum is **m ≈ 1/9 to 1/12** (so that the jet does not interfere with neighbouring buckets).

**(iii) Coefficient of velocity (C_v):** The ratio of the **actual velocity of the jet** at nozzle exit to the **theoretical velocity** derived from the available head.

$$C_v = \frac{V_{1,\text{actual}}}{V_{1,\text{theoretical}}} = \frac{V_{1,\text{actual}}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$

Typical value: **0.96 to 0.99** for a well-designed nozzle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Pelton wheel**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a Pelton wheel test, the net head at the nozzle is **40 m**, the jet diameter is **30 mm**, the mean runner diameter is **1.0 m**, the speed is **360 rpm**, and the discharge is **0.04 m³/s**. The tight-side and slack-side spring balance readings are **25 kgf** and **5 kgf** respectively, and the brake-drum diameter is **40 cm**. Calculate: **(i)** Brake Power, **(ii)** Water Power, **(iii)** Overall Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Construction of a Pelton Wheel:**

A Pelton wheel consists of the following main parts:

1. **Nozzle and Needle Valve Assembly** — Controls the flow rate and jet velocity. The needle (spear) moves axially to throttle the flow. A *deflector* is also provided for emergency shutdown.
2. **Runner with Buckets** — A disc-shaped wheel on which a number of **double-symmetrical buckets** (typically 16 to 24) are bolted. The bucket has a central ridge that **splits the jet into two halves**, deflecting it through an angle of about **165°–170°**.
3. **Casing** — A simple housing that prevents splashing and allows safe tail-race conditions. It is **not pressure-tight**.
4. **Brake Drum and Rope Brake** — Mounted on the shaft for loading the turbine.
5. **Shaft and Bearings** — Transmit mechanical power to the generator (or the dynamometer in the lab).
6. **Governor Mechanism** — Adjusts the needle position to maintain constant speed under varying load.

**Working Principle:**

Water from a high-head reservoir is brought through a penstock to the nozzle. As it passes through the nozzle, the **pressure energy is converted into kinetic energy** — producing a high-velocity jet. The jet strikes the buckets mounted on the periphery of the runner, and the **change in momentum** of the jet produces an impulsive force on the buckets. This force creates a torque that rotates the runner. The mechanical energy of the shaft is then either coupled to a generator (in industry) or dissipated through a **rope brake** (in the lab) for measurement.

#### Solution to (b):

**Given data:**

- H = 40 m
- d = 30 mm = 0.03 m
- D = 1.0 m
- N = 360 rpm
- Q = 0.04 m³/s
- W = 25 kgf, S = 5 kgf
- Brake-drum diameter = 40 cm → R = 0.20 m

**Step 1 — Brake Power (with g = 9.81 m/s²):**

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R g}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 360 \times (25 - 5) \times 0.20 \times 9.81}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{6.2832 \times 360 \times 20 \times 0.20 \times 9.81}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{88{,}754.5}{60}$$

$$\boxed{BP \approx 1479.2 \text{ W} \approx 1.479 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 2 — Water Power:**

$$WP = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 40$$

$$WP = 392.4 \times 40 = 15{,}696 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{WP = 15.696 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 3 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{1479.2}{15{,}696} = 0.0942$$

$$\boxed{\eta_o \approx 9.42\%}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check (speed ratio):**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 40} = 28.0 \text{ m/s}$$

$$u = \frac{\pi \times 1.0 \times 360}{60} = 18.85 \text{ m/s}$$

$$K_u = \frac{18.85}{28.0} = 0.673$$

The speed ratio is too high (should be ~0.45 for max efficiency), which explains the low overall efficiency. **This comment carries a bonus 1–2 marks in the KTU viva.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

> **(a)** Explain the procedure for conducting a **performance test on a Pelton wheel** in the laboratory, and list the instruments used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Pelton wheel develops **1.5 kW** of brake power under a net head of **50 m**, with a discharge of **0.03 m³/s**. Determine: **(i)** Overall efficiency, **(ii)** Jet velocity, **(iii)** Peripheral velocity of the bucket if the speed ratio is **0.46**, and **(iv)** Mean diameter of the runner if the speed is **400 rpm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Experimental Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Preliminary Inspection:** Check the oil level in the bearings, the rope condition, the spring balance zero, and the tachometer calibration.
2. **Start the Pump:** Open the main supply valve slowly. With the **nozzle needle fully closed**, the runner is rotated at "no-load" speed (free wheeling).
3. **Set the Head:** Adjust the inlet valve until the pressure gauge reads the **desired net head H**. Maintain this constant throughout the test (since this is a *constant-head* test).
4. **First Reading (Zero Load):** With the **rope brake slack** (no load applied), note the maximum free-running speed **N₀**. Also measure discharge Q for verification.
5. **Apply Incremental Loads:** Tighten the **rope brake** by adding weight to the dead-weight side, or by tightening the turnbuckle. For each increment:
   - Wait for the speed to **stabilize**.
   - Record **N (rpm)**, **W and S (kgf)**, **time t (s)** for collecting a known volume **V (L)** in the measuring tank.
6. **Repeat for 6–8 loads** up to the maximum permitted by the rig.
7. **Stop the test** by closing the nozzle needle first, then the supply valve, then the pump.

**Instruments Used:**

- Bourdon-type pressure gauge (0–10 bar)
- Nozzle with calibrated needle and flow control
- Measuring tank of known cross-section + stopwatch
- Two spring balances (0–50 kg range)
- Rope brake with dead-weight hanger
- Tachometer (contact or non-contact)
- Stopwatch (0.01 s resolution)
- Thermometer (optional, for water-temperature correction)

**Data Table Format (for lab record):**

| Sl. No. | N (rpm) | W (kgf) | S (kgf) | Time (s) | Volume (L) | Q (L/s) | BP (W) | WP (W) | η (%) |
|---------|---------|---------|---------|----------|------------|---------|--------|--------|-------|

#### Solution to (b):

**Given data:** BP = 1.5 kW = 1500 W, H = 50 m, Q = 0.03 m³/s, K_u = 0.46, N = 400 rpm.

**(i) Overall Efficiency:**

$$WP = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 \times 50 = 14{,}715 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_o = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{1500}{14{,}715}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_o = 0.1019 = 10.19\%}$$

**(ii) Jet Velocity:**

$$V_1 = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 50} = \sqrt{981}$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = 31.32 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(iii) Peripheral Velocity of the Bucket:**

$$u = K_u \times V_1 = 0.46 \times 31.32$$

$$\boxed{u = 14.41 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(iv) Mean Diameter of the Runner:**

From the peripheral velocity formula $u = \pi D N / 60$:

$$D = \frac{60 \cdot u}{\pi \cdot N} = \frac{60 \times 14.41}{\pi \times 400}$$

$$D = \frac{864.6}{1256.64}$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.688 \text{ m} \approx 68.8 \text{ cm}}$$

> **Board valuation note:** Always carry units through the calculation — marks are awarded for **correct units** in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Lab Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the g-multiplier** in the brake-power formula when spring-balance readings are in **kgf**. KTU valuators specifically deduct 1 mark for this. Always write the formula as `BP = 2πN(W − S)Rg/60` and clearly state the units of W and S.
>
> 2. **Mixing up units in the discharge calculation.** If you measure volume in **litres** and time in **seconds**, you get Q in **L/s**. Convert to **m³/s** by dividing by 1000 *before* using the water-power formula — otherwise WP will be off by a factor of 1000, and your efficiency will be a comical 0.01%!
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "empty" spring-balance reading (S) and using only W.** The slack-side reading is not zero — rope friction and asymmetry give a finite S. Always use **(W − S)**, never just **W**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to plot the characteristic curves.** The full-marks lab report **must** contain:
>    - N vs BP
>    - N vs η
>    - N vs Q
>    - (Bonus) W vs N
>
> 5. **Not maintaining constant head.** If H varies by more than ±5% during a test run, the test must be **repeated**. Mark deductions of 1–2 marks apply.
>
> 6. **Incorrect use of g = 9.81.** Use **9.81 m/s²** consistently, not 10 or 9.8. KTU evaluators are strict about this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Impulse turbine** = **Pressure-to-Kinetic conversion at the nozzle**, **constant pressure (atmospheric)** through the runner.
- **Pelton wheel** is the canonical impulse turbine; used for **high heads (above 300 m)** and **low discharges**.
- **Three energy-conversion stages:** (1) PE → KE in nozzle, (2) KE → mechanical work in bucket, (3) mechanical work → heat in brake drum.
- **Optimum speed ratio K_u = 0.46** for maximum hydraulic efficiency in an ideal Pelton wheel.
- **Optimum jet ratio m = d/D ≈ 1/9** to prevent jet-bucket interference.
- **Bucket exit angle β ≈ 165°–170°** — the jet is *almost* reversed, not fully reversed (180°) to prevent water from striking the back of the next bucket.
- **Always convert units carefully** — kgf → N (multiply by 9.81), L/s → m³/s (divide by 1000), rpm → rad/s (multiply by 2π/60).
- **Characteristic curves drawn at constant H, variable load:** the curve of N vs η is **inverted U-shaped**; the peak is the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.
- **Typical efficiencies of a Pelton wheel:** η_h ≈ 90–95%, η_m ≈ 95–98%, η_o ≈ 85–90% (in industrial installations). Laboratory rigs show lower values due to scale and friction effects.
- **The most common KTU viva question:** *"Why is the speed ratio less than 0.5 and not equal to 0.5?"* — Answer: friction at the bucket surfaces and the bucket exit angle not being exactly 180° (it is around 165–170°) means optimum K_u is slightly less than 0.5 in real practice.
- **When the brake load is increased beyond a point, the runner may stall** — the bucket cannot be pushed by the jet if it is too slow. This is a safety limit of the test rig.
- **Always include a "no-load" run** at the start of the test to record the **free-wheeling speed N₀** — this is the bucket speed when u = V₁/2, and is the theoretical maximum K_u.
- **Theorems to recall:** Euler turbine equation; Bernoulli's theorem (applied to the nozzle); momentum-conservation law (applied to the bucket).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU revision mantra (mnemonic — "PRIME"):**
> **P** = Pressure (converted in nozzle)
> **R** = Runner at atmospheric pressure
> **I** = Impulse (momentum change)
> **M** = Maximum efficiency at K_u ≈ 0.46
> **E** = Euler equation is the foundation

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance test on reaction turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance test on reaction turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Reaction Turbine** is a hydraulic machine in which the runner operates fully submerged in flowing water, and the energy conversion from hydraulic (pressure + kinetic) to mechanical (rotational) form occurs **simultaneously** through a continuous pressure drop across the rotating blades. The water **reacts** against the curved blades, producing a reactive force (Newton's Third Law) that rotates the runner. Unlike an impulse turbine (like the Pelton wheel), the runner of a reaction turbine is enclosed in a casing filled with water under pressure, and the fluid pressure changes as it flows over the blades.

**Performance testing** of reaction turbines is the standard laboratory procedure used to determine the relationship between the **input power**, **output power (shaft power)**, **efficiencies**, **head**, **discharge**, and **rotational speed** of the turbine, culminating in the plotting of standard **characteristic curves** (constant head, constant speed, constant efficiency/musso curves) that predict prototype behavior from model data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Two specific reaction turbines are mandated for study: the **Francis Turbine** (mixed-flow, medium head) and the **Kaplan Turbine** (axial-flow, low head with adjustable blades). Both are tested for performance under varying load conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are **swimming underwater** and pushing water backward with your cupped hands — the water pushes you forward (Newton's Third Law). Now imagine doing this continuously with curved blade paddles attached to a shaft — the shaft will spin. That spinning paddle wheel, enclosed inside a pipe full of flowing water, is essentially how a reaction turbine works. The water inside is pressurized; it pushes the blades as it exits them, causing rotation.

A **Francis turbine** is like pushing water sideways (mixed flow), while a **Kaplan turbine** is like pushing water straight backward (axial flow) — much like a ship's propeller but working in reverse and enclosed in a casing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Experiment:**
> - Density of water: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Specific weight of water: $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$

### 1.3 Classification of Reaction Turbines Tested

| Feature | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Flow direction | Mixed (radial → axial) | Purely axial |
| Head range | Medium to high (**30 m – 600 m**) | Low (**up to 70 m**) |
| Specific speed | Medium (**60 – 400**) | High (**300 – 1000**) |
| Runner blades | Fixed, curved | Adjustable (pitch control) |
| Guide vanes | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| Draft tube | Yes (always) | Yes (always) |
| Typical prototype | Hydroelectric dams | Run-of-river, tidal plants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

The complete energy conversion in a reaction turbine occurs in **three discrete stages**, each occurring in a dedicated component:

**Stage 1 — Spiral Casing (Pressure Energy Maximization):**
Water enters the turbine through a spiral/scroll casing that distributes it symmetrically around the runner. The cross-sectional area of the casing **decreases** along the flow direction, so by **continuity** ($A \cdot V = \text{constant}$), the velocity decreases, and by **Bernoulli's principle**, the pressure energy increases. The water leaves the casing with **maximum pressure and minimum kinetic energy**.

**Stage 2 — Guide Vanes / Stay Vanes (Velocity Triangularization):**
- **Stay vanes** convert the swirling, turbulent flow from the casing into a smooth, axially symmetric flow.
- **Guide vanes** (movable, ring-shaped) direct the water at the **correct angle** onto the runner blades and also act as a **primary flow regulation** device. Closing the guide vanes reduces discharge, hence input power.

**Stage 3 — Runner (Kinetic → Mechanical Energy):**
Water enters the runner at an angle, passes through the curved blades (where its pressure drops and momentum changes), and exits into the draft tube. The reactive impulse on the curved blades produces the **torque** on the shaft.

**Stage 4 — Draft Tube (Pressure Recovery):**
