The draft tube is a **gradually expanding diffuser** that converts the high exit velocity of water back into pressure energy, **recovering a significant portion of the kinetic energy** that would otherwise be lost. The pressure at the runner exit is reduced below atmospheric (in some designs), increasing the effective head on the runner. The **net head** on the runner is the sum of the **head supplied at the inlet plus the suction head from the draft tube**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a draft tube is essential for reaction turbines:** Without it, the kinetic energy leaving the runner would be wasted. By the time water exits the draft tube, its velocity is nearly zero, recovering almost the full exit velocity head. This recovered head is "free" energy that adds to the effective head across the runner.

### 2.2 Power and Efficiency — Derivation Chain

**Step 1 — Input Hydraulic Power ($P_{in}$):**
The input power to the turbine is the rate at which hydraulic energy is supplied by the flowing water.

$$
P_{in} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric discharge in m³/s and $H$ is the net head in meters.

**Step 2 — Output Shaft Power ($P_{out}$):**
The output (brake) power is the mechanical power delivered by the shaft, measured by a brake-drum and rope/pronely brake dynamometer.

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = T \omega
$$

where $N$ = rotational speed in rpm, $T$ = torque in N·m, and $\omega$ = angular velocity in rad/s.

**Step 3 — Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$):**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \,\rho g Q H}
$$

**Step 4 — Three Component Efficiencies:**

$$
\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m
$$

- **Hydraulic efficiency** $\eta_h = \dfrac{\text{Runner power}}{\text{Water power}} = \dfrac{\text{Kinetic energy utilized}}{\text{Kinetic energy supplied by guide vane}}$
- **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft power}}{\text{Runner power}}$ (accounts for bearing friction, windage, water friction)
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{used}}{Q_{supplied}}$ — usually taken as **1.0** for reaction turbines (no leakage paths comparable to impulse turbines).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM CHEAT SHEET for this experiment. Memorize and apply it directly.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input power | $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$ | W | $Q$ from venturimeter, $H$ from pressure tappings |
| 2 | Shaft (brake) power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | $T$ from brake-drum: $T = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g$ |
| 3 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} | m^3/s | used in venturimeter calibration |
| 4 | Head across venturimeter | $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \dfrac{(x_1 - x_2)(\rho_m - \rho)}{\rho}$ | m | U-tube manometer with mercury |
| 5 | Discharge (venturi) | $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m³/s | $C_d$ typically **0.95 – 0.98** |
| 6 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{out}}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (SI) | Francis: 60–400, Kaplan: 300–1000 |
| 7 | Unit speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm/m^{1/2} | Used to plot constant-head curves |
| 8 | Unit discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s/m^{1/2} | Used to plot constant-head curves |
| 9 | Unit power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | W/m^{3/2} | Used to plot constant-head curves |
| 10 | Torque (rope brake) | $T = (W - S) \cdot R$ (in N·m) | N·m | $W$ = load, $S$ = spring balance, $R$ = brake drum radius |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The characteristic curves obtained from this lab test are **scaled using the unit-quantities (Step 7, 8, 9) and affinity laws** to predict the performance of the full-scale prototype turbine from the model. This is the foundation of **turbine model testing** in hydraulic research labs like those of BHEL, IIT, and international hydropower authorities.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Discharge Through Venturimeter

The venturimeter is mounted on the supply pipe leading to the turbine. Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between the inlet (1) and throat (2) of the venturi, and using the **continuity equation**:

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation (no elevation change, ideal flow):**

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Rearranging to isolate the head difference:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the differential head $h$:**

Let $h = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$. Then:

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the continuity equation:**

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_1$ into the head equation:**

$$
h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \frac{A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by the throat area to obtain the ideal discharge:**

$$
Q_{ideal} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ to account for real (lossy) flow:**

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}}
$$

> **Logic of the conversion:** Bernoulli captures the energy balance; continuity links the two cross-sections; the algebraic substitution eliminates $V_1$; the $C_d$ factor (typically 0.95–0.98 for a well-machined venturi) empirically corrects for friction losses and vena-contracta effects not captured by ideal theory.

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Head on the Reaction Turbine

The net head available at the runner is **not** simply the difference between the supply tank level and the tailrace level. It must account for **pressure tappings** at the turbine inlet and the draft tube exit.

**Step 1 — Measure the inlet pressure $P_i$ at the runner entry using a Bourdon gauge or piezometer:**

$$
\frac{P_i}{\rho g} + \frac{V_i^2}{2g} + z_i
$$

**Step 2 — Measure the outlet pressure $P_o$ at the draft tube exit:**

$$
\frac{P_o}{\rho g} + \frac{V_o^2}{2g} + z_o
$$

**Step 3 — The net head is the total energy difference between the entry and exit of the runner system:**

$$
H_{net} = \left(\frac{P_i}{\rho g} + \frac{V_i^2}{2g} + z_i\right) - \left(\frac{P_o}{\rho g} + \frac{V_o^2}{2g} + z_o\right)
$$

**Step 4 — In the standard KTU lab setup, the manometer readings at the venturimeter give $h$, and the pressure tappings on the turbine give the supply head and tailrace head. The total net head is computed as:**

$$
\boxed{H_{net} = H_{gross} - h_f}
$$

where $H_{gross}$ = difference between supply sump water level and tailrace level, and $h_f$ = head loss in the supply pipe (computed from the venturi reading and pipe friction factor).

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Speed

Specific speed is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would develop **unit power (1 kW)** under **unit head (1 m)**. It is the **single most important non-dimensional selector** for choosing between Francis and Kaplan turbines.

**Step 1 — Define unit quantities using dimensional homogeneity:**

The power scales as $H^{3/2}$ and the discharge as $H^{1/2}$ for a geometrically similar machine. Therefore:

$$
N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}}
$$

**Step 2 — For unit power, set $P_u = 1$ kW. The corresponding speed is the specific speed:**

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P \text{ (kW)}}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

**Step 3 — Converting output power from Watts to kW (division by 1000):**

$$
\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P_{out} / 1000}}{H^{5/4}}}
$$

> **Conversion logic note:** The exponent 5/4 comes from $1 + (3/2) \cdot (1/2)$ — i.e., 1 for the direct speed term, plus (3/2) for the power term and (1/2) for the head term, which together give $1 + 3/4 = 7/4$ for the power part — but re-arranged with the standard form: $N_s = N P^{1/2} H^{-5/4}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Procedure vs Observation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following matrix is the actual procedural protocol for the KTU lab viva. Know every cell.**

| Trial # | Guide Vane Opening | $h$ (m) | $Q$ (m³/s) | $N$ (rpm) | $W$ (kg) | $S$ (kg) | $T$ (N·m) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta_o$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Full | 0.40 | 0.012 | 1450 | 8.0 | 1.0 | … | … | … | … |
| 2 | 75% | 0.32 | 0.010 | 1460 | 6.5 | 1.2 | … | … | … | … |
| 3 | 50% | 0.22 | 0.0085 | 1475 | 4.5 | 1.5 | … | … | … | … |
| … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … |

After tabulating, plot:
- **Constant Head Curves** ($H$ = constant): $N_u$ vs $Q_u$, $P_u$ vs $Q_u$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q_u$ — all on the same $Q_u$ axis.
- **Constant Speed Curves** ($N$ = constant): $H$ vs $Q$, $P$ vs $Q$, $\eta_o$ vs $Q$.
- **Main / Musso Curves** (iso-efficiency lines): $\eta_o$ on $H$–$Q$ plane.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Francis Turbine Test Rig Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Sump - Constant Head Tank] --> B[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    B --> C[Centrifugal Pump Set with Motor]
    C --> D[Control Valve - Main Inlet Regulation]
    D --> E[Venturimeter with U-tube Manometer]
    E --> F[Piezo Ring Tapping at Inlet]
    F --> G[Spiral Casing of Francis Turbine]
    G --> H[Guide Vane Assembly - Stay Vanes and Guide Vanes]
    H --> I[Francis Runner - Mixed Flow Blades]
    I --> J[Draft Tube - Gradually Expanding Diffuser]
    J --> K[Tailrace Measuring Tank with Notch]
    I --> L[Turbine Shaft]
    L --> M[Brake Drum and Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    M --> N[Load W and Spring Balance S]
    L --> O[Speed Sensor - Tachometer]
    L --> P[Mechanical Power Output Shaft]
    E --> Q[Manometer Reading x1 minus x2 gives h]
    F --> R[Inlet Pressure Tapping P1]
    J --> S[Outlet Pressure Tapping P2]
    Q --> T[Data Acquisition and Tabulation]
    R --> T
    S --> T
    N --> T
    O --> T
    T --> U[Plot Characteristic Curves and Compute Efficiencies]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Kaplan Turbine Test Rig Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large Sump or Laboratory Reservoir] --> B[Submersible or Axial Flow Pump]
    B --> C[Delivery Pipe with Main Control Valve]
    C --> D[Venturimeter for Discharge Measurement]
    D --> E[Pressure Tapping at Runner Inlet]
    E --> F[Scroll Casing of Kaplan Turbine]
    F --> G[Guide Vane Ring with Linkage]
    G --> H[Adjustable Kaplan Runner Blades via Servo]
    H --> I[Axial Flow Runner Hub and Blades]
    I --> J[Draft Tube with Elbow and Tailrace]
    J --> K[Tailrace Collecting Tank with V Notch]
    I --> L[Vertical Shaft]
    L --> M[Mechanical Brake Drum with Rope Brake]
    M --> N[Pronely Brake with Load and Spring Balance]
    L --> O[Tachometer for Rotational Speed N]
    D --> P[Manometer Differential Reading h]
    E --> Q[Inlet Pressure Head P1 by rho g]
    J --> R[Outlet Pressure Head P2 by rho g]
    H --> S[Blade Angle Adjustment Servo]
    P --> T[Data Logger and Calculator Module]
    Q --> T
    R --> T
    N --> T
    O --> T
    S --> T
    T --> U[Specific Speed Calculation and Iso efficiency Curves]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Conversion Chain in Reaction Turbines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hydraulic Energy at Inlet] --> B[Pressure Energy - Spiral Casing]
    B --> C[Kinetic Energy - Guide Vanes]
    C --> D[Kinetic plus Pressure - Runner Inlet]
    D --> E[Mechanical Energy on Shaft via Reactive Force]
    E --> F[Residual Kinetic Energy - Draft Tube]
    F --> G[Recovered Pressure Energy - Tailrace]
    G --> H[Final Loss to Tailrace - near zero velocity]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The flow proceeds top-to-bottom. Each block is a real, physical component of the lab rig. The data acquisition block $T$ receives the four primary measurements ($h$, $P_1$, $P_2$, $W$, $S$, $N$) and computes the seven derived quantities ($Q$, $H_{net}$, $P_{in}$, $P_{out}$, $T$, $\eta_o$, $N_s$).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between an impulse turbine and a reaction turbine. Why is a draft tube essential for a reaction turbine but not for a Pelton wheel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Impulse Turbine (e.g., Pelton) | Reaction Turbine (e.g., Francis, Kaplan) |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure at runner | Atmospheric | Below atmospheric (submerged) |
| Energy conversion | KE → ME only | KE + Pressure → ME |
| Runner enclosure | Open | Cased, full of water |
| Flow through runner | Once through, atmospheric | Continuous under pressure |

**Why a draft tube is essential (1 mark):** In a reaction turbine, water leaves the runner with high kinetic energy. If discharged directly to the tailrace, this KE is lost. The draft tube is a **gradually expanding diffuser** that decelerates the flow and converts the exit KE into **useful pressure head** before the water reaches the tailrace. This effectively **increases the net head** available across the runner and improves overall efficiency. A Pelton wheel discharges its jet to atmosphere after a single impulse, so no further energy recovery is possible — hence no draft tube is required.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define specific speed of a turbine. What is its significance in selecting between Francis and Kaplan turbines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The specific speed $N_s$ of a turbine is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run to develop a unit power (1 kW) under a unit head (1 m).

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P \text{ (kW)}}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):** Specific speed is the standard **turbine selection parameter**. As a thumb rule:
- **Low $N_s$ (10 – 60):** Pelton wheel (impulse)
- **Medium $N_s$ (60 – 400):** Francis turbine
- **High $N_s$ (300 – 1000):** Kaplan turbine

A higher specific speed indicates an axial-flow, high-discharge, low-head design (Kaplan); a lower specific speed indicates a radial/mixed-flow, low-discharge, high-head design (Pelton). Francis turbines occupy the intermediate band.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
In a performance test on a Francis turbine, the following observations are recorded:
- Net head $H = 8$ m
- Discharge $Q = 0.05$ m³/s
- Rotational speed $N = 1000$ rpm
- Load on the brake drum $W = 12$ kg
- Spring balance reading $S = 2$ kg
- Effective brake-drum radius $R = 0.20$ m

Calculate the **(i)** shaft power, **(ii)** input power, **(iii)** overall efficiency, and **(iv)** specific speed. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Torque on the shaft:**
For a rope brake, the net load is $(W - S)$ and torque is:
$$
T = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g = (12 - 2) \cdot 0.20 \cdot 9.81 = 10 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 9.81
$$

$$
T = 19.62 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step (ii) — Shaft (brake) power:**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 19.62}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 1000 \cdot 19.62}{60} = \frac{123{,}272}{60} \approx 2054.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.05 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Input hydraulic power:**
$$
P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 8 = 3924 \text{ W} \approx 3.92 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iv) — Overall efficiency:**
$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{2054.5}{3924} = 0.5235 \approx 52.35\%
$$

**Step (v) — Specific speed:**
$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P_{out} \text{ (kW)}}}{H^{5/4}} = \frac{1000 \cdot \sqrt{2.05}}{8^{5/4}}
$$

Compute $8^{5/4}$: $8^{1/4} = 8^{0.25} = 1.6818$, so $8^{5/4} = 8 \cdot 1.6818 = 13.45$. And $\sqrt{2.05} = 1.432$.

$$
N_s = \frac{1000 \cdot 1.432}{13.45} = 106.5
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $N_s \approx 106$ lies in the range **60–400**, the turbine classification is consistent with a **Francis turbine** (medium specific speed). ✓

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Kaplan turbine**. Explain why it is preferred for low-head, high-discharge installations. Also, state the function of its **adjustable runner blades**. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A Kaplan turbine consists of the following main parts:
- **Spiral casing** (scroll case) — to distribute water symmetrically around the runner.
- **Guide vane mechanism** — adjustable, ring of vanes that regulate the flow rate and direct water axially onto the runner.
- **Runner hub with adjustable blades** — typically 3 to 8 blades mounted on a hub that can rotate about their own axis to change pitch. The hub is connected via an **oil-pressure servomotor** for blade angle adjustment.
- **Draft tube** — usually a **conical or elbow type** to recover exit kinetic energy.
- **Vertical shaft** — transmits the mechanical power from the runner to the generator above.

**Working (2 marks):** Water from the penstock enters the spiral casing, is set into axial rotation by the guide vanes, and enters the runner with a strong **axial** velocity component. The water flows through the curved, adjustable runner blades axially; the change in momentum (reactive force) produces a torque on the runner. The water leaves the runner with reduced whirl and is decelerated in the draft tube before reaching the tailrace.

**Why preferred for low-head, high-discharge (1 mark):** Kaplan turbines have a high specific speed (300–1000) due to their **axial flow geometry**, allowing them to handle very large discharges economically. They are installed where the head is too low for Francis or Pelton turbines (typically below 70 m) but the flow rate is enormous — e.g., run-of-river, tidal, and small hydropower projects.

**Function of adjustable runner blades (1 mark):** At part-load or varying head conditions, the guide vane closure alone cannot maintain high efficiency. By **simultaneously rotating the runner blades** (via the servomotor) to match the inlet flow angle, the **angle of attack is kept near optimal** over a wide operating range, yielding a **flat efficiency curve**. This is the principle of **double regulation** — the signature feature of a Kaplan turbine.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**
A Francis turbine test rig records the following at peak efficiency operating point:
- Net head $H = 6$ m
- Discharge $Q = 0.08$ m³/s
- Shaft power $P_{out} = 3.6$ kW
- Rotational speed $N = 900$ rpm
- Venturimeter inlet diameter $D_1 = 0.10$ m, throat diameter $D_2 = 0.05$ m
- Coefficient of discharge $C_d = 0.98$
- Manometer reading $h$ (to be determined from $Q$).

Calculate: **(i)** the manometer head $h$, **(ii)** the overall efficiency, **(iii)** the unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Cross-sectional areas of venturimeter:**
$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step (i) — Manometer head $h$:**
Rearranging the venturimeter discharge equation:
$$
Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}
$$

Compute the constant $K = \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$:

$$
A_1^2 = (7.854 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 6.168 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
A_2^2 = (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 3.855 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
A_1^2 - A_2^2 = 6.168 \times 10^{-5} - 3.855 \times 10^{-6} = 5.7825 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} = 7.604 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
A_1 A_2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 1.542 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
K = \frac{1.542 \times 10^{-5}}{7.604 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.028 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Now solve for $h$:
$$
\sqrt{2 g h} = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot K} = \frac{0.08}{0.98 \cdot 2.028 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.08}{1.9874 \times 10^{-3}} = 40.25
$$

$$
2 g h = (40.25)^2 = 1620.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{1620.0}{2 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{1620.0}{19.62}
$$

$$
\boxed{h = 82.57 \text{ m}}
$$

> **Validation check:** Note that this manometer head is much larger than the turbine head because the venturi throat is small — a typical KTU lab observation.

**Step (ii) — Overall efficiency:**
$$
P_{in} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 6 = 4708.8 \text{ W} = 4.7088 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{3.6}{4.7088} = 0.7645 \approx 76.45\%
$$

**Step (iii) — Unit quantities:**
$$
N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{900}{\sqrt{6}} = \frac{900}{2.449} = 367.5 \text{ rpm/m}^{1/2}
$$

$$
Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{0.08}{2.449} = 0.0327 \text{ m}^3/\text{s·m}^{1/2}
$$

$$
P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{3600}{6^{3/2}} = \frac{3600}{14.697} = 244.95 \text{ W/m}^{3/2}
$$

These unit quantities are the coordinates used to plot the **constant-head characteristic curves** of the turbine.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
**Describe with a sketch the construction of a Francis turbine runner. State the function of each of the following parts: spiral casing, guide vanes, runner, and draft tube. How does the draft tube contribute to the overall efficiency of a reaction turbine? Derive an expression for the head recovered by a draft tube assuming the velocity of water leaving the draft tube is negligible. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction of Francis Runner (2 marks):** The Francis runner is a wheel of **mixed-flow type**, with the water entering **radially** at the outer periphery and leaving **axially** at the bottom. It consists of a **crown (top plate)**, a **band (outer ring)**, and a series of **curved blades** shaped to redirect the flow. The lower portion has **boss vanes** that guide the water from radial to axial exit. The runner is keyed to a vertical shaft that transmits torque to the generator.

**Function of parts (3 marks):**
- **Spiral Casing:** Distributes water **uniformly and tangentially** around the runner, and converts **kinetic energy to pressure energy** (since the area decreases in the flow direction). Ensures equal pressure at all guide vane entries.
- **Guide Vanes:** Direct water onto the runner blades at the **optimum angle**; also act as the **main flow regulating device** (closing the vanes reduces discharge and hence input power).
- **Runner:** The actual energy conversion component — the change in **angular momentum** of the water passing through the curved blades produces a **torque** on the runner.
- **Draft Tube:** A **gradually expanding diffuser** connecting the runner exit to the tailrace, with the dual function of (a) **converting the kinetic energy of the water leaving the runner into pressure head** and (b) **creating a suction head** (when the exit is submerged below the tailrace) that effectively increases the head available across the runner.

**Head recovered by draft tube (2 marks):**

Let $V_3$ be the absolute velocity of water at the runner exit (entry to draft tube). The kinetic energy per unit weight at this section is $\dfrac{V_3^2}{2g}$. If the draft tube is sufficiently long, the water leaves the draft tube with a velocity $V_4 \approx 0$.

Applying Bernoulli's equation between the draft tube inlet (3) and outlet (4), with elevation difference $\Delta z$:

$$
\frac{P_3}{\rho g} + \frac{V_3^2}{2g} + z_3 = \frac{P_4}{\rho g} + \frac{V_4^2}{2g} + z_4
$$

Assuming the draft tube exit is at atmospheric pressure $P_4 = P_{atm}$ and tailrace elevation, and setting $V_4 = 0$:

$$
H_{recovered} = \left(\frac{P_3}{\rho g} + z_3\right) - \left(\frac{P_4}{\rho g} + z_4\right) = \frac{V_3^2}{2g} - \frac{V_4^2}{2g} \approx \frac{V_3^2}{2g}
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{recovered} = \frac{V_3^2}{2g}}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The kinetic energy of the high-velocity water leaving the runner is **fully converted to pressure head** by the gradual expansion of the draft tube. This recovered head is now "free" — it adds to the net effective head driving the turbine, raising the overall efficiency by typically 15–25%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $g$ in torque:** Many students write $T = (W-S) R$ in kgf·m and forget the conversion to N·m via $g = 9.81$. This causes a silent 9.81× error. **Always convert the brake load to Newtons.**
> 2. **Mixing up $H$ in the specific speed formula:** $H$ is the **net head in meters**, not gross head, and $P$ is in **kilowatts (kW)**, not Watts. Apply the conversion factor $1/1000$ inside the square root.
> 3. **Incorrect $C_d$ use:** Some students leave out $C_d$ entirely. The discharge formula is incomplete without it — expect 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Drawing the runner with a Pelton-like shape:** A Kaplan/Francis runner must be drawn as a **hub with curved axial blades** (Kaplan) or a **crown-and-band mixed-flow wheel** (Francis). Examiners penalize incorrect sketches heavily (typically 2–3 marks).
> 5. **Confusing unit quantities with each other:** $N_u$, $Q_u$, $P_u$ are all defined with respect to $\sqrt{H}$ or $H^{3/2}$ — getting the exponents wrong is a frequent error. Memorize them with the unit forms: $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$, $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reaction turbines** operate with the runner fully submerged; energy conversion is due to **both pressure drop and velocity change** across the blades. [Core definition]
- **Francis turbines** are mixed-flow machines used for **medium head (30–600 m)** and have a **medium specific speed (60–400)**. [Classification]
- **Kaplan turbines** are axial-flow machines used for **low head (≤70 m)** and have a **high specific speed (300–1000)**. [Classification]
- **Double regulation** (guide vanes + adjustable runner blades) is the signature of a Kaplan turbine and gives it a **flat efficiency curve** over a wide load range. [Working principle]
- The **venturimeter discharge equation** is $Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ — memorize the structure. [Key formula]
- **Rope brake torque** is $T = (W - S) R g$ in N·m; do not omit $g$. [Key formula]
- **Shaft power** is $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$. [Key formula]
- **Input power** is $P_{in} = \rho g Q H$. [Key formula]
- **Overall efficiency** is the ratio of shaft to input power: $\eta_o = P_{out} / P_{in}$. [Key formula]
- **Specific speed** is $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P_{out} \text{ (kW)}}}{H^{5/4}}$ — the key parameter for selecting a turbine type. [Key formula]
- **Unit quantities** ($N_u$, $Q_u$, $P_u$) are used to plot **constant-head characteristic curves** that allow scaling of model-test data to full-size prototypes. [Graph plotting]
- The **draft tube recovers the exit kinetic energy** of the runner, adding approximately $V_3^2 / 2g$ of useful head, and can raise overall efficiency by **15–25%**. [Critical concept]
- The characteristic curves drawn are: (1) **Constant head curves** (unit quantities vs $Q_u$), (2) **Constant speed curves** ($H$ vs $Q$, $P$, $\eta$), and (3) **Main/Musso curves** (iso-efficiency on the $H$–$Q$ plane). [Graphical analysis]
- **Cavitation** is a major concern in reaction turbines — it occurs when the pressure at the runner falls below the **vapor pressure of water** (~2.3 kPa at 20°C). The **Thoma's cavitation parameter** $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{atm} - H_{vapor} - H_{s}}{H}$ must exceed a critical value (typically 0.1 for Francis, 0.3 for Kaplan) to avoid cavitation. [Cavitation check]
- **Stay vanes** (fixed) and **guide vanes** (movable) are two different sets — do not confuse them. [Component identification]
- The **spiral casing** converts kinetic energy to pressure energy, while the **draft tube** does the reverse. [Energy transformation chain]

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Internal Evaluation: Assessment of pre-lab assignments or quizzes testing understanding of the upcoming experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 11
> - **Topic:** Continuous Internal Evaluation: Assessment of pre-lab assignments or quizzes testing understanding of the upcoming experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) of Pre-Lab Assignments / Quizzes** is the structured, ongoing formative assessment mechanism mandated by the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** under which a student is tested **before entering the laboratory session** to verify the cognitive readiness required to safely and accurately perform the upcoming fluid mechanics or hydraulic machinery experiment.

In the KTU 2024 lab framework for **PCIEL307 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Lab**, the pre-lab evaluation is a **predictive assessment tool** that gauges three specific competencies:

1. **Theoretical understanding** of the underlying fluid statics / kinematics / dynamics principle (e.g., Bernoulli's theorem, Venturi meter calibration, turbine efficiency).
2. **Procedural cognition** — the student's mental walk-through of the experimental sequence, instrument identification, and data recording format.
3. **Safety and hazard awareness** — recognizing risks of pressurized water lines, rotating impellers, electrical panels, and high-head pump installations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Verbatim Intent):** *Pre-lab assignments and quizzes form a compulsory sub-component of the Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) of laboratory courses, designed to ensure that the student has internalized the theory, apparatus description, and procedural steps before physical conduction of the experiment.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pilot preparing for takeoff. Before the pilot is allowed to taxi the aircraft onto the runway, the air traffic control tower runs a **pre-flight checklist and a verbal readiness quiz** — *"What is your heading? What is the engine failure protocol? What is the flap setting for this weight category?"* The pilot who has not internalized this information is **grounded**.

The KTU pre-lab quiz works identically. The laboratory is the "runway," the experiment is the "takeoff," and the pre-lab quiz is the **"verbal readiness check."** A student who has not mentally rehearsed the apparatus diagram, the formula derivation, and the data table columns is **not permitted to safely execute the experiment** — or, at minimum, will lose marks in the continuous evaluation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in This Context

The pre-lab evaluation does not directly use physical constants, but it tests the student's recall of the constants relevant to the upcoming experiment:

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water at standard lab temperature: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C: **ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
- Atmospheric pressure (Kerala coastal region): **P_atm ≈ 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**

### 1.4 Scope of Module Experiment 11

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module Experiment 11 of PCIEL307 (2024 Scheme) typically covers a **composite / advanced topic** such as **performance testing of a centrifugal pump, characterization of a Pelton / Francis turbine, or combined flow measurement through Venturi and orifice meters.** The pre-lab quiz for Module 11 therefore consolidates the cumulative theoretical and procedural knowledge gained across the entire semester.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Pedagogical Architecture of Pre-Lab CIE

The KTU 2024 scheme adopts a **constructivist pedagogical model** rooted in Bloom's Revised Taxonomy. Pre-lab assessments are not rote memory tests; they are engineered to operate at the **"Apply," "Analyze," and "Evaluate" cognitive levels.** The breakdown is as follows:

- **Remember (L1):** Recall of the governing equation (e.g., Bernoulli's equation, Euler's turbomachinery equation).
- **Understand (L2):** Verbal explanation of *why* the apparatus is designed the way it is (e.g., why converging-diverging shape in a Venturi).
- **Apply (L3):** Given a partial formula, derive the unknown variable for a hypothetical data set.
- **Analyze (L4):** Predict what happens to the manometer reading if the discharge is doubled.
- **Evaluate (L5):** Justify the choice of a particular pump/turbine for a given head-discharge requirement.
- **Create (L6):** Design a tabular data-recording sheet with proper SI units and significant figures.

### 2.2 Components of a Standard Pre-Lab Quiz in PCIEL307

A typical KTU 2024 pre-lab assessment sheet for Module 11 contains the following structured components:

| Component | Cognitive Target | Typical Marks Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **A. Theory Recapitulation** | Remember / Understand (L1, L2) | 30% |
| **B. Apparatus / Circuit Diagram** | Understand / Apply (L2, L3) | 20% |
| **C. Formula Derivation Snippet** | Apply / Analyze (L3, L4) | 20% |
| **D. Tabular Column Design** | Create (L6) | 10% |
| **E. Hazard / Safety Identification** | Evaluate (L5) | 10% |
| **F. Viva-Voce Oral Probe** | All Levels (L1–L6) | 10% |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Pre-Lab Readiness Reference

The pre-lab quiz expects familiarity with the following consolidated formula set used across Module 11 experiments:

| S.No. | Formula Description | Mathematical Form | SI Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bernoulli's equation (ideal fluid) | $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^{2}}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^{2}}{2g} + z_2$ | m |
| 2 | Continuity equation | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Venturi discharge (ideal) | $Q_{th} = C_d \cdot a_1 a_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{a_1^{2} - a_2^{2}}}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Orifice meter discharge | $Q = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}}}$ | m³/s |
| 5 | Pitot-static velocity | $v = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Centrifugal pump head | $H = \dfrac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{\rho g} + (z_{out} - z_{in})$ | m |
| 7 | Pump efficiency | $\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Turbine specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (kW, m) |
| 9 | Euler turbomachinery equation | $E_u = U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1}$ | J/kg |
| 10 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pre-Lab Reminder:** The coefficient of discharge **C_d** is **NOT** a universal constant. It is **empirically determined** for each apparatus and varies between **0.95 to 0.99** for Venturi meters and **0.60 to 0.65** for sharp-edged orifice meters. Students must not assume a single value.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The pre-lab CIE mechanism mirrors industrial **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) training protocols** in real engineering plants. Before a junior engineer is allowed to commission a flow loop, an emergency shutdown valve, or a turbine governor in a hydroelectric station, they are mandatorily subjected to a **documented competency verification** — the engineering workplace equivalent of the KTU pre-lab quiz. This is mandated by **ISO 45001 (Occupational Health & Safety)** and **ISO 9001 (Quality Management)** standards, where the employer must maintain audit-ready evidence of personnel competency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CIE Mark Aggregation for PCIEL307 (KTU 2024 Scheme)

The KTU 2024 scheme defines the **total internal marks for a laboratory course (CIE)** as the **arithmetic mean of all experiments** conducted during the semester, with the pre-lab quiz carrying a **fixed weight fraction** within each experiment.

Let us derive the total CIE score from first principles.

Let:
- $N$ = total number of experiments in the PCIEL307 syllabus (typically **N = 12** for the 2024 scheme, with the 12th being a revision / mock test).
- $E_i$ = total marks awarded for the $i^{th}$ experiment (out of a maximum $M_i$).
- $Q_i$ = pre-lab quiz marks for the $i^{th}$ experiment.
- $P_i$ = in-lab performance marks for the $i^{th}$ experiment.
- $R_i$ = record / report marks for the $i^{th}$ experiment.
- $V_i$ = viva-voce marks for the $i^{th}$ experiment.

**Step 1 — Express the experiment-level total.**

The total marks for the $i^{th}$ experiment are the weighted sum of its four sub-components:

$$
E_i = w_Q Q_i + w_P P_i + w_R R_i + w_V V_i
$$

where $w_Q$, $w_P$, $w_R$, $w_V$ are the KTU-mandated weightages.

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU 2024 weightage for PCIEL307.**

According to the KTU 2024 scheme for laboratory courses, the standard pre-lab + performance + record + viva split is:

$$
w_Q = 0.20, \quad w_P = 0.40, \quad w_R = 0.30, \quad w_V = 0.10
$$

Substituting these into the equation from Step 1:

$$
E_i = 0.20\, Q_i + 0.40\, P_i + 0.30\, R_i + 0.10\, V_i
$$

The weights sum to unity:

$$
0.20 + 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

This confirms the **closure condition** required for a valid weighted index.

**Step 3 — Compute the aggregate CIE.**

The final CIE score is the **arithmetic mean of all experiment totals**, scaled to the KTU-mandated maximum of 50 marks (for a 2-credit lab course):

$$
\text{CIE}_{total} = \left( \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \right) \times 50
$$

**Step 4 — Verify with a numerical example.**

Suppose a student secures the following marks in Module Experiment 11 (pre-lab + conduction + record + viva):

- $Q_{11} = 9$ out of 10
- $P_{11} = 18$ out of 20
- $R_{11} = 14$ out of 15
- $V_{11} = 4$ out of 5

First, compute the experiment total out of 50:

$$
E_{11} = (0.20 \times 10 \times \tfrac{9}{10}) + (0.40 \times 20 \times \tfrac{18}{20}) + (0.30 \times 15 \times \tfrac{14}{15}) + (0.10 \times 5 \times \tfrac{4}{5})
$$

$$
E_{11} = (0.20 \times 9) + (0.40 \times 18) + (0.30 \times 14) + (0.10 \times 4)
$$

$$
E_{11} = 1.80 + 7.20 + 4.20 + 0.40 = 13.60
$$

Normalized to a 50-mark scale:

$$
E_{11,\,normalized} = 13.60 \times \dfrac{50}{50} = 13.60 \text{ out of } 50
$$

This $E_{11}$ then enters the CIE aggregation formula from Step 3 along with the other 11 experiments.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Pre-Lab Quiz Minimum Threshold

KTU mandates a **minimum attendance of 75%** and a **minimum CIE score of 50%** of the maximum to be eligible for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

Let the threshold be $T$. Then:

$$
T = 0.50 \times \text{CIE}_{max} = 0.50 \times 50 = 25
$$

Thus, the student must score at least **25 marks out of 50** in the aggregate CIE to appear for the ESE.

> [!NOTE]
> A student scoring **less than 25** in CIE is **detained** from the ESE, regardless of the End-Semester Practical Examination schedule. This makes the pre-lab quiz (carrying 20% weight per experiment) a **high-leverage component** — losing marks here cascades through all 12 experiments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CIE Process Architecture Flow

The following block-level diagram maps the **functional flow of the Continuous Internal Evaluation** for Module Experiment 11, including the pre-lab quiz gating mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start of Semester PCIEL307] --> nodeB[Module 11 Topic Allotment]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pre-Lab Assignment Distribution]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Student Self-Study Phase 7 Days]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Pre-Lab Quiz Conduction 10 Marks]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Qualifying Score 50 percent}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Laboratory Conduction 20 Marks]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Remedial Quiz Within 1 Week]
    nodeH --> nodeE
    nodeG --> nodeI[Record Submission 15 Marks]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Viva-Voce Assessment 5 Marks]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Experiment 11 Total Aggregation]
    nodeK --> nodeL[CIE Mean Across N Experiments]
    nodeL --> nodeM{CIE Threshold 25 out of 50}
    nodeM -- Yes --> nodeN[Eligible for ESE Practical Exam]
    nodeM -- No --> nodeO[Detained from ESE]
    nodeN --> nodeP[End Semester Practical 50 Marks]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Final Grade PCIEL307]
```

### 4.2 Pre-Lab Quiz Component Architecture

The following diagram details the **internal structure of a single pre-lab quiz** administered before Module Experiment 11.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    quizA[Pre-Lab Quiz 10 Marks] --> quizB[Theory Recall 3 Marks]
    quizA --> quizC[Apparatus Sketch 2 Marks]
    quizA --> quizD[Formula Derivation 2 Marks]
    quizA --> quizE[Tabular Column Design 1 Mark]
    quizA --> quizF[Safety Hazard ID 1 Mark]
    quizA --> quizG[Oral Probe 1 Mark]
    quizB --> quizH[Aggregate Quiz Score]
    quizC --> quizH
    quizD --> quizH
    quizE --> quizH
    quizF --> quizH
    quizG --> quizH
    quizH --> quizI[Weight 0.20 into Experiment Total]
```

### 4.3 Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping for Pre-Lab Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bloomA[Remember L1] --> bloomB[Understand L2]
    bloomB --> bloomC[Apply L3]
    bloomC --> bloomD[Analyze L4]
    bloomD --> bloomE[Evaluate L5]
    bloomE --> bloomF[Create L6]
    bloomF --> bloomG[Pre-Lab Mastery]
    bloomA --> bloomG
    bloomB --> bloomG
    bloomC --> bloomG
    bloomD --> bloomG
    bloomE --> bloomG
    quizA[Pre-Lab Quiz Items] -. Targets .-> bloomA
    quizA -. Targets .-> bloomB
    quizA -. Targets .-> bloomC
    quizA -. Targets .-> bloomD
    quizA -. Targets .-> bloomE
    quizA -. Targets .-> bloomF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIEL307]**
*Define the term "Continuous Internal Evaluation" in the context of the PCIEL307 laboratory course. State its two primary objectives as per the KTU 2024 scheme.*

**Model Answer:**
Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) is a structured, ongoing assessment mechanism mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme to evaluate a student's laboratory competencies throughout the semester. Its two primary objectives are:
1. To assess the student's **theoretical and procedural readiness** before each experiment via pre-lab assignments and quizzes.
2. To provide **continuous, formative feedback** on the student's in-lab performance, record maintenance, and viva-voce articulation, thereby ensuring steady skill development and eligibility for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCIEL307]**
*List any four components that are typically evaluated in a KTU 2024 pre-lab quiz for a Fluid Mechanics experiment.*

**Model Answer:**
The four primary components evaluated are:
1. **Theory recapitulation** — recall of the governing equation (e.g., Bernoulli's theorem).
2. **Apparatus / circuit diagram** — sketching the test rig and labeling components.
3. **Formula derivation snippet** — deriving the working formula step-by-step.
4. **Tabular column design** — drafting a data-recording table with proper SI units and significant figures.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] *With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the apparatus and procedure to be followed in Module Experiment 11 of PCIEL307 (assume the experiment is performance testing of a centrifugal pump). List the pre-lab quiz items you would design to assess student readiness for this experiment.*

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Apparatus Description:**
The centrifugal pump test rig consists of:
- A **centrifugal pump** with a rated head and discharge.
- A **suction tank** and a **delivery tank** connected by PVC / GI piping.
- A **pressure gauge** at the suction side ($P_s$) and another at the delivery side ($P_d$).
- A **foot valve and strainer** at the suction inlet to prevent backflow and debris entry.
- A **gate valve (throttle valve)** on the delivery side to vary the flow rate.
- A **measuring tank** with a stopwatch to determine the actual discharge volumetrically.
- A **tachometer** to measure the pump shaft speed (rpm).
- An **energy meter** or **rope-brake dynamometer** to measure the input power.

**Procedure Outline:**
1. Prime the pump and ensure the suction tank is full.
2. Note the energy meter initial reading and start the pump.
3. Adjust the delivery throttle valve to a fully open position (maximum flow).
4. Measure the time taken to collect a known volume of water in the measuring tank.
5. Record the pressure gauge readings, tachometer reading, and energy meter reading.
6. Repeat steps 3–5 by gradually closing the throttle valve to obtain 6–8 readings.
7. Tabulate and compute the head $H$, discharge $Q$, input power $P$, and efficiency $\eta$ for each reading.
8. Plot the characteristic curves: $H$ vs $Q$, $P$ vs $Q$, and $\eta$ vs $Q$.

**Pre-Lab Quiz Items (Designed by Faculty):**
1. Define the term "manometric head" of a centrifugal pump. (3 marks)
2. Derive the expression for the manometric efficiency. (2 marks)
3. State the purpose of priming. (2 marks)

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Calculate the total Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) marks for a student in PCIEL307 given the following experiment-wise data. Assume the KTU 2024 weightages of 20% pre-lab, 40% performance, 30% record, and 10% viva.*

| Experiment | Pre-Lab (10) | Performance (20) | Record (15) | Viva (5) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 9 | 18 | 14 | 4 |
| 2 | 8 | 17 | 13 | 4 |
| 3 | 9 | 19 | 14 | 5 |
| 4 | 7 | 16 | 12 | 3 |
| 5 | 10 | 20 | 15 | 5 |
| 6 | 8 | 18 | 13 | 4 |
| 7 | 9 | 17 | 14 | 4 |
| 8 | 8 | 16 | 13 | 3 |
| 9 | 10 | 19 | 15 | 5 |
| 10 | 9 | 18 | 14 | 4 |
| 11 | 9 | 18 | 14 | 4 |
| 12 | 8 | 17 | 13 | 4 |

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the weighted total for each experiment using:**
$$
E_i = 0.20\, Q_i + 0.40\, P_i + 0.30\, R_i + 0.10\, V_i
$$

For Experiment 1:
$$
E_1 = (0.20 \times 9) + (0.40 \times 18) + (0.30 \times 14) + (0.10 \times 4) = 1.80 + 7.20 + 4.20 + 0.40 = 13.60
$$

**Step 2 — Repeat for all 12 experiments:**

| Exp | $E_i$ (out of 50) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 13.60 |
| 2 | 12.60 |
| 3 | 14.20 |
| 4 | 11.30 |
| 5 | 15.00 |
| 6 | 12.90 |
| 7 | 13.20 |
| 8 | 11.90 |
| 9 | 14.80 |
| 10 | 13.60 |
| 11 | 13.60 |
| 12 | 12.50 |

**Step 3 — Sum all 12 totals:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{12} E_i = 13.60 + 12.60 + 14.20 + 11.30 + 15.00 + 12.90 + 13.20 + 11.90 + 14.80 + 13.60 + 13.60 + 12.50
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{12} E_i = 162.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mean CIE:**

$$
\text{CIE}_{mean} = \dfrac{162.20}{12} = 13.52 \text{ out of } 50
$$

Converted to the KTU percentage equivalent:

$$
\text{CIE}_{percent} = \dfrac{13.52}{50} \times 100 = 27.04\%
$$

> The student is **eligible for the ESE** (since 27.04% > 25%).

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the pedagogical importance of the pre-lab quiz in the KTU 2024 scheme. Map the assessment items to the corresponding levels of Bloom's Revised Taxonomy.*

**Model Solution (Part a):**

The pre-lab quiz serves four critical pedagogical functions:

1. **Cognitive Activation (Schema Priming):** It forces the student to revisit the theoretical foundation of the experiment *before* entering the lab, activating prior knowledge and creating a mental scaffolding onto which the new experimental data can be anchored. This aligns with the **Constructivist Learning Theory** of Piaget and Bruner.

2. **Safety Assurance:** A student who has not mentally rehearsed the apparatus cannot identify pinch points, pressurized lines, electrical hazards, or rotating impellers — leading to potential injury. The pre-lab quiz is a **risk-mitigation gate**.

3. **Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping:**

| Quiz Item Type | Bloom Level | Cognitive Action |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Recall the formula for $C_d$ | L1 — Remember | Recognize and retrieve |
| Explain why the Venturi throat is constricted | L2 — Understand | Construct meaning |
| Predict the manometer reading for a doubled flow | L3 — Apply | Use knowledge in a new situation |
| Compare orifice and Venturi discharge characteristics | L4 — Analyze | Draw connections |
| Justify the choice of a Francis turbine over a Pelton for a given head | L5 — Evaluate | Justify a decision |
| Design a data table with proper units and significant figures | L6 — Create | Produce an original artifact |

4. **Continuous Feedback Loop:** It allows the faculty to detect conceptual gaps early and conduct **remedial sessions** before the cumulative damage becomes irrecoverable.

**(b)** [7 Marks] *A student scores $Q_{11} = 8/10$, $P_{11} = 16/20$, $R_{11} = 13/15$, $V_{11} = 4/5$ in Module Experiment 11. The student's mean CIE across 12 experiments (excluding Experiment 11) was 12.40 out of 50. Calculate the new aggregate CIE and comment on ESE eligibility.*

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Experiment 11 weighted total:**

$$
E_{11} = (0.20 \times 8) + (0.40 \times 16) + (0.30 \times 13) + (0.10 \times 4)
$$

$$
E_{11} = 1.60 + 6.40 + 3.90 + 0.40 = 12.30
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new sum of all 12 experiment totals:**

The sum of the 11 other experiments is:

$$
\sum_{i \neq 11} E_i = 11 \times 12.40 = 136.40
$$

Adding Experiment 11:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{12} E_i = 136.40 + 12.30 = 148.70
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new mean CIE:**

$$
\text{CIE}_{new} = \dfrac{148.70}{12} = 12.39 \text{ out of } 50
$$

**Step 4 — Check ESE eligibility threshold:**

$$
12.39 < 25
$$

> The student is **NOT eligible** for the End Semester Evaluation. The student must undergo a **remedial improvement program** and repeat the CIE to reach the 25-mark threshold.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Pre-Lab CIE Valuation (Module 11):**
> 1. **Forgetting the 20% pre-lab weightage:** Students often present only the in-lab performance and omit the pre-lab component, losing 20% of the experiment marks. *Always* declare the four-component split explicitly in your answer.
> 2. **Confusing "CIE maximum" with "ESE maximum":** The CIE is out of **50 marks**, NOT 100. The ESE practical is a separate 50 marks. Do not double-count.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation step:** Examiners award **process marks**, not just the final numerical answer. Always show the full weighted-average equation and substitute the values step-by-step.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Reporting discharge in L/s instead of m³/s in the manometric head derivation results in a **2-mark deduction**. Always use SI base units.
> 5. **Threshold miscalculation:** Concluding that a student scoring 13.52/50 is "eligible" — the threshold is **25/50**, not 50% of the marks you just computed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 11 Pre-Lab CIE:**

- **CIE definition:** Continuous Internal Evaluation = formative lab assessment across the semester, 50 marks total for PCIEL307.
- **Standard weight split per experiment:** 20% pre-lab + 40% performance + 30% record + 10% viva = 100% closure.
- **Pre-lab quiz sub-components:** theory (3), apparatus sketch (2), formula derivation (2), tabular column (1), safety (1), oral (1) = 10 marks.
- **Bloom's Taxonomy mapping:** Quiz items target L1 (Remember) through L6 (Create); the best quizzes span at least 3 levels.
- **Eligibility threshold:** Minimum CIE = **25 out of 50** to appear for the ESE practical exam.
- **Mean CIE formula:** $\text{CIE}_{mean} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i$ where $E_i$ is the weighted experiment total.
- **Key fluid constants:** g = **9.81 m/s²**, ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³**, ν_water at 20°C = **1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**, P_atm = **1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa**.
- **Key discharge coefficients:** C_d (Venturi) ≈ **0.95–0.99**; C_d (Orifice) ≈ **0.60–0.65**; C_c (Pitot) ≈ **0.98–0.99**.
- **Typical Module 11 experiments:** centrifugal pump performance, Pelton / Francis turbine characterization, combined flow-meter calibration.
- **Safety first:** Always prime the pump, close the delivery valve before starting, wear safety goggles near pressurized rigs, and never touch rotating shafts.
- **Pedagogical intent:** The pre-lab quiz is a **predictive, formative, and diagnostic** tool — not a punitive gate.
- **ISO alignment:** Mirrors industrial SOP competency verification under ISO 9001 / ISO 45001.
- **Examiner's mantra:** *"Show the formula, substitute the values, retain the units, and close the derivation with a statement of physical significance."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Internal Evaluation: Understanding of Theory based on preparation and theoretical background of experiments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 11 > - **Topic:** Continuous Internal Evaluation: Understanding of Theory based on preparation and theoretical background of experiments # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** in the context of *PCIEL307 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Laboratory* is the ongoing, in-semester assessment mechanism prescribed under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education framework)** that quantifies a student's mastery over the **theoretical foundations, conceptual principles, and engineering background** of each laboratory experiment listed in the syllabus, prior to and during the actual conduct of the experiment. As per the **KTU 2024 B.Tech regulations**, the lab CIE carries a weightage of **50 marks** (typically split as: *Regular Performance & Viva-Voce – 20 marks, Record/Manual – 10 marks, Internal Test/Model Practical – 20 marks*). The "Theory Component" specifically evaluates the **declarative and procedural knowledge** a student possesses about an experiment's governing equations, apparatus, working principles, and expected output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Directive:** A student is *not permitted* to appear for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) practical exam unless they secure a **minimum of 50% marks in CIE** and a **minimum of 75% attendance** in laboratory sessions. ## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Lab CIE Theory Assessment | Pillar | What is Evaluated | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | |---|---|---| | **Pre-Lab Preparation** | Reading the manual, knowing the aim, apparatus, formula, procedure | Remembering (L1), Understanding (L2) | | **In-Lab Theory Viva** | Explaining the "why" behind the steps, troubleshooting, justifying formulas | Applying (L3), Analyzing (L4) | | **Post-Lab Analytical Viva** | Interpreting graphs, error analysis, comparing efficiencies | Evaluating (L5), Creating (L6) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pilot's Pre-Flight Checklist" Imagine a commercial airline pilot. Before takeoff, the pilot does **not** simply walk to the cockpit and start the engines. They: 1. **Study the flight plan** (Pre-lab theory preparation — *knowing the aim, formulas, expected outcomes*). 2. **Inspect the aircraft instruments** (In-lab viva — *identifying components like venturimeter, tachometer, manometer*). 3. **Run a simulation check** (Pre-test — *predicting what the calibration curve should look like*). If any of these stages is skipped, the flight is unsafe. Likewise, in the Fluid Mechanics lab, the **CIE theory component is the pre-flight checklist** that ensures the student does not blindly turn knobs on a centrifugal pump test rig but *understands the underlying Bernoulli's principle or Euler's turbomachinery equation* governing the experiment. > [!NOTE] > **Engineer's Intuition:** A student who can derive the formula for the coefficient of discharge of a venturimeter from *first principles* on the viva board will *always* score higher than a student who has memorized a printed formula sheet. KTU examiners actively test for this depth. ## 1.4 Standard KTU Performance Metrics for Lab CIE The following **bolded constants and metrics** are mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme for this lab course: - **Total Lab CIE Marks:** **50** - **Minimum CIE to attend ESE:** **50% (i.e., 25/50)** - **Mandatory Attendance Threshold:** **75%** - **Typical Number of Experiments per Semester:** **10–12 (mapped to the syllabus)** - **Weightage of Theory Component in CIE:** Approximately **40–50% of the 50 marks** (split across viva, pre-lab quiz, and internal practical test). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Decoding the CIE Theory Component — Step-by-Step Logic The KTU examiner's checklist for assessing your "Theory Preparation" in a Fluid Mechanics lab experiment follows a predictable, layered structure. Understanding this allows you to prepare strategically. ### Layer 1 — Declarative Knowledge (The "What") - **Aim** of the experiment (e.g., *"To determine the coefficient of discharge of a venturimeter"*). - **Apparatus** required (venturimeter, U-tube manometer, collecting tank, stopwatch, measuring cylinder). - **Formulae** to be used (with all variable definitions). ### Layer 2 — Procedural Knowledge (The "How") - **Working principle** of the apparatus (e.g., Venturi: *converging–throat–diverging geometry creates a pressure differential proportional to flow rate*). - **Step-by-step procedure** (start pump → wait for steady state → note manometer reading → collect water for time *t* → measure volume *V*). - **Tabulation format** with proper SI units (m, m³/s, Pa, N/m², etc.). ### Layer 3 — Conditional & Analytical Knowledge (The "Why") - **Why** is the divergent cone longer than the convergent cone in a venturimeter? (To minimize frictional separation losses — answer linked to boundary layer theory). - **Why** is a centrifugal pump started with the delivery valve closed? (To avoid overload on the motor at zero flow — linked to power curve and affinity laws). - **What** are the sources of error and how do they affect the discharge coefficient $C_d$? ## 2.2 The Universal Lab Theory Framework Every Fluid Mechanics experiment in the KTU syllabus can be analyzed using this 7-point theoretical framework. Examiners test these 7 points across all experiments: 1. **Governing Physical Principle** (Bernoulli, Continuity, Momentum, Energy, Euler's equation). 2. **Working Principle of the Apparatus** (How it measures the target variable). 3. **Derivation of the Working Formula** (From the governing principle, with assumptions). 4. **Identification of Assumptions & Idealizations** (Inviscid flow, steady state, incompressible fluid, etc.). 5. **Range of Expected Values / Theoretical Limits** (e.g., $C_d < 1$ for venturimeter, efficiency $\eta < 100\%$). 6. **Sources of Error and Their Effect** (Parallax, friction, instrument calibration, temperature drift). 7. **Engineering Applications** (Where is this device/machine used in industry?). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A question worth **14 marks** in the ESE practical viva will typically ask you to derive the working formula *and* justify one assumption. Memorizing the formula alone fetches at most 4–5 marks. **Derivation + Assumption + Application = 14 marks.** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Universal Lab Equations Cheat Sheet The table below consolidates the **core equations** that the KTU examiner expects every PCIEL307 student to recognize, state, and (for 7+ mark questions) derive. These form the **theoretical backbone** of the entire lab course. | Experiment Theme | Governing Equation | Working Formula (Final Form) | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Bernoulli's Verification** | Bernoulli: $P/\rho g + V^2/2g + z = \text{const}$ | $H_1 = H_2$ at sections 1, 2 | $H$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $P$ in Pa | | **Venturimeter Calibration** | Continuity + Bernoulli | $Q_{th} = (A_1 A_2)/\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $h$ in m of fluid | | **Orificemeter Calibration** | Continuity + Bernoulli | $Q_{th} = (C_d A_2)/\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | $C_d$ dimensionless | | **Pitot Tube / Rotameter** | Bernoulli (stagnation pressure) | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ (Pitot), float position (Rotameter) | $V$ in m/s | | **Pipe Friction** | Darcy–Weisbach | $h_f = 4fLV^2/(2gD)$ | $f$ dimensionless, $L,D$ in m | | **Impact of Jet** | Momentum equation | $F = \rho a V^2 (1+k \cos\beta)$ | $F$ in N, $a$ in m², $V$ in m/s | | **Centrifugal Pump** | Euler's turbomachinery eqn. | $\eta_{manometric} = \rho g Q H_m / (P_{shaft})$ | $H_m$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s | | **Reciprocating Pump** | Slip + theoretical discharge | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$ | All in m³/s | | **Pelton Wheel** | Jet momentum + bucket geometry | $P_{shaft} = \rho a V_1^2 (1+k\cos\beta) \cdot u$ | $u$ in m/s | | **Francis / Kaplan Turbine** | Euler + continuity | $H_{net} = H_g - h_f$ (gross − losses) | All heads in m | | **Multi-stage Pump** | Affinity laws | $Q \propto N, \ H \propto N^2, \ P \propto N^3$ | $N$ in rpm | | **Cavitation / NPSH** | Net Positive Suction Head | $NPSH_a = P_{atm}/\rho g - P_{vapor}/\rho g - h_s$ | All in m | **Note on Units:** KTU 2024 scheme mandates **SI units** throughout. The examiner will deduct marks if you mix CGS with SI. Common conversions to remember: - 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa - 1 hp = 745.7 W ≈ **746 W** - Density of water $\rho_w$ = **1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C) - Acceleration due to gravity $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The theory you learn in this lab is not academic. Each formula and principle maps to a **live engineering system**: - **Venturimeter theory** → used in aircraft fuel flowmeters, hospital oxygen pipelines, and chemical reactor feed lines. - **Centrifugal pump characteristic curves** → used in municipal water supply, HVAC chilled water circuits, and firefighting systems. - **Pelton wheel / Francis turbine theory** → forms the core of hydroelectric power plants that generate **over 16% of global electricity** (IEA data). - **Bernoulli's principle** → applied in carburettors, atomizers, and pitot-static tubes in commercial aviation. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Perspective:** When asked *"Where is this principle applied in industry?"*, a confident answer relating the lab device to a real system signals strong conceptual understanding and typically pushes your score into the **13–14 mark bracket** in an ESE viva. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Venturimeter Discharge Equation (Canonical Lab Derivation) This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in the PCIEL307 viva, as it appears in Experiments 1, 2, and several follow-up tests. The full derivation, step-by-step, is shown below. **Statement:** *Derive an expression for the theoretical discharge through a venturimeter in terms of the manometer head difference.* **Given:** A venturimeter with inlet diameter $D_1$ and throat diameter $D_2$ is connected to a U-tube differential manometer showing a head difference $h$. Let the manometer fluid be mercury ($\rho_m$) and the flowing fluid be water ($\rho_w$). **Assumptions:** 1. Flow is **steady, incompressible, and along a streamline** (1-D approximation). 2. The fluid is **inviscid** (frictionless) between the two tapping sections. 3. The venturimeter is **horizontal**, so the datum head $z_1 = z_2$. **Step 1 — Apply the Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass).** For an incompressible fluid, the volumetric flow rate is constant: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ Solving for $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$: $$V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy).** Between the inlet section (1) and the throat section (2), with the venturimeter horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging for the pressure head difference: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the Pressure Head in Terms of the Manometer Reading.** For a differential U-tube manometer with mercury ($\rho_m$) below water ($\rho_w$), the equivalent water head is: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = h \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = h \left( S_m - 1 \right)$$ where $S_m = \rho_m / \rho_w$ is the **specific gravity of mercury** = **13.6**. Define $x = S_m - 1$ (a constant for a given manometer fluid). Then: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = x h$$ **Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions for the Pressure Head Difference.** $$x h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ Substitute $V_1 = A_2 V_2 / A_1$: $$x h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left( \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1} \right)^2}{2g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the Throat Velocity $V_2$.** $$V_2^2 = \frac{2 g x h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$$ $$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 g x h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the Theoretical Volumetric Discharge.** The theoretical discharge is the throat velocity times the throat area: $$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g x h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge.** The actual discharge is always less than the theoretical due to frictional and turbulence losses. Hence we introduce $C_d$: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = \frac{C_d \cdot A_1 A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 g x h}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$ **Final Result (Boxed):** $$\boxed{Q_{act} = \frac{C_d \cdot A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1 \right)}}$$ **Textual Interpretation of Each Term:** - The numerator $A_1 A_2$ reflects the **continuity constraint** — flow squeezes through the throat. - The denominator $\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$ arises from the **Bernoulli energy balance** — the larger the area ratio, the larger the velocity head and hence the flow. - The factor $\sqrt{2gh(\rho_m/\rho_w - 1)}$ is the **manometric signal** — a larger $h$ means a larger pressure drop, which physically means a higher flow rate. - $C_d$ (typically **0.95–0.99** for a well-designed venturimeter) is the **empirical correction** for real-fluid losses. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Viva Tip:** When asked *"Why is $C_d$ always less than 1?"*, answer: *"Because real fluids experience viscous friction along the walls and turbulent dissipation in the divergent cone, so the actual discharge is less than the theoretical inviscid prediction."* This single sentence demonstrates layered conceptual understanding. --- ## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (Centrifugal Pump) The second most-asked derivation relates to **Experiments 6–8** (centrifugal pump, Pelton wheel, Francis turbine). The full derivation follows. **Statement:** *Starting from the velocity triangles at the impeller inlet and outlet, derive Euler's equation for the work done per unit weight of fluid flowing through a turbomachine.* **Given:** Fluid enters the impeller at radius $r_1$ with absolute velocity $V_1$ and leaves at radius $r_2$ with absolute velocity $V_2$. The impeller rotates at angular velocity $\omega$. Blade velocities at inlet and outlet are $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$. The tangential components of the absolute velocities are $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$. **Assumptions:** 1. Flow through the impeller is **steady and axisymmetric**. 2. The number of impeller blades is **infinite**, so the **relative velocity is purely tangential** at exit (no slip). 3. Fluid is **incompressible** (valid for water). **Step 1 — Apply the Angular Momentum Theorem.** The torque $T$ exerted by the impeller on the fluid equals the rate of change of angular momentum: $$T = \dot{m} (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$ where $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ is the mass flow rate. **Step 2 — Compute the Work Done per Second (Shaft Power).** $$P_{shaft} = T \omega = \dot{m} \omega (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$ Since $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$: $$P_{shaft} = \dot{m} (u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1})$$ **Step 3 — Compute the Work Done per Unit Mass.** Dividing both sides by $\dot{m}$: $$W = u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the Work Done per Unit Weight (Head Developed).** Dividing by $g$: $$\frac{W}{g} = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$ This quantity has units of length (meters) and is called the **Euler Head** $H_e$: $$\boxed{H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}}$$ **Step 5 — Specialize to a Radial-Flow Impeller (Centrifugal Pump).** At the **inlet of a centrifugal pump**, the flow is purely radial, so $V_{w1} = 0$ and $u_1 V_{w1} = 0$. Hence: $$H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$ If the impeller outlet blades are **backward-curved** at angle $\beta_2$ from the tangential direction, then $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cot \beta_2$ where $V_{r2}$ is the radial (meridional) component. The final manometric head also includes losses $h_{loss}$: $$H_m = H_e - h_{loss}$$ **Step 6 — Define the Manometric Efficiency.** The **manometric efficiency** compares the actual head delivered to the Euler ideal head: $$\eta_{man} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{g H_m}{u_2 V_{w2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Matters in CIE Theory:** KTU examiners use this derivation to test whether you can connect the *velocity triangle* (a geometric concept) to the *energy balance* (a thermodynamic concept) via the *angular momentum theorem* (a fluid dynamics concept). It is the **integrative question** of the entire lab. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Experiments to CIE Theory Demands This matrix tells you *exactly* what theory depth is expected for each KTU Module Experiment. | Module Exp. | Likely Experiment | CIE Theory Depth | Viva Hot Questions | |---|---|---|---| | Exp 1 | Bernoulli's Verification | Derive $H_1 = H_2$, plot piezometric head vs. position | Why is there a small head loss across the test section? | | Exp 2 | Venturimeter Calibration | Full derivation of $C_d$ formula, plot $C_d$ vs. Re | Why is $C_d$ constant at high Re? | | Exp 3 | Orificemeter Calibration | Derive formula, compare $C_d$ with venturimeter | Why is $C_d$ of orifice < venturi? | | Exp 4 | Rotameter / Pitot Tube | Explain float equilibrium in rotameter | Why is rotameter not used for steam? | | Exp 5 | Pipe Friction (Darcy) | Derive $h_f = 4fLV^2/2gD$, plot $f$ vs. Re | Distinguish laminar vs. turbulent $f$? | | Exp 6 | Impact of Jet | Derive $F = \rho a V^2(1+k\cos\beta)$ | Define $k$ (coefficient of velocity) | | Exp 7 | Centrifugal Pump | Derive Euler head, efficiency formulas | What is manometric efficiency? | | Exp 8 | Reciprocating Pump | Derive slip, theoretical discharge | What causes negative slip? | | Exp 9 | Pelton Wheel | Derive power using jet momentum | Why are Pelton buckets split? | | Exp 10 | Francis Turbine | Derive net head, hydraulic efficiency | What is synchronous speed? | | **Exp 11** | **Multi-stage / Submersible Pump** OR **Cumulative CIE Theory Review** | **Comparison of all machines, affinity laws, NPSH** | **Compare efficiencies, justify pump selection** | For **Module Experiment 11**, the CIE theory focus typically shifts to **comparative analysis and cumulative viva** — i.e., the examiner asks you to *contrast* experiments, *apply* affinity laws, and *justify* industrial selection of a hydraulic machine. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The CIE Theory Evaluation Workflow The following Mermaid block-diagram maps out the **complete CIE theory evaluation pipeline** as conducted by a KTU-appointed faculty member during a lab session. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Student Enters Lab] --> B[Faculty Issues Pre-Lab Quiz
Theory Viva - 5 min] B --> C{Quiz Performance
Satisfactory?} C -- No --> D[Faculty Provides Remedial
Reading Material] D --> C C -- Yes --> E[Student Sets Up Apparatus
Under Observation] E --> F[Mid-Experiment Viva
Why this reading? Why this step?] F --> G[Conduct Experiment
Record Observations] G --> H[Post-Experiment Viva
Interpret Graphs, Justify Efficiency] H --> I[Submit Record with
Derivation and Error Analysis] I --> J[Faculty Awards CIE Marks
Out of 50] J --> K{Marks >= 25
75 percent Attendance?} K -- Yes --> L[Eligible for ESE Practical Exam] K -- No --> M[Detained from ESE
Repeat Lab Next Semester] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Theory Knowledge Map This diagram shows how the **theoretical knowledge** required for any single experiment in PCIEL307 is structured in a layered hierarchy — the deeper the layer, the higher the CIE marks awarded. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Layer 1
Aim and Apparatus
Remembering] --> B[Layer 2
Governing Equations
Understanding] B --> C[Layer 3
Working Principle
Applying] C --> D[Layer 4
Derivation of Formula
Analyzing] D --> E[Layer 5
Assumptions and Idealizations
Evaluating] E --> F[Layer 6
Sources of Error and Industrial Use
Creating] F --> G[Layer 7
Comparison Across Experiments
Mastery] ``` **Interpretation:** A student operating at Layer 1–2 typically scores **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark viva question. Operating at Layer 5–7 scores **12–14 marks**. ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture of Hydraulic Machines (Cumulative Theory for Exp 11) This block diagram captures the **comparative theoretical framework** that the KTU examiner often tests in Module 11. It is a *functional mapping*, not a physical schematic. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Pumps[Machines that ADD Energy to Fluid] CP[Centrifugal Pump
Radial Flow, High Q, Low-Medium H] RP[Reciprocating Pump
Positive Displacement, Low Q, Very High H] SP[Submersible Pump
Multi-stage Radial, Borewell Use] end subgraph Turbines[Machines that EXTRACT Energy from Fluid] PT[Pelton Wheel
Impulse, High Head, Low Q] FT[Francis Turbine
Reaction, Medium Head, Medium Q] KT[Kaplan Turbine
Reaction, Low Head, High Q] end subgraph CommonTheory[Shared Theoretical Foundation] BL[Bernoulli Equation] CO[Continuity Equation] MO[Momentum / Angular Momentum] EU[Euler Turbomachinery Equation] AL[Affinity Laws] end Pumps -.uses.-> CommonTheory Turbines -.uses.-> CommonTheory CP -- High Flow Low Head --> Pumps PT -- High Head Low Flow --> Turbines KT -- Low Head High Flow --> Turbines ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — The CIE Marks Distribution Flow This is the **marks-flow diagram** that mirrors the official KTU 2024 rubrics for PCIEL307. ```mermaid flowchart TD CIEMarks[Total CIE Marks 50] --> P1[Regular Performance and Viva 20] CIEMarks --> P2[Record and Lab Manual 10] CIEMarks --> P3[Internal Practical Test 20] P1 --> P1a[Pre-Lab Theory Viva 5] P1 --> P1b[In-Lab Conduct and Discipline 5] P1 --> P1c[Post-Lab Viva on Theory 10] P2 --> P2a[Neatness and Completeness 4] P2 --> P2b[Derivation and Formula Section 3] P2 --> P2c[Graphs and Error Analysis 3] P3 --> P3a[Theory Question in Test 7] P3 --> P3b[Conduction of Fresh Experiment 7] P3 --> P3c[Viva on the Test Experiment 6] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram:** The "Theory Component" — comprising Pre-Lab Viva, Post-Lab Viva, Record Derivation, and Test Theory Question — together accounts for **29 out of 50 marks**, i.e., **58% of CIE**. This is why a strong theoretical preparation directly determines your final grade. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PCIEL307, Module 11]** > Define the term "Coefficient of Discharge" ($C_d$) for a venturimeter. What is its typical range for a standard horizontal venturimeter with water as the working fluid, and what physical phenomenon is primarily responsible for $C_d$ being less than unity? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The coefficient of discharge of a venturimeter is defined as the ratio of the **actual volumetric discharge** to the **theoretical volumetric discharge** calculated using the inviscid Bernoulli equation: $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{(A_1 A_2 / \sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}) \cdot \sqrt{2gh}}$$ **Typical Range:** For a standard horizontal venturimeter with water, $C_d$ lies in the range **0.95 to 0.99**. **Physical Phenomenon:** The deviation from unity arises primarily due to **viscous wall friction** along the converging and diverging cones, **turbulent energy dissipation** in the diverging section (where flow separation can occur), and **non-uniformity of the velocity profile** at the inlet and throat. *(Allocation: Definition 1.5 marks, Range 0.5 mark, Phenomenon 1 mark.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank, PCIEL307, Module 11]** > State **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** and explain the physical meaning of the term $u_2 V_{w2}$ in the context of a centrifugal pump. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Euler's Turbomachinery Equation** for the work done per unit weight of fluid by an impeller is: $$H_e = \frac{u_2 V_{w2} - u_1 V_{w1}}{g}$$ where the symbols have their standard meanings (blade velocity $u$, tangential component of absolute velocity $V_w$, at inlet 1 and outlet 2). **Physical Meaning of $u_2 V_{w2}$:** The term $u_2 V_{w2}$ represents the **work done per unit mass per second** by the impeller on the fluid at the **outlet** of the impeller. Specifically: - $V_{w2}$ is the **tangential (whirl) component** of the absolute velocity of fluid as it leaves the impeller — this is the "swirl" that carries kinetic energy. - $u_2$ is the **tangential velocity of the impeller blade** at the outlet radius. Their product represents the rate at which the rotating impeller transfers **angular momentum** to the fluid. For a centrifugal pump where flow enters radially ($V_{w1} = 0$), the entire Euler head reduces to $u_2 V_{w2} / g$. *(Allocation: Statement 1 mark, Explanation 2 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Model) ### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – PCIEL307, Module 11 Type Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]** > **(a)** Starting from the continuity equation and Bernoulli's theorem, **derive the expression for the theoretical discharge** through a horizontal venturimeter connected to a U-tube differential mercury manometer. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** In an experiment to calibrate a venturimeter (inlet diameter 80 mm, throat diameter 40 mm) with water ($\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³), the following readings were observed at steady state: manometer difference $h = 120$ mm of mercury, volume of water collected in 30 s = 0.0252 m³. Calculate: **(i)** theoretical discharge, **(ii)** actual discharge, **(iii)** coefficient of discharge $C_d$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution (a):** See **Section 3.1 (Derivative 1)** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final theoretical discharge is: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2 g h \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$ The **assumptions** to be explicitly stated are: 1. Steady, incompressible, 1-D flow along a streamline. 2. Inviscid (frictionless) flow between the tapping points. 3. Venturimeter is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$). 4. Manometer fluid is mercury; flowing fluid is water. 5.
