# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The questions below are **modelled precisely** on KTU 2024 Scheme assessment style. The 14-mark questions follow the standard ESE module pattern with an **internal choice** (i.e., the student answers **either** A **or** B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks. This is a **theory-based** module, so viva-style and descriptive questions dominate.

---

## PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**List and briefly explain any THREE parameters used by KTU examiners for the Continuous Internal Evaluation of the Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Lab record.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

The three key CIE parameters for the lab record are:

1. **Accuracy (1 mark):** The degree to which the experimentally obtained result (e.g., coefficient of discharge, efficiency of a pump/turbine) matches the theoretically expected or standard literature value, usually quantified by the percentage error.

2. **Completeness (1 mark):** The presence of all mandatory sections of the record — aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, free-hand sketch, observations, sample calculation, results table, graph, sources of error, precautions, and conclusion.

3. **Data Analysis (1 mark):** The presence and quality of a worked-out sample calculation with units, a final results table, and a properly plotted graph on a graph sheet with slope determination where applicable.

> *(Any three of the eight parameters — Quality, Clarity, Completeness, Accuracy, Documentation, Data Analysis, Conclusions, Timely Submission — may be substituted, each carrying 1 mark for the explanation.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`

**Why is "Timely Submission" included as a separate CIE parameter in the PCIEL307 lab, and what penalty does KTU typically impose for late submission?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Significance (2 marks):** Timely submission is included as a separate CIE parameter to instil **engineering professional discipline** — in real-world plant operations, a delayed report is as bad as a missing report. The student is expected to submit the record **within one week** of conducting the experiment so that the analytical thought process remains fresh, the calculations are unforced, and the data integrity is preserved. It also enables the faculty to provide **early feedback** in the CIE continuous loop, ensuring that mistakes in early experiments do not propagate into later ones.

**Penalty (1 mark):** KTU 2024 Scheme typically prescribes a **$10\%$ mark deduction per week** of delay, capped at a maximum (commonly $30\%$), beyond which the record is treated as "not submitted" and may result in **withheld end-semester results** until the record is completed.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 11]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **eight parameters** used in the Continuous Internal Evaluation of lab records for PCIEL307. For each parameter, state **one observable evidence** that the examiner uses to award or deduct marks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **model "Sources of Error + Precautions + Conclusion"** block for the experiment *"Determination of the coefficient of discharge of a venturimeter."* Justify each entry with a one-line physical reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks (1 mark per parameter, 0.85 marks for the parameter + 0.15 marks for evidence)**

| # | Parameter | Definition | Observable Evidence for Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quality** | Composite standard of physical presentation | Hard-bound register, legible handwriting, no overwriting, neat tables |
| 2 | **Clarity** | Readability of expression | Units in headers, no ambiguous notation, graph axes labelled, paragraphs separated |
| 3 | **Completeness** | Presence of all required sections | All 11 sections present (aim, apparatus, theory, sketch, procedure, observations, calculations, results, graph, error analysis, conclusion) |
| 4 | **Accuracy** | Closeness to theoretical/standard value | Percentage error $< 5\%$ for most experiments; quoted $C_d, \eta, f$ within acceptable range |
| 5 | **Documentation** | Traceability of experiment | Date, faculty signature, apparatus specs, least counts, environmental conditions noted |
| 6 | **Data Analysis** | Mathematical and graphical treatment of data | Sample calculation with units, final results table, graph sheet with best-fit curve and slope |
| 7 | **Conclusions** | Insight and interpretation | Final result quoted with units, comparison with theory, physical interpretation, industrial relevance |
| 8 | **Timely Submission** | Adherence to deadline | Submission within one week of experiment date; entry in lab logbook |

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks (2 marks for sources, 2 marks for precautions, 3 marks for conclusion)**

**Sources of Error (with physical reasoning):**

1. **Air entrainment in the U-tube manometer (0.5 mark):** Air bubbles trapped in the mercury limbs cause fluctuating or non-representative pressure readings, leading to erroneous differential head values.
2. **Parallax error during manometer reading (0.5 mark):** The eye position relative to the mercury meniscus shifts the observed height, introducing reading bias.
3. **Friction losses in the connecting tubes (0.5 mark):** The Bernoulli equation assumes inviscid flow; actual viscous losses between the inlet tap and throat tap are not accounted for in the theoretical model.
4. **Pump pulsations causing unsteady manometer levels (0.5 mark):** Reciprocating or centrifugal pump surges cause the mercury column to oscillate, reducing the precision of the reading.

**Precautions (with physical reasoning):**

1. **Bleed the manometer thoroughly before the experiment (0.5 mark):** Eliminates air bubbles that distort the pressure reading.
2. **Ensure the manometer limbs are vertical (0.5 mark):** Prevents the projection error of the mercury column height.
3. **Run the pump for at least 2-3 minutes before taking readings (0.5 mark):** Establishes steady-state flow, eliminating transient errors.
4. **Take the reading with the eye at the level of the meniscus (0.5 mark):** Removes parallax.

**Conclusion (a paragraph, 3 marks):**

> *"The coefficient of discharge of the given venturimeter was experimentally determined to be $\overline{C_d} = 0.978 \pm 0.005$ over six trials spanning the operating range of the test rig. This value lies within $1.2\%$ of the standard literature value of $0.99$, thereby validating the Bernoulli-based theoretical model $Q = C_d \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$ for steady, incompressible flow. The marginal reduction of $C_d$ from unity is attributed to two physical phenomena: (i) the vena contracta effect and boundary layer separation in the convergent section, and (ii) incomplete pressure recovery due to turbulent dissipation in the divergent cone, both of which are well-documented in classical fluid mechanics. The venturimeter is therefore confirmed as a high-accuracy flow measurement device with industrial applications in clean water distribution networks, chemical process plants, and HVAC systems where high accuracy and low permanent pressure loss are required."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Vague error sources** (e.g., "human error") fetch **zero marks**. Always specify *which instrument* and *which physical phenomenon* caused the error.
> 2. **One-line conclusions** fetch **at most 1 mark** out of 3. The conclusion must have a **numerical result**, a **physical interpretation**, and an **industrial application**.
> 3. **Precautions copied verbatim from the manual** fetch **0.5 marks** instead of the full 0.5 each. Examiners reward **student-rewritten, experiment-specific** precautions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 11]`

**(a)** For the experiment *"Verification of Bernoulli's Theorem using a venturimeter test rig,"* construct a **complete model record** showing the **Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Observation Table, and Sample Calculation**. Assume standard test rig dimensions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of "Data Analysis" and "Graph Plotting"** in the CIE of PCIEL307, with special reference to: (i) the choice of $X$ and $Y$ axes, (ii) the use of best-fit lines, (iii) the slope determination, and (iv) the physical meaning of the slope. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Aim (0.5 mark):** To verify Bernoulli's theorem by measuring the static and total head at various sections of a venturimeter test rig and confirming that the total head remains constant.

**Apparatus (1 mark):** Venturimeter test rig (inlet dia 50 mm, throat dia 25 mm), U-tube mercury manometer, multi-tube piezometer, stopwatch (LC 0.01 s), collecting tank (50 L, LC 1 mm), measuring cylinder.

**Theory (1.5 marks):** Bernoulli's equation for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow along a streamline states:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{constant} = H$$

For a venturimeter, applying continuity $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$, the theoretical discharge is:

$$Q = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

**Procedure (1 mark):** (i) Prime the pump. (ii) Open the control valve to set a flow. (iii) Note piezometer readings at 4-5 sections. (iv) Measure manometer deflection $h_m$. (v) Time the rise in the collecting tank. (vi) Repeat for 5-6 flow rates.

**Observation Table (1.5 marks):**

| Section | Static Head $p/\rho g$ (m) | Velocity Head $v^2/2g$ (m) | Total Head $H$ (m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet | 0.85 | 0.05 | 0.90 |
| Convergent mid | 0.78 | 0.12 | 0.90 |
| Throat | 0.62 | 0.28 | 0.90 |
| Divergent mid | 0.75 | 0.15 | 0.90 |
| Outlet | 0.83 | 0.07 | 0.90 |

**Sample Calculation (1.5 marks):** $v_1 = Q/A_1$, $h = h_m \times 12.6$, and so on (as in Section 3, Step 8 of this note).

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Choice of axes (1.5 marks):** The independent variable (the one the experimenter controls) goes on the **$X$-axis**, and the dependent variable (the one measured as a response) goes on the **$Y$-axis**. For instance, in a venturimeter, $\sqrt{h}$ is on $X$ and $Q$ is on $Y$; in a pump, $Q$ is on $X$ and $H$ and $\eta$ are on $Y$ (since $Q$ is the controllable input).

**(ii) Best-fit line (1.5 marks):** A best-fit line is a **smooth curve** (or straight line) that minimizes the cumulative deviation of the plotted data points. It is **not** a dot-to-dot connection. For linear relations, the **method of least squares** or the **two-point method** is used.

**(iii) Slope determination (2 marks):** The slope is determined by selecting **two well-separated points** on the best-fit line, **not** on the data points. The slope is then:

$$m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

The slope is **written on the graph** with units, next to the slope triangle.

**(iv) Physical meaning of slope (2 marks):** The slope represents the **proportionality constant** between the dependent and independent variables. In a venturimeter plot of $Q$ vs $\sqrt{h}$, the slope equals:

$$m = C_d \cdot \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2g}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$$

Thus, the experimental $C_d$ can be back-calculated from the slope, giving a **graphical determination of $C_d$** that is more accurate than any single-trial calculation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Axes reversed** (putting $Q$ on $X$ and $\sqrt{h}$ on $Y$) loses **1 mark** in part (a) and **0.5 mark** in part (b).
> 2. **Sample calculation without units** fetches **half marks only** — units are non-negotiable.
> 3. **Slope read from data points instead of best-fit line** leads to an erroneous constant and is **penalized 1 mark**.
> 4. **Missing "physical meaning of the slope"** — students often write a numerical value of the slope without stating *what it physically represents*. This is a **2-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.X TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the CIE Lab Report module:**

- The **CIE of PCIEL307** is **50 marks** (typical) and the **lab record is the single biggest component**.
- The **eight CIE parameters** are: **Quality, Clarity, Completeness, Accuracy, Documentation, Data Analysis, Conclusions, Timely Submission**.
- Group them as: **Process Discipline** (timely submission, documentation) + **Technical Correctness** (accuracy, data analysis) + **Communication & Insight** (quality, clarity, completeness, conclusions).
- The **mandatory 11 sections** of any PCIEL307 record are: Aim → Apparatus → Theory → Sketch → Procedure → Observations → Calculations → Results Table → Graph → Errors & Precautions → Conclusion.
- The **free-hand sketch** is non-negotiable; copied diagrams fetch zero marks.
- **Units in headers**, not in cells, of every table.
- **Sample calculation with units at every step** is the single most marks-rich section.
- **Graph on graph sheet** (not plain paper) with axes labelled, slope triangle marked, slope value written with units.
- **Percentage error** is the standard measure of accuracy.
- **Conclusion must be a paragraph** containing: numerical result + comparison with theory + physical interpretation + industrial application.
- **Timely submission** is **within 1 week** of conduction; late submissions attract **$10\%$ per week deduction**.
- A **sanity check** of the result (e.g., $C_d$ in $[0.95, 0.99]$ for venturimeter) is a **marks-earning habit**.
- The record is examined **twice** (CIE internal + ESE external) — a poor record pulls down marks in both.
- The CIE is the **training ground for industry-grade engineering documentation** (ISO 9001 / ISO 17025 / API 610 standards).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 11
> - **Topic:** Continuous Internal Evaluation: Viva Voce including ability to explain the experiment, results, and underlying principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme**, the **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** for the laboratory course **PCIEL307 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Lab** is a structured, ongoing assessment process. **Module Experiment 11** specifically refers to the **Viva Voce** component, which is a **direct oral examination** conducted by the faculty-in-charge to assess a student's:

1. **Conceptual Clarity**: Understanding of the underlying fluid mechanics and hydraulic machinery principles governing the experiment.
2. **Experimental Competence**: Ability to explain the apparatus, procedure, observations, and result interpretation.
3. **Analytical Reasoning**: Capacity to justify deviations, perform error analysis, and correlate theory with practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – CIE Weightage (PCIEL307):**
> The CIE carries **100% weightage** for lab courses. A portion (typically **40–50%** of internal marks) is dedicated to the **Viva Voce, Record, and Internal Assessment** components. The viva voce is **mandatory** for all students and cannot be skipped.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Viva Voce** as a **"Doctor's Diagnostic Check-up"** for your engineering knowledge. Just like a doctor asks probing questions to understand not just the symptoms but also the root cause of an illness, the examiner uses the viva to check **how deeply you understand the experiment** — beyond the surface-level "cookbook" approach of simply recording readings.

**Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a **chef being interviewed** on a cooking show. Anyone can follow a recipe (like a student copying lab readings), but the chef must explain **why** salt enhances flavor (the underlying principle), **what** happens if you overcook (error analysis), and **how** the dish would taste with ingredient substitutions (variations and applications). The KTU viva voce tests exactly this — your ability to be the **"chef"** and not just a **"recipe follower."**

---

## 1.3 Objectives of the Viva Voce (KTU 2024 Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Objectives Tested in the Viva Voce:**
> - **Application**: Linking Bernoulli's equation, continuity equation, momentum equation, and energy concepts to real apparatus.
> - **Calculation**: Manually verifying results, computing coefficients ($C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$), and graphical analysis.
> - **Troubleshooting**: Identifying sources of error (parallax, friction losses, manometer deflection errors).
> - **Communication**: Articulating technical answers clearly and concisely.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Fluid Mechanics Lab

The following constants and metrics are **frequently tested** in the viva voce:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of water ($\rho$) | **1000** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | At 4°C, used in nearly all FM lab calculations |
| Acceleration due to gravity ($g$) | **9.81** | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Head-to-pressure conversions, impact of jet |
| Specific weight of water ($\gamma$) | **9810** | $\text{N/m}^3$ | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ |
| Dynamic viscosity of water ($\mu$) | **$1.001 \times 10^{-3}$** | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | At 20°C, used in Reynolds number |
| Kinematic viscosity of water ($\nu$) | **$1.004 \times 10^{-6}$** | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | At 20°C |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **$1.01325 \times 10^5$** | $\text{Pa}$ | For gauge/absolute pressure conversions |
| Surface tension of water | **0.0728** | $\text{N/m}$ | At 20°C, capillary effects |

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip for Viva**: Always quote $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ unless specifically given otherwise. Examiners often cross-check these values to test your awareness of standard conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structure of the CIE Viva Voce Evaluation

The KTU 2024 scheme CIE viva is structured around **four pillars** of assessment:

### Pillar 1: Theory Recall (≈ 30% weightage)
- Definitions of key terms (e.g., streamline, laminar flow, Euler's number).
- Working principles of major apparatus (Venturimeter, Pelton wheel, Centrifugal pump).
- Derivation of governing equations on demand.

### Pillar 2: Experimental Procedure (≈ 25% weightage)
- Step-by-step explanation of how the experiment was conducted.
- Identification of apparatus parts and their functions.
- Justification for choosing specific equipment.

### Pillar 3: Result Analysis (≈ 30% weightage)
- Verification of numerical values computed in the record.
- Graphical interpretation (e.g., characteristic curves of pumps/turbines).
- Discussion of sources of error and percentage error calculation.

### Pillar 4: Application & Higher-Order Thinking (≈ 15% weightage)
- Real-world applications of the experiment.
- Modifications or alternative methods.
- Industrial relevance (e.g., why a Pelton wheel is used for high-head applications).

---

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet for the Fluid Mechanics Lab Viva

The following table consolidates the **most-tested formulas** across all experiments in PCIEL307. Mastery of these is **essential** for the viva.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet – PCIEL307 (Module 5 Experiments)**

| Experiment | Governing Principle | Key Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bernoulli's Theorem Verification** | Bernoulli's Equation | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $V$ = Velocity (m/s), $z$ = Elevation (m) |
| **Venturimeter** | Continuity + Bernoulli | $Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}$, $C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $A$ = Area (m²), $h$ = Manometer head (m) |
| **Orificemeter** | Continuity + Bernoulli | $Q_{th} = \frac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | $C_d$ = Coefficient of discharge |
| **Pitot Tube** | Stagnation Pressure | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$, $h$ = Difference in piezometric head | Measures stagnation + static pressure |
| **Notches (Rectangular, Triangular, Trapezoidal)** | Discharge over a notch | $Q = \frac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g} H^{3/2}$ (Rectangular); $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ (Triangular) | $L$ = Notch length, $H$ = Head over notch, $\theta$ = Notch angle |
| **Coefficient of Friction in Pipes** | Darcy-Weisbach Equation | $h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2gd}$, $f = \frac{h_f \cdot g \cdot d \cdot \pi^2}{16 L Q^2}$ (for laminar) | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = pipe length, $d$ = diameter |
| **Reynolds Number** | Flow regime | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter; $Re < 2000$ laminar, $> 4000$ turbulent |
| **Pelton Wheel** | Momentum equation | $u = \frac{C_v \sqrt{2gH}}{2}$, $\eta = \frac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\text{Water Power}} = \frac{2u(C_v - u)}{C_v^2}$ | $u$ = bucket velocity, $C_v$ = jet velocity |
| **Francis Turbine** | Reaction turbine | $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H}{P_{shaft}}$, $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | $N_s$ = specific speed |
| **Centrifugal Pump** | Euler's equation | $H_m = \frac{P_{out} - P_{in}}{\rho g}$, $\eta = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{\text{Input Power}}$ | $H_m$ = manometric head |
| **Reciprocating Pump** | Slip theory | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$, $\%\text{slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | Slip = negative slip possible |
| **Impact of Jet** | Momentum theorem | $F = \rho Q (V - u) = \rho A V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ | $F$ = force on plate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Heuristic**: In the viva, you will be asked to **derive at least one of these formulas on the spot**. Practice writing them on a blank sheet — the **derivation logic** matters more than the final equation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these principles equips a graduate for careers in:

- **Hydraulic Engineering**: Design of dams, spillways, irrigation channels (Notches/Weirs, Bernoulli).
- **Municipal Water Supply**: Sizing pipes, selecting pumps, estimating friction losses.
- **Power Generation**: Hydroelectric plants (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan turbines), pumped storage.
- **Oil & Gas**: Flow measurement in pipelines (Venturimeter, Orifice meter).
- **HVAC & Building Services**: Pump selection, duct flow, pressure drop calculations.
- **Process Industries**: Calibration of flow meters, pump performance testing.

> [!TIP]
> When asked *"Where is this experiment used in industry?"* in the viva, always give a **specific, modern example** (e.g., *"Venturimeters are used in carburettors and aircraft fuel systems"*) rather than a vague answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Venturimeter Discharge Equation (Most-Asked Derivation)

This is the **single most-tested derivation** in the PCIEL307 viva. Below is the complete, step-by-step derivation with **every algebraic transition** explicitly shown.

### Given:
- Inlet diameter $= D_1$, Throat diameter $= D_2$
- Inlet area $= A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4}$, Throat area $= A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4}$
- Pressure at inlet $= P_1$, Pressure at throat $= P_2$
- Velocities $V_1$ at inlet, $V_2$ at throat
- Differential manometer reading $= h$ (in metres of manometric fluid)

### Step 1: Apply the Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For an incompressible fluid in steady flow:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

Solving for $V_1$:

$$
V_1 = \frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}
$$

This is the **first key step** — establish the mass conservation link.

### Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)
Assuming horizontal venturimeter ($z_1 = z_2$) and no friction loss (ideal):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Pressure Difference Using the Manometer Reading
The differential manometer (typically U-tube with mercury, $S_{Hg} = 13.6$) gives:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{water}) \cdot g \cdot h = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h \cdot \left( \frac{S_{Hg}}{S_{water}} - 1 \right)
$$

Simplifying for $S_{Hg}/S_{water} = 13.6$:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h \cdot (13.6 - 1) = 12.6 \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

Substituting into Step 2:

$$
\frac{12.6 \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

$$
12.6 \cdot h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $V_1$ From Continuity
Replace $V_1$ with $\frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}$:

$$
12.6 \cdot h = \frac{V_2^2 - \left(\frac{A_2 V_2}{A_1}\right)^2}{2g}
$$

Factor out $V_2^2$:

$$
12.6 \cdot h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right]
$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_2$

$$
V_2^2 = \frac{2g \cdot 12.6 \cdot h}{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2g \cdot 12.6 \cdot h}}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2}}
$$

### Step 6: Compute the Theoretical Discharge
$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2$:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2gh(13.6 - 1)}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}
$$

Equivalently, in compact notation:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2g \cdot h'}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}
$$

where $h' = 12.6h$ for mercury-water manometer, or $h' = h \cdot \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ in general.

### Step 7: Introduce the Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)
Real fluids have viscous losses, so actual discharge is lower:

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

$$
Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{th}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical $C_d$ values for viva recall:**
> - Venturimeter: $C_d = 0.95 - 0.99$
> - Orificemeter: $C_d = 0.60 - 0.65$
> - Notch (Rectangular): $C_d = 0.60 - 0.62$
> - Notch (Triangular): $C_d = 0.58 - 0.62$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Common Viva Questions to Experiments

This matrix helps you **anticipate examiner questions** based on the experiment being discussed:

| Examiner's Likely Question Category | Sample Question | Expected Competence | Key Concept Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle-Based** | "Why does a Venturimeter have a throat?" | High | Continuity + Bernoulli |
| **Numerical Verification** | "Your $C_d$ came out to 0.97. Is this acceptable? Why?" | High | Comparison with standard range |
| **Troubleshooting** | "If the manometer reading fluctuates wildly, what is the issue?" | Medium | Air bubble, leak, pulsating flow |
| **Comparative Analysis** | "Why is $C_d$ for a venturi higher than that of an orifice?" | High | Geometry, separation, vena contracta |
| **Graphical Interpretation** | "What does the hump in your pump characteristic curve indicate?" | High | Maximum efficiency, NPSH |
| **Application-Based** | "Name two industries where Pelton wheels are preferred over Francis turbines." | Medium | Specific speed, head range |
| **Conceptual** | "What is negative slip in a reciprocating pump?" | High | Slip theory, delivery vs. suction stroke |
| **Error Analysis** | "What is the percentage error in your experiment and is it within limits?" | High | Theoretical vs. actual, tolerances |
| **Modifications** | "How would you improve the accuracy of this experiment?" | Very High | Metacognition, experimental design |

> [!TIP]
> **Strategy**: When asked a question, **always structure your answer in three parts**:
> 1. **State the principle** (1 sentence).
> 2. **Apply it to the experiment** (1–2 sentences with specifics).
> 3. **Conclude with a numerical or graphical observation** (your actual result).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, CIE Component]`
**Q: Define the Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$) for a Venturimeter. What is its typical range, and why is it less than unity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)** of a Venturimeter is defined as the ratio of the **actual discharge** to the **theoretical discharge** computed from the Bernoulli's equation:

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}
$$

**Typical Range**: $C_d = 0.95 \text{ to } 0.99$ for a standard Venturimeter.

**Why $C_d < 1$**: In real flow, viscous friction between the fluid layers and the wall, along with minor losses due to sudden expansion at the throat, cause a **reduction in the actual velocity** compared to the ideal Bernoulli-predicted velocity. Hence, the actual discharge is always less than the theoretical discharge, making $C_d < 1$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, CIE Component]`
**Q: Differentiate between a Pelton Wheel and a Francis Turbine based on the principle of operation, head range, and specific speed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pelton Wheel | Francis Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Impulse turbine (uses kinetic energy of jet) | Reaction turbine (uses both pressure and kinetic energy) |
| **Head Range** | High head (above **300 m**) | Medium head (**30 m to 300 m**) |
| **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** | Low (10–60 metric units) | Medium (60–400 metric units) |
| **Casing** | No casing required; operates at atmospheric pressure | Fully enclosed casing; flow under pressure |
| **Example Use** | Himalayan hydroelectric projects | Indian medium-head power stations |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - CIE Model Paper, Module 5]`

**Sub-question (a) [7 Marks]:** 
**A Venturimeter with inlet diameter 200 mm and throat diameter 100 mm is fitted to a horizontal pipe carrying water. A differential U-tube mercury manometer shows a deflection of 200 mm. Calculate: (i) The theoretical discharge through the venturimeter, (ii) The actual discharge if $C_d = 0.98$, and (iii) The pressure difference between inlet and throat in $\text{kPa}$.**

**Sub-question (b) [7 Marks]:**
**Explain the working principle of a Pelton Wheel with a neat sketch. Derive an expression for the power developed by a single jet Pelton wheel in terms of bucket velocity ($u$) and jet velocity ($V$).**

---

### Question A – Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:**
- Inlet diameter $D_1 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Throat diameter $D_2 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Manometer deflection $h = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- $C_d = 0.98$
- Specific gravity of mercury $S_{Hg} = 13.6$

**Step 1: Compute the Areas**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 0.007854 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Effective Head $h'$**

$$
h' = h \times \left( \frac{S_{Hg}}{S_{water}} - 1 \right) = 0.2 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.2 \times 12.6 = 2.52 \text{ m of water}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Theoretical Discharge $Q_{th}$**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A_1 A_2 \sqrt{2 g h'}}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{(0.03142)(0.007854) \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52}}{\sqrt{(0.03142)^2 - (0.007854)^2}}
$$

Compute the numerator: $0.03142 \times 0.007854 = 2.467 \times 10^{-4}$

$$
\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 2.52} = \sqrt{49.4424} = 7.031 \text{ m/s}
$$

Numerator $= 2.467 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.031 = 1.735 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator:

$$
\sqrt{(0.03142)^2 - (0.007854)^2} = \sqrt{9.872 \times 10^{-4} - 6.168 \times 10^{-5}} = \sqrt{9.255 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.03042
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{1.735 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03042} = 0.05703 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 57.03 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Actual Discharge $Q_{act}$**

$$
Q_{act} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.98 \times 0.05703 = 0.05589 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 55.89 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate the Pressure Difference $\Delta P$**

$$
\Delta P = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h' = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.52 = 24,721 \text{ Pa} = 24.72 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $Q_{th} = 0.0570 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- (ii) $Q_{act} = 0.0559 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- (iii) $\Delta P = 24.72 \, \text{kPa}$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Working Principle of Pelton Wheel:**

The Pelton wheel is an **impulse turbine** in which a high-velocity water jet strikes a series of buckets mounted on the periphery of a runner. The kinetic energy of the jet is converted into mechanical (rotational) energy, which is transmitted to a shaft connected to a generator.

**Key Components:**
1. **Nozzle**: Converts pressure head into kinetic energy (high-velocity jet).
2. **Runner with Buckets**: Splits the jet and changes its momentum direction.
3. **Casing**: Prevents water splashing (no pressure containment).
4. **Brake Jet / Spear**: Controls flow rate.
5. **Deflector**: Deflects water away from buckets during emergencies.

**Derivation of Power:**

Let:
- $V$ = Velocity of the jet (m/s)
- $u$ = Tangential velocity of the bucket (m/s)
- $\rho$ = Density of water (kg/m³)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the jet (m²)
- $\theta$ = Angle of deflection of the jet at the bucket exit (typically 165°–170°)

**Step 1: Mass flow rate of water**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V
$$

**Step 2: Velocity of water relative to the bucket**

$$
V_r = V - u
$$

**Step 3: Force on the bucket (momentum equation)**

The component of the relative velocity change in the direction of motion is:

$$
\Delta V = V_r (1 + \cos\theta) = (V - u)(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

Force exerted on the bucket:

$$
F = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta V = \rho A V (V - u)(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

**Step 4: Power developed by the wheel**

$$
P = F \cdot u = \rho A V u (V - u)(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

For a Pelton wheel, $\theta \approx 165°$, so $\cos\theta \approx -0.966$, giving $1 + \cos\theta \approx 0.034$. For maximum power, $u = V/2$.

**Step 5: Maximum Power Condition**

Differentiating $P$ w.r.t. $u$ and setting to zero: $u = V/2$

$$
P_{max} = \rho A V \cdot \frac{V}{2} \cdot \left(V - \frac{V}{2}\right) (1 + \cos\theta) = \frac{\rho A V^3 (1 + \cos\theta)}{8}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]`

**Sub-question (a) [7 Marks]:** 
**During an experiment on a centrifugal pump, the following readings were observed at a particular speed: Manometer reading = +0.20 m (suction) and +2.50 m (delivery), vacuum gauge = -0.30 bar, pressure gauge = 1.5 bar, discharge = 5 L/s, input power = 1.2 kW. Calculate the manometric head, total head, and overall efficiency of the pump. Take the centres of the gauges at 0.5 m and 1.0 m above the datum respectively.**

**Sub-question (b) [7 Marks]:**
**Define a Notch. With a neat sketch, derive the discharge equation for a **triangular (V-notch)** weir, assuming the velocity of approach is negligible.**

---

### Question B – Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:**
- Suction manometer: $h_s = 0.20 \text{ m}$ (vacuum)
- Delivery manometer: $h_d = 2.50 \text{ m}$
- Vacuum gauge: $P_s = -0.30 \text{ bar} = -30,000 \text{ Pa}$
- Pressure gauge: $P_d = 1.5 \text{ bar} = 150,000 \text{ Pa}$
- Suction centre above datum: $z_s = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Delivery centre above datum: $z_d = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- Discharge $Q = 5 \text{ L/s} = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Input Power $P_{in} = 1.2 \text{ kW} = 1200 \text{ W}$

**Step 1: Convert Manometric Heads to Pressure Heads**

Suction side:

$$
H_s = h_s + \frac{P_s}{\rho g} = 0.20 + \frac{-30000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 0.20 - 3.058 = -2.858 \text{ m}
$$

Delivery side:

$$
H_d = h_d + \frac{P_d}{\rho g} = 2.50 + \frac{150000}{1000 \times 9.81} = 2.50 + 15.29 = 17.79 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Manometric Head $H_m$**

$$
H_m = H_d - H_s + (z_d - z_s) = 17.79 - (-2.858) + (1.0 - 0.5) = 20.648 + 0.5 = 21.148 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Water Power $P_{water}$**

$$
P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 21.148 = 1037.2 \text{ W} \approx 1.037 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Overall Efficiency $\eta$**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{input}} = \frac{1.037}{1.2} = 0.864 = 86.4\%
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Manometric Head $H_m = 21.15 \text{ m}$
- Water Power = $1.04 \text{ kW}$
- Overall Efficiency $\eta = 86.4\%$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Definition of a Notch:**
A **notch** is a device used for measuring the discharge of a small channel or tank. It is essentially an opening made on a vertical plate through which water flows, with the upstream liquid level above the bottom edge of the notch (called the **crest** or **sill**).

**Types**: Rectangular, Triangular (V-notch), Trapezoidal.

**Derivation for V-Notch:**

Consider a V-notch with semi-vertical angle $\theta/2$ cut in a vertical plate. Let $H$ be the head of water above the vertex of the notch.

**Step 1: Consider an Elementary Strip**
At a depth $h$ below the free surface, consider a thin horizontal strip of thickness $dh$ and width $b$. From the geometry of the V-notch:

$$
\frac{b/2}{H - h} = \tan(\theta/2) \implies b = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2)
$$

**Step 2: Area of the Strip**

$$
dA = b \cdot dh = 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh
$$

**Step 3: Velocity Through the Strip (Torricelli's Theorem)**

$$
V = \sqrt{2g \cdot h}
$$

**Step 4: Discharge Through the Strip**

$$
dQ = C_d \cdot dA \cdot V = C_d \cdot 2(H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2gh} \cdot dh
$$

**Step 5: Integrate from $h = 0$ to $h = H$**

$$
Q = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) h^{1/2} \, dh
$$

Expand $(H - h) h^{1/2} = H h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}$:

$$
Q = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \left[ H \cdot \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2} - \frac{2}{5} h^{5/2} \right]_0^H
$$

$$
Q = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \left[ \frac{2H^{5/2}}{3} - \frac{2H^{5/2}}{5} \right]
$$

$$
Q = 2 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot 2H^{5/2} \left[ \frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{5} \right]
$$

$$
Q = 4 C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{2}{15}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}}
$$

This is the **standard discharge equation for a triangular notch** (V-notch).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Students often forget to convert mm to m or kPa to Pa. This costs **1–2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Manometer Sign Convention**: For a vacuum gauge, the pressure is **negative** relative to atmospheric. Students frequently add instead of subtract, leading to wrong manometric head.
> 3. **Forgetting the Centre Distance**: In pump experiments, the manometric head calculation **must include the vertical distance between gauge centres** $(z_d - z_s)$. Skipping this is a common **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Viva Etiquette**: When asked to derive, **do not remain silent**. Talk through each step aloud. Examiners reward **logical progression** more than silent writing.
> 5. **Never say "I don't know"**: Instead say *"Based on the principle, I believe it is..."*. Partial answers earn partial marks in KTU scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for PCIEL307 Viva Voce:**

- [ ] **Bernoulli's Equation**: Memorize the three energy terms: pressure head, velocity head, elevation head, and the constant $H$ (total head).
- [ ] **Continuity Equation**: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ — applies to incompressible flow only.
- [ ] **Venturimeter $C_d$**: $\approx 0.97$ — highest among flow meters because there is **no vena contracta**.
- [ ] **Orificemeter $C_d$**: $\approx 0.62$ — lower because of **vena contracta** (jet contracts below the orifice).
- [ ] **Reynolds Number**: Laminar $< 2000 <$ Transitional $< 4000 <$ Turbulent.
- [ ] **Friction Factor**: For laminar flow, $f = 64/Re$. For turbulent, use Moody's chart.
- [ ] **Pelton Wheel**: Impulse turbine, $u = V/2$ for max power, suitable for high head, low flow.
- [ ] **Francis Turbine**: Reaction turbine, mixed flow, medium head (30–300 m).
- [ ] **Centrifugal Pump**: Manometric head calculation requires **suction + delivery + centre distance**.
- [ ] **Reciprocating Pump**: Slip can be **negative** (theoretical discharge less than actual).
- [ ] **V-Notch Discharge**: $Q = \frac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} H^{5/2}$ — note the $H^{5/2}$ (not $H^{3/2}$ like rectangular).
- [ ] **Impact of Jet Force**: $F = \rho A V^2 (1 - \cos\theta)$ — for a flat plate, $\theta = 90°$, so $F = \rho A V^2$.
- [ ] **Specific Speed**: Determines turbine/pump selection based on head and flow.
- [ ] **Hydraulic Jump**: Occurs when supercritical flow changes to subcritical; energy loss occurs.
- [ ] **Cavitation**: Occurs when local pressure drops below vapour pressure; can damage pump impellers.
- [ ] **Always quote** $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified.
- [ ] **Viva Strategy**: State the principle → Apply to the experiment → Quote your actual result → Conclude.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Pro Tip**: The viva voce is **not a memory test** — it is a **comprehension test**. An examiner would rather hear a student say *"I am not 100% sure, but here is my reasoning..."* than a memorized paragraph recited without understanding. **Show your work, think aloud, and connect theory to practice.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE (End Semester Examination) – Laboratory Course Protocol:**
> The End Semester Examination of **PCIEL307 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Lab** is a **continuous, performance-based assessment** that evaluates a student's ability to **(a) understand the working principle** of a fluid mechanics experiment, **(b) plan the preliminary work** (aim, apparatus, theory, formula), **(c) organize the procedure** in a logical sequence, and **(d) develop a stepwise algorithm/flowchart** for data acquisition, computation, and result interpretation. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the ESE is mapped to **Course Outcomes (COs)** at the **Apply / Analyze / Evaluate** levels of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

In simple engineering language, the ESE of a lab course is **not** merely "writing what you remember." It is a structured, engineering-design exercise in which the student must:

1. **Deconstruct** the experiment into functional blocks.
2. **Sequence** the operations (start-up → steady state → measurement → shut-down).
3. **Translate** the procedure into a deterministic **algorithm** (stepwise math + logic).
4. **Validate** the result by re-arranging the algorithm with numerical data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The "Cooking Recipe Engineering" View:**
> Think of a Fluid Mechanics experiment as a **recipe for a dish you have never cooked before**.
> - **Aim (Preliminary Work)** = What dish am I making? (Pelton Wheel Performance Curve)
> - **Apparatus** = What utensils do I need? (Tachometer, Stopwatch, Manometer, Dead-weight gauge)
> - **Theory** = What is the science of cooking? (Bernoulli's equation, Euler's equation for turbomachinery)
> - **Procedure** = Step-by-step actions in the kitchen (Start pump → Open valve → Wait for steady head → Note readings → Close valve)
> - **Algorithm** = The decision-tree *behind* the recipe: "If head is constant for 30 s, then take reading; else, wait."
> - **Design** = Choosing the right pot size (specifying the range of discharge / pressure / speed for which the test is valid).

When the examiner asks, *"Write the procedure and algorithm,"* the examiner is essentially asking: **"Can you engineer the experiment as a reproducible, logical, mathematical process?"**

## 1.3 Mandatory Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Density of water, ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Specific weight of water, γ = 9810 N/m³**
> - **Kinematic viscosity of water at 20 °C, ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - **Atmospheric pressure, P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 10.33 m of water**
> - **1 HP = 745.7 W ; 1 kW = 1000 W**
> - **1 kgf/cm² = 98.066 kPa ≈ 10 m of water head**
> - **Coefficient of velocity, Cv = 0.95 to 0.99 (for sharp-edged orifice / nozzle)**
> - **Bucket speed ratio, Ku = u / V₁ = 0.45 to 0.50 (for maximum efficiency of Pelton wheel)**
> - **Coefficient of friction in bucket, k = 0.85 to 0.98 (Pelton wheel)**

## 1.4 Scope of "Module Experiment 11" in PCIEL307

The 11th experiment in the KTU 2024-scheme syllabus of PCIEL307 is typically a **performance / characteristics study** of a turbomachine. The most commonly chosen experiments at this slot are:

| Sl. No. | Representative Experiment | Skill Tested |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 11.1 | Performance characteristics of a **Pelton Wheel** | Impact-of-jet + tangential impulse |
| 11.2 | Performance characteristics of a **Centrifugal Pump** | Euler head, manometric head, slip |
| 11.3 | Performance characteristics of a **Francis Turbine** | Reaction turbine, draft tube |
| 11.4 | Verification of **Bernoulli's Theorem** | Energy conservation along a streamline |
| 11.5 | **Cavitation** in a centrifugal pump | NPSH, cavitation parameter |

For the purpose of this note, we will use the **Pelton Wheel Performance Test** as the canonical example, because it exercises **all four** cognitive skills the ESE tests: procedure understanding, planning, organization, and algorithm development.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of the ESE – A Pedagogical Deconstruction

The KTU 2024 ESE for a lab course is not a "viva-only" or "report-only" test. It is a **structured, time-bound performance assessment** with four distinct deliverables, each carrying a definite weightage pattern.

### 2.1.1 Pillar I – Preliminary Work (Aim + Apparatus + Theory + Formula)

> **Why this matters:** The examiner uses this section to verify whether the student *understands* the engineering purpose of the experiment *before* touching the apparatus. Marks are awarded for **clarity of aim**, **correct identification of apparatus**, **dimensionally consistent formulae**, and **sketch of the setup with labelled parts**.

**Sub-components expected:**

1. **Aim** – A single, precise statement of what is to be determined (e.g., *"To determine the efficiency and power of a Pelton wheel at various loads and to plot the characteristic curves."*).
2. **Apparatus** – A bulleted list of the **physical instruments** (not the formulae). For a Pelton wheel: *Pelton wheel test rig, pressure gauge, tachometer, stopwatch, dead-weight tension brake, measuring tank, energy meter.*
3. **Theory** – A *concise* (4–6 line) paragraph of the governing physics (e.g., *"When a high-velocity water jet strikes the buckets, the impulse force produces a torque, causing the wheel to rotate. The hydraulic power is converted into mechanical (shaft) power, with losses at the nozzle, at the bucket, and due to friction in the bearing."*).
4. **Formula** – Only the **final working formulae** (with units), not intermediate algebra.
5. **Setup Sketch** – A neat line diagram with **arrowed flow path** and **labelled components**.

### 2.1.2 Pillar II – Procedure (Sequential, Reproducible Engineering Protocol)

> **Why this matters:** A procedure is *not* a list of disconnected actions. It is a **cause-effect chain** that begins with a *safe, reproducible initial state* and ends with a *defined shut-down state*, with **explicit steady-state conditions** in between.

**Engineering grammar of a well-written procedure:**

| Step Type | Verb Convention | Example (Pelton Wheel) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Safety / Pre-start check | "Inspect", "Ensure" | Ensure the nozzle is clean and the supply valve is closed. |
| Initialization | "Start", "Open", "Set" | Start the pump and slowly open the supply valve. |
| Conditioning | "Allow to settle", "Wait" | Allow the system to reach steady head for 2 minutes. |
| Measurement | "Note", "Record", "Tabulate" | Note the pressure head (H), speed (N), and spring-balance readings. |
| Iteration | "Vary", "Adjust", "Repeat" | Increase the brake load in steps of 1 kg. |
| Termination | "Close", "Stop", "Drain" | Close the supply valve and stop the pump. |

### 2.1.3 Pillar III – Design (Range Selection, Justification of Constants)

> **Why this matters:** Design means *selecting the test range* so that the characteristic curve covers the **operating envelope** of the machine. A student who runs the test only at one or two points gets zero partial credit for the design pillar.

For a Pelton wheel, the design decisions are:
- **Range of pressure head (H):** 5 m to 25 m of water (in steps of 2 m).
- **Range of brake load (W):** 0 kg to 6 kg (in steps of 0.5 kg).
- **Number of readings per setting:** Minimum 3 (to compute an average).
- **Constant parameters (must be tabulated once):** Nozzle diameter (d), bucket angle (β), jet ratio (Ku), wheel diameter (D), shaft diameter.

### 2.1.4 Pillar IV – Algorithm (Stepwise Logic for Calculation)

> **Why this matters:** The algorithm is the **mathematical skeleton** of the experiment. It transforms *raw observations* into *engineering deliverables* (efficiency, power, characteristic curves). The examiner awards marks for **(a) correct order of computation, (b) proper unit handling, (c) use of g = 9.81 m/s² consistently, and (d) final tabulation with computed and observed columns**.

**Structure of a KTU-standard algorithm:**

```
START
  1. Read constants: d, D, A (measuring tank area), k (friction factor)
  2. Read observations: H, N, W (brake load), R (rise), t (time)
  3. Compute V₁ = Cv × √(2gH)
  4. Compute u   = πDN/60
  5. Compute Q   = A × R / t
  6. Compute Wt  = ρ × Q × (V₁ − u) × (1 + k cos φ) × u
  7. Compute Psp = Wt × g            // shaft power (Watts)
  8. Compute Wb  = (W − S) × 9.81 × R_wheel   // brake power
  9. Compute Pbp = Wb × N / 60 × 2π
  10. Compute η_overall = Pbp / Psp
  11. Print / Tabulate
STOP
```

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) – For Pelton Wheel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table verbatim. KTU examiners routinely award 3–4 marks for the formula sheet alone in the Preliminary Work section.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Definition of Symbols |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Velocity of jet | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | $C_v$ = coeff. of velocity, $H$ = gross head (m) |
| 2 | Bucket velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = wheel dia, $N$ = rpm |
| 3 | Discharge | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot R}{t}$ | m³/s | $A$ = tank area, $R$ = rise, $t$ = time |
| 4 | Work done / s (frictionless) | $W = \rho Q V_1 (1 + \cos\phi) u$ | W | $\phi$ = bucket exit angle |
| 5 | Work done / s (with friction) | $W = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + k \cos\phi) u$ | W | $k$ = bucket friction coeff. |
| 6 | Shaft power developed | $P_{sh} = W \cdot g$ (or in kW) | W or kW | When W in kgf·m/s, multiply by 9.81 |
| 7 | Water power input | $P_w = \rho g Q H$ | W | $H$ = net head |
| 8 | Brake power output | $P_b = 2\pi N (W-S) R_{brake} / 60$ | W | $W$ = load, $S$ = spring balance reading |
| 9 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k\cos\phi)}{V_1^{\,2}}$ | – | ratio of work done to KE of jet |
| 10 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sh}}$ | – | ratio of brake to shaft power |
| 11 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m = \dfrac{P_b}{P_w}$ | – | ratio of output to input power |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility – Where This Skill Is Used in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> The ESE skill-set directly mirrors the **Engineer's Working Life in Industry**:
> - In a **pump manufacturing company** (Kirloskar, CRI, Grundfos), every prototype is tested using a *test-bed procedure* that follows the same four pillars.
> - In a **hydropower plant** (NHPC in Kerala: Idukki, Sabarigiri), field engineers *design* load schedules, *plan* turbine shut-down protocols, and *develop algorithms* in SCADA software for remote monitoring.
> - In **consultancy** (water-supply schemes, irrigation projects), a junior engineer's *first deliverable* is often a procedure + algorithm document for a new test rig.
> - In **research** (M.Tech / Ph.D. work), the algorithm you write in B.Tech 3rd year evolves into the **methodology section** of a journal paper.

Thus, the ESE is **not** a "memory test." It is a **pre-employment skill drill**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Mandate:** Every algebraic transition and every numerical computation is written out in full. No step is skipped, abbreviated, or replaced by "..." or "similarly." The student should be able to reproduce every line on the answer script.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Velocity of Jet (V₁)

The jet issues from a sharp-edged nozzle under a head $H$. Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between the free surface of the supply tank (point 0) and the vena contracta of the jet (point 1):

$$
\frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{V_0^{\,2}}{2g} + z_0 \;=\; \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^{\,2}}{2g} + z_1 + h_L
$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

1. Both points 0 and 1 are at **atmospheric pressure** $\Rightarrow$ $P_0 = P_1 = P_{atm}$, so the pressure terms cancel.
2. The tank cross-section $A_0 \gg$ nozzle area $A_1$ $\Rightarrow$ by continuity, $V_0 \approx 0$.
3. Let $z_0 - z_1 = H$ (the gross head, in metres of water).
4. The head loss $h_L$ is lumped into a single coefficient $C_v$ by the definition:

$$
V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}
$$

where

$$
C_v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \Sigma \zeta}}
$$

and $\Sigma \zeta$ is the sum of all loss coefficients (entrance + friction + nozzle). For a well-designed nozzle, $C_v = 0.95$ to $0.99$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bucket Velocity (u)

The wheel rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute. The mean bucket travels along the pitch circle of diameter $D$. The linear (tangential) speed is:

$$
u = \frac{\text{Circumference}}{\text{Time per revolution}} = \frac{\pi D}{60/N} = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \text{[m/s]}
$$

**Why this matters algorithmically:** the very first thing the algorithm must do is convert rpm → m/s, *before* any efficiency calculation. KTU examiners routinely deduct ½ mark for omitting this conversion.

## 3.3 Derivation of Work Done per Second on a Pelton Bucket

Consider a single bucket, with the jet velocity $V_1$ along the $x$-axis and the bucket moving with velocity $u$ in the same direction. By **relative motion analysis** (Galilean frame attached to the bucket):

$$
V_{r1} = V_1 - u
$$

The water enters and is turned through an angle $\phi$ (typically $165°$, so $\cos\phi$ is negative; the sign is taken care of in the formula).

Relative velocity at exit:

$$
V_{r2} = k \cdot V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u)
$$

where $k$ is the friction coefficient ($0.85 \le k \le 0.98$, dimensionless).

**Absolute velocity of whirl at inlet** (component along the direction of bucket motion):

$$
V_{w1} = V_1 - u
$$

**Absolute velocity of whirl at exit** (with bucket turning the jet through $\phi$):

$$
V_{w2} = -k(V_1 - u)\cos\phi
$$

**Work done per unit weight** (Euler's turbomachinery equation):

$$
W = \frac{1}{g}(V_{w1} \pm V_{w2}) \cdot u
$$

For a Pelton wheel, $V_{w1}$ and $V_{w2}$ are in **opposite** directions, so we **add** their magnitudes:

$$
W = \frac{u}{g}\bigl[V_{w1} + k(V_1 - u)\cos\phi\bigr]
$$

**Work done per unit time** (multiplying by $\rho g Q$):

$$
W_t = \rho g Q \cdot W = \rho g Q \cdot \frac{u}{g}\bigl[(V_1 - u) + k(V_1 - u)\cos\phi\bigr]
$$

Simplifying (the $g$ cancels beautifully):

$$
W_t = \rho Q (V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\phi) \cdot u
$$

**Shaft power (in Watts):**

$$
P_{sh} = W_t \quad \text{(when W is in N·m/s = W)}
$$

**Shaft power (in kgf·m/s, the older gravimetric unit):**

$$
P_{sh} = W_t / 9.81 \quad \text{(kgf·m/s)}
$$

**Hydraulic efficiency** (ratio of work done to the kinetic energy of the jet):

$$
\eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done / s}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho Q V_1^{\,2}} = \frac{2u(V_1-u)(1+k\cos\phi)}{V_1^{\,2}}
$$

**Maximum $\eta_h$** occurs when $\dfrac{d\eta_h}{du} = 0$, yielding the classic result:

$$
u = \frac{V_1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = 0.5
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical maximum $\eta_h$ (frictionless, $\phi = 165°$):**
>
> $$
> \eta_{h,\max} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot (1 + \cos 165°)}{1^2} = 0.5(1 - 0.966) \approx 0.983
> $$
>
> In practice, with friction and a typical nozzle loss, the **overall efficiency** of a Pelton wheel is **75 % to 85 %**.

## 3.4 Complete Algorithm – Pelton Wheel ESE (Numerical Demonstration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (typical ESE question):**
> A Pelton wheel has a mean bucket diameter of **600 mm**, the nozzle diameter is **50 mm**, the gross head is **30 m of water**, and $C_v = 0.98$. The wheel runs at **500 rpm**, the bucket angle $\phi = 165°$, the bucket friction coefficient $k = 0.9$, and the discharge is **0.025 m³/s**. The brake load is **60 N** and the spring balance reads **10 N**, with a brake-drum effective radius of **400 mm**. Compute the overall efficiency.

**Step 1: Constants (read once, tabulate).**

$$
D = 0.6\ \text{m},\quad d = 0.05\ \text{m},\quad g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2,\quad \rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Jet velocity.**

$$
V_1 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 30}
$$

$$
V_1 = 0.98 \times \sqrt{588.6} = 0.98 \times 24.262 = 23.777\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Bucket velocity.**

$$
u = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 500}{60} = \frac{942.48}{60} = 15.708\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Speed ratio check.**

$$
K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{15.708}{23.777} = 0.661
$$

> **Examiner's note:** Since $K_u > 0.5$, the wheel is running **faster than the optimum**; $\eta_h$ will be lower than the theoretical max. This is a *comment* the ESE student should record.

**Step 5: Work done per second.**

$$
W_t = 1000 \times 0.025 \times (23.777 - 15.708) \times (1 + 0.9 \times \cos 165°) \times 15.708
$$

Computing each factor:

$$
V_1 - u = 23.777 - 15.708 = 8.069\ \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\cos 165° = -\cos 15° = -0.9659
$$

$$
1 + k\cos\phi = 1 + 0.9 \times (-0.9659) = 1 - 0.8693 = 0.1307
$$

$$
W_t = 1000 \times 0.025 \times 8.069 \times 0.1307 \times 15.708
$$

$$
W_t = 25 \times 8.069 \times 0.1307 \times 15.708 = 25 \times 16.547 = 413.68\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 6: Hydraulic efficiency.**

$$
\eta_h = \frac{2 \times 15.708 \times 8.069 \times 0.1307}{23.777^2} = \frac{33.118}{565.27} = 0.0586 = 5.86\ \%
$$

> **Observation:** $\eta_h$ is very low because $K_u = 0.66$ is far from the optimum 0.5.

**Step 7: Shaft power (assuming $\eta_m = 0.9$ for the demonstration).**

$$
P_{sh} = W_t = 413.68\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 8: Brake power.**

$$
P_b = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R_{brake}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 500 \times (60-10) \times 0.4}{60}
$$

$$
P_b = \frac{2\pi \times 500 \times 50 \times 0.4}{60} = \frac{62831.85}{60} = 1047.2\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 9: Mechanical efficiency.**

$$
\eta_m = \frac{P_b}{P_{sh}} = \frac{1047.2}{413.68} = 2.53
$$

> **Sanity-check failure:** $\eta_m > 1$ is physically impossible. This is because in step 6 we have used an *unfavourable* $K_u$. In a real ESE, this would be a clue to re-examine the design.

**Step 10: Recompute with the corrected optimum $u$.**

If the wheel were to be re-designed for $K_u = 0.5$:

$$
u_{opt} = 0.5 \times 23.777 = 11.89\ \text{m/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_{opt} = \frac{11.89 \times 60}{\pi \times 0.6} = 378.5\ \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 11: Water power input.**

$$
P_w = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 30 = 7357.5\ \text{W} = 7.36\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 12: Overall efficiency (using $\eta_o = P_b / P_w$ at the re-designed rpm, for a hypothetical, well-designed scenario).**

Assuming a typical $\eta_o = 0.80$ for a Pelton wheel at design point:

$$
P_b = 0.80 \times 7357.5 = 5886\ \text{W} = 5.886\ \text{kW} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_b \approx 7.89\ \text{HP}
$$

> **Final tabulation for the answer script:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Jet velocity | $V_1$ | 23.78 | m/s |
| Bucket velocity | $u$ | 15.71 | m/s |
| Speed ratio | $K_u$ | 0.661 | – |
| Work done / s | $W_t$ | 413.7 | W |
| Water power | $P_w$ | 7.36 | kW |
| Brake power (re-design) | $P_b$ | 5.89 | kW |
| Overall efficiency | $\eta_o$ | 0.80 | – |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Algorithm – Pelton Wheel ESE (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `s1`, `cond1`).
> - Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are never used as standalone node names.
> - No bold/italic tags inside quoted node labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START: Read Constants D, d, H, N, k, phi"] --> s2["Compute Jet Velocity V1 = Cv x sqrt 2 g H"]
    s2 --> s3["Compute Bucket Velocity u = pi D N over 60"]
    s3 --> s4{"Check Speed Ratio Ku = u over V1"}
    s4 -->|"Ku in 0.45 to 0.50"| s5["OPTIMUM: Proceed to calculation"]
    s4 -->|"Ku less than 0.45"| s6["WHEEL SLOW: Note under-speed comment"]
    s4 -->|"Ku greater than 0.50"| s7["WHEEL FAST: Note over-speed comment"]
    s5 --> s8["Compute Discharge Q = A R over t"]
    s6 --> s8
    s7 --> s8
    s8 --> s9["Compute Relative Velocity Vr1 = V1 minus u"]
    s9 --> s10["Compute Whirl Components Vw1 and Vw2"]
    s10 --> s11["Compute Work Done per second Wt = rho Q Vr1 1 plus k cos phi u"]
    s11 --> s12["Compute Shaft Power Psh = Wt"]
    s12 --> s13["Compute Brake Power Pb = 2 pi N W minus S Rbrake over 60"]
    s13 --> s14["Compute Efficiencies etah etam etao"]
    s14 --> s15["Tabulate All Computed Values vs Load"]
    s15 --> s16["Plot Characteristic Curves etao vs N, P vs Q"]
    s16 --> s17["STOP: Report Result with Units"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```


## 4.4 Decision-Tree for "Algorithm vs Flowchart" Choice in ESE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Is the experiment a single-pass calculation?} -->|"Yes e.g. venturi coefficient"| a1[Use a Linear Algorithm in Pseudocode]
    q1 -->|"No iterative readings e.g. turbine curve"| a2[Use a Flowchart with Decision Boxes]
    q2{Is the procedure dependent on a condition?} -->|"Yes e.g. steady state"| a3[Insert a Decision Box: Has reading stabilised?]
    q2 -->|"No"| a4[Use a Linear Block Sequence]
    q3{Does the algorithm involve a loop?} -->|"Yes e.g. vary load, take 5 readings"| a5[Use a For-loop or Repeat-until block in pseudocode]
    q3 -->|"No"| a6[Use a Single-Pass Algorithm]
```
