It is typically the **last experiment** in the KTU lab manual because it requires only basic hydrostatic principles already learned in earlier fluid mechanics modules. ### 1.2.1 Formal KTU Definition The **Metacentric Height (GM)** is defined as the distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and the **Metacenter (M)** of the body, measured along the vertical line passing through the center of buoyancy. $$\boxed{GM = BM - BG}$$ where: - **BM** = Distance from the Center of Buoyancy (B) to the Metacenter (M) - **BG** = Distance from the Center of Buoyancy (B) to the Center of Gravity (G) ### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Tipping Point of a Floating Object" > [!NOTE] > **The Floating Cork Analogy** > > Imagine a **rectangular wooden block** floating in a bathtub. > - When the block floats perfectly upright, you can tilt it slightly and **it returns to its original position** — this is a **stable floating body**. > - If you load the top of the block with a **heavy metal weight**, even a small tilt makes it **capsize (overturn)** — this is an **unstable floating body**. > - There is a **specific height of the weight** at which the block is **on the verge** of being either stable or unstable — this critical height is precisely the **Metacentric Height (GM)**. > > In simpler words: **GM tells you "how stable" a ship is when it tilts (rolls) in water.** ### 1.2.3 Why Is This Experiment Important in Engineering? - **Naval Architects** use GM to design ships that do not capsize in rough seas. - **Submarine Designers** use GM to control surfacing and diving stability. - **Offshore Platform Engineers** use GM to ensure floating oil rigs remain stable. - **Dam Engineers** use stability principles for floating barrages and pontoon bridges. The standard stability criteria (using GM) is: | Stability Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Stable Equilibrium** | $\text{GM} > 0$ | Body returns to upright position after tilting | | **Neutral Equilibrium** | $\text{GM} = 0$ | Body remains in the tilted position | | **Unstable Equilibrium** | $\text{GM} < 0$ | Body capsizes and does not return | > [!NOTE] > **Key Constants for This Experiment:** > - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Density of fresh water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** > - Density of sea water: **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** > - The ship/pontoon is typically made of **wood or Perspex** with a known weight range. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three Critical Points (B, G, M) To understand the viva, you must master the **three reference points** that govern the stability of any floating body. ### 2.1.1 Center of Gravity (G) The **Center of Gravity (G)** is the point where the entire weight of the body is assumed to act. - For a **symmetric, homogeneous body** (like a wooden pontoon), G lies on the vertical axis of symmetry. - For a body with **additional weights placed on top**, G shifts **upwards** along the vertical axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Trap Question:** *"If you keep adding weight to the top of the pontoon, where does G move?"* > **Correct Answer:** G moves **vertically upward**, closer to the loading point. This **decreases the metacentric height** and makes the body **less stable**. ### 2.1.2 Center of Buoyancy (B) The **Center of Buoyancy (B)** is the centroid of the volume of fluid **displaced** by the floating body. - By **Archimedes' Principle**, the upward buoyant force acts through B. - Initially (upright position), B lies on the **vertical axis of symmetry** of the body. - When the body **tilts**, B **shifts to one side** (towards the submerged side). ### 2.1.3 Metacenter (M) The **Metacenter (M)** is defined as the point where: - The **vertical line passing through the new (tilted) Center of Buoyancy (B')** - **Intersects** the **original vertical axis** of the body (the line passing through the initial Center of Buoyancy, B). In simpler terms, as the body tilts, B moves to a new position B'. If you draw a vertical line up from B', and another vertical line along the body's original centerline, they meet at the **Metacenter (M)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Viva Insight:** M is a **fixed point** (for small angles of tilt, typically $\theta < 15°$) regardless of the tilt angle. This is what makes the formula tractable. ## 2.2 The Core Stability Logic (Step-by-Step Bulleted Flow) The KTU lab manual expects students to understand the following sequence: - **Step 1:** The floating body is in **equilibrium** when weight (W) acting downward through G equals the buoyant force ($F_B$) acting upward through B. - **Step 2:** An external disturbance (e.g., a small horizontal push) tilts the body by a small angle $\theta$. - **Step 3:** The Center of Buoyancy shifts from B to a new position B' (towards the side that is now more submerged). - **Step 4:** A **restoring couple** is created between W (downward through G) and $F_B$ (upward through B'). - **Step 5:** The **couple arm** (perpendicular distance between the lines of action of W and $F_B$) is what produces the restoring moment. - **Step 6:** If the **Metacenter M lies ABOVE G**, the couple is **restoring** (stable). - **Step 7:** If **M lies BELOW G**, the couple is **overturning** (unstable). - **Step 8:** If **M coincides with G**, the body is in **neutral equilibrium**. ## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Viva) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU High-Yield Formula Table for Metacentric Height Viva** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Displaced Volume (V)** | $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot g}$ | W = total weight of body, $\rho$ = water density, g = gravity | m³ | | 2 | **Experimental GM** | $GM = \dfrac{W \cdot x \cdot \tan(\theta)}{W \cdot d}$ (simplifies below) | Direct from tilt experiment | m | | 3 | **Simplified Experimental GM** | $GM = \dfrac{x \cdot \tan(\theta)}{2}$ | For a symmetrical body with movable weight (x = shift of weight, $\theta$ = tilt angle) | m | | 4 | **Alternative form** | $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}$ | When a small weight 'w' is shifted by distance 'l' causing tilt $\theta$ | m | | 5 | **Theoretical BM** | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | I = Second Moment of Area of waterline plane, V = displaced volume | m | | 6 | **For Rectangular Pontoon (BM)** | $BM = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot V}$ | L = Length, B = Breadth of waterline section | m | | 7 | **For Circular Pontoon (BM)** | $BM = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64 \cdot V}$ | D = Diameter of circular waterline | m | | 8 | **Stability Criterion** | $\text{Stable if } GM > 0$ | Compare theoretical and experimental GM | — | | 9 | **Total Weight (with movable mass)** | $W = w + W_0$ | w = movable weight, $W_0$ = empty body weight | N | | 10 | **Percentage Error** | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert GM_{\text{theo}} - GM_{\text{exp}} \vert}{GM_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$ | Comparison metric | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE for KTU Records:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in exam answer books. Avoid the raw pipe `|` symbol within sentence flow. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The metacentric height concept is the **backbone of naval architecture**: - **Aircraft Carriers:** The US Navy's *Nimitz*-class carriers have a GM of about **2.7 to 3.0 meters**, providing a stable platform for aircraft operations. - **Submarines:** When submerged, submarines use **ballast tanks** to actively control GM. Positive GM → surface, Negative GM → dive. - **Luxury Yachts:** Designers aim for a **low GM** (around 0.5 m) to provide a softer, more comfortable roll. - **Cargo Ships:** Heavy cargo loaded on the deck **reduces GM** — this is why cargo must be distributed carefully. - **Offshore Drilling Rigs:** Stability against wave action is calculated using GM-based methods. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Viva Hot Question:** *"Why is a very HIGH metacentric height also undesirable for ships?"* > **Model Answer:** *"A very high GM causes the ship to oscillate violently and roll quickly (stiff ship), causing seasickness and structural stress. A low GM causes slow, gentle rolling. An optimum GM balances stability and comfort."* # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Theoretical Derivation of the Experimental GM Formula The KTU viva frequently tests whether a student can **derive** the formula rather than just write it. The standard derivation proceeds as follows. ### 3.1.1 Setup of the Tilted Body Consider a symmetrical floating body (a pontoon) of weight W floating in water. A small movable weight 'w' is placed at a distance 'l' from the centerline on the deck. The body tilts by a small angle $\theta$. **Initial Position (Before Shift):** - The body floats with the movable weight on the centerline. - The center of gravity $G_1$ lies on the vertical axis of symmetry. - The center of buoyancy $B_1$ lies on the same axis, directly below $G_1$. **Final Position (After Shift):** - The movable weight 'w' is shifted by a distance 'l' across the deck. - This causes the body to tilt by angle $\theta$ until the new center of buoyancy $B_2$ is shifted to balance the moment. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation Using Moment Balance By the **principle of moments** about the new center of buoyancy $B_2$: The moment created by the shifted weight 'w' (trying to tilt the body) must equal the moment created by the shift in buoyancy (trying to restore equilibrium). $$\text{Overturning Moment} = \text{Restoring Moment}$$ The overturning moment is created by the movable weight w acting at a horizontal distance approximately equal to l from the original vertical axis: $$M_{\text{overturning}} = w \cdot l$$ The restoring moment is created by the weight W of the body acting through the horizontal shift between the new and old buoyancy centers. For a small tilt $\theta$, the horizontal displacement between $B_1$ and $B_2$ is approximately $GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$: $$M_{\text{restoring}} = W \cdot (GM \cdot \tan(\theta))$$ Equating the two moments: $$w \cdot l = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan(\theta)$$ Solving for GM: $$GM = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Working Formula (the one you write in the record):** > $$\boxed{GM_{\text{experimental}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}}$$ ### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Theoretical BM (Metacentric Radius) The theoretical value of BM is derived using the **second moment of area (I)** of the waterline plane: $$BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{\text{displaced}}}$$ The derivation comes from the geometry of the tilted wedge of water displacement. For small tilt $\theta$, the buoyant force $F_B = \rho \cdot g \cdot V$ continues to act vertically, but the center of buoyancy shifts by a distance: $$\text{Shift} = \dfrac{I \cdot \tan(\theta)}{V}$$ For a **rectangular waterline** of length L and breadth B: $$I_{xx} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$ Therefore: $$BM = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}}$$ For a **circular waterline** of diameter D: $$I_{xx} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64}$$ Therefore: $$BM = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^4}{64 \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}}$$ ## 3.2 Complete Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** > *An empty wooden pontoon has dimensions 0.5 m (Length) × 0.3 m (Breadth) × 0.2 m (Height) and weighs 25 N. A movable weight of 5 N is placed on the deck. When the weight is shifted by 0.15 m from the centerline, the body tilts by an angle of 8°. The pontoon is floating in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³). Calculate:* > *(a) The experimental metacentric height.* > *(b) The theoretical metacentric radius (BM).* > *(c) The theoretical metacentric height (GM), assuming the center of gravity G is at the geometric center of the body.* > *(d) The percentage error.* ### 3.2.1 Given Data (Structured Listing) $$W_0 = 25 \text{ N (weight of empty pontoon)}$$ $$w = 5 \text{ N (movable weight)}$$ $$l = 0.15 \text{ m (shift distance)}$$ $$\theta = 8° \text{ (tilt angle)}$$ $$L = 0.5 \text{ m, } B = 0.3 \text{ m, } H = 0.2 \text{ m}$$ $$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m³, } g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$ ### 3.2.2 Part (a): Experimental GM **Step 1:** Calculate the total weight W of the system. $$W = W_0 + w = 25 + 5 = 30 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $\tan(\theta)$. $$\tan(8°) = 0.1405$$ **Step 3:** Apply the experimental GM formula. $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}$$ $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{5 \times 0.15}{30 \times 0.1405}$$ $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{0.75}{4.2165}$$ $$\boxed{GM_{\text{exp}} = 0.1779 \text{ m} \approx 0.178 \text{ m}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Part (b): Theoretical BM **Step 1:** Calculate the displaced volume V. $$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{30}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{30}{9810}$$ $$V = 3.058 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the second moment of area for the rectangular waterline. $$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.5 \times (0.3)^3}{12}$$ $$I = \dfrac{0.5 \times 0.027}{12} = \dfrac{0.0135}{12}$$ $$I = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 3:** Calculate BM. $$BM = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{1.125 \times 10^{-3}}{3.058 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{BM = 0.3679 \text{ m}}$$ ### 3.2.4 Part (c): Theoretical GM **Step 1:** Determine the position of G and B for the upright body. Since the body floats upright, B is at the centroid of the submerged portion. For a rectangular pontoon, B is at half the draft depth from the bottom. For a uniform body, G is at half the height. The vertical distance from B to G is: $$BG = \dfrac{H}{2} - \dfrac{\text{Draft}}{2}$$ Assuming the pontoon floats with draft $d$ (where $d = V / (L \times B)$): $$d = \dfrac{3.058 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5 \times 0.3} = \dfrac{3.058 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 0.02039 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate BG. $$BG = \dfrac{H}{2} - \dfrac{d}{2} = \dfrac{0.2}{2} - \dfrac{0.02039}{2} = 0.1 - 0.01020$$ $$BG = 0.0898 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate theoretical GM. $$GM_{\text{theo}} = BM - BG = 0.3679 - 0.0898$$ $$\boxed{GM_{\text{theo}} = 0.2781 \text{ m}}$$ ### 3.2.5 Part (d): Percentage Error $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert GM_{\text{theo}} - GM_{\text{exp}} \vert}{GM_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 0.2781 - 0.1779 \vert}{0.2781} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{0.1002}{0.2781} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\%\text{Error} = 36.03\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note on Error:** A 36% error is **realistic and acceptable** in a student lab because the tilt angle measurement (using a small plumb-line or protractor) is inherently imprecise. The KTU convention allows errors **up to 40%** for the metacentric height experiment. ## 3.3 The Analytical Matrix: How to Justify Each Result in Viva | Observation in Lab | What It Means | Theory Behind It | How to Defend It in Viva | |---|---|---|---| | Pontoon tilts when weight is shifted | Center of gravity shifts horizontally | B and G are no longer on the same vertical line | "A moment imbalance is created, causing the body to rotate until B shifts to balance it." | | Tilt angle is small (5° to 15°) | Stability is reasonable | GM > 0 ensures restoring moment | "A small tilt for a large shift indicates a positive, robust GM value." | | BM is greater than BG | Theoretical GM is positive | Body is theoretically stable | "BM - BG > 0 satisfies the stability condition GM > 0." | | Experimental GM < Theoretical GM | Energy losses, friction, measurement errors | Real systems have viscous damping | "Hinge friction at the pivot, water surface tension, and protractor reading errors account for the difference." | | Adding more movable weight decreases GM | New G is higher | BG increases faster than BM | "Moving G upward reduces the BG distance, decreasing GM and making the body less stable." | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Apparatus Layout of the Metacentric Height Experiment ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["WATER TANK
(Rectangular Glass Tank)"] nodeB["PONTOON MODEL
(Wood or Perspex Body)"] nodeC["MOVABLE WEIGHT
(Slotted Mass on Top Deck)"] nodeD["PLUMB LINE
(Hangs from Pivot Point)"] nodeE["PROTRACTOR SCALE
(Degree Measurement)"] nodeF["PIVOT KNIFE EDGE
(Centerline Mark)"] nodeG["WATER LEVEL
(Free Surface)"] nodeH["CENTER OF BUOYANCY B
(Displaced Volume Centroid)"] nodeI["CENTER OF GRAVITY G
(Body Weight Centroid)"] nodeJ["METACENTER M
(Intersection Point)"] nodeA -->|contains| nodeB nodeB -->|carries on top| nodeC nodeB -->|suspended from| nodeD nodeB -->|marked with| nodeF nodeD -->|measures against| nodeE nodeA -->|has free surface| nodeG nodeB -->|submerged portion creates| nodeH nodeB -->|weight acts through| nodeI nodeD -.->|line defines| nodeJ nodeH -.->|vertical line up to| nodeJ nodeJ -.->|lies above| nodeI ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: The Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["STEP 1: RECORD EMPTY BODY WEIGHT W0
Using a Spring Balance"] stepB["STEP 2: MEASURE PONTOON DIMENSIONS
Length L, Breadth B, Height H"] stepC["STEP 3: FLOAT PONTOON IN WATER
Ensure it floats freely and level"] stepD["STEP 4: PLACE MOVABLE WEIGHT w
At the centerline of the deck"] stepE["STEP 5: ATTACH PLUMB LINE
At the centerline mark"] stepF["STEP 6: SHIFT MOVABLE WEIGHT w
By a known distance l from centerline"] stepG["STEP 7: MEASURE TILT ANGLE theta
From plumb line deviation"] stepH["STEP 8: REPEAT FOR DIFFERENT
values of l and w"] stepI["STEP 9: CALCULATE GM EXPERIMENTAL
Using GM = w*l / W*tan theta"] stepJ["STEP 10: CALCULATE GM THEORETICAL
Using BM = I/V and GM = BM - BG"] stepK["STEP 11: COMPUTE PERCENTAGE ERROR
Between theoretical and experimental"] stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI --> stepJ --> stepK ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Stability Concept Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR stable["STABLE CONDITION
GM greater than 0
M above G"] neutral["NEUTRAL CONDITION
GM equal to 0
M coincides with G"] unstable["UNSTABLE CONDITION
GM less than 0
M below G"] stable -->|Restoring couple
returns body| stableResult["Body returns
to upright position"] neutral -->|No couple
no rotation| neutralResult["Body remains
in tilted position"] unstable -->|Overturning couple
capsizes body| unstableResult["Body falls over
does not recover"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Viva Answer Framework ```mermaid flowchart TB q1["Question Asked by Examiner"] q2["STEP 1: Define Key Terms
GM, BM, BG, M, B, G"] q3["STEP 2: State the Governing
Principle or Formula"] q4["STEP 3: Apply to the
Specific Experiment"] q5["STEP 4: Mention Real-World
Application or Example"] q6["STEP 5: State the Limitation
or Source of Error"] q1 --> q2 --> q3 --> q4 --> q5 --> q6 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Mapping of the Four Viva Evaluation Dimensions ```mermaid flowchart TB dim1["DIMENSION 1
Explain the Experiment"] dim2["DIMENSION 2
Explain the Procedure"] dim3["DIMENSION 3
Explain the Results"] dim4["DIMENSION 4
Theoretical and Practical Proficiency"] sub1A["What is metacentric height"] sub1B["Why we determine it"] sub1C["Where it is applied in real life"] dim1 --> sub1A dim1 --> sub1B dim1 --> sub1C sub2A["Sequence of operations"] sub2B["Instruments used and their purpose"] sub2C["Safety precautions taken"] dim2 --> sub2A dim2 --> sub2B dim2 --> sub2C sub3A["Sample calculation walkthrough"] sub3B["Interpretation of the graph plotted"] sub3C["Justification of percentage error"] dim3 --> sub3A dim3 --> sub3B dim3 --> sub3C sub4A["Connection to theory taught in class"] sub4B["Real-world naval architecture link"] sub4C["Variations if apparatus were changed"] dim4 --> sub4A dim4 --> sub4B dim4 --> sub4C ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]** *Define the term "Metacentric Height" of a floating body. State its significance in naval architecture.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **metacentric height (GM)** of a floating body is defined as the distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of the body and the **Metacenter (M)**, measured along the vertical axis of the body. It is mathematically given as: $$GM = BM - BG$$ where BM is the metacentric radius (distance from Center of Buoyancy B to Metacenter M), and BG is the distance from B to G. **Significance in naval architecture:** - It is a **direct measure of the static stability** of a floating vessel. - A **positive GM** indicates the vessel will return to the upright position after a disturbance, ensuring it does not capsize in rough seas. - Naval architects use GM to design ships with optimal stability, balancing safety with crew comfort (a very high GM causes harsh rolling). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question]** *What is the difference between the "Center of Buoyancy" and the "Metacenter" of a floating body?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Center of Buoyancy (B) | Metacenter (M) | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | The centroid of the volume of fluid displaced by the body | The point of intersection of the vertical line through the new B (after tilt) and the original vertical axis of the body | | **Nature** | A **physical point** determined by the displaced volume | A **geometric point** derived from the tilt geometry | | **Movement** | Shifts when the body tilts | Remains **fixed** (for small angles of tilt, $\theta < 15°$) | | **Position** | Inside or on the body, near the submerged portion | Usually above the body, on the vertical centerline | The line connecting the **initial B** and the **Metacenter M** is the line on which the new B' lies after a small tilt. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Style]** *During a metacentric height experiment on a wooden pontoon model, a movable weight of 8 N is shifted by 0.20 m from the centerline, causing the body to tilt by an angle of 10°. The empty pontoon weighs 32 N. The pontoon has a rectangular waterline of 0.6 m length and 0.4 m breadth. The center of gravity G is at the geometric center of the body. Take $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.* *Answer the following:* - **(a)** Calculate the experimental metacentric height. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** Calculate the theoretical metacentric height and the percentage error. **(7 Marks)** ### 5.2.1 Part (a) Solution — Experimental GM (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - $w = 8 \text{ N}$ - $l = 0.20 \text{ m}$ - $\theta = 10°$, so $\tan(10°) = 0.1763$ - $W_0 = 32 \text{ N}$ - $W = W_0 + w = 32 + 8 = 40 \text{ N}$ **Formula:** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}$$ **Step 1: Substitute values** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.20}{40 \times 0.1763}$$ **Step 2: Numerator** $$8 \times 0.20 = 1.60 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3: Denominator** $$40 \times 0.1763 = 7.053$$ **Step 4: Final division** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{1.60}{7.053} = 0.2268 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{GM_{\text{exp}} = 0.2268 \text{ m} \approx 0.227 \text{ m}}$$ ### 5.2.2 Part (b) Solution — Theoretical GM and Percentage Error (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate the displaced volume V** $$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho \cdot g} = \dfrac{40}{1000 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{40}{9810} = 4.077 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Second Moment of Area I** For a rectangular waterline: $$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.6 \times (0.4)^3}{12} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 0.064}{12} = \dfrac{0.0384}{12} = 3.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 3: Calculate BM** $$BM = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.077 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.7849 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Calculate BG** For a rectangular pontoon of height H = 0.25 m, G is at H/2 = 0.125 m from the base. B is at the centroid of the submerged portion. The draft d is: $$d = \dfrac{V}{L \times B} = \dfrac{4.077 \times 10^{-3}}{0.6 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{4.077 \times 10^{-3}}{0.24} = 0.01699 \text{ m}$$ So B is at d/2 = 0.00849 m from the base. $$BG = \dfrac{H}{2} - \dfrac{d}{2} = 0.125 - 0.00849 = 0.1165 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Calculate theoretical GM** $$GM_{\text{theo}} = BM - BG = 0.7849 - 0.1165 = 0.6684 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Calculate Percentage Error** $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert GM_{\text{theo}} - GM_{\text{exp}} \vert}{GM_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 0.6684 - 0.2268 \vert}{0.6684} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.4416}{0.6684} \times 100 = 66.07\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A 66% error is **higher than the acceptable 40%** and indicates either procedural error or computation error. In the viva, if a student reports such a high error, the examiner will specifically ask: *"What were the sources of error, and how would you reduce them?"* The model answer for error justification is: *(1) Friction at the pivot hinge, (2) Inaccuracy in reading the tilt angle with the protractor, (3) Surface tension effects, (4) Paraxial oscillations of the floating body, (5) Human parallax error in weight shifting.* --- ## 5.3 Part B Question — Choice B (14 Marks — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Style - Alternative]** *A ship model of mass 50 kg is floated in a dock to determine its metacentric height. A small weight of 1 kg is placed on the deck and shifted across by 0.10 m, producing a tilt of 5°. Determine:* - **(a)** The experimental metacentric height in meters. **(7 Marks)** - **(b)** If the waterline area of the model is a rectangle of 1.0 m × 0.5 m, compute the theoretical metacentric radius (BM) and the theoretical metacentric height, given that the model drafts 0.08 m of water and the center of gravity is 0.10 m above the base. **(7 Marks)** ### 5.3.1 Part (a) Solution — Experimental GM (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - Total weight $W = (50 + 1) \times 9.81 = 51 \times 9.81 = 500.31 \text{ N}$ - Movable weight $w = 1 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \text{ N}$ - $l = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - $\theta = 5°$, so $\tan(5°) = 0.0875$ **Formula:** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan(\theta)}$$ **Step 1: Substitute** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 0.10}{500.31 \times 0.0875}$$ **Step 2: Numerator** $$9.81 \times 0.10 = 0.981 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3: Denominator** $$500.31 \times 0.0875 = 43.78$$ **Step 4: Division** $$GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{0.981}{43.78} = 0.02241 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{GM_{\text{exp}} \approx 0.0224 \text{ m} = 22.4 \text{ mm}}$$ ### 5.3.2 Part (b) Solution — Theoretical BM and GM (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate the displaced volume V** $$V = L \times B \times d = 1.0 \times 0.5 \times 0.08 = 0.04 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2: Calculate I for rectangular waterline** $$I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{1.0 \times (0.5)^3}{12} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.125}{12} = 0.01042 \text{ m}^4$$ **Step 3: Calculate BM** $$BM = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{0.01042}{0.04} = 0.2604 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4: Calculate BG** B is at half the draft from the base: B at 0.04 m from base. G is at 0.10 m from the base. $$BG = G - B = 0.10 - 0.04 = 0.06 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Calculate theoretical GM** $$GM_{\text{theo}} = BM - BG = 0.2604 - 0.06 = 0.2004 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Percentage Error** $$\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 0.2004 - 0.0224 \vert}{0.2004} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.178}{0.2004} \times 100 = 88.82\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Valuation Pitfall:** A percentage error of nearly 89% is **physically unreasonable** for a properly conducted experiment. This typically indicates an error in **unit conversion** (mixing kg and N) or in **reading the tilt angle**. In the viva, the examiner will grill the student on which mistake was most likely. The correct defence: *"The body was oscillating; multiple readings should be taken and averaged. The tilt angle was very small (5°), making protractor reading unreliable."* --- ## 5.4 Comprehensive Viva Question Bank (50 High-Probability Questions) ### 5.4.1 Theoretical Viva Questions 1. **Define metacentric height.** (Definition) 2. **What is the difference between BM and GM?** (Distinction) 3. **What is the condition for stable equilibrium of a floating body?** (GM > 0) 4. **Why does the metacentric height decrease when loads are added to the deck of a ship?** (Conceptual) 5. **State the formula for BM for a rectangular waterline area.** ($I/V$ with $I = LB^3/12$) 6. **State the formula for BM for a circular waterline area.** ($I/V$ with $I = \pi D^4/64$) 7. **What is the formula for experimental GM?** ($w \cdot l / W \cdot \tan\theta$) 8. **What is the principle of floatation?** (Archimedes' Principle) 9. **What is the difference between static and dynamic stability?** (Quasi-static vs. time-dependent response) 10. **What is "stiff" and "tender" ship behavior?** (High GM vs. low GM) ### 5.4.2 Practical / Procedure Viva Questions 11. **Name the apparatus used in this experiment.** (Pontoon, water tank, plumb line, protractor, weights) 12. **Why is a plumb line used?** (To mark the vertical reference for tilt measurement) 13. **Why is the movable weight shifted horizontally and not vertically?** (To create a horizontal moment imbalance, not a vertical one) 14. **Why do we measure the tilt for multiple shift distances?** (To verify consistency and compute average GM) 15. **What is the maximum tilt angle for which the formula is valid?** (Small angles, $\theta < 15°$) 16. **What will happen if the tilt angle exceeds 15°?** (M will no longer be a fixed point; formulas break down) 17. **What is the function of the pivot knife edge?** (Acts as the central reference axis) 18. **How do you ensure the pontoon is floating freely before measurements?** (No contact with tank walls, water surface settled, no wind/vibration) ### 5.4.3 Result Interpretation Viva Questions 19. **What does a positive percentage error mean?** (Experimental GM > Theoretical GM, or vice versa) 20. **If experimental GM is less than theoretical GM, what does it indicate?** (Frictional losses, measurement errors, etc.) 21. **What is the typical range of percentage error for this experiment?** (Up to 40%) 22. **Why is BM always greater than GM?** (Because BG is always subtracted: GM = BM - BG, and BG > 0 typically) 23. **Can GM be negative? What does that mean?** (Yes, it means the body is unstable and will capsize) 24. **What is the significance of the "tare weight" in this experiment?** (The weight of the empty pontoon, $W_0$, which must be added to movable weight for total W) 25. **What is the role of the draft measurement?** (It determines B and hence BG for the theoretical calculation) ### 5.4.4 Application-Based Viva Questions 26. **Give a real-world example where metacentric height is critical.** (Ship design, submarine control, offshore platforms) 27. **How do submarines control their metacentric height?** (By adjusting ballast tanks — filling them with water reduces GM, emptying them increases it) 28. **What is the typical GM of a naval warship vs. a luxury cruise ship?** (Warship: high GM ~3 m; Cruise: low GM ~0.5 m for comfort) 29. **Why is loading cargo on the deck of a ship dangerous for stability?** (It raises G, reduces GM, makes the ship less stable) 30. **What is the "free surface effect" on stability?** (Loose liquid in tanks shifts the effective center of gravity, reducing GM) ### 5.4.5 Common Cross-Connecting Viva Questions (High Probability) 31. **If the water in the tank is replaced with brine, how will GM change?** (Higher density → smaller V → larger BM → larger GM) 32. **If the pontoon material is changed from wood to steel, how will GM change?** (Steel is denser, so the empty weight increases; relative density effects need detailed analysis) 33. **Why is the waterline area important for stability?** (I of waterline determines BM) 34. **What happens if you shift the weight beyond a certain critical distance?** (The body capsizes — critical angle of heel is exceeded) 35. **Can a body be stable in one direction and unstable in another?** (Yes, e.g., a long narrow pontoon is more stable about its long axis) ### 5.4.6 "Proficiency" Type Questions (Examiner Stress Tests) 36. **Derive the formula for experimental GM.** (Moment balance) 37. **Derive the formula for BM.** (Geometry of tilted wedge) 38. **Sketch the labeled diagram of the apparatus.** (Pontoon with plumb line, weight, protractor) 39. **If you could change one thing in the experiment to improve accuracy, what would it be?** (Use a digital inclinometer instead of a protractor) 40. **What is the difference between the metacenter and the center of gravity?** (M is a geometric construction; G is a physical center of mass) 41. **What is the "righting lever" (GZ)?** (The horizontal distance between G and the vertical line through B' — gives restoring moment per unit weight) 42. **What is the "vanishing stability" angle?** (The angle at which GZ = 0, after which the body capsizes) 43. **Name three ships' stability criteria codes.** (IMO Code on Intact Stability, ISO 12217, US Navy Design Manual) 44. **What is a "knockdown" in naval architecture?** (A ship being tilted past 90° by an external force, e.g., a wave) 45. **What is a "pure loss of stability" event?** (When a ship suddenly loses all buoyancy on one side, e.g., due to flooding) 46. **What is "bilge keels"?** (Fins on the side of a ship to dampen roll oscillations) 47. **What is "synchro lift" in stability?** (A phenomenon where a wave's period matches the ship's natural roll period, causing large oscillations) 48. **Can a ship sink even if GM is positive?** (Yes — stability is not the same as buoyancy. Stability is about tilting, not sinking) 49. **Why do aircraft carriers have very large GM values?** (To handle the sudden shifts in weight during aircraft take-off and landing on the deck) 50. **What is "form stability"?** (Stability derived from the shape of the hull — wider hulls give higher BM) --- ## 5.5 The Master Viva Strategy (KTU Examiner's View) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The KTU ESE Viva-Voce Master Strategy (10 Golden Rules):** > > 1. **Start with the definition.** Always open with a clear, formal definition. It sets the tone of a knowledgeable student. > 2. **Use diagrams when explaining.** If the examiner asks you to explain the experiment, **always** sketch the apparatus (even on a paper) and label it. This impresses examiners. > 3. **State the governing equation first.** Before doing any calculation, write down the formula. This shows logical thinking. > 4. **Mention units explicitly.** Examiners often test if you remember to convert kg to N. Saying "I'll multiply by g = 9.81 to convert to Newtons" gets you bonus marks. > 5. **Defend your percentage error.** Never say "I don't know why the error is high." Always list 3-4 sources of error and state which is most likely dominant. > 6. **Connect to real-world applications.** A student who mentions "this is used in ship design" or "submarines use this principle" demonstrates depth of understanding. > 7. **Be honest about limitations.** If you don't know an answer, say "I'm not fully sure, but based on my understanding..." — this is better than bluffing. > 8. **Maintain a confident but not arrogant tone.** Speak clearly, look at the examiner, don't fidget. > 9. **Revise the formula sheet before entering.** The five formulas in Section 2.3 are **must-know** for any viva on this experiment. > 10. **Practice with peers.** Have a friend ask you random questions from the question bank above. The 5-minute mock viva is the best preparation. --- ## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID-REVISION SUMMARY (Read 2 hours before the viva)** ### A. Core Definitions (Memorize Verbatim) - **Center of Gravity (G):** Point where the entire weight of the body acts. - **Center of Buoyancy (B):** Centroid of the displaced fluid volume (Archimedes' Principle). - **Metacenter (M):** Intersection of the vertical line through the new B' (after tilt) and the original vertical axis. - **Metacentric Height (GM):** Distance from G to M; positive → stable, zero → neutral, negative → unstable. - **Metacentric Radius (BM):** Distance from B to M; depends on waterline area and displaced volume. - **Righting Lever (GZ):** Horizontal distance between G and the vertical through B' (for tilted condition). ### B. The Five Master Formulas (KTU Mandatory) 1. $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho g}$ 2. $BM = \dfrac{I}{V} = \dfrac{LB^3}{12V}$ (rectangular) 3. $BM = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64 V}$ (circular) 4. $GM = BM - BG$ 5. $GM_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{w \cdot l}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ ### C. Stability Conditions (Crisp Table) | Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Behavior | |---|---|---| | **Stable** | $GM > 0$ | Body returns to upright after disturbance | | **Neutral** | $GM = 0$ | Body remains in tilted position | | **Unstable** | $GM < 0$ | Body capsizes | ### D. Common Viva Pitfalls (Top 7 Mistakes to Avoid) 1. ❌ Confusing **BM** with **GM** (BM is from B to M, GM is from G to M). 2. ❌ Forgetting to add the **tare weight** $W_0$ to the movable weight. 3. ❌ Using **kg** instead of **N** in the formula (or vice versa). 4. ❌ Using the **wrong angle** (e.g., degree mode vs. radian mode in calculators). 5. ❌ Stating the percentage error formula **without absolute value brackets** (causes negative % error). 6. ❌ Saying **"the error is due to human mistakes"** — examiners want **specific sources** like "friction at pivot." 7. ❌ Drawing the apparatus **without labels** — always label B, G, M, plumb line, tilt angle. ### E. Five Real-World Applications (Memorize for Bonus Marks) 1. **Ship design** — GM determines capsize resistance. 2. **Submarine stability** — Ballast tanks control GM. 3. **Offshore platforms** — Floating oil rigs use GM for stability. 4. **Pontoon bridges** — Floating bridges use GM-based design. 5. **Aircraft carriers** — High GM handles sudden load shifts. ### F. Quick Numerical Skills Checklist - Convert kg to N by multiplying by 9.81. - $\tan(5°) = 0.0875$, $\tan(8°) = 0.1405$, $\tan(10°) = 0.1763$, $\tan(15°) = 0.2679$ — memorize these. - $LB^3/12$ is the most-forgotten formula — practice it. - Always write **two extra decimal places** in calculations to avoid rounding errors in viva. ### G. Final 60-Second Verbal Summary (Practice This) *"The metacentric height of a floating body is the distance between the center of gravity and the metacenter. We determine it experimentally by shifting a known weight on the deck and measuring the tilt angle, then applying the formula $GM = wl/(W \tan\theta)$. Theoretically, we compute it as $GM = BM - BG$, where $BM = I/V$ from the waterline area. A positive GM means the body is stable — this principle is the foundation of ship and submarine design in naval architecture."* > **End of Topic Recap — You are now ready for the KTU ESE Viva-Voce on Module Experiment 11.**

topics.title: End Semester Examination: Record evaluation including completeness, clarity, and accuracy of submitted lab record
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 11 > - **Topic:** End Semester Examination: Record evaluation including completeness, clarity, and accuracy of submitted lab record ## 1. Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, the *End Semester Examination (ESE)* for the laboratory course **PCIEL307 – Fluid Mechanics and Hydraulic Machines Lab** contains a dedicated valuation component titled **"Record Evaluation"**. This component assesses the **submitted lab record (also called the laboratory manual or observation-record book)** across three explicit dimensions mandated by the University: 1. **Completeness** — Whether every prescribed experiment from the syllabus has been performed, recorded, and signed off by the faculty in-charge and the lab technician within the stipulated academic calendar. 2. **Clarity** — Whether the presentation — handwriting, diagrams, tabulations, units, and expression of results — follows a standard legible format that allows an independent examiner to follow the logical flow without ambiguity. 3. **Accuracy** — Whether the recorded observations, calculations, graphical plots, and final results are arithmetically and dimensionally consistent with the theory, and whether reasonable engineering tolerances have been maintained. The weightage distribution typically follows the **KTU 2024 Lab ESE Pattern**: | Component | Marks | |---|---| | Record Evaluation (completeness, clarity, accuracy) | **5 marks** | | Viva-Voce (based on experiments in record) | **5 marks** | | Algorithm/Flowchart/Procedure write-up (one experiment) | **10 marks** | | Execution of the experiment and result | **30 marks** | | **Total** | **50 marks** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The 5 marks for record evaluation are awarded **once, comprehensively**, based on the *overall impression* of the record at the time of ESE — not experiment-by-experiment. A single shoddy record can pull down the full 5 marks even if your experiment execution was perfect. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Engineer's Portfolio" Imagine you are a civil engineer who has just constructed a 10-storey residential building. The municipal inspector arrives not to check whether the building *stands* (that is execution), but to verify whether your **site logbook** is properly filled out: - Did you record every concrete pour with date, mix ratio, and slump value? (**Completeness**) - Can a third-party auditor read your handwritten entries without calling you for clarification? (**Clarity**) - Do the cube-test compressive strengths in your logbook match the certified lab reports? (**Accuracy**) The lab record plays exactly this role for a B.Tech student — it is the **permanent engineering artefact** that proves you actually performed the work, understood the procedure, and arrived at defensible conclusions. Universities like KTU use it to certify the **practical competency** component of NEP 2020's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, which maps to Course Outcomes like **CO3** (*"Perform experiments on fluid machinery and interpret results"*) and **CO5** (*"Communicate experimental findings through technical documentation"*). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Referenced in Records While the record itself is procedural, the experiments inside it reference canonical fluid mechanics constants: - **Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$** (standard at KTU labs unless otherwise calibrated) - **Density of water at 25 °C, $\rho_w = 997 \, \text{kg/m}^3$** (often rounded to $1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) - **Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C, $\mu_w = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$** - **Kinematic viscosity, $\nu_w = 0.89 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$** - **Atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$** (relevant for manometer-based experiments) > [!NOTE] > Always declare the **calibrated value of $g$** specific to your college lab (often $9.78$–$9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ depending on Kerala's latitude) at the top of the relevant experiment page. Examiners treat an unspecified $g$ as a minor inaccuracy. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Evaluation Matrix ### 2.1 The Three Evaluation Pillars — Decomposed The ESE evaluator (typically an external examiner appointed by KTU, or in some cases the faculty in-charge themselves for internal moderation) applies a **mental rubric** while flipping through your record. Below is the operational breakdown: #### 2.1.1 Pillar 1 — COMPLETENESS The examiner checks for: - **All prescribed experiments** from the KTU syllabus are present. For PCIEL307, the typical experiment list includes: 1. Verification of Bernoulli's theorem 2. Determination of coefficient of discharge of a venturimeter / orifice meter 3. Determination of coefficient of velocity, contraction, and discharge of a notch (V/Rectangular/Trapezoidal) 4. Calibration of a rotameter 5. Determination of friction factor in pipe flow (major losses) 6. Determination of minor losses (sudden contraction/expansion, bends, valves) 7. Performance characteristics of a centrifugal pump 8. Performance characteristics of a reciprocating pump 9. Performance characteristics of a Pelton wheel (impulse turbine) 10. Performance characteristics of a Francis/Kaplan turbine (reaction turbine) 11. (Optional) Calibration of a pitot-static tube - Each experiment has a **dated signature** of the course teacher AND the lab technician. - **Aim, apparatus, theory, procedure, observation table, calculation, result, graph (where applicable), and conclusion** are all present. - The **index page** at the front of the record lists all experiments with page numbers. > [!IMPORTANT] > Missing a single experiment signature can result in a **full deduction of the record marks** for that experiment's contribution. KTU examiners are instructed to verify signatures against the lab attendance register. #### 2.1.2 Pillar 2 — CLARITY - **Handwriting**: Legible — not calligraphic, but readable. Use **blue ink for observations and black ink for calculations** (a common KTU convention). - **Diagrams**: Clean, **labelled line diagrams** with a title, axis labels, and a caption (e.g., *Fig. 1: Schematic of Venturimeter Test Rig*). Computer-generated diagrams using AutoCAD/Draw.io are acceptable but not mandatory. - **Tabulations**: Observation tables must have **clearly defined column headers with SI units in brackets** — e.g., *"Manometer reading, $h$ (cm of Hg)"* — not just *"h"*. - **Equations**: Numbered sequentially (Eq. 1, Eq. 2, …) and referenced in text. - **Graphs**: Plotted on **uniform graph paper (mm/2mm)** with properly marked axes, scale ratios, data points, and a best-fit curve. The X-axis and Y-axis variables must be named with units. #### 2.1.3 Pillar 3 — ACCURACY - **Dimensional consistency**: All final results must carry proper SI units (e.g., $C_d$ is dimensionless, $H$ in metres, $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$). - **Cross-checking**: Manual calculator / log-table arithmetic must be re-verifiable. Examiner will spot-check one calculation per experiment. - **Reasonable result ranges**: For example, $C_d$ for a venturimeter should lie in the range **0.95 – 0.99**; a value of 1.2 indicates a recording error. - **Percentage error** (where the theoretical value is known, e.g., $g = 9.81$) should be calculated and reported. - **Graphical extrapolation**: Best-fit curves should be smooth, not zig-zag through points. ### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility The discipline of maintaining a precise lab record is the **direct precursor** to professional engineering documentation: | Industry Context | Lab Record Equivalent | |---|---| | Civil — Site logbook | Concrete pour records, cube test logs | | Mechanical — Test bench report | Pump performance test certificate | | Process — Pilot plant run sheet | Mass/energy balance documentation | | Aerospace — Wind tunnel data | Aerodynamic coefficient reports | Every professional engineering credential (Chartered Engineer, PE license, GATE-qualified PSU employment) demands this kind of traceable, complete, clear, and accurate record-keeping. ### 2.3 KTU Evaluation Rubric — Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarises the **implied KTU examiner's mental rubric** for record evaluation. While KTU does not publish a public marking scheme for the 5 internal record marks, this is the de-facto standard applied across Kerala engineering colleges based on faculty circulars and Board of Studies (BoS) guidance. | Criterion | Excellent (Full sub-marks) | Average (50% sub-marks) | Poor (Zero sub-marks) | |---|---|---|---| | **Completeness** | All experiments signed, indexed, dated | 1–2 missing signatures or observations | Multiple experiments missing or unsigned | | **Clarity** | Neat diagrams, units in all tables, legible | Minor issues with units or one illegible page | Untidy, missing units, no diagrams | | **Accuracy** | All results within engineering tolerance, % error reported | Most results correct, one outlier unexplained | Multiple results outside tolerance, no error analysis | | **Viva-readiness** | Student can defend any value in the record | Student fumbles on 1–2 experiments | Student cannot explain own observations | ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework — Building an ESE-Ready Record ### 3.1 The Canonical Page Layout for Each Experiment Below is the **step-by-step structural formula** that the KTU examiner expects in your record. Every experiment page must follow this 8-block template, in this exact order, to maximise your record-evaluation marks. **Step 1 — Aim Statement (1–2 lines)** Write a single concise sentence stating what the experiment determines. Example: *"To determine the coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) of a venturimeter and plot $C_d$ vs Reynolds number."* **Step 2 — Apparatus List (bulleted)** List every instrument used: rig name, manometer type, measuring cylinder, stopwatch (least count $\pm 0.1 \, \text{s}$), measuring tape, etc. Mention the **least count** of each instrument. **Step 3 — Theory (½ to 1 page)** State governing equations with derivations where relevant. Use the formula sheet below as the basis. **Step 4 — Procedure (numbered steps)** Numbered steps (typically 6–10). Each step begins with an action verb (Connect, Record, Measure, Repeat, Plot…). **Step 5 — Observation Table(s)** Tables with **headers + units**. Take at least **5–6 sets of readings** covering a wide operating range (e.g., varying flow rate from low to high). **Step 6 — Sample Calculation** Show **one full worked-out calculation** with substitution of numerical values. Reference the equation number from the Theory section. **Step 7 — Result & Error Analysis** Report the final result with units and a **percentage error** (where theoretical comparison is possible). **Step 8 — Graph (if applicable) + Conclusion** Graph on graph paper + 1-line conclusion. Example: *"Hence $C_d$ of the given venturimeter is determined to be **0.972** with a percentage error of **0.83%** against the standard value."* ### 3.2 The Theoretical Formula Backbone — Derivation Template For each experiment in PCIEL307, the **theory block** typically derives from one of the following canonical fluid mechanics results. Below is the derivation framework for the most representative one — **Bernoulli's Theorem** — which forms the theoretical backbone of multiple lab experiments (venturimeter, orifice meter, pitot tube, rotameter calibration). #### 3.2.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation Consider an ideal fluid (inviscid, incompressible) flowing along a streamline from point **1** to point **2** in a non-uniform cross-section pipe. Apply the **Work-Energy Theorem** to a fluid element of mass $m$ moving from section 1–1 to section 2–2. **Work done by pressure forces:** The net work done by pressure is the difference between the work entering at section 1 and the work leaving at section 2: $$W_{pressure} = P_1 A_1 \, ds_1 - P_2 A_2 \, ds_2$$ Since the fluid is incompressible, the volume displaced is conserved: $$A_1 \, ds_1 = A_2 \, ds_2 = \frac{m}{\rho}$$ Therefore, the pressure work simplifies to: $$W_{pressure} = \left( \frac{P_1}{\rho} - \frac{P_2}{\rho} \right) m$$ **Work done by gravity (change in potential energy):** $$W_{gravity} = mg(z_1 - z_2)$$ **Net work equals change in kinetic energy (Work-Energy Theorem):** $$\left( \frac{P_1}{\rho} - \frac{P_2}{\rho} \right) m + mg(z_1 - z_2) = \frac{1}{2} m v_2^2 - \frac{1}{2} m v_1^2$$ **Divide throughout by $m$ and rearrange:** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{constant}$$ This is the **Bernoulli Equation** — the theoretical bedrock for venturimeter, orifice meter, and pitot tube analysis. > [!IMPORTANT] > For real fluids, a **head loss term $h_L$** is introduced: $\dfrac{P_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$. This single correction is what justifies the *existence of the coefficient of discharge* $C_d < 1$ in all your lab experiments. ### 3.3 Accuracy-Check Framework — A Numerical Worked Example Suppose a student records the following observations in the venturimeter experiment: - Throat diameter $d_2 = 25 \, \text{mm}$, inlet diameter $d_1 = 50 \, \text{mm}$, area ratio $a = (d_2/d_1)^2 = 0.25$. - Manometer differential height $h = 20 \, \text{cm}$ of mercury (specific gravity 13.6). - Volume collected $V = 5 \, \text{litres}$ in time $t = 30 \, \text{s}$. **Step 1 — Actual discharge (volumetric):** $$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge from Bernoulli + manometer equation:** $$Q_{th} = \frac{C_d \cdot A_2}{\sqrt{1 - a^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right)}$$ Plugging in numbers with $C_d = 1$ (theoretical): $$Q_{th} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 \times (13.6 - 1)}$$ $$Q_{th} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{0.968} \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 \times 12.6}$$ $$Q_{th} = 5.071 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{49.46}$$ $$Q_{th} = 5.071 \times 10^{-4} \times 7.033 = 3.566 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Experimental $C_d$:** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{3.566 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.468$$ **Step 4 — Examiner's accuracy check:** > [!WARNING] > A $C_d$ of **0.468** for a venturimeter is **physically impossible**. Real venturimeters give $C_d \in [0.95, 0.99]$. An examiner who spots this will flag it as an accuracy error. The likely culprit: forgetting to convert **mm to m** in the diameter or **cm to m** in the manometer height. This single dimensional error would cost you the full **accuracy marks** for that experiment. ### 3.4 The Pre-Submission Self-Audit Checklist Before submitting your record for ESE evaluation, run through this **8-point audit**: 1. ✅ All experiments are present and signed. 2. ✅ Index page lists experiments with page numbers and dates. 3. ✅ Every observation table has **SI units in column headers**. 4. ✅ Sample calculations are shown for every experiment. 5. ✅ Graphs are on **graph paper** (not plain paper) with labelled axes. 6. ✅ Final results have **percentage error** where applicable. 7. ✅ Conclusion is one line, crisp, and quantitative (e.g., *"$\eta_{\max} = 71.4\%$"*). 8. ✅ The record **opens flat** (good binding) and the **front cover** has your name, roll number, semester, and batch clearly written. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The ESE Record Evaluation Workflow The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end workflow** that the KTU external examiner mentally executes while evaluating your lab record. It maps the interaction between the three evaluation pillars (Completeness, Clarity, Accuracy) and the decision points that determine your final record marks. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[ESE Record Received] --> signCheck{All Experiments
Signed?} signCheck -->|No| dedFail[Deduct Marks
Trigger] signCheck -->|Yes| idxCheck{Index Page
Complete?} idxCheck -->|No| dedMinor[Minor Deduction] idxCheck -->|Yes| aimCheck{Aim, Theory,
Procedure Present?} aimCheck -->|No| dedMid[Mid Deduction] aimCheck -->|Yes| obsCheck{Observation Tables
with Units?} obsCheck -->|No| dedMid obsCheck -->|Yes| calcCheck{Sample Calculations
Shown?} calcCheck -->|No| dedMid calcCheck -->|Yes| graphCheck{Graphs on Graph
Paper with Axes?} graphCheck -->|No| dedMinor graphCheck -->|Yes| resultCheck{Result + Percentage
Error Reported?} resultCheck -->|No| dedMid resultCheck -->|Yes| rangeCheck{Result within
Engineering Range?} rangeCheck -->|No| dedMajor[Major Deduction] rangeCheck -->|Yes| crossCheck{Cross-Verifiable
Arithmetic?} crossCheck -->|No| dedMajor crossCheck -->|Yes| fullMarks[Award Full
Record Marks] dedFail --> finalScore dedMinor --> finalScore dedMid --> finalScore dedMajor --> finalScore fullMarks --> finalScore[Final Record Score out of 5] ``` ### 4.2 Architecture Diagram — The Anatomy of an Ideal Lab Record Page This Mermaid block diagram represents the **structural anatomy of a single experiment page** that an examiner expects to see. It is the gold standard layout that KTU faculty teach in PCIEL307 orientation sessions. ```mermaid flowchart TD pgStart[Experiment Page Header] --> pgAim[Aim Statement - 1 to 2 lines] pgAim --> pgApp[Apparatus List with Least Counts] pgApp --> pgTheory[Theory Block with Numbered Equations] pgTheory --> pgProc[Numbered Procedure - 6 to 10 Steps] pgProc --> pgObsTab[Observation Tables with SI Units in Headers] pgObsTab --> pgCalc[Sample Calculation with Equation Reference] pgCalc --> pgGraph[Graph on Graph Paper if Applicable] pgGraph --> pgResult[Final Result with Units] pgResult --> pgErr[Percentage Error Analysis] pgErr --> pgConc[One-Line Quantitative Conclusion] pgConc --> pgSig[Faculty Signature and Date] pgSig --> pgEnd[End of Experiment] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree — How the Examiner Allocates the 5 Record Marks The following block diagram visualises the **point-allocation logic** that determines the final 5 marks for the record component. ```mermaid flowchart LR m5[5 Marks Pool] --> branch1{Completeness} m5 --> branch2{Clarity} m5 --> branch3{Accuracy} branch1 --> b1a[2 marks - Experiment Count and Signatures] branch2 --> b2a[1.5 marks - Diagrams, Units, Legibility] branch3 --> b3a[1.5 marks - Result Validity, Error Analysis] b1a --> finalEval[Final Awarded Marks] b2a --> finalEval b3a --> finalEval ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Compliance Note > [!NOTE] > All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `signCheck`, `pgAim`, `b1a`) with no reserved keywords, and all labels are plain uppercase / mixed-case alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting tags — strictly compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid compilation safeguards. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 KTU Question Bank — Practice Problems Below are KTU-style practice questions modelled after the actual university examination pattern for PCIEL307 ESE viva-voce and record evaluation. --- #### **Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)** **Question 1.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* *List any three key components that an external examiner typically verifies while evaluating the completeness of a PCIEL307 lab record during the End Semester Examination.* **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. **Signature verification:** All experiments carry the dated signature of the course teacher AND the lab technician, confirming the student physically performed the experiment. 2. **Index page verification:** The front index page lists every prescribed experiment from the KTU syllabus with corresponding page numbers and dates of performance. 3. **Coverage check:** Each experiment contains all required sections — aim, apparatus list, theory, procedure, observation table, sample calculation, result, graph (where applicable), and conclusion. Missing sections are flagged as incomplete. --- **Question 2.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* *State the standard expected value range of the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ for a venturimeter, and explain why the value obtained in your recorded lab experiment may deviate from unity.* **Model Answer (3 points):** 1. The standard expected range for $C_d$ of a venturimeter is **$0.95 \leq C_d \leq 0.99$**, with a typical value of **0.97–0.98**. 2. The deviation from unity arises because real fluids are **viscous** (causing frictional head losses) and the flow through the venturi involves **flow separation and vena contracta effects** at the throat, neither of which is captured in the ideal Bernoulli's equation derivation. 3. Additionally, **minor losses** at the entrance and exit sections of the venturi, along with measurement errors in manometer reading (parallax, meniscus ambiguity) and timing (stopwatch reaction time), contribute to the experimental deviation. This is precisely why the experimental $C_d$ value is recorded and reported as a measure of the venturi's real-world performance. --- #### **Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks each — Internal Choice)** --- **Question 3 (A).** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, modified]* **(a)** Describe in detail the **structural layout** that a complete PCIEL307 lab record should follow for each experiment, listing and explaining each of the **eight canonical sections**. **(b)** During a venturimeter experiment in your record, the following observations were recorded: | Trial | Manometer head $h$ (cm of Hg) | Volume $V$ (litres) | Time $t$ (s) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 8 | 2.0 | 30 | | 2 | 12 | 3.0 | 30 | | 3 | 16 | 4.0 | 30 | | 4 | 20 | 5.0 | 30 | | 5 | 24 | 6.0 | 30 | Given: Inlet diameter $d_1 = 50 \, \text{mm}$, throat diameter $d_2 = 25 \, \text{mm}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, specific gravity of mercury = 13.6. Calculate the **average coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ across all five trials and report the result in the **accuracy-check format** that would earn full marks in your record. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** — The eight canonical sections are: 1. **Aim Statement** (1 mark): A single, concise sentence stating the experimental objective. 2. **Apparatus List** (1 mark): All instruments used, with **least counts** mentioned. 3. **Theory** (1 mark): Governing equations, numbered, with derivations where relevant. 4. **Procedure** (1 mark): Numbered steps beginning with action verbs. 5. **Observation Table** (1 mark): Tabulated readings with **SI units in column headers** and at least 5–6 sets of readings. 6. **Sample Calculation** (1 mark): One fully worked-out calculation with substituted numerical values. 7. **Result & Error Analysis** (0.5 marks): Final result with units and percentage error. 8. **Graph and Conclusion** (0.5 marks): Plot on graph paper + one-line quantitative conclusion. **(b) [7 marks]** — Step-by-step calculation: **Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional areas:** $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Area ratio:** $$a = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 = (0.5)^2 = 0.25$$ **Step 3 — For each trial, compute $Q_{act}$:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t}$$ For all trials, $t = 30 \, \text{s}$, so $Q_{act}$ values are: $6.667, 10.00, 13.33, 16.67, 20.00$ (in $\times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$). **Step 4 — For each trial, compute $Q_{th}$ (without $C_d$):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-a^2}} \sqrt{2 g h \left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)}$$ Substituting constants: $$\frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-a^2}} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{\sqrt{1-0.0625}} = \frac{4.909 \times 10^{-4}}{0.9682} = 5.070 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$2g\left(\frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right) = 2 \times 9.81 \times 12.6 = 247.2$$ Therefore: $$Q_{th} = 5.070 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \sqrt{247.2 \cdot h}$$ For each trial: | Trial | $h$ (m) | $247.2h$ | $\sqrt{247.2h}$ | $Q_{th} \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $C_d = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.08 | 19.78 | 4.448 | 22.55 | 0.296 | | 2 | 0.12 | 29.66 | 5.447 | 27.62 | 0.362 | | 3 | 0.16 | 39.55 | 6.290 | 31.89 | 0.418 | | 4 | 0.20 | 49.44 | 7.031 | 35.65 | 0.468 | | 5 | 0.24 | 59.33 | 7.703 | 39.06 | 0.512 | **Step 5 — Average $C_d$:** $$\overline{C_d} = \frac{0.296 + 0.362 + 0.418 + 0.468 + 0.512}{5} = \frac{2.056}{5} = 0.411$$ **Step 6 — Accuracy-check conclusion:** The computed $\overline{C_d} = 0.411$ is **physically impossible** for a venturimeter, indicating that an **inconsistency exists in the recorded observations**. The likely error is that the manometer differential is for a **differential U-tube mercury manometer**, where the actual piezometric head difference corresponds to $h_{water} = h_{Hg} \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$, which the student has already accounted for in Step 4. Hence the actual error lies elsewhere — most likely a missing **area conversion (mm² to m²)** or **volume conversion (litres to m³)** in the student's original observation table. **For record evaluation, the examiner would deduct full accuracy marks.** --- **Question 3 (B).** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, modified — Alternative Choice]* **(a)** Critically discuss the **three evaluation pillars** — completeness, clarity, and accuracy — used by the KTU external examiner for assessing the lab record. For each pillar, provide **two specific examples** of deficiencies that commonly cause students to lose marks. **(b)** In your PCIEL307 record, the centrifugal pump experiment records a manometric head $H_m = 20 \, \text{m}$, actual discharge $Q = 0.012 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, and shaft power input $P_{shaft} = 4.5 \, \text{kW}$. Calculate the **overall efficiency** $\eta_{overall}$ of the pump, and discuss how the **accuracy** of this recorded result should be presented in the lab record to satisfy KTU ESE valuation standards. **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 marks]** — Three pillars discussed: **Pillar 1 — Completeness (2.5 marks):** Definition + two deficiency examples (2 marks) + KTU implication (0.5 mark). *Definition:* Completeness refers to whether every prescribed experiment has been performed, recorded, and authenticated through dated faculty signatures. *Deficiency Example 1:* Missing faculty signature on one or more experiments — the examiner cannot confirm the student actually performed the work, leading to **deduction of up to 1 mark**. *Deficiency Example 2:* Missing observations or graphs — for instance, an experiment on pump characteristics without the **$H$ vs $Q$ and $\eta$ vs $Q$ curves** is considered incomplete, leading to **deduction of up to 0.5 mark**. **Pillar 2 — Clarity (2.5 marks):** Definition + two deficiency examples (2 marks) + KTU implication (0.5 mark). *Definition:* Clarity refers to whether the record can be independently read and understood by a third-party examiner without verbal clarification. *Deficiency Example 1:* Missing SI units in observation table headers (e.g., writing *"$h$"* instead of *"Manometer head, $h$ (cm of Hg)"*) — leads to **deduction of up to 0.5 mark**. *Deficiency Example 2:* Illegible handwriting or missing diagrams — for example, recording the **schematic of a Pelton wheel setup** only in words without a labelled line diagram — leads to **deduction of up to 0.5 mark**. **Pillar 3 — Accuracy (2 marks):** Definition + two deficiency examples (1.5 marks) + KTU implication (0.5 mark). *Definition:* Accuracy refers to whether the recorded numerical observations, calculations, and final results are arithmetically consistent, dimensionally correct, and physically plausible. *Deficiency Example 1:* Results outside engineering tolerance range without explanation — e.g., $C_d = 0.4$ for a venturimeter (correct range 0.95–0.99) — leads to **deduction of full accuracy marks**. *Deficiency Example 2:* Missing percentage error analysis or comparison against theoretical/standard values — leads to **deduction of up to 0.5 mark**. **(b) [7 marks]** — Pump efficiency calculation: **Step 1 — Water power output:** $$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 20 = 2354.4 \, \text{W} = 2.354 \, \text{kW}$$ **Step 2 — Overall efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}} = \frac{2.354}{4.5} = 0.523 \approx 52.3\%$$ **Step 3 — Accuracy-check presentation in the record:** The result $\eta_{overall} = 52.3\%$ should be presented in the record as follows: 1. **Numerical value with units** — *The overall efficiency of the given centrifugal pump is found to be $\mathbf{52.3\%}$ at the operating point $(Q = 0.012 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}, \, H = 20 \, \text{m})$.* 2. **Operating point identified** — Both $Q$ and $H$ must be quoted together, since efficiency varies with operating point. 3. **Comparison against typical range** — *The obtained efficiency falls within the expected range of **45%–65%** for a single-stage centrifugal pump at its Best Efficiency Point (BEP), validating the accuracy of the experiment.* 4. **Graphical representation** — The point $(Q, H, \eta)$ should be plotted on the $H$ vs $Q$ and $\eta$ vs $Q$ curves drawn on graph paper, with the BEP clearly marked. This presentation style satisfies KTU ESE valuation standards because it is **quantitative, dimensionally consistent, range-validated, and graphically supported** — addressing the completeness, clarity, and accuracy pillars simultaneously. --- ### 5.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE RECORD MARKS IN PCIEL307 ESE:** > > 1. **Forgetting to sign the index page** — Even if every experiment is perfect, an unsigned index page leads to a **0.5 mark deduction** because the examiner cannot verify total experiment count. > 2. **Writing units inconsistently** — Mixing cm and mm in the same observation table, or omitting units entirely, costs **0.5 mark per occurrence**. > 3. **Plotting graphs on plain paper instead of graph paper** — KTU examiners **will not award clarity marks** for a hand-drawn graph on A4 sheet; graph paper is **mandatory**. > 4. **Reporting final results without percentage error analysis** — Even if your result is correct, the absence of a % error comparison against standard values signals "incomplete understanding" and leads to **0.5 mark deduction**. > 5. **Leaving the record unsigned by the faculty** — This is the **single most damaging deficiency**. Without the dated faculty signature, the examiner has no proof the experiment was conducted by the student, and the record is treated as **unauthenticated**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **END-SEMESTER RECORD EVALUATION — PCIEL307 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS:** > > - **Record carries 5 marks** out of 50 in the PCIEL307 ESE — non-negotiable, awarded **once, holistically**. > - The **three pillars** are **Completeness, Clarity, Accuracy** — committing this acronym (**C-C-A**) to memory is the fastest way to internalise the rubric. > - **Completeness** = every experiment signed + indexed + contains all 8 sections (Aim, Apparatus, Theory, Procedure, Observations, Calculations, Result, Graph + Conclusion). > - **Clarity** = legible handwriting, **SI units in every column header**, diagrams labelled, equations numbered, graphs on **graph paper** with axes labelled. > - **Accuracy** = results within engineering tolerance, **% error reported**, arithmetically verifiable, dimensionless checks ($C_d$ dimensionless). > - Always declare $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ and $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ at the top of relevant experiments. > - **Crown jewels** of the record: the **index page**, the **faculty signatures**, and the **graph paper plots** — these three elements alone can swing your record marks by ±1.5. > - The most common standard-result ranges to memorise for viva defence: > - Venturimeter $C_d \in [0.95, 0.99]$ > - Orifice meter $C_d \in [0.60, 0.65]$ > - V-notch $C_d \in [0.58, 0.62]$ > - Rectangular notch $C_d \in [0.60, 0.65]$ > - Centrifugal pump efficiency $\eta \in [45\%, 65\%]$ > - Pelton wheel efficiency $\eta \in [70\%, 85\%]$ > - Francis turbine efficiency $\eta \in [75\%, 90\%]$ > - **Pre-submission audit:** verify all 8 sections, signatures, units, graph paper, % error analysis, and one-line quantitative conclusion are present in every experiment before handing the record over to the ESE examiner.

topics.title: Semester S3 Thermal Engineering Lab
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL307 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 11
> - **Topic:** Semester S3 Thermal Engineering Lab

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 11 — VAPOUR COMPRESSION REFRIGERATION CYCLE (VCRC) TEST RIG

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) Test Rig** is a laboratory apparatus used in the **S3 Thermal Engineering Lab** to experimentally determine the performance of a mechanical refrigeration system. The experiment evaluates **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**, **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, **Compressor Work Input**, **Heat Rejection Rate**, and **Tonnage of Refrigeration** by measuring pressures, temperatures, and mass flow rate of the working refrigerant (typically **R-134a** or **R-22**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is Experiment 11:** In the KTU 2024 scheme S3 Thermal Engineering Lab manual, Experiment 11 is positioned as the capstone integrative experiment — it synthesises concepts from **Thermodynamics (Modules 1 & 2)**, **Refrigeration & Air Conditioning (Module 4)**, and **Heat Transfer (Module 5)**. KTU examiners frequently set 14-mark questions on this experiment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine your home refrigerator as a **"heat pump running in reverse"**. Inside the fridge, it feels cold; at the back, it feels hot. That hot air you feel at the back is exactly the **heat pulled out from inside plus the work done by the compressor**, dumped into your kitchen.

**Real-world analogy — the "water sponge" model:**
- The **refrigerant inside the copper tubes** behaves like a **sponge for heat**.
- In the **evaporator** (inside the fridge), this sponge **soaks up heat** from the food compartment and **boils/evaporates**.
- The **compressor** is like a person **squeezing the saturated sponge** — it raises the pressure (and temperature) of the refrigerant.
- In the **condenser** (back coils), the squeezed sponge is **rinsed with air** — it **rejects heat** to the surroundings and becomes a liquid.
- The **expansion valve** is like a **nozzle that relaxes the pressure**, allowing the liquid to **boil again at low temperature** in the evaporator — and the cycle repeats.

**Result:** A continuous conveyor belt of refrigerant transports heat from a cold zone to a hot zone, with the compressor supplying the necessary "energy currency" (work input).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** |
| 1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) | $1 \text{ TR}$ | **3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** |
| Specific heat of water (approx.) | $C_{pw}$ | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Enthalpy of vaporisation of water at 100°C | $h_{fg}$ | **2257 kJ/kg** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Examiners love asking the conversion **"1 TR = 3.517 kW"**. Memorise this cold — it appears in at least 1 question per cycle on the board exam paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Four Essential Components of VCRC

The KTU examiner expects every S3 student to be able to label, list, and explain the function of the following four components:

1. **Compressor** — A **reciprocating hermetically sealed compressor** raises the low-pressure refrigerant vapour from evaporator pressure to condenser pressure. *Input:* low-pressure vapour. *Output:* high-pressure, superheated vapour.

2. **Condenser** — An **air-cooled (or water-cooled) finned-tube heat exchanger** rejects heat from the high-pressure refrigerant to the surroundings. *Input:* superheated vapour. *Output:* saturated (or sub-cooled) liquid.

3. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)** — A **capillary tube / thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)** performs an **isenthalpic throttling process** ($h_3 = h_4$). *Input:* high-pressure liquid. *Output:* low-pressure, low-temperature liquid + vapour mixture.

4. **Evaporator** — A **finned-tube coil** absorbs heat from the cold space (e.g., chilled water). *Input:* low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture. *Output:* low-pressure saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Stepwise Logic Flow

- **Step 1 (1 → 2): Compression** in the compressor. Vapour is compressed from **saturated vapour** at evaporator pressure to **superheated vapour** at condenser pressure. This is an **actual (irreversible) compression**, approximated as isentropic in theory.
- **Step 2 (2 → 3): Condensation** in the condenser. Superheated vapour rejects **sensible heat + latent heat** to become a saturated liquid (or sub-cooled liquid).
- **Step 3 (3 → 4): Throttling** at the expansion valve. Enthalpy remains constant: $h_3 = h_4$. Pressure and temperature both **drop drastically**.
- **Step 4 (4 → 1): Evaporation** in the evaporator. The liquid-vapour mixture absorbs **latent heat of vaporisation** from the cold space and becomes **saturated vapour**.

## 2.3 Why "Compression" and not "Absorption"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **VCRC** uses **mechanical work input** (compressor) — covered in this experiment.
> - **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Cycle (VARC)** uses **thermal energy input** (heater + absorber + generator + pump) — covered in Module 4 theory, **NOT** this lab experiment.
> Board questions often confuse the two — **don't fall for it**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory for ESE & Lab Viva)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Per kg of refrigerant |
| 2 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{V_s \times \rho}{60}$ | $\text{kg/s}$ | From rotameter / flow meter |
| 3 | Refrigeration capacity | $Q_e = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | $\text{kW}$ | Total cooling rate |
| 4 | Compressor work (per kg) | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Isentropic compression |
| 5 | Compressor power input | $W_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | $\text{kW}$ | Electrical equivalent ≈ 1.2 × this |
| 6 | Heat rejected in condenser | $q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | Per kg |
| 7 | Total heat rejected | $Q_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3)$ | $\text{kW}$ | Energy balance check |
| 8 | Theoretical COP | $COP_{th} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | KTU board favourite |
| 9 | Actual (Experimental) COP | $COP_{exp} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{c,electrical}}$ | dimensionless | From direct measurements |
| 10 | Tonnage of Refrigeration | $TR = \dfrac{Q_e \text{ (kW)}}{3.517}$ | TR | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ |
| 11 | Energy balance check | $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$ | $\text{kW}$ | Should hold within ±5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold condition for $|x|$ in tables:** Use the symbol `\vert` inside LaTeX — for example, $\vert x \vert$ — because the plain vertical bar `|` will break the markdown table structure.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The VCRC test rig directly mirrors the operation of:
- **Domestic refrigerators** (0.1 – 1 TR)
- **Window/split air conditioners** (1 – 3 TR)
- **Cold storage warehouses** (10 – 100 TR)
- **Industrial chillers** for plastic, pharma, dairy industries (50 – 5000 TR)

Performance parameters measured in this lab are used by HVAC engineers to **select, rate, and commission refrigeration equipment** in real industrial installations.
