<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Coefficient of Performance (COP) from First Principles

**Starting definition:**
The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired output (heat extracted from the cold space) to the required input (work supplied to the compressor).

$$COP_{R} \;=\; \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} \;=\; \frac{Q_e}{W_c}$$

**Applying steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to each component:**

For the **evaporator** (control volume, steady-state, neglecting KE & PE):

$$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}_e$ = rate of heat absorption in the evaporator (kW)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant (kg/s)
- $h_1$ = specific enthalpy at evaporator exit (saturated vapour) (kJ/kg)
- $h_4$ = specific enthalpy at evaporator inlet (after throttling) (kJ/kg)

For the **compressor** (assuming adiabatic, isentropic compression):

$$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

where:
- $\dot{W}_c$ = compressor work rate (kW)
- $h_2$ = specific enthalpy at compressor exit (superheated vapour) (kJ/kg)

**Substituting into the COP definition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_R &= \frac{\dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)} \\[4pt]
COP_R &= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \\[4pt]
COP_R &= \frac{RE}{w_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **theoretical (Carnot-equivalent ideal)** COP of the simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle on a per-unit-mass basis.

**Deriving Tonnage of Refrigeration:**

A refrigeration system is rated in **Tons of Refrigeration (TR)**. Historically, 1 TR is the heat required to melt 1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.185 kg) of ice in 24 hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{m_{ice} \times h_{fusion}}{t_{24h}} \\[4pt]
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{907.185 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}}{24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} \\[4pt]
1 \text{ TR} &= \frac{303907 \text{ kJ}}{86400 \text{ s}} \\[4pt]
1 \text{ TR} &= 3.517 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} \approx 210 \text{ kJ/min}$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Dec 2023 / May 2024 patterns):**
> A Vapour Compression Refrigeration Test Rig uses refrigerant **R-134a**. The following steady-state readings were recorded:
> - Evaporator pressure: $P_e = 1.4 \text{ bar}$
> - Condenser pressure: $P_c = 8.0 \text{ bar}$
> - Temperature at compressor inlet (state 1): $T_1 = -10°C$ (saturated vapour)
> - Temperature at compressor exit (state 2): $T_2 = 45°C$ (superheated)
> - Temperature at condenser exit (state 3): $T_3 = 30°C$ (sub-cooled liquid)
> - Mass flow rate of refrigerant: $\dot{m} = 0.05 \text{ kg/s}$
> - Electrical power consumed by compressor: $W_{electrical} = 2.0 \text{ kW}$
>
> **Required:**
> (a) Calculate the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**, **Compressor work per kg**, **Heat rejected in condenser per kg**, and **Theoretical COP**.
> (b) Calculate the **Total Refrigeration Capacity** in **kW** and **TR**, then compute the **Experimental COP**, and comment on the deviation between theoretical and experimental values.

### 3.2.1 Reading Enthalpies from R-134a Property Tables

From standard R-134a saturated and superheated tables at the given state points:

| State | Condition | Pressure | Temperature | Enthalpy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturated Vapour | $1.4 \text{ bar}$ | $-10°C$ | $h_1 = 244.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| 2 | Superheated Vapour | $8.0 \text{ bar}$ | $45°C$ | $h_2 = 271.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| 3 | Sub-cooled Liquid | $8.0 \text{ bar}$ | $30°C$ | $h_3 = 92.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| 4 | After throttling | $1.4 \text{ bar}$ | — | $h_4 = h_3 = 92.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |

> Note: State 4 enthalpy is found via **isenthalpic throttling** because the expansion valve is treated as a **steady-flow throttling device** (no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE/PE change): $h_4 = h_3$.

### 3.2.2 Solution to Part (a) — Per-Unit-Mass Analysis

**Step 1 — Refrigerating Effect (per kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt]
RE &= 244.50 - 92.93 \\[4pt]
RE &= 151.57 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compressor work input (per kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 271.71 - 244.50 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 27.21 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected in condenser (per kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_c &= h_2 - h_3 \\[4pt]
q_c &= 271.71 - 92.93 \\[4pt]
q_c &= 178.78 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Coefficient of Performance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{th} &= \frac{RE}{w_c} \\[4pt]
COP_{th} &= \frac{151.57}{27.21} \\[4pt]
COP_{th} &= 5.57 \;\; (\text{dimensionless})
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Solution to Part (b) — Capacity, Tonnage & Experimental COP

**Step 5 — Total Refrigeration Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= \dot{m} \times RE \\[4pt]
Q_e &= 0.05 \times 151.57 \\[4pt]
Q_e &= 7.5785 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Tonnage of Refrigeration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TR &= \frac{Q_e}{3.517} \\[4pt]
TR &= \frac{7.5785}{3.517} \\[4pt]
TR &= 2.155 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the system delivers approximately **2.15 TR** of cooling.

**Step 7 — Experimental (Actual) COP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{exp} &= \frac{Q_e}{W_{electrical}} \\[4pt]
COP_{exp} &= \frac{7.5785}{2.0} \\[4pt]
COP_{exp} &= 3.79
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Energy Balance Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_c &= \dot{m} \times q_c \\[4pt]
Q_c &= 0.05 \times 178.78 \\[4pt]
Q_c &= 8.939 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy balance check: $Q_e + W_c$ (where $W_c$ here is the **isentropic** ideal compressor work, not electrical input)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{c,ideal} &= \dot{m} \times w_c = 0.05 \times 27.21 = 1.3605 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
Q_e + W_{c,ideal} &= 7.5785 + 1.3605 = 8.939 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
Q_c &= 8.939 \text{ kW} \;\;\checkmark \text{ (within rounding tolerance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Deviation Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Deviation} &= \frac{COP_{th} - COP_{exp}}{COP_{th}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5.57 - 3.79}{5.57} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 31.96 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (mandatory KTU board answer statement):**
> The **experimental COP (3.79)** is **lower** than the **theoretical COP (5.57)** by approximately **32%**. This deviation is **expected and unavoidable** because:
> 1. The actual compression process is **not isentropic** (irreversibilities, friction, heat loss).
> 2. The compressor has **mechanical and electrical losses** ($W_{electrical} > W_{c,ideal}$).
> 3. Heat exchangers (condenser/evaporator) have **finite temperature differences** and **pressure drops**, violating ideal assumptions.
> 4. **Sub-cooling and superheating** effects differ from ideal cycle assumptions.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
> *"Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. Why is the COP of a refrigerator always greater than 1?"* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — board-marked key pattern):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the **desired refrigerating effect** (heat extracted from the cold space) to the **work input** supplied to the compressor.

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Why COP > 1 (1 mark):** The desired effect $Q_e$ is always **larger** than the work input $W_c$ because the **refrigerant absorbs additional heat from the surroundings** (in addition to the equivalent of work input). Energy balance dictates $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$, hence $Q_e < Q_c$ but $Q_e > W_c$, making COP always greater than unity. Typical refrigerator COPs range from **2 to 6**.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
> *"State the function of the throttling device in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle. Why is the throttling process considered isenthalpic?"* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Function (1.5 marks):** The throttling device (capillary tube or TXV) performs **two essential functions**:
1. It reduces the **pressure** of the refrigerant from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure.
2. It creates a **sudden temperature drop** by flash evaporation, enabling the refrigerant to absorb heat at low temperature in the evaporator.

**Isenthalpic justification (1.5 marks):** The throttling process is considered isenthalpic because it satisfies all four SFEE assumptions simultaneously:
- **No work** interaction: $W = 0$ (no shaft, no electrical work)
- **No heat transfer**: $Q = 0$ (process is rapid, no time for heat exchange)
- **Negligible KE change**: $\Delta KE \approx 0$ (small change in velocity)
- **Negligible PE change**: $\Delta PE \approx 0$ (small elevation difference)

Applying SFEE: $h_3 = h_4$. Therefore, enthalpy at state 4 **equals** enthalpy at state 3.

---

### Question 11A — Full-Length Board Question (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
> *(a)* Draw the **schematic diagram** and the **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** of a simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) using R-134a. Label all **four state points** and the **four processes** clearly. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* A VCRC test rig using **R-134a** operates between evaporator pressure of **1.6 bar** and condenser pressure of **9.0 bar**. The compressor inlet is at saturated vapour state, and the isentropic compression exit is superheated to **50°C**. The refrigerant is sub-cooled to **32°C** at the condenser exit. The mass flow rate is **0.08 kg/s**.  
> Compute:  
> (i) Refrigerating Effect (RE) per kg  
> (ii) Compressor work per kg  
> (iii) Theoretical COP  
> (iv) Refrigeration capacity in kW and TR  
> (v) Heat rejected in condenser per kg  
> **[7 Marks]**

#### SOLUTION TO 11A(a) — Diagram & Labelling [7 Marks]

**Award scheme as per KTU valuation key:**
- Schematic diagram with all four components correctly labelled → **3 marks**
- P-h diagram with state points 1, 2, 3, 4 correctly placed → **2 marks**
- Process labels (compression, condensation, throttling, evaporation) → **1 mark**
- Direction of flow arrows + mention of saturated curves → **1 mark**

**Schematic (text-rendered — students must draw in exam):**

```
   +------+   (1->2)   +-----+   (2->3)   +-----+   (3->4)   +------+
   |COMP  | ---------> |COND | ---------> |EXV  | ---------> |EVAP  |
   +------+  S.H.Vap   +-----+   Liq      +-----+   Mix       +------+
      ^                                                            |
      |          (4->1)  Sat. Vapour                                |
      +------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**P-h diagram (verbal description for answer sheet):**
- **x-axis:** Enthalpy $h$ (kJ/kg)
- **y-axis:** Pressure $p$ (bar) — logarithmic scale
- **Dome:** Saturated liquid curve (left) and saturated vapour curve (right)
- **Point 1:** Inside the dome, on the saturated vapour line, at $P_e = 1.6$ bar
- **Point 2:** Outside the dome (superheated region), at $P_c = 9.0$ bar and $T_2 = 50°C$
- **Point 3:** On the saturated liquid line (sub-cooled region shown left of the dome), at $P_c = 9.0$ bar, $T_3 = 32°C$
- **Point 4:** Inside the dome, on constant enthalpy line $h_4 = h_3$, at $P_e = 1.6$ bar

**Processes:**
- **1 → 2:** Isentropic compression (curve rising to the right inside superheated region)
- **2 → 3:** Heat rejection in condenser (horizontal line at $P_c$, moving left through dome)
- **3 → 4:** Throttling (vertical line at $h_4 = h_3$, moving down from $P_c$ to $P_e$)
- **4 → 1:** Heat absorption in evaporator (horizontal line at $P_e$, moving right inside dome)

#### SOLUTION TO 11A(b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Read enthalpies from R-134a tables (1 mark):**

| State | Condition | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $h_1$ | Saturated vapour at 1.6 bar | $h_1 = 247.28 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_2$ | Superheated vapour at 9.0 bar, 50°C | $h_2 = 271.71 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_3$ | Sub-cooled liquid at 9.0 bar, 32°C | $h_3 = 96.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_4$ | After throttling | $h_4 = h_3 = 96.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |

**Step 2: Refrigerating Effect per kg (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= h_1 - h_4 \\[4pt]
RE &= 247.28 - 96.50 \\[4pt]
RE &= 150.78 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compressor work per kg (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_c &= h_2 - h_1 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 271.71 - 247.28 \\[4pt]
w_c &= 24.43 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Theoretical COP (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{th} &= \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{150.78}{24.43} = 6.17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Refrigeration Capacity (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= \dot{m} \times RE = 0.08 \times 150.78 = 12.06 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Tonnage of Refrigeration (0.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TR &= \frac{Q_e}{3.517} = \frac{12.06}{3.517} = 3.43 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Heat rejected in condenser per kg (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_c &= h_2 - h_3 = 271.71 - 96.50 = 175.21 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$** — most common mistake. Throttling is isenthalpic; this step is worth at least **1 mark**.
> 2. **Reading wrong table row** — students confuse saturated liquid vs saturated vapour at the same pressure. State 1 = vapour side, State 3 = liquid side.
> 3. **TR conversion error** — using 3.5 instead of 3.517 loses 0.5 marks. Use exact value **3.517**.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of isentropic compression** explicitly in the answer. Examiners deduct **0.5 marks** for this omission.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — every numerical answer MUST carry units (kJ/kg, kW, TR, °C, bar).

### Question 11B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
> *(a)* With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Test Rig** as used in the S3 Thermal Engineering Lab. List the **major instruments** used and state the **experimental procedure** to determine the COP. **[7 Marks]**
>
> *(b)* During a test on a VCRC test rig using R-134a, the following readings are taken:
> - Evaporator pressure = **2.0 bar**; Condenser pressure = **10 bar**
> - Compressor inlet temperature = **−5°C** (sat. vapour)
> - Compressor exit temperature = **48°C** (superheated)
> - Condenser exit temperature = **34°C** (sub-cooled)
> - Mass flow rate of refrigerant = **0.06 kg/s**
> - Power consumed by compressor = **2.5 kW**
>
> Calculate:
> (i) Refrigerating Effect per kg
> (ii) Compressor work per kg
> (iii) Theoretical and Experimental COP
> (iv) Refrigeration capacity in TR
> (v) Comment on why experimental COP is less than theoretical COP.  
> **[7 Marks]**

#### SOLUTION TO 11B(a) — Construction & Working [7 Marks]

**Award scheme per KTU board key:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The VCRC test rig consists of the following major components mounted on a single MS (Mild Steel) skid frame:
- **Compressor** (0.5 to 1 HP hermetically sealed reciprocating type)
- **Air-cooled condenser** with finned copper tubes and axial fan
- **Filter-drier** with molecular sieve and moisture indicator sight glass
- **Thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)** or **capillary tube** as throttling device
- **Evaporator** as a chilled water coil immersed in an insulated water tank
- **Refrigerant** (R-134a) charged in the system

**Working (2 marks):**
The low-pressure saturated vapour refrigerant from the evaporator is drawn into the compressor, where it is compressed to high-pressure superheated vapour. This hot vapour passes through the condenser, where it rejects heat to the atmosphere (via the axial fan) and condenses to a high-pressure sub-cooled liquid. The liquid then passes through the filter-drier (to remove moisture and contaminants) and through the expansion valve, where it undergoes throttling — pressure and temperature drop drastically. The cold low-pressure refrigerant then enters the evaporator, where it absorbs heat from the chilled water, vaporises, and returns to the compressor inlet. The cycle repeats continuously.

**Instruments used (1 mark):**
- 2 Pressure gauges (evaporator and condenser)
- 6 Thermometers (at compressor inlet/exit, condenser inlet/exit, evaporator inlet/exit)
- 1 Rotameter (refrigerant flow measurement)
- 1 Energy meter / Wattmeter (electrical input to compressor)
- 1 Temperature indicator / digital scanner

**Experimental procedure (1 mark):**
1. Switch on the system and allow it to reach steady state (~20–30 minutes).
2. Record all pressure and temperature readings at steady state.
3. Record the refrigerant mass flow rate from the rotameter.
4. Record the compressor electrical power input.
5. Repeat readings 3 times and take the average.
6. Calculate enthalpies from R-134a property tables at the recorded $P$ and $T$.
7. Compute RE, $w_c$, $COP_{th}$, $COP_{exp}$, capacity in TR.

#### SOLUTION TO 11B(b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Read enthalpies from R-134a tables (1 mark):**

| State | Condition | Value |
|---|---|---|
| $h_1$ | Sat. vapour at 2.0 bar, −5°C | $h_1 = 247.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_2$ | Superheated at 10 bar, 48°C | $h_2 = 270.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_3$ | Sub-cooled liquid at 10 bar, 34°C | $h_3 = 101.61 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |
| $h_4$ | After throttling | $h_4 = h_3 = 101.61 \text{ kJ/kg}$ |

**Step 2: RE per kg (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= h_1 - h_4 = 247.32 - 101.61 = 145.71 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compressor work per kg (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_c &= h_2 - h_1 = 270.50 - 247.32 = 23.18 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Theoretical COP (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{th} &= \frac{145.71}{23.18} = 6.286
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Refrigeration capacity (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_e &= \dot{m} \times RE = 0.06 \times 145.71 = 8.7426 \text{ kW} \\[4pt]
TR &= \frac{8.7426}{3.517} = 2.486 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Experimental COP (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP_{exp} &= \frac{Q_e}{W_{electrical}} = \frac{8.7426}{2.5} = 3.497
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Comment on deviation (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Deviation} &= \frac{COP_{th} - COP_{exp}}{COP_{th}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{6.286 - 3.497}{6.286} \times 100 \approx 44.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (mandatory 3-point explanation for full marks):**
The experimental COP is lower than theoretical COP because:
1. **Real compression is irreversible** (not isentropic) — friction, leakage, and heat loss in the compressor.
2. **Electrical & mechanical losses** in the motor-compressor unit — actual electrical power > ideal isentropic work.
3. **Heat exchanger irreversibilities** — finite temperature differences in condenser & evaporator; refrigerant pressure drops in tubes and valves.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q11B:**
> 1. Students often **confuse $h_3$ (sub-cooled) with $h_3$ (saturated liquid at same pressure)**. Read the **temperature column carefully** when the table is in sub-cooled region.
> 2. **Wrong TR formula** — Board key uses $3.517$ kW/TR, not $3.5$ or $3.52$.
> 3. **Missing the 3-point comment** — examiners specifically look for 3 distinct reasons (irreversibility, losses, non-ideal heat exchange). Vague one-line answers lose **0.5 to 1 full mark**.

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Pin this for last-night revision before KTU ESE.**

- **Core definition:** VCRC test rig is used to experimentally determine COP, RE, compressor work, refrigeration capacity (in kW and TR), and condenser heat rejection of a refrigeration system.

- **Four components (must list in order):** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve (TXV/capillary) → Evaporator → back to Compressor.

- **State points on P-h diagram:**
  - **1:** Saturated vapour (evaporator exit / compressor inlet)
  - **2:** Superheated vapour (compressor exit / condenser inlet)
  - **3:** Sub-cooled liquid (condenser exit / valve inlet)
  - **4:** Two-phase mixture (valve exit / evaporator inlet)

- **Key isenthalpic relation:** $h_4 = h_3$ (throttling is the only process where enthalpy is preserved).

- **Most-used formulas (board exam gold):**
  - $RE = h_1 - h_4$
  - $w_c = h_2 - h_1$
  - $COP_{th} = \dfrac{RE}{w_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$
  - $COP_{exp} = \dfrac{Q_e}{W_{electrical}}$
  - $Q_e = \dot{m} \times RE$
  - $TR = \dfrac{Q_e \text{ (kW)}}{3.517}$

- **Mandatory conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min}$

- **Energy balance check:** $Q_c = Q_e + W_c$ (must hold within ±5%).

- **Three golden reasons experimental COP < theoretical COP:**
  1. Irreversible (non-isentropic) compression
  2. Mechanical + electrical losses in motor-compressor
  3. Non-ideal heat transfer in condenser and evaporator

- **Assumptions in ideal VCRC:**
  1. Isentropic compression
  2. No pressure drop in condenser and evaporator
  3. Saturated vapour at compressor inlet
  4. Saturated liquid at condenser exit (we relax this to sub-cooled in the worked example)

- **Common student traps to avoid:**
  - ❌ Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$ (loses 1 mark)
  - ❌ Using $W_{electrical}$ in theoretical COP formula (must use $w_c = h_2 - h_1$)
  - ❌ Writing TR with incorrect conversion factor (must use **3.517**, not 3.5)
  - ❌ Missing units in numerical answers
  - ❌ Not labelling the P-h diagram in clockwise direction with state points 1-2-3-4

- **Real-world connection:** The same R-134a VCRC tested in this lab is used in domestic refrigerators, window ACs, cold storage warehouses, and industrial chillers — making this experiment directly relevant to HVAC industry practice.
